definition: An object is beautiful if and only if (i) it exhibits symmetry, (ii) its parts are harmoniously arranged, and (iii) it produces pleasure upon contemplation.
conditions: exhibits symmetry
parts harmoniously arranged
produces pleasure upon contemplation
counterexample: A wild and irregular mountain landscape exhibits no symmetry and its parts are not harmoniously arranged, yet it produces intense pleasure upon contemplation and is universally regarded as beautiful.
missing_condition: Beauty may arise from grandeur or expressive power, not only from formal properties such as symmetry and harmony.
passage: It has been maintained that an object is beautiful if and only if it exhibits symmetry, its parts are harmoniously arranged, and it produces pleasure upon contemplation. This definition, though it captures a familiar class of beautiful objects — Greek temples, certain musical compositions, the proportions of the human face — proves too narrow upon examination. Consider a wild and irregular mountain landscape, with jagged peaks, disordered clouds, and cascading torrents. There is no symmetry to be found, and its parts are not arranged in any relation that might be called harmonious. Yet the scene produces intense pleasure upon contemplation, and we do not hesitate to call it beautiful. The difficulty is instructive. The definition has identified certain formal properties which are sufficient for beauty in some cases, but it has failed to accommodate the beauty of the irregular, the vast, and the sublime. One suspects that beauty is a wider category than the analysis allows, admitting of instances that owe their beauty to grandeur or expressive power rather than to proportion and regularity.

definition: An object is a work of art if and only if (i) it is produced by a human being, (ii) it is produced with the intention to be appreciated aesthetically, and (iii) it belongs to a recognised artistic medium.
conditions: produced by human being
produced with aesthetic intention
belongs to recognised artistic medium
counterexample: A perfect forgery of a painting is produced by a human being, with the intention that it be appreciated aesthetically, and it belongs to the medium of painting. The three conditions are met, yet we are reluctant to call the forgery a genuine work of art.
missing_condition: The object must be an original expression, not a mere replication of another's creative achievement.
passage: One might define a work of art as an object produced by a human being, with the intention that it be appreciated aesthetically, and belonging to a recognised artistic medium such as painting, sculpture, or music. Yet this definition admits of a troublesome case. Consider a forger of exceptional skill who produces a painting indistinguishable from a masterwork by Vermeer. The forgery is produced by a human being, it is produced with the clear intention that it be appreciated aesthetically — the forger, after all, desires that viewers admire it — and it belongs to the recognised medium of painting. The three conditions are satisfied in full. Yet when the forgery is discovered, we feel strongly that it is not a genuine work of art, or at any rate not a work of art in the same sense as the original. Something is missing from the definition: perhaps the requirement that the object be an original expression, embodying the creative vision of its maker rather than merely replicating the achievement of another.

definition: A person has good taste if and only if (i) he can discriminate fine differences in aesthetic quality, (ii) his judgements are consistent over time, and (iii) his judgements accord with those of other persons of acknowledged taste.
conditions: discriminates fine differences
judgements consistent over time
accords with acknowledged taste
counterexample: A man consistently discriminates fine differences in the aesthetic quality of popular sentimental paintings, and his judgements accord perfectly with those of a large community of like-minded enthusiasts. The three conditions are met, yet we should not on that account say he has good taste.
missing_condition: The objects of judgement must be of genuine aesthetic merit, not merely popular or fashionable.
passage: It is sometimes supposed that a person has good taste if and only if he can discriminate fine differences in aesthetic quality, his judgements are consistent over time, and his judgements accord with those of other persons of acknowledged taste. This analysis, however, proves vulnerable to the following case. Imagine a man who devotes himself to the appreciation of popular sentimental paintings — scenes of weeping children, sunsets over cottages, and the like. Within this genre, he discriminates with extraordinary fineness: he can detect the slightest variation in colouring, composition, and emotional tone. His judgements are perfectly consistent over time, and they accord with those of a large and enthusiastic community of like-minded collectors, each of whom regards the others as persons of taste. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to say that this man has good taste. His discrimination, though genuine, is exercised upon objects of little aesthetic worth. The definition has failed to distinguish between mere consistency of judgement and the capacity to recognise genuine aesthetic excellence.

definition: An experience is aesthetic if and only if (i) attention is directed to the formal properties of an object, (ii) the contemplation is disinterested, and (iii) the experience is accompanied by pleasure or displeasure.
conditions: attention to formal properties
disinterested contemplation
accompanied by pleasure or displeasure
counterexample: A jewel thief studies the formal properties of a diamond with disinterested contemplation and experiences pleasure at its brilliance. The three conditions are met, yet his experience is not aesthetic but acquisitive.
missing_condition: The pleasure must arise from the formal properties themselves, not from associations with the object's monetary or practical value.
passage: The view that an experience is aesthetic if and only if attention is directed to the formal properties of an object, the contemplation is disinterested, and the experience is accompanied by pleasure or displeasure has a distinguished pedigree. Yet it admits of a troublesome case. Suppose a jewel thief, while appraising a diamond he intends to steal, finds himself attending to the formal properties of the stone — its cut, its symmetry, the play of light through its facets. His contemplation is, in a sense, disinterested: he is not at that moment thinking of the price he will obtain, but simply looking at the stone. And the experience is accompanied by pleasure. The three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet we should be reluctant to call this an aesthetic experience in the proper sense, for the pleasure is deeply entangled with the thief's awareness of the diamond's value and his anticipation of possessing it. The definition, it seems, fails to require that the pleasure arise from the formal properties themselves, rather than from extraneous associations.

definition: A work of art is expressive of an emotion if and only if (i) the artist experienced the emotion during creation, (ii) the work evokes the same emotion in the audience, and (iii) the work contains formal properties associated with that emotion.
conditions: artist experienced the emotion
work evokes same emotion in audience
formal properties associated with emotion
counterexample: An actor composes a sorrowful poem while in a state of perfect contentment, using formal properties associated with sorrow, and the poem reliably evokes sorrow in readers. The first condition fails, yet the work is plainly expressive of sorrow.
missing_condition: Expressiveness may depend on the formal properties and their effect alone, without requiring that the artist actually experienced the emotion.
passage: A natural theory of artistic expression holds that a work of art is expressive of an emotion if and only if the artist experienced the emotion during creation, the work evokes the same emotion in the audience, and the work contains formal properties associated with that emotion. But consider the following case. A poet, in a state of perfect contentment, sits down to compose a poem about grief. He is a craftsman of great skill, and he deploys the formal properties associated with sorrow — slow rhythms, descending cadences, images of loss — with consummate art. The resulting poem reliably evokes deep sorrow in its readers. Two of the three conditions are satisfied: the work evokes sorrow and contains the appropriate formal properties. But the first condition fails entirely, for the poet experienced no sorrow whatever during the act of composition. Yet we should not hesitate to say that the poem is expressive of sorrow. The definition, it appears, has confused the biographical circumstances of creation with the expressive character of the work itself.

definition: A work or object is elegant if and only if (i) it achieves its purpose with a minimum of means, (ii) its parts are harmoniously proportioned, and (iii) it conveys an impression of effortlessness.
conditions: achieves purpose with minimum of means
parts are harmoniously proportioned
conveys an impression of effortlessness
counterexample: A mathematical proof achieves its result by the shortest possible route, its steps are perfectly proportioned, and it reads as though no effort were required. Yet the proof establishes a trivial identity that surprises no one. We should hesitate to call it elegant; it is merely neat.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the object of elegance achieve something unexpected or difficult — that the economy of means be exercised upon a worthy challenge.
passage: One might propose that a work or object is elegant if and only if it achieves its purpose with a minimum of means, its parts are harmoniously proportioned, and it conveys an impression of effortlessness. This analysis captures much: the spare lines of a Georgian facade, the concise proof of a deep theorem, the unhurried stroke of a master calligrapher. In each case, economy, proportion, and ease combine to produce the quality we call elegance. Yet the analysis admits counterexamples. Consider a mathematical proof of the proposition that the sum of any number and zero is that number. The proof may proceed in a single step, its parts are trivially well proportioned, and it certainly conveys an impression of effortlessness, for no effort is required. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should not call this proof elegant; it is merely neat, or even pedestrian. What is missing is the sense that elegance requires the economy of means to be exercised upon a task of sufficient difficulty or complexity — that the effortlessness be, as it were, hard-won. A trivial result achieved trivially satisfies the letter of the definition whilst wholly failing to exhibit the quality it purports to analyse.

definition: A dramatic work is a tragedy if and only if (i) its protagonist suffers a reversal of fortune, (ii) the reversal arises from the protagonist's own actions or character, and (iii) the work elicits pity and fear in the audience.
conditions: protagonist suffers a reversal of fortune
reversal arises from protagonist's own actions or character
work elicits pity and fear in the audience
counterexample: A melodrama depicts a wholly virtuous heroine whose fortune is destroyed by a villain's machinations. The audience feels pity and fear, and there is a clear reversal, but the reversal does not arise from the heroine's own actions or character. By the second condition, this is no tragedy — yet many would call it one.
missing_condition: The definition is too narrow in requiring that the reversal originate in the protagonist's own character; tragedies of fate or external circumstance are thereby excluded.
passage: A traditional analysis, owing much to Aristotle, holds that a dramatic work is a tragedy if and only if its protagonist suffers a reversal of fortune, the reversal arises from the protagonist's own actions or character, and the work elicits pity and fear in the audience. This captures the canonical examples admirably: Oedipus, whose curiosity brings about his ruin; Macbeth, whose ambition destroys him. In each case, the protagonist's hamartia is the engine of his downfall. Yet the definition excludes a class of works that are commonly, and not unreasonably, called tragedies. Consider a melodramatic play in which a wholly virtuous heroine is ruined by the machinations of a villain against whom she is powerless. She suffers a devastating reversal of fortune, and the audience is moved to pity and fear. The first and third conditions are satisfied. But the second is not: the reversal does not arise from her own character or actions but from external malice. On the proposed analysis, the work is not a tragedy. Yet one might reasonably protest that tragedies of fate or circumstance — in which the protagonist is destroyed by forces beyond his control — constitute a genuine, if distinct, species of the tragic. The definition has been unduly influenced by a particular tradition and fails to accommodate the broader range of works that merit the title.

definition: A work is a comedy if and only if (i) it depicts characters of lower or ordinary station, (ii) it is designed to provoke laughter, and (iii) it concludes with a happy resolution.
conditions: depicts characters of lower or ordinary station
designed to provoke laughter
concludes with a happy resolution
counterexample: A satirical novel depicts characters of ordinary station, provokes frequent laughter, and concludes with the protagonists contentedly settled. Yet its purpose is not amusement but savage moral criticism, and the laughter it provokes is bitter. One hesitates to call this a comedy in the same sense as a light theatrical farce.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between comic and satirical works; it omits the requirement that the laughter be fundamentally good-natured or that the resolution be genuinely festive.
passage: An analysis of comedy might propose that a work is a comedy if and only if it depicts characters of lower or ordinary station, is designed to provoke laughter, and concludes with a happy resolution. This is serviceable for the standard cases: the Restoration comedy of manners, the farce, the romantic comedy in which misunderstandings are cleared and lovers united. In each instance the conditions are plainly satisfied. Yet consider a satirical novel that depicts ordinary citizens in a provincial town. It is undeniably designed to provoke laughter; the reader laughs frequently and loudly. It concludes with the protagonists contentedly settled in their mediocrity. All three conditions are met. Yet the laughter the work provokes is bitter and derisive; its purpose is not to amuse but to expose moral corruption and intellectual vanity. The happy ending is ironic, a portrait of complacency rather than genuine resolution. One should hesitate to class this work alongside the festive comedy that celebrates reconciliation. The definition has failed to distinguish the comic from the satirical, treating all laughter as equivalent and all happy endings as genuine. Comedy, it appears, requires not merely laughter and resolution, but a particular spirit — one of festivity, reconciliation, or at least good humour — that satire characteristically lacks.

definition: A work of art is original if and only if (i) it is not a copy or reproduction of an existing work, (ii) it introduces formal or thematic elements not present in prior works, and (iii) these elements are the product of the artist's own invention.
conditions: not a copy or reproduction
introduces novel formal or thematic elements
novel elements are the artist's own invention
counterexample: An artist, entirely ignorant of the Impressionist movement, independently arrives at techniques closely resembling those of Monet. His work is not a copy, the techniques are his own invention, and they are novel relative to his own experience. Yet they are not novel in the relevant historical sense. One is uncertain whether to call the work original.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify the relevant frame of reference for novelty — whether it is the artist's own corpus, the local tradition, or the entire history of art.
passage: It is tempting to analyse originality as follows: a work of art is original if and only if it is not a copy of an existing work, it introduces formal or thematic elements not present in prior works, and these elements are the product of the artist's own invention. This captures the intuition that originality involves novelty and creative agency. The analysis, however, encounters a difficulty when the frame of reference for novelty is considered. Suppose an artist, working in complete isolation and entirely ignorant of the Impressionist movement, independently arrives at techniques closely resembling those developed by Monet and Renoir some decades earlier. His paintings are not copies; the techniques are genuinely his own invention; and they are novel relative to his own prior work and that of his local tradition. Yet in the broader context of art history, the techniques are familiar — they were introduced half a century before. Whether the work is original depends entirely upon the frame of reference one adopts, and the definition provides no guidance on this point. It speaks of 'prior works' without specifying whether these include all works that have ever existed, or only those accessible to the artist. The analysis is thus structurally incomplete: it invokes novelty as a condition without fixing the domain against which novelty is to be measured.

definition: A work of art is authentic if and only if (i) it was produced by the artist to whom it is attributed, (ii) it was produced by the process characteristic of that artist's practice, and (iii) it has not been materially altered since its completion.
conditions: produced by the attributed artist
produced by the artist's characteristic process
not materially altered since completion
counterexample: A Renaissance master paints a panel, employing his characteristic technique, and the panel has survived unaltered. Yet the painting was executed entirely by his own hand only because the master was forced to paint under duress by a patron who dictated every compositional decision. All three conditions are met, yet the work's authenticity as an expression of the master's artistic vision is in doubt.
missing_condition: The definition neglects the requirement that the work express the artist's own artistic intention and not merely be physically produced by his hand.
passage: A standard account proposes that a work of art is authentic if and only if it was produced by the artist to whom it is attributed, was produced by the process characteristic of that artist's practice, and has not been materially altered since its completion. These conditions serve well in ordinary cases of authentication: the painting is genuine if it was done by the master's hand, in his usual technique, and has not been over-painted. Yet one may imagine a case in which every condition is satisfied and yet the work's authenticity, in a deeper sense, is questionable. Suppose a Renaissance master paints a panel entirely by his own hand, employing his characteristic technique of layered glazes, and the work has survived intact. But suppose further that the master painted under severe duress, with a powerful patron dictating every compositional choice — the arrangement of figures, the colour of drapery, the gesture of every hand. The three conditions are met: the attribution is correct, the process is characteristic, and the panel is unaltered. Yet one might reasonably question whether the work is authentic in the sense that matters aesthetically — as an expression of the artist's own vision and judgement. The definition has confined authenticity to questions of physical provenance and neglected the expressive dimension: that an authentic work be the embodiment of the artist's free creative intention.

definition: A work is kitsch if and only if (i) it employs conventional imagery designed to elicit a predictable emotional response, (ii) it lacks formal complexity or ambiguity, and (iii) it is produced for mass consumption.
conditions: employs conventional imagery for predictable emotional response
lacks formal complexity or ambiguity
produced for mass consumption
counterexample: A propaganda poster employs conventional patriotic imagery, lacks formal complexity, and is produced for mass distribution. It satisfies all three conditions. Yet its purpose is political persuasion, not the cheap emotional gratification that characterises kitsch; it is propaganda rather than kitsch proper.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that kitsch aim at sentimental self-indulgence rather than at any external practical or ideological purpose.
passage: One analysis of kitsch proposes that a work is kitsch if and only if it employs conventional imagery designed to elicit a predictable emotional response, it lacks formal complexity or ambiguity, and it is produced for mass consumption. This analysis captures the paradigmatic examples: the sentimental calendar print, the porcelain figurine of a weeping child, the romantic novel that follows an invariable formula. In each, the imagery is conventional, the form is simple, and the product is aimed at the widest possible audience. Yet consider a wartime propaganda poster. It employs highly conventional patriotic imagery — the flag, the resolute soldier — designed to produce a predictable surge of feeling. It is formally simple, lacking any ambiguity that might complicate its message. And it is produced for mass distribution. All three conditions are met. Yet one should hesitate to classify propaganda as kitsch; its purpose is political mobilisation, not the sentimental self-indulgence that we associate with kitsch. The definition has identified certain formal features of kitsch but has failed to capture its distinctive aim: the invitation to wallow in easy emotion for its own sake, without any purpose beyond the pleasurable rehearsal of familiar feelings.

definition: An element is decorative if and only if (i) it is applied to a functional object or surface, (ii) it serves no structural or practical function, and (iii) its purpose is to enhance the visual appearance of the object or surface.
conditions: applied to a functional object or surface
serves no structural or practical function
purpose is to enhance visual appearance
counterexample: The flying buttresses of a Gothic cathedral are structural elements essential to the building's stability. Yet their elaborately carved surfaces and soaring forms are universally regarded as decorative. The element is structural, not merely applied, yet it is also decorative.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly treats the decorative and the structural as mutually exclusive, failing to accommodate elements that are simultaneously functional and ornamental.
passage: A common account holds that an element is decorative if and only if it is applied to a functional object or surface, serves no structural or practical function, and its purpose is to enhance the visual appearance of the object. This captures the ornamental moulding, the painted border, the gilt frame: each is applied to a functional surface, is structurally superfluous, and exists to beautify. Yet the analysis cannot accommodate cases in which the decorative and the structural coincide. Consider the flying buttresses of a Gothic cathedral. These elements are essential to the building's structural integrity; without them, the walls would collapse under the lateral thrust of the vaulting. They therefore serve a practical function of the first importance — the second condition is not met. Yet their elaborately carved surfaces, their rhythmic repetition along the exterior, and their soaring forms are universally appreciated as decorative. They enhance the visual appearance of the cathedral whilst also holding it up. The definition has erred in treating the decorative and the functional as mutually exclusive categories. It fails to recognise that an element may be at once necessary to the structure and ornamental to the eye — that decoration need not be mere addition but may inhere in the form of the functional element itself.

definition: An object exhibits craftsmanship if and only if (i) it was produced by skilled manual labour, (ii) the producer exercised deliberate control over the process of making, and (iii) the finished object displays technical proficiency.
conditions: produced by skilled manual labour
producer exercised deliberate control over the process
finished object displays technical proficiency
counterexample: A potter of the highest skill throws a vessel on the wheel with full deliberate control, and the vessel displays flawless technical proficiency. Yet the vessel is one of ten thousand identical pieces produced for a factory catalogue. All three conditions are met, yet the object seems to lack the individuality we associate with true craftsmanship.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the object bear the mark of individual judgement or creative engagement, distinguishing craftsmanship from mere factory skill.
passage: A natural definition proposes that an object exhibits craftsmanship if and only if it was produced by skilled manual labour, the producer exercised deliberate control over the process, and the finished object displays technical proficiency. This accommodates the standard cases: the hand-joined cabinet, the hand-thrown pot, the hand-stitched saddle. In each, skilled hands work deliberately, and the result evinces mastery. But consider a factory in which a highly skilled potter produces ten thousand identical vessels. Each is thrown by hand on a wheel; the potter exercises complete deliberate control; and each vessel is technically flawless. All three conditions are satisfied for every vessel. Yet one might observe that a product of such routinised repetition, however skilfully executed, lacks something essential to craftsmanship — a quality of individual attention, of creative engagement with the particular object. The ten-thousandth pot is indistinguishable from the first; the maker's judgement has been replaced by habit. What the definition has failed to capture is the role of individual discernment — the craftsman's responsiveness to the particularities of each piece, which distinguishes genuine craftsmanship from the mechanical exercise of a learned technique.

definition: A work displays imaginative power if and only if (i) it presents content that departs from the actual or the familiar, (ii) this departure is coherent rather than arbitrary, and (iii) it is the product of the artist's mental invention rather than observation.
conditions: content departs from the actual or familiar
departure is coherent rather than arbitrary
product of mental invention rather than observation
counterexample: A landscape painter depicts a scene precisely as he observes it, departing in no respect from the actual. His painting is a product of observation, not invention. Yet the selectivity of his eye — what he chooses to frame, to illuminate, to omit — strikes us as profoundly imaginative.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly equates imagination with departure from actuality, failing to recognise that imaginative power may be exercised in the act of selection and arrangement within the observed world.
passage: One might propose that a work displays imaginative power if and only if it presents content that departs from the actual or the familiar, the departure is coherent rather than arbitrary, and it is the product of the artist's mental invention rather than observation. This analysis captures the force of the term as applied to works of fantasy: the coherent invented world, the surprising yet plausible fictional scenario. Yet it fails when applied to certain works of the first rank. Consider a landscape painter who stations himself before a scene and paints precisely what he sees. No element of his painting departs from the actual; every tree, every shadow corresponds to what he observes. The work is a product of close attention to the visible world, not of mental invention. Yet the painting may strike us as profoundly imaginative. The artist's choice of what to frame, what to illuminate, what to suppress — these acts of selection and emphasis reveal a way of seeing that transforms the familiar into something arresting. The definition has erred in equating imagination with the invention of non-actual content. Imaginative power may equally manifest itself in the arrangement and emphasis of actual content — in seeing what is there in a manner that others have not contrived to see.

definition: A composition is harmonious if and only if (i) its parts bear perceptible relations to one another, (ii) no part is incongruous with the whole, and (iii) the arrangement of parts produces a unified impression.
conditions: parts bear perceptible relations to one another
no part is incongruous with the whole
arrangement produces a unified impression
counterexample: A monotone painting — a canvas of uniform grey — has parts that bear perceptible relations (they are identical), no incongruity is possible, and the impression is perfectly unified. All three conditions are met, yet the result is not harmony but monotony.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that harmony involve a reconciliation of differences — that the parts be diverse enough for their unification to be a genuine achievement.
passage: One analysis holds that a composition is harmonious if and only if its parts bear perceptible relations to one another, no part is incongruous with the whole, and the arrangement produces a unified impression. This captures much of what we mean by harmony: the well-balanced painting in which colour, form, and composition cooperate; the musical passage in which the voices blend. Yet the analysis proves too permissive. Consider a canvas painted entirely in a uniform shade of grey. Its parts — if we can speak of parts — bear the most perceptible relation to one another: they are identical. No part is incongruous, for there is nothing to be incongruous with. The impression is perfectly unified. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the result is not harmony but monotony — the very antithesis of what we prize in a harmonious composition. What the definition has omitted is the requirement that harmony involve a reconciliation of differences: that the parts be sufficiently various for their unification to constitute a genuine achievement. A composition in which there is nothing to reconcile is not harmonious; it is merely uniform. Harmony, it seems, presupposes diversity brought into accord.

definition: An object is ugly if and only if (i) it produces a negative aesthetic response in the observer, (ii) the response is occasioned by the sensory qualities of the object, and (iii) the response is not attributable to moral disapproval or mere personal distaste.
conditions: produces a negative aesthetic response
response is occasioned by sensory qualities
response is not attributable to moral disapproval or personal distaste
counterexample: A Baroque painting of a martyrdom depicts torn flesh with such vivid realism that the observer recoils. The negative response is sensory and aesthetic, not moral. All three conditions are met, yet the painting is widely celebrated as a masterpiece. Ugliness of subject and aesthetic merit coexist in a way the definition cannot accommodate.
missing_condition: The definition conflates the ugliness of the depicted subject with the ugliness of the work itself, failing to distinguish between what is represented and the manner of its representation.
passage: It is natural to suppose that an object is ugly if and only if it produces a negative aesthetic response in the observer, the response is occasioned by the object's sensory qualities, and the response is not reducible to moral disapproval or mere personal distaste. This seems to capture paradigm cases: the ungainly building whose proportions offend the eye, the discordant noise that grates upon the ear. Yet the analysis faces a difficulty when we consider representational art. A Baroque painting of a martyrdom may depict torn flesh and contorted limbs with such vivid realism that the observer recoils. The response is negative, it is occasioned by the sensory qualities of the painting, and it is not moral disapproval — the observer does not condemn the artist but is repelled by what he sees. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the painting is a masterpiece, celebrated precisely for the power with which it renders its terrible subject. We do not call the painting ugly; we call it magnificent. The definition has conflated the ugliness of the subject depicted with the ugliness of the work as such. A painting may represent what is ugly — suffering, deformity, decay — without itself being ugly; indeed, the skill and vision with which it renders its subject may constitute the highest aesthetic achievement.

definition: A work is grotesque if and only if (i) it combines elements normally kept separate — the human and the animal, the comic and the horrific, the beautiful and the repulsive — (ii) the combination produces a sense of disorientation in the observer, and (iii) the disorientation is aesthetically significant rather than merely accidental.
conditions: combines elements normally kept separate
combination produces a sense of disorientation
disorientation is aesthetically significant
counterexample: A surrealist painting places a locomotive in a fireplace, combining the domestic and the industrial in a way that disorients the viewer, and the disorientation is central to the work's purpose. All three conditions are met, yet we call the painting surreal, not grotesque.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the grotesque involve a distortion of organic or bodily form; mere incongruity of category is insufficient.
passage: An analysis of the grotesque might propose that a work is grotesque if and only if it combines elements normally kept separate — the human and the animal, the comic and the horrific — the combination produces disorientation in the observer, and this disorientation is aesthetically significant rather than accidental. The analysis captures familiar examples: the gargoyle that fuses human and bestial features, the Rabelaisian scene in which the hilarious and the revolting are inseparable. Yet its scope is too wide. Consider a surrealist painting that places a locomotive emerging from a domestic fireplace. The work combines elements normally kept separate — the industrial and the domestic, the enormous and the confined — and produces a powerful sense of disorientation that is central to the work's aesthetic purpose. All three conditions are met. Yet we should not call this painting grotesque; we call it surreal. The grotesque, one might observe, requires something the definition omits: a distortion of organic or bodily form, a violation of the integrity of the living body through fusion, exaggeration, or decomposition. The surrealist image disorients by placing familiar objects in unfamiliar relations; the grotesque disorients by deforming the body itself. The definition has identified the genus — unsettling combination — but has failed to specify the differentia that marks the grotesque as a distinct species within it.

definition: An artist is a genius if and only if (i) his work is highly original, (ii) his creative powers are innate rather than acquired, and (iii) his work sets new standards by which subsequent work is judged.
conditions: work is highly original
creative powers innate rather than acquired
work sets new standards for subsequent work
counterexample: Bach's creative powers were developed through decades of meticulous study, imitation, and practice; he copied out the works of Vivaldi and others as a young man and absorbed the techniques of his predecessors with systematic thoroughness. The second condition fails — his powers were acquired, not innate. Yet Bach is universally regarded as a genius of the highest order.
missing_condition: The definition requires that genius be innate, but many acknowledged geniuses developed their extraordinary powers through sustained study and disciplined labour. Genius may consist not in the absence of training but in the capacity to transmute training into something unprecedented.
passage: One might define artistic genius as follows: an artist is a genius if and only if his work is highly original, his creative powers are innate rather than acquired, and his work sets new standards by which subsequent work is judged. The definition captures the romantic conception of genius — the figure who creates as if by divine gift, without precedent and without instruction. Yet the definition encounters a celebrated difficulty when one considers the career of Johann Sebastian Bach. Bach's work is highly original; no composer before him achieved so comprehensive a mastery of counterpoint, harmony, and form. His work has set standards by which all subsequent music is judged; the Well-Tempered Clavier alone has served as a touchstone for two and a half centuries. But Bach's creative powers were not innate in any straightforward sense. He was trained from early childhood by his elder brother; he copied out the compositions of Vivaldi, Buxtehude, and others with painstaking care; he absorbed the techniques of the French and Italian schools through years of systematic study. The second condition fails. Bach's genius was not a gift that descended upon him unbidden but a capacity that was developed through decades of disciplined labour. The definition has confused the product of genius — which is original and standard-setting — with the process by which genius is formed, which may involve sustained and conscious acquisition of skill.

definition: A work of genius is one that (i) could not have been produced by any other person, (ii) transcends the conventions of its genre, and (iii) is immediately recognised as extraordinary by competent judges.
conditions: could not have been produced by any other person
transcends conventions of its genre
immediately recognised as extraordinary by competent judges
counterexample: The poetry of Emily Dickinson transcends the conventions of its genre and could not have been produced by any other person — her idiom is wholly distinctive. Yet her work was not immediately recognised as extraordinary by competent judges; it was scarcely published during her lifetime, and its greatness was acknowledged only decades after her death. The third condition fails, yet her work is now universally regarded as the product of genius.
missing_condition: The definition requires immediate recognition, but the history of art is replete with geniuses whose work was ignored, ridiculed, or misunderstood in their own time. Recognition may be a consequence of genius, but it is neither immediate nor reliable.
passage: One might propose that a work of genius is one that could not have been produced by any other person, that transcends the conventions of its genre, and that is immediately recognised as extraordinary by competent judges. The third condition is intended to distinguish genuine genius from mere eccentricity — the genius produces something that even experts cannot ignore. The definition handles certain cases well: Mozart's mature operas were recognised as extraordinary from their first performances. Yet the definition founders upon the numerous works of genius that were not immediately recognised. Consider the poetry of Emily Dickinson. Her work transcends the conventions of her genre — her metres are irregular, her rhymes are slant, her imagery is startling, and her compression of thought is without precedent in English verse. Her idiom is so distinctive that it could not have been produced by any other person; one recognises a Dickinson poem at a glance. But her work was not immediately recognised as extraordinary by competent judges. She published fewer than a dozen poems during her lifetime, and those that appeared were often edited to conform to conventional expectations. The full extent of her achievement was recognised only decades after her death. The third condition fails entirely. The definition has assumed that genius compels immediate assent, but the history of art teaches otherwise.

definition: A work possesses a distinctive style if and only if (i) it exhibits recurring formal features, (ii) those features are attributable to the individual artist rather than to the tradition, and (iii) the features are intentionally deployed.
conditions: exhibits recurring formal features
features attributable to individual artist not tradition
features intentionally deployed
counterexample: The mature paintings of Cezanne exhibit a distinctive handling of colour and form — the passage of small planes of colour, the flattening of perspective, the constructive brushstroke — that is unmistakably his own. Yet Cezanne himself reported that he was not entirely in command of his method; he spoke of struggling to realise his sensations and of failing to achieve what he intended. Many of his most characteristic stylistic features emerged from the tension between intention and execution, not from deliberate deployment. The third condition fails, yet Cezanne's style is among the most distinctive in the history of painting.
missing_condition: The definition requires intentional deployment, but a distinctive style may emerge from habitual practice, temperamental inclination, or the unplanned interaction of intention and medium. Style is often something an artist discovers rather than designs.
passage: One might define distinctive style as follows: a work possesses a distinctive style if and only if it exhibits recurring formal features, those features are attributable to the individual artist rather than to the tradition in which he works, and the features are intentionally deployed. The definition captures the deliberate stylist — the architect who chooses his vocabulary of forms, the prose writer who cultivates a particular cadence. Yet it excludes a great many artists whose styles, though unmistakable, were not the product of conscious design. Consider the mature paintings of Cezanne. They exhibit a distinctive handling of colour and form that is recognisable at a glance: the passage of small, discrete planes of colour, the flattening of perspective, the constructive brushstroke that builds the surface of the canvas like masonry. These features are attributable to Cezanne and not to the Impressionist tradition from which he emerged. But Cezanne himself reported that he was not fully in command of his technique. He spoke repeatedly of struggling to realise his sensations, of failing to achieve what he intended, of a gap between conception and execution. Many of his most characteristic stylistic features arose from this struggle — from the interaction of his ambition with the resistance of his medium — rather than from deliberate deployment. The third condition fails. The definition has assumed that style is a matter of intention, but in Cezanne's case, and in many others, style is something discovered in the course of working, not planned in advance.

definition: An artist is inspired if and only if (i) he experiences a sudden influx of creative ideas, (ii) the ideas come unbidden and without deliberate effort, and (iii) the resulting work is of higher quality than his usual production.
conditions: experiences sudden influx of creative ideas
ideas come unbidden without deliberate effort
resulting work of higher quality than usual
counterexample: Beethoven's late string quartets are the product of inspiration by any reasonable assessment, yet they were not composed in sudden influxes. Beethoven's sketchbooks reveal that he laboured over these works for months, testing and rejecting ideas, revising passages dozens of times. The ideas did not come unbidden; they were wrested from the material through sustained deliberate effort. The first and second conditions fail, yet the quartets are among the most inspired works in the history of music.
missing_condition: The definition models inspiration on the sudden vision or flash of insight, but inspiration may manifest as a sustained elevation of creative capacity that operates through, rather than instead of, deliberate effort. The definition cannot accommodate the artist who is inspired precisely in his labour.
passage: One might propose that an artist is inspired if and only if he experiences a sudden influx of creative ideas, the ideas come unbidden and without deliberate effort, and the resulting work is of higher quality than his usual production. This captures the popular image of inspiration: the poet who wakes with a stanza fully formed, the composer who hears a melody in a dream. Yet the definition excludes what is perhaps the most important form of artistic inspiration. Consider Beethoven's late string quartets — the works of opus 127, 130, 131, 132, and 135. They are, by universal acknowledgement, among the most profoundly inspired works in the history of music. Yet they were not composed in sudden influxes. Beethoven's sketchbooks, which survive in great numbers, reveal that he laboured over these quartets for months, sometimes years. He tested melodic ideas and rejected them; he drafted passages and revised them dozens of times; he struggled with problems of form and proportion that yielded only to persistent effort. The ideas did not come unbidden; they were extracted from the musical material through sustained, conscious, deliberate toil. The first and second conditions fail. The definition has modelled inspiration upon the romantic notion of effortless creation, but for Beethoven, and for a great many other artists, inspiration is inseparable from labour.

definition: A work of art is an imitation if and only if (i) it represents an object or scene from the natural world, (ii) its value consists in the accuracy of the representation, and (iii) the viewer can identify the object represented.
conditions: represents object or scene from natural world
value consists in accuracy of representation
viewer can identify object represented
counterexample: A caricature by Daumier represents a political figure — one can identify the person depicted — and it represents a scene from the social world. But its value does not consist in accuracy; on the contrary, its power lies in deliberate distortion, in the exaggeration of features that reveals character more vividly than any faithful likeness could. The second condition fails, yet the caricature is an imitation in a perfectly good sense — it imitates a person, albeit by way of expressive distortion rather than faithful copying.
missing_condition: The definition ties the value of imitation to accuracy, but imitation may also operate through selective distortion, exaggeration, or idealisation, and the resulting work may be more revealing than any accurate copy. The definition confuses imitation with copying.
passage: One might define artistic imitation as follows: a work of art is an imitation if and only if it represents an object or scene from the natural world, its value consists in the accuracy of the representation, and the viewer can identify the object represented. The definition captures the trompe-l'oeil painting and the wax figure: each aims at accurate representation, and the viewer's pleasure consists in marvelling at the fidelity of the likeness. Yet the definition is too narrow. Consider a caricature by Honore Daumier. Daumier depicts a political figure — a judge, a parliamentarian, a bourgeois — and the viewer can identify the person represented; the third condition is satisfied. The work represents a figure from the social world; in a broad sense, the first condition is met. But the value of the caricature does not consist in accuracy. On the contrary, its power lies in deliberate and systematic distortion: the nose is enlarged, the brow is furrowed, the chin is elongated, the posture is exaggerated. It is precisely through this distortion that Daumier reveals the character of his subject — his pomposity, his vanity, his cruelty — more vividly than any faithful portrait could. The second condition fails. The definition has equated imitation with accurate copying, but the tradition of imitation in art is far broader, encompassing idealisation, caricature, and expressive distortion as legitimate modes of representing the world.

definition: The form of a work of art consists of (i) the arrangement of its parts, (ii) the relations among those parts, and (iii) the perceptible pattern that emerges from the arrangement, where form is independent of content.
conditions: arrangement of parts
relations among parts
perceptible pattern emerging from arrangement, independent of content
counterexample: In a poem by George Herbert, such as 'Easter Wings,' the form — the visual shape of the poem on the page — directly expresses the content: the lines narrow and expand to represent the fall and rise of the soul. The form is not independent of the content; it is constitutive of it. The third condition, with its requirement of independence, fails, yet the poem has form in the fullest sense.
missing_condition: The definition insists on the independence of form from content, but in many works of art — concrete poetry, programme music, narrative architecture — form and content are interdependent, each shaping and being shaped by the other. The separation assumed by the definition is a theoretical abstraction that does not hold universally.
passage: One might propose that the form of a work of art consists of the arrangement of its parts, the relations among those parts, and the perceptible pattern that emerges from the arrangement, where form is independent of content. The independence clause is intended to capture the intuition that form is something distinct from what the work says or represents — it is the how, not the what. This works well enough for abstract art: the form of a fugue by Bach consists in the arrangement of voices, the relations of imitation and inversion, and the perceptible pattern of entry and response, and these can be described without reference to any extra-musical content. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty with works in which form and content are inseparable. Consider George Herbert's poem 'Easter Wings,' a devotional lyric in which the printed lines of the poem narrow to a point at the centre and expand again at the margins, so that the poem on the page has the visual shape of a pair of wings. The form — the narrowing and expansion — directly expresses the content: the contraction of the soul in sin and its expansion in redemption. The form is not independent of the content; it is constitutive of it. One cannot describe the form without describing the content, or the content without describing the form. The third condition fails. The definition has assumed a separation of form and content that is theoretically attractive but does not hold in all cases.

definition: The content of a work of art is (i) the subject matter it represents, (ii) the ideas or emotions it expresses, and (iii) it is extractable from the work and statable in non-artistic terms.
conditions: subject matter represented
ideas or emotions expressed
extractable and statable in non-artistic terms
counterexample: The content of a late Rothko painting — a large canvas divided into soft-edged rectangles of sombre colour — resists extraction. The painting expresses something — a mood, a gravity, an intimation of the numinous — but this something cannot be stated in non-artistic terms without falsification. Any prose paraphrase betrays the content, for the content is inseparable from the particular arrangement of colour, scale, and surface. The third condition fails, yet the painting has content.
missing_condition: The definition requires that content be extractable and paraphrasable, but some artistic content is so intimately bound to the medium and form that it cannot be restated without loss. The definition has confused content with paraphrasable meaning.
passage: One might define the content of a work of art as the subject matter it represents, the ideas or emotions it expresses, where this content is extractable from the work and statable in non-artistic terms. The extractability condition distinguishes content from form: the content is what can be taken out and put into words, while the form is the manner of presentation that remains behind. This definition works for narrative art: the content of a novel can be summarised, the ideas of a philosophical poem can be restated in prose, the subject of a portrait can be described. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty with works whose content resists paraphrase. Consider a late painting by Rothko — a large canvas divided into two or three soft-edged rectangles of deep, sombre colour. The painting expresses something: a mood of gravity, an intimation of the numinous, a quality of depth that arrests the viewer and compels contemplation. But this content cannot be stated in non-artistic terms without falsification. Any prose description — 'it is about mortality,' 'it expresses spiritual longing' — captures at most a fraction of what the painting conveys and distorts even that fraction by translating it into a medium to which it does not belong. The third condition fails. The content of the Rothko is inseparable from the particular configuration of colour, scale, and surface that constitutes the painting itself.

definition: A work is a narrative if and only if (i) it recounts a sequence of events, (ii) the events are connected by causal or temporal relations, and (iii) the events are recounted by an identifiable narrator.
conditions: recounts sequence of events
events connected by causal or temporal relations
events recounted by identifiable narrator
counterexample: A film such as Dziga Vertov's Man with a Movie Camera recounts a sequence of events — scenes of urban life — connected by temporal and associative relations. Yet there is no identifiable narrator; there is no voice, no character, no persona who tells the story. The camera observes, but no one narrates. The third condition fails, yet the film is widely regarded as a narrative work — a visual narrative of city life.
missing_condition: The definition requires an identifiable narrator, but many narratives — especially in film, visual art, and experimental literature — present sequences of events without any narrating figure. Narrative may be constituted by the arrangement of events alone, without a mediating narrator.
passage: One might define a narrative as a work that recounts a sequence of events, where the events are connected by causal or temporal relations, and the events are recounted by an identifiable narrator. The third condition distinguishes narrative from mere chronicle: a narrative has a teller, a voice that organises the material and addresses the audience. This definition captures the novel, the oral tale, the memoir. In each case a narrator — whether an author, a character, or an impersonal voice — recounts the events. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty with works that are plainly narrative but possess no identifiable narrator. Consider a film such as Dziga Vertov's Man with a Movie Camera. The film recounts a sequence of events — scenes of city life from dawn to dusk — connected by temporal succession and visual association. It is, by common critical assessment, a narrative of urban existence. But there is no identifiable narrator. No voice speaks; no character tells the story; no titles explain the action. The camera observes, the editor arranges, but no persona narrates. The third condition fails. The definition has assumed that narrative requires a narrating agent, but in cinema, in visual art, and in certain experimental forms of literature, narrative may be constituted by the arrangement of events alone, without any mediating figure who 'tells' them.

definition: An element in a work of art is symbolic if and only if (i) it represents something beyond itself, (ii) the representation is conventional or culturally established, and (iii) the viewer or reader can decode the symbolic meaning.
conditions: represents something beyond itself
representation conventional or culturally established
viewer or reader can decode symbolic meaning
counterexample: The white whale in Melville's Moby-Dick represents something beyond itself — it has been interpreted as nature, as God, as the unknowable, as obsession, as America itself. It functions as a symbol of extraordinary power. Yet its symbolic meaning is not conventional or culturally established; there is no tradition in which a white whale stands for any particular thing. Nor can the reader 'decode' the meaning in the way one decodes an allegory; the symbol resists definitive interpretation. The second and third conditions fail, yet Moby Dick is among the most potent symbols in literature.
missing_condition: The definition restricts symbolism to conventional and decodable signs, but the most powerful symbols in art — Melville's whale, Kafka's castle, Beckett's tree — operate precisely through their resistance to definitive interpretation. They are symbols not because their meaning is established but because their meaning is inexhaustible.
passage: One might define symbolic meaning as follows: an element in a work of art is symbolic if and only if it represents something beyond itself, the representation is conventional or culturally established, and the viewer or reader can decode the symbolic meaning. This captures the standard cases of symbolism: the cross represents Christianity, the laurel wreath represents victory, the skull represents mortality. In each case the symbolic association is conventional, and the viewer who knows the convention can decode the meaning. Yet the definition proves too narrow for the most important symbols in literature. Consider the white whale in Melville's Moby-Dick. It represents something beyond itself — of that there is no doubt; critics have interpreted it as a symbol of nature, of God, of the unknowable, of obsession, of the inscrutable malice of the universe. It functions as a symbol of extraordinary power. But its symbolic meaning is not conventional or culturally established. There is no tradition in which a white whale stands for any determinate thing. Nor can the reader decode the meaning in the way one decodes a conventional allegory; the symbol resists definitive interpretation, and every reading leaves a remainder. The second and third conditions fail. The definition has modelled symbolism upon the conventional sign, but the most powerful symbols in art derive their force precisely from their inexhaustibility — from the fact that they cannot be decoded and set aside.

definition: A work is well-proportioned if and only if (i) its parts stand in mathematically harmonious ratios to one another, (ii) the ratios conform to an established canon of proportion, and (iii) the proportions are perceptible to the viewer.
conditions: parts in mathematically harmonious ratios
ratios conform to established canon
proportions perceptible to viewer
counterexample: The Parthenon incorporates subtle deviations from strict mathematical regularity — its columns lean slightly inward, its stylobate curves gently upward — that are designed to correct optical illusions and produce an appearance of perfect proportion. The actual ratios do not conform to the established canon; they deliberately depart from it. The second condition fails, yet the Parthenon is regarded as one of the most perfectly proportioned buildings in existence.
missing_condition: The definition requires conformity to a canon, but good proportion may consist in the skilled departure from canonical ratios — in adjustments that correct for the conditions of perception and produce an effect of harmony that strict adherence to the canon would not achieve.
passage: One might define good proportion as follows: a work is well-proportioned if and only if its parts stand in mathematically harmonious ratios to one another, the ratios conform to an established canon of proportion, and the proportions are perceptible to the viewer. The definition captures the classical ideal: the Doric temple whose columns stand in a fixed ratio to the entablature, the Renaissance facade whose bays are regulated by the golden section. Yet the definition encounters a celebrated difficulty with the Parthenon. The builders of the Parthenon incorporated subtle deviations from strict mathematical regularity throughout the structure. The columns lean slightly inward; the stylobate — the platform upon which the columns stand — curves gently upward at its centre; the corner columns are slightly thicker than the others. These refinements were designed to correct optical illusions: a perfectly straight stylobate appears to sag, perfectly vertical columns appear to lean outward, and corner columns appear thinner against the sky. The actual ratios of the Parthenon do not conform to the established canon; they deliberately depart from it. The second condition fails. Yet the Parthenon is regarded, by near-universal consensus, as one of the most perfectly proportioned buildings ever erected. Good proportion, it appears, may consist not in adherence to a mathematical canon but in the skilled departure from that canon in the service of perceptual harmony.

definition: A sequence has rhythm if and only if (i) it consists of regularly recurring elements, (ii) the recurrence is periodic, and (iii) the pattern of recurrence is perceptible to the listener or viewer.
conditions: consists of regularly recurring elements
recurrence is periodic
pattern perceptible to listener or viewer
counterexample: The prose of Virginia Woolf possesses a distinctive rhythm that is perceptible to any attentive reader — a rhythm of long, flowing sentences punctuated by short, declarative ones. Yet this rhythm is not periodic; the long sentences vary in length, the punctuations occur at irregular intervals, and the pattern, though recognisable, does not recur at fixed periods. The second condition fails, yet the prose has rhythm in a perfectly genuine sense.
missing_condition: The definition requires strict periodicity, but rhythm in prose, in free verse, and in much modern music is constituted by patterns of recurrence that are recognisable without being strictly periodic. The definition has modelled rhythm upon the metronome and has thereby excluded the subtler, non-periodic rhythms of literary and musical art.
passage: One might define rhythm as follows: a sequence has rhythm if and only if it consists of regularly recurring elements, the recurrence is periodic, and the pattern of recurrence is perceptible to the listener or viewer. The definition captures the clearest cases: the iambic pentameter of English verse, the four-four time of a march, the regular pulse of a waltz. In each case the recurring element — the stressed syllable, the downbeat — returns at fixed intervals. Yet the definition proves too restrictive. Consider the prose of Virginia Woolf. Her writing possesses a distinctive rhythm that is immediately perceptible to any attentive reader: long, sinuous sentences that accumulate clauses and qualifications, punctuated by short, declarative statements that arrive with the force of an interrupted breath. The rhythm is real and recognisable. But it is not periodic. The long sentences vary in length — one may run for half a page, the next for a few lines. The short statements occur at irregular intervals. The pattern is recognisable but not predictable; it is felt rather than counted. The second condition fails. The definition has modelled rhythm upon the strict periodicity of the metronome, but rhythm in prose, in free verse, and in a great deal of modern music is constituted by patterns of recurrence that are recognisable without being regular. What is essential to rhythm is the sense of patterned movement, not the mathematical regularity of the pattern.

definition: A work is ironic if and only if (i) its surface meaning differs from its intended meaning, (ii) the discrepancy is deliberate on the part of the author, and (iii) the audience is expected to recognise the discrepancy.
conditions: surface meaning differs from intended meaning
discrepancy deliberate on author's part
audience expected to recognise discrepancy
counterexample: Consider dramatic irony in a tragedy: the audience knows that the hero is walking into a trap, but the hero does not. The surface meaning of the hero's confident words does not differ from the hero's intended meaning — he means exactly what he says. The irony consists not in a discrepancy between what the speaker means and what his words mean, but in a discrepancy between what the speaker knows and what the audience knows. The first condition fails in its standard interpretation, yet dramatic irony is a genuine and important form of irony.
missing_condition: The definition models irony on verbal irony — saying one thing and meaning another — but dramatic irony, situational irony, and structural irony operate through discrepancies between knowledge, expectation, and outcome rather than between surface and intended meaning. The definition captures one species of irony and mistakes it for the genus.
passage: One might define irony as follows: a work is ironic if and only if its surface meaning differs from its intended meaning, the discrepancy is deliberate on the part of the author, and the audience is expected to recognise the discrepancy. This captures the standard cases of verbal irony: Swift's 'A Modest Proposal,' in which the surface meaning — that the Irish poor should sell their children as food — differs violently from the intended meaning — that English policy towards Ireland is monstrous. The discrepancy is deliberate, and the reader is expected to see through the surface to the real meaning beneath. Yet the definition does not accommodate dramatic irony. Consider a tragedy in which the hero, confident and cheerful, walks unknowingly towards his destruction. The audience knows what the hero does not: that his friend has betrayed him, that his enemies await him, that his confidence is misplaced. The hero's words mean exactly what he intends them to mean; there is no discrepancy between his surface meaning and his intended meaning. He is not saying one thing and meaning another. The irony consists entirely in the discrepancy between what the hero knows and what the audience knows — between the character's ignorance and the spectator's foreknowledge. The first condition fails. The definition has modelled all irony upon verbal irony and has thereby excluded the dramatic, situational, and structural ironies that are among the most powerful resources of literary art.

definition: A work of art produces catharsis if and only if (i) it arouses strong emotions in the audience, (ii) the emotions are painful — pity, fear, grief — and (iii) the audience experiences emotional relief or purgation upon the conclusion of the work.
conditions: arouses strong emotions in audience
emotions are painful — pity fear grief
audience experiences relief or purgation at conclusion
counterexample: A listener who hears the slow movement of Beethoven's seventh symphony may experience a profound emotional response — a deep melancholy, a sense of solemnity, a feeling of being moved to the core — but may find that the emotion does not resolve into relief or purgation at the movement's close. The feeling persists; it lingers; the listener carries it away from the concert hall. The third condition fails, yet the experience is, by common testimony, cathartic in some important sense.
missing_condition: The definition models catharsis on the purgative model — emotions are aroused and then discharged — but catharsis may also consist in a deepening or clarification of emotion that does not terminate in relief. The audience may leave not purged but transformed, and this transformation is as genuinely cathartic as any purgation.
passage: One might propose that a work of art produces catharsis if and only if it arouses strong emotions in the audience, the emotions are painful — pity, fear, grief — and the audience experiences emotional relief or purgation upon the conclusion of the work. The definition captures the classical account derived from Aristotle: the spectator of tragedy is moved to pity and fear, and these emotions are purged in the course of the dramatic action, leaving the spectator refreshed and restored. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty with works that produce something one is inclined to call catharsis without producing relief. Consider a listener who hears the slow movement of Beethoven's seventh symphony. The movement arouses strong emotions — a deep melancholy, a sense of solemnity, an ache that intensifies as the theme is repeated and varied. The emotions are, in a broad sense, painful. But when the movement ends, the listener does not experience relief or purgation. The feeling does not discharge; it lingers. The listener leaves the concert hall carrying the emotion with him, altered by it rather than relieved of it. The third condition fails. Yet the experience is described, by many who have had it, as cathartic. The definition has modelled catharsis exclusively on the purgative model, but catharsis may also consist in a deepening, a clarification, or a transformation of emotion that leaves the audience not purged but changed.

definition: An artistic imitation is successful if and only if (i) the imitation produces in the viewer an experience similar to what the original object would produce, (ii) the viewer is aware that he is viewing an imitation and not the original, and (iii) the imitation is produced in the same medium as the works that characterise its art form.
conditions: produces experience similar to original
viewer aware it is imitation not original
produced in medium characteristic of art form
counterexample: A photograph of a landscape produces in the viewer an experience similar to what the landscape itself would produce, and the viewer is aware that he views a photograph and not the real scene. But photography was, for much of its history, denied the status of art precisely because it was produced by a mechanical device rather than in a traditional artistic medium — paint, stone, charcoal. The third condition was held to fail. Yet a photograph may be as successful an imitation of nature as any oil painting, and its artistic merit does not depend upon the medium in which it is produced.
missing_condition: The definition ties successful imitation to a traditional medium, but the history of art is a history of new media — photography, cinema, digital art — that imitate the world as successfully as any traditional medium. What matters is the quality of the imitation, not the material through which it is achieved.
passage: One might hold that an artistic imitation is successful if and only if it produces in the viewer an experience similar to what the original object would produce, the viewer is aware that he is viewing an imitation and not the original, and the imitation is produced in the same medium as the works that characterise its art form. The third condition is intended to exclude mechanical reproduction and restrict imitation to the traditional artistic media. Yet the definition encounters a celebrated difficulty with photography. A photograph of a landscape — a view of the Alps, say, or of the English Lake District — may produce in the viewer an experience remarkably similar to what the landscape itself would produce: the sense of space, the play of light, the arrangement of forms. The viewer is aware that he views a photograph; no confusion between imitation and reality obtains. But photography, for much of its history, was denied the status of an artistic medium. It was produced by a mechanical device — a camera and a chemical process — rather than by the hand of the artist working in paint or stone. The third condition was held to fail. Yet a fine photograph may imitate nature with as much power and subtlety as any oil painting. The definition has tied successful imitation to the accidents of a particular medium, when what matters is the quality of the experience the imitation affords, not the material means by which that experience is produced.

definition: A work of art has significant form if and only if (i) it possesses an arrangement of lines, colours, and shapes that produces an aesthetic emotion, (ii) this arrangement is independent of representational content, and (iii) the aesthetic emotion it produces is uniform across all competent viewers.
conditions: arrangement of lines colours shapes produces aesthetic emotion
arrangement independent of representational content
aesthetic emotion uniform across competent viewers
counterexample: Two equally competent art critics may stand before the same painting by Mondrian — an arrangement of black lines, white rectangles, and primary colours — and experience quite different aesthetic emotions. One may feel exhilaration and clarity; the other may feel severity and restraint. Both are competent; both respond to the form. The third condition fails, yet both are responding to what is, by any reasonable account, significant form.
missing_condition: The definition requires uniformity of aesthetic response among competent viewers, but aesthetic emotion is notoriously variable even among the most informed and sensitive observers. What significant form produces is aesthetic attention, not a uniform emotional response.
passage: One might propose that a work of art has significant form if and only if it possesses an arrangement of lines, colours, and shapes that produces an aesthetic emotion, this arrangement is independent of representational content, and the aesthetic emotion it produces is uniform across all competent viewers. The third condition is intended to secure the objectivity of significant form: if the form is genuinely significant, it should produce the same response in all who are qualified to perceive it. Yet the condition proves too strong. Consider two art critics of equal competence and sensitivity standing before a painting by Mondrian — a composition of black lines enclosing rectangles of white, red, yellow, and blue. One critic experiences a feeling of exhilaration: the clarity of the forms, the precision of the balance, the luminosity of the colours fill him with delight. The other experiences a feeling of austerity and restraint: the same forms convey to him a severity, a discipline, a renunciation of ornament that is impressive but cool rather than exhilarating. Both critics are responding to the same formal properties; neither is incompetent. But their aesthetic emotions differ. The third condition fails. The definition has assumed that significant form produces a determinate, uniform response, but the testimony of competent observers suggests otherwise. What significant form compels is aesthetic attention — the recognition that the arrangement demands to be contemplated — not a specific emotional content that is the same for all.

definition: An object x is beautiful if and only if (i) x possesses a harmony of parts, (ii) x elicits disinterested pleasure in a competent observer, and (iii) x exhibits bilateral symmetry.
conditions: x possesses a harmony of parts
x elicits disinterested pleasure in a competent observer
x exhibits bilateral symmetry
counterexample: A sunset over the sea is widely regarded as beautiful. Its colours blend in a harmony of parts, and it elicits disinterested pleasure in any observer of cultivated sensibility. Yet a sunset possesses no bilateral symmetry whatsoever; its forms are irregular, its cloud-patterns asymmetrical, its gradations of light diffuse and unpredictable. The third condition fails, yet the sunset is beautiful.
missing_condition: The definition mistakes bilateral symmetry for a necessary feature of beauty, when in truth many paradigmatically beautiful objects — sunsets, mountain ranges, the play of light on water — are wholly asymmetrical. What is wanted is a broader notion of formal coherence, not the narrow geometrical requirement of symmetry about an axis.
passage: One might propose that an object is beautiful if and only if it possesses a harmony of parts, it elicits disinterested pleasure in a competent observer, and it exhibits bilateral symmetry. The definition has considerable initial plausibility. Greek sculpture, Renaissance architecture, and the human face — all paradigm cases of beauty — display bilateral symmetry in abundance. The Parthenon is symmetrical about its central axis; the face of the Apollo Belvedere mirrors itself from left to right. One might suppose that symmetry is the structural principle underlying beauty's formal aspect. Yet one must consider the sunset. A sunset over the sea is among the most frequently cited instances of natural beauty, and it satisfies the first two conditions admirably. Its colours blend in subtle harmony, and the pleasure it affords is disinterested in the fullest sense: we do not wish to possess or consume the sunset, but merely to contemplate it. However, a sunset possesses no bilateral symmetry. Its cloud-formations are irregular, its gradations of colour asymmetrical, its luminous effects entirely unpredictable. The same may be said of a waterfall, a wind-blown field of wildflowers, or the aurora borealis. These are objects of acknowledged beauty, yet none exhibits the geometrical property the definition requires. The difficulty reveals that the definition has confused a property frequently accompanying beauty — symmetry — with a necessary condition of beauty itself. What is needed is a notion of formal coherence broad enough to encompass both the symmetrical and the asymmetrical beautiful.

definition: An object x is a work of art if and only if (i) x is produced by a human agent, (ii) x is intended to be contemplated for its aesthetic qualities, and (iii) x employs a recognised artistic medium such as paint, stone, or musical tone.
conditions: x is produced by a human agent
x is intended to be contemplated for its aesthetic qualities
x employs a recognised artistic medium such as paint, stone, or musical tone
counterexample: Marcel Duchamp's readymade, Bottle Rack (1914), was selected by a human agent and presented for contemplation of its aesthetic and conceptual qualities. Yet it is a mass-produced iron object; it employs no recognised artistic medium in the traditional sense — it is not made of paint, stone, or musical tone, but of commercial iron manufactured for domestic utility. The third condition fails, yet it is widely acknowledged as a work of art.
missing_condition: The definition restricts art to traditional media, but a work of art may employ any material whatsoever, including industrially manufactured objects, provided the relevant institutional and intentional conditions are met.
