The concept of an artistic medium is far more elastic than the definition allows.
passage: Suppose one defines a work of art as follows: an object is a work of art if and only if it is produced by a human agent, it is intended to be contemplated for its aesthetic qualities, and it employs a recognised artistic medium such as paint, stone, or musical tone. This definition captures a great many paradigm cases. A painting employs pigment upon canvas; a sculpture employs stone or bronze; a symphony employs musical tones arranged according to principles of harmony and form. The three conditions appear jointly sufficient for the cases one first considers. Yet one must attend to certain developments in the practice of art itself. Consider the readymade objects exhibited in galleries during the early twentieth century — an iron bottle rack, for instance, selected from a department store and presented without material alteration. Such an object is chosen by a human agent, and it is intended for contemplation of a kind that is, at the very least, partly aesthetic. But it employs no recognised artistic medium in the traditional sense. It is made of commercial iron, manufactured for the purpose of drying bottles. The definition excludes it. Yet the critical and institutional consensus treats such objects as works of art, and their influence upon subsequent artistic practice has been considerable. The difficulty is that the definition has identified a contingent feature of many works of art — the employment of traditional media — with a necessary condition of art itself. The history of art reveals that artists may employ virtually any material, and a satisfactory definition must accommodate this latitude.

definition: An experience is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) the object of the experience is vast in physical magnitude, (ii) the experience involves a feeling of being overwhelmed, and (iii) the experience is accompanied by physical fear or danger.
conditions: the object of the experience is vast in physical magnitude
the experience involves a feeling of being overwhelmed
the experience is accompanied by physical fear or danger
counterexample: One may stand in a great cathedral and experience the sublime. The vaulted nave is vast; the experience involves a feeling of being overwhelmed by the grandeur of the space and the solemnity of the architecture. Yet there is no physical fear or danger whatsoever. One is perfectly safe within the cathedral; the feeling is one of awe and elevation, not of peril. The third condition fails, yet the experience is paradigmatically sublime.
missing_condition: The definition conflates the sublime with the terrifying, when in truth the sublime may arise from vastness and overwhelming grandeur without any element of fear or physical danger. The mathematical sublime — the apprehension of infinite magnitude — involves no danger at all.
passage: One might hold that an experience is an experience of the sublime if and only if the object of the experience is vast in physical magnitude, the experience involves a feeling of being overwhelmed, and the experience is accompanied by physical fear or danger. There is much to recommend this view. The storm at sea, the erupting volcano, the precipice of the mountain — these are standard instances of the sublime, and in each case the observer is confronted with vastness, is overwhelmed, and is sensible of danger. The definition appears to capture what is essential to sublimity. Yet one must consider the experience of standing within a great cathedral — the nave of Chartres, perhaps, or the interior of St. Peter's in Rome. The vaulted space is vast, and the experience of gazing upward into the shadowed heights of the ceiling involves a genuine feeling of being overwhelmed. Critics and philosophers have described such experiences as sublime without hesitation. But there is no danger. The observer is sheltered, safe, and at no physical risk whatsoever. The feeling is one of awe and solemn elevation, not of terror. The third condition fails entirely. The same may be said of contemplating the night sky from a calm meadow, or of apprehending the vastness of geological time: these experiences are sublime, yet they involve no physical peril. The definition has mistaken a feature characteristic of one species of the sublime — the dynamical sublime of threatening natural forces — for a necessary condition of all sublimity. What is wanted is a definition that accommodates both the fearful and the tranquil varieties of the experience.

definition: A person S has good taste if and only if (i) S consistently prefers artworks that are highly regarded by experts, (ii) S is able to articulate reasons for preferring those works, and (iii) S has received formal education in the fine arts.
conditions: S consistently prefers artworks that are highly regarded by experts
S is able to articulate reasons for preferring those works
S has received formal education in the fine arts
counterexample: Consider a farmer in a provincial village who has never received formal education of any kind, yet who, over the course of many years, has developed a refined sensitivity to folk music. He reliably distinguishes excellent performances from mediocre ones, and his preferences align closely with those of musicologists who study the tradition. He can articulate, in plain but precise language, why one rendition is superior to another. The first two conditions are satisfied, yet the third is not: he has received no formal education in the fine arts. His taste is nonetheless good.
missing_condition: The definition requires formal education, but good taste may be cultivated through sustained attentive experience alone, without any institutional instruction. The requirement of formal education confuses one common pathway to taste with a necessary condition of it.
passage: Suppose one defines good taste as follows: a person has good taste if and only if that person consistently prefers artworks that are highly regarded by experts, is able to articulate reasons for those preferences, and has received formal education in the fine arts. The definition appears well-motivated. Training in the fine arts cultivates perceptual discrimination; the ability to articulate reasons demonstrates that one's preferences are not merely capricious; and agreement with expert opinion suggests that one's judgements track genuine aesthetic merit. Yet one must consider the case of a provincial farmer who has never attended any institution of learning, but who has spent decades listening to and performing folk music within a living tradition. Over the years he has developed an exquisite sensitivity to the nuances of performance — to phrasing, to rhythmic subtlety, to the expressive shaping of melodic lines. His preferences coincide, as it happens, with those of trained musicologists who have studied the tradition. He can explain, in plain but remarkably precise language, why one performance excels and another falls short. The first two conditions are satisfied. Yet the third is not: he has received no formal education of any description. His taste, however, is manifestly good — indeed, it may surpass that of many conservatory graduates. The definition confuses one common means of cultivating taste — formal education — with a necessary condition of its possession. Taste may be formed by sustained attentive experience within a living tradition, and no adequate account of taste can exclude such formation.

definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E is directed at a perceptual object, (ii) E involves pleasure, and (iii) E is unrelated to any practical purpose or bodily need.
conditions: E is directed at a perceptual object
E involves pleasure
E is unrelated to any practical purpose or bodily need
counterexample: Consider the experience of listening to a solemn requiem mass composed in memory of the dead. The listener's experience is directed at a perceptual object — the musical performance — and is wholly unrelated to any practical purpose or bodily need. Yet the experience is not pleasurable in any straightforward sense; it is one of profound grief, solemnity, and sombre reflection. The second condition fails. Nevertheless, the experience is paradigmatically aesthetic: it is a case of absorbed, contemplative engagement with a work of art.
missing_condition: The definition requires pleasure, but aesthetic experience may involve grief, melancholy, awe, or even a species of displeasure. What is essential is not pleasure but a particular mode of absorbed contemplative attention, which may be directed at objects that elicit emotions far removed from pleasure.
passage: One might propose that an experience is aesthetic if and only if it is directed at a perceptual object, it involves pleasure, and it is unrelated to any practical purpose or bodily need. This definition accommodates a wide range of cases: the pleasure of contemplating a beautiful painting, the delight of hearing a well-performed sonata, the enjoyment of watching a graceful dance. In each case the experience is perceptual, pleasurable, and divorced from practical concern. The three conditions appear jointly sufficient. Yet one must consider the experience of attending a performance of a solemn requiem — the Mozart Requiem, perhaps, or that of Fauré. The listener is absorbed in the music; the experience is directed at a perceptual object and is entirely unrelated to practical purposes. But the experience is not pleasurable. It is pervaded by grief, by a weight of solemnity, by an apprehension of mortality that is anything but pleasant. One may emerge from such a performance deeply moved, shaken, even distressed — and yet the experience is aesthetic in the fullest sense. The same is true of the experience of contemplating a tragic painting, or of reading a poem that treats of loss and desolation. The definition has mistaken a frequent accompaniment of aesthetic experience — pleasure — for a necessary condition of it. What appears to be essential is not pleasure but a distinctive mode of absorbed contemplative attention, and this mode may be exercised upon objects that occasion not delight but sorrow, awe, or a complex admixture of emotions that resists reduction to pleasure.

definition: A painting P represents an object O if and only if (i) P visually resembles O, (ii) the artist intended P to depict O, and (iii) a competent viewer can identify O by looking at P without further information.
conditions: P visually resembles O
the artist intended P to depict O
a competent viewer can identify O by looking at P without further information
counterexample: Consider a highly abstract cubist portrait in which a human face is decomposed into geometric planes, sharp angles, and fragmented forms. The artist intended the painting to depict a particular person, and art historians confirm that it does so. A viewer who is told the title may discern the face, but a competent viewer confronting the painting without further information would not reliably identify the subject. The third condition fails. Yet the painting represents the person in question; it is a portrait, and it functions as a depiction within the practice of painting.
missing_condition: The definition requires unaided identification by a viewer, but representation in painting does not always yield immediate recognisability. Many legitimate depictions require contextual knowledge, titles, or familiarity with artistic conventions to be understood. What is essential is that the painting functions as a depiction within the relevant practice, not that it be transparent to any uninstructed eye.
passage: Suppose one holds that a painting represents an object if and only if the painting visually resembles the object, the artist intended it to depict the object, and a competent viewer can identify the object by looking at the painting without further information. The definition works well for a great many cases. A Dutch still life resembles the fruit it depicts; the artist intended to portray those objects; and any competent viewer can identify them at a glance. A Constable landscape resembles the scene it represents, and one can identify the depicted countryside without instruction. Yet one must consider the case of a cubist portrait — a painting in which the human face is decomposed into overlapping geometric planes, sharp angular forms, and fragmented surfaces that bear little resemblance to any face as ordinarily perceived. The artist intended the painting to depict a particular person, and within the tradition of cubist portraiture it functions as a representation of that person. Art historians identify the subject with confidence. But a viewer confronting the painting without any contextual information — without the title, without knowledge of the sitter, without familiarity with the conventions of cubism — would not reliably identify the subject as a human face, much less as the particular individual depicted. The third condition fails. The definition has assumed that representation requires a degree of visual transparency that is characteristic of certain traditions of painting but not of all. It mistakes one historically contingent mode of depiction — the illusionistic — for the essence of pictorial representation itself.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) the artist who created W felt E during the process of creation, (ii) W tends to arouse E in its audience, and (iii) E is a simple, nameable emotion such as joy, sadness, or anger.
conditions: the artist who created W felt E during the process of creation
W tends to arouse E in its audience
E is a simple, nameable emotion such as joy, sadness, or anger
counterexample: Consider a late Beethoven string quartet — the Opus 132, perhaps — which is widely said to express a complex emotional state for which no simple name exists: a mingling of convalescent gratitude, spiritual struggle, and hard-won serenity. The work tends to arouse something akin to this complex state in sensitive listeners, and Beethoven composed it during a period of illness and recovery. Yet the emotion expressed is not a simple, nameable emotion such as joy, sadness, or anger. The third condition fails, yet the quartet is one of the most expressive works in the musical repertoire.
missing_condition: The definition restricts expression to simple, nameable emotions, but many of the most expressive works of art convey complex, nuanced emotional states that resist reduction to a single label. Artistic expression often consists precisely in articulating what ordinary emotional vocabulary cannot name.
passage: One might define artistic expression thus: a work of art expresses an emotion if and only if the artist felt that emotion during creation, the work tends to arouse that emotion in its audience, and the emotion is a simple, nameable one such as joy, sadness, or anger. The definition accommodates many straightforward cases. A joyful folk song was composed in a spirit of joy and tends to arouse joy in its hearers; the emotion is simple and nameable. A mournful elegy was written in sorrow and tends to occasion sorrow; again, the emotion is readily identified. Yet one must consider works of art that are among the most expressive ever produced — the late string quartets of Beethoven, for instance, or the final cantos of Dante's Paradiso. The Opus 132 quartet, composed during a period of illness and recovery, is universally described as deeply expressive. But what emotion does it express? Critics speak of convalescent gratitude mingled with spiritual struggle, of a hard-won serenity that has passed through suffering, of an emotional complexity that defies any single name. The work arouses something in the attentive listener, but that something is not joy, not sadness, not anger — it is a state for which ordinary language has no ready label. The third condition fails. The definition has assumed that the emotions art expresses are drawn from a limited repertoire of named feelings, when in truth the power of art frequently consists precisely in its capacity to articulate emotional states that lie beyond the reach of common vocabulary.

definition: An object x is beautiful if and only if (i) x possesses formal unity, (ii) x possesses sensory richness of colour, texture, or sound, and (iii) x is an artefact produced with the intention of creating beauty.
conditions: x possesses formal unity
x possesses sensory richness of colour, texture, or sound
x is an artefact produced with the intention of creating beauty
counterexample: A butterfly's wing possesses formal unity in its patterning and extraordinary sensory richness of colour and texture. It is widely regarded as beautiful; poets and philosophers have cited it as a paradigm of natural beauty for centuries. Yet the butterfly's wing is not an artefact, nor was it produced with any intention whatsoever. It is a product of natural selection. The third condition fails, yet the wing is beautiful.
missing_condition: The definition restricts beauty to artefacts, thereby excluding all natural beauty. Yet natural objects — flowers, crystals, birdsong, the night sky — are paradigmatic instances of the beautiful. Any adequate analysis of beauty must accommodate both artificial and natural beauty.
passage: Suppose one holds that an object is beautiful if and only if it possesses formal unity, it possesses sensory richness of colour, texture, or sound, and it is an artefact produced with the intention of creating beauty. The first two conditions are plausible: formal unity and sensory richness are often cited as hallmarks of the beautiful, and together they seem to capture a great deal. The third condition restricts beauty to things made by human hands with aesthetic intent, which may appear to ensure that the definition tracks genuine cases of beauty rather than mere sensory agreeableness. Yet one must consider the butterfly's wing. A morpho butterfly displays a pattern of iridescent blue that possesses remarkable formal unity: the scales are arranged in symmetrical, repeating structures of great intricacy. The sensory richness is extraordinary — the colour shifts with the angle of observation, and the texture reveals minute structures of astonishing delicacy under magnification. The wing is beautiful by any standard; natural historians and philosophers alike have cited it as among the most beautiful objects in the natural world. But the wing is not an artefact. It was not produced by any human agent, nor was it produced with any intention at all. It is the result of evolutionary processes operating over millennia. The third condition excludes it. The definition has confined beauty to the products of human making, and in doing so it has excluded what may well be the most abundant and varied domain of the beautiful — the natural world. Flowers, crystals, the song of the nightingale, the pattern of frost upon a windowpane: all are beautiful, and none is an artefact.

definition: A judgement of taste is valid if and only if (i) the judge is in a state of disinterested contemplation, (ii) the judge has sufficient acquaintance with the object, and (iii) the judgement is shared by the majority of persons who contemplate the object.
conditions: the judge is in a state of disinterested contemplation
the judge has sufficient acquaintance with the object
the judgement is shared by the majority of persons who contemplate the object
counterexample: Consider the case of Johann Sebastian Bach's music in the decades following his death. A small number of connoisseurs, contemplating the works with full disinterestedness and deep acquaintance, judged them to be of the highest beauty. Yet the majority of musical listeners in that period regarded Bach as old-fashioned and of limited interest. The third condition fails; the judgement was not shared by the majority. Yet the minority judgement proved to be correct, and it is now universally acknowledged that Bach's music is among the most beautiful ever composed.
missing_condition: The definition requires majority agreement, but the history of taste reveals many cases in which a discerning minority apprehended genuine aesthetic merit that the majority overlooked. Validity of taste cannot be reduced to consensus; what is needed is sensitivity and competence, not numerical preponderance.
passage: One might propose that a judgement of taste is valid if and only if the judge is in a state of disinterested contemplation, the judge has sufficient acquaintance with the object, and the judgement is shared by the majority of persons who contemplate the object. The first two conditions are well-chosen: disinterestedness ensures that the judgement is not corrupted by personal interest or practical concern, and acquaintance ensures that the judge has engaged with the object in sufficient depth. The third condition introduces a democratic element — the requirement that a valid judgement of taste must command majority assent. This has a certain appeal, for one might suppose that widespread agreement is evidence of objective merit. Yet one must consider the reception of Johann Sebastian Bach's music in the decades after his death. In the latter half of the eighteenth century, a small number of connoisseurs — among them Mozart and, later, Mendelssohn — recognised the extraordinary beauty and depth of Bach's compositions. These listeners were disinterested, and their acquaintance with the works was profound. Yet the majority of musical listeners regarded Bach as an antiquated contrapuntist of limited interest, preferring the lighter galant style then in fashion. The third condition was not satisfied: the majority did not share the minority judgement. But the minority was right. It is now universally acknowledged that Bach's music is among the greatest achievements of Western art. The definition mistakes consensus for validity, when in truth a valid judgement of taste may be the possession of a discerning few who perceive what the majority, through insufficient cultivation or attention, does not.

definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E is an experience of sensory perception, (ii) E is voluntary and deliberately undertaken, and (iii) E involves a heightened awareness of form.
conditions: E is an experience of sensory perception
E is voluntary and deliberately undertaken
E involves a heightened awareness of form
counterexample: A person walking through a forest without any aesthetic intention suddenly hears a nightingale and is arrested by the beauty of its song. The experience is perceptual and involves a heightened awareness of the melodic form of the birdsong. Yet the experience was not voluntary or deliberately undertaken; it came upon the walker unbidden and unlooked-for. The second condition fails, yet the experience is genuinely aesthetic — indeed, such involuntary encounters are often described as among the most vivid aesthetic experiences.
missing_condition: The definition requires that aesthetic experience be voluntary, but many of the most striking aesthetic experiences are involuntary — they seize the perceiver without warning. What is essential is the quality of the attentive response, not the voluntariness of its initiation.
passage: Suppose one defines aesthetic experience as follows: an experience is aesthetic if and only if it is an experience of sensory perception, it is voluntary and deliberately undertaken, and it involves a heightened awareness of form. The definition captures many familiar cases. A person who visits a gallery and stands before a painting, attending carefully to its composition and colour, has a voluntary perceptual experience involving heightened awareness of form. A concert-goer who listens attentively to a symphony has deliberately placed himself in a position to perceive musical form. In each case, the experience is chosen, perceptual, and formally attentive. Yet one must consider the case of a person walking through a forest with no aesthetic purpose in mind — perhaps returning home from an errand. Suddenly, a nightingale begins to sing, and the walker is arrested. The beauty of the song is so striking that the walker stops, listens, and is wholly absorbed in the melodic form of the birdsong, in the purity and complexity of the sound. This is a genuine aesthetic experience; it is perceptual, and it involves a heightened awareness of form. But it was not voluntary. The walker did not set out to have an aesthetic experience; the experience came upon him unbidden, occasioned by an encounter he had neither sought nor anticipated. The second condition fails. The definition has assumed that aesthetic experience must be deliberately undertaken, when in truth some of the most vivid and memorable aesthetic experiences are those that arrive without warning — the unexpected sight of a rainbow, the sound of distant bells carried on the wind, the sudden apprehension of formal beauty in an object one had not thought to contemplate.

definition: A picture P represents a subject S if and only if (i) P was created by applying pigment to a surface, (ii) the arrangement of pigment in P bears a visual resemblance to S, and (iii) the artist intended P to depict S.
conditions: P was created by applying pigment to a surface
the arrangement of pigment in P bears a visual resemblance to S
the artist intended P to depict S
counterexample: An engraving or etching represents its subject — a landscape, a portrait, a scene of daily life — through lines incised into a metal plate and transferred to paper by means of ink and pressure. The artist intends the image to depict its subject, and the arrangement of lines bears a visual resemblance to the subject. Yet no pigment is applied to a surface in the relevant sense; the medium is incised line, not brushed or daubed colour. The first condition fails, yet the engraving is a picture that represents its subject.
missing_condition: The definition restricts pictorial representation to the medium of applied pigment, excluding engravings, etchings, drawings in graphite or charcoal, and photographs. Representation is a function of pictorial structure, not of the particular physical medium employed.
passage: One might hold that a picture represents a subject if and only if it was created by applying pigment to a surface, the arrangement of pigment bears a visual resemblance to the subject, and the artist intended the picture to depict that subject. The definition accommodates oil paintings, watercolours, frescoes, and tempera works admirably. In each case, pigment is applied to a surface — canvas, paper, plaster, or panel — and the resulting arrangement of colour resembles the subject the artist intended to portray. For a great many pictures, the definition yields the correct verdict. Yet one must consider the engraving. Dürer's engravings, for instance, represent their subjects with extraordinary precision and detail. The Knight, Death and the Devil depicts a mounted knight, a skeletal figure, and a devil with unmistakable clarity. The artist intended the work to depict these subjects, and the arrangement of lines bears a marked visual resemblance to them. But the engraving was not created by applying pigment to a surface. It was created by incising lines into a copper plate with a burin, then transferring ink to paper through the mechanical pressure of a press. The first condition fails. The same difficulty arises for etchings, lithographs, drawings in graphite or charcoal, and indeed for photographs. All of these are pictures that represent their subjects, yet none involves the application of pigment in the manner the definition requires. The definition has mistaken one particular medium of picture-making for the essence of pictorial representation, when in truth representation is a matter of pictorial structure and function, not of the physical process by which the picture is produced.

definition: A piece of music M expresses sadness if and only if (i) M is in a minor key, (ii) M has a slow tempo, and (iii) M employs descending melodic lines.
conditions: M is in a minor key
M has a slow tempo
M employs descending melodic lines
counterexample: The opening of Mozart's Symphony No. 40 in G minor is widely regarded as expressing a particular species of restless, agitated sadness. It is in a minor key and employs certain descending figures. Yet its tempo is not slow; it is marked Molto allegro, and its rhythmic character is urgent and driving. The second condition fails, yet the movement expresses sadness — or, more precisely, a troubled, anxious melancholy — with extraordinary force.
missing_condition: The definition requires slow tempo, but musical sadness may be expressed through agitated, rapid movement as well as through languorous slowness. What the definition fails to recognise is that sadness itself takes many forms — restless grief, nervous melancholy, frantic despair — and the musical means of expressing these forms are correspondingly varied.
passage: Suppose one holds that a piece of music expresses sadness if and only if it is in a minor key, it has a slow tempo, and it employs descending melodic lines. The definition has an obvious appeal. Many paradigmatically sad musical works — the Adagio of Barber, the slow movement of Beethoven's Seventh Symphony, many of Chopin's nocturnes — are in minor keys, proceed at a slow tempo, and feature descending melodic contours. One might suppose that these three features jointly constitute the musical expression of sadness. Yet one must consider the opening movement of Mozart's Symphony No. 40 in G minor. This movement is universally described as expressing a particular species of sadness: a restless, agitated, troubled melancholy. It is in a minor key, and it employs descending figures among its melodic materials. But its tempo is Molto allegro — very fast. The rhythmic character is urgent, driving, almost breathless; the musical surface is animated and turbulent. The second condition fails entirely. Yet the expressive character of the movement is unmistakably sad. The definition has assumed that sadness in music must be slow, when in truth sadness takes many forms. There is the languorous sadness of a slow elegy, but there is also the restless sadness of anxious grief, the frantic sadness of despair, the bitter sadness of agitation. The musical means appropriate to these different forms of sadness are correspondingly varied, and no definition that insists upon slow tempo can accommodate the full range of musical sadness.

definition: An object O is sublime if and only if (i) O is a natural object or phenomenon, (ii) O exceeds the ordinary scale of human perception, and (iii) the contemplation of O produces a mixed feeling of awe and terror.
conditions: O is a natural object or phenomenon
O exceeds the ordinary scale of human perception
O is a natural object or phenomenon
counterexample: The interior of the Pantheon in Rome has been described as sublime by numerous writers and travellers. The vast dome exceeds the ordinary scale of human perception, and its contemplation produces a powerful feeling of awe. Yet the Pantheon is not a natural object or phenomenon; it is a work of architecture, a human artefact constructed of concrete and stone. The first condition fails, yet the Pantheon is sublime.
missing_condition: The definition restricts the sublime to natural objects, but architecture, music, and even mathematical ideas have been described as sublime. The sublime is a mode of aesthetic response to overwhelming magnitude or power, and it is not confined to the natural world.
passage: One might propose that an object is sublime if and only if it is a natural object or phenomenon, it exceeds the ordinary scale of human perception, and its contemplation produces a mixed feeling of awe and terror. The definition captures many standard cases: the Alpine peak, the thunderstorm, the open ocean, the starry sky. These are all natural phenomena of vast scale that occasion awe mingled with a sense of one's own insignificance. Yet one must consider the case of architecture. The Pantheon in Rome, with its great coffered dome open to the sky through the oculus, has been described as sublime by writers from the Renaissance onward. The dome's diameter exceeds forty-three metres, and upon entering the rotunda the visitor is struck by a feeling of awe at the sheer magnitude of the enclosed space. The experience involves something that might fairly be described as a mixed feeling of elevation and overwhelm. But the Pantheon is not a natural object. It is a work of architecture, designed and constructed by human beings. The first condition excludes it. The same difficulty arises for other artefacts that are commonly called sublime: the great fugues of Bach, the tragedies of Aeschylus, the mathematical concept of infinity. None of these is a natural object, yet each has been described as sublime with full seriousness by competent judges. The definition has confined the sublime to nature, when in truth the sublime is a mode of aesthetic response that may be occasioned by works of human making no less than by the phenomena of the natural world.

definition: An object x is a work of art if and only if (i) x is the product of skilled craftsmanship, (ii) x serves no practical or utilitarian function, and (iii) x is intended for display or performance before an audience.
conditions: x is the product of skilled craftsmanship
x serves no practical or utilitarian function
x is intended for display or performance before an audience
counterexample: A finely crafted Persian carpet is the product of extraordinary skilled craftsmanship and is widely regarded as a work of art. Museums display such carpets, and scholars study their formal properties — their patterns, colours, and compositional structures. Yet a Persian carpet serves a practical function: it covers a floor, provides warmth, and is walked upon daily. The second condition fails, yet the carpet is a work of art.
missing_condition: The definition excludes objects that serve practical functions, but many works of art — carpets, pottery, cathedrals, furniture — are also functional objects. The boundary between art and utility is not sharp, and a satisfactory definition must allow that an object may be both useful and a work of art.
passage: Suppose one defines a work of art as follows: an object is a work of art if and only if it is the product of skilled craftsmanship, it serves no practical or utilitarian function, and it is intended for display or performance before an audience. The definition captures many paradigm cases. A painting on canvas is crafted with skill, serves no practical function, and is intended for display in a gallery or home. A symphony is the product of compositional skill, serves no utilitarian purpose, and is performed before an audience. The three conditions seem jointly sufficient. Yet one must consider the Persian carpet. The finest Persian carpets are products of extraordinary craftsmanship, requiring years of labour and the mastery of complex techniques of knotting, dyeing, and pattern composition. Their formal properties — the intricacy of their geometric and floral designs, the harmony of their colours, the precision of their execution — have been studied by art historians and exhibited in the world's great museums. They are works of art by any reasonable standard. But they serve a practical function. They cover floors. They provide warmth and comfort. They are walked upon, sat upon, and subjected to daily use. The second condition excludes them. The same difficulty arises for decorated pottery, illuminated manuscripts, cathedrals, and finely crafted furniture — all of which are products of skill, all of which serve practical functions, and all of which are acknowledged works of art. The definition has drawn the boundary between art and utility too sharply, when in truth many of the greatest works of art in human history have been objects of use.

definition: An object x is beautiful if and only if (i) x is pleasing to the eye, (ii) x possesses proportion and order, and (iii) x is rare or unusual in its kind.
conditions: x is pleasing to the eye
x possesses proportion and order
x is rare or unusual in its kind
counterexample: A common daisy growing in a field is pleasing to the eye and possesses proportion and order — its petals radiate symmetrically from a golden centre. It is beautiful, and poets have said so since antiquity. Yet the daisy is not rare or unusual; it is among the most common flowers in the meadows of Europe, found in extraordinary abundance. The third condition fails, yet the daisy is beautiful.
missing_condition: The definition requires rarity, but beauty is not a function of scarcity. Common objects — daisies, clear skies, the sound of running water — may be beautiful, and their beauty is not diminished by their abundance. The definition confuses a feature that may enhance our appreciation of beauty with a necessary condition of beauty itself.
passage: One might hold that an object is beautiful if and only if it is pleasing to the eye, it possesses proportion and order, and it is rare or unusual in its kind. The inclusion of rarity may seem well-motivated: we are often most struck by beauty in objects that are extraordinary — the flawless diamond, the albino peacock, the orchid found only upon a single mountainside. The rarity of such objects appears to contribute to our sense of their beauty, and one might suppose that rarity is an essential ingredient. Yet one must consider the common daisy. The daisy is pleasing to the eye: its white petals radiate in orderly fashion from a bright golden centre, and its proportions are harmonious and well-defined. It is beautiful, and it has been celebrated as such by poets, painters, and ordinary observers since antiquity. Burns wrote of it, Wordsworth admired it, and Chaucer called it the flower he most loved. But the daisy is not rare. It is among the most common wildflowers in the meadows and roadsides of Europe, growing in such profusion that one may count hundreds in a single field. The third condition fails entirely. The beauty of the daisy is not diminished by its abundance; if anything, the sight of a meadow full of daisies is more beautiful than the sight of a single specimen. The definition has confused a feature that sometimes heightens our appreciation of beauty — rarity — with a necessary condition of beauty itself. Beauty is indifferent to scarcity, and many of the most beautiful things in the world are also among the most common.

definition: A person S has good taste if and only if (i) S takes pleasure in objects that possess genuine aesthetic merit, (ii) S is unmoved by objects that lack such merit, and (iii) S's aesthetic responses are immediate and unreflective.
conditions: S takes pleasure in objects that possess genuine aesthetic merit
S is unmoved by objects that lack such merit
S's aesthetic responses are immediate and unreflective
counterexample: Consider a thoughtful critic who, upon first hearing a complex fugue by Bach, finds it austere and difficult. Over repeated listenings, guided by careful analysis of the contrapuntal structure, the critic comes to appreciate and take deep pleasure in the work. The critic's eventual response is not immediate and unreflective; it is the product of sustained, deliberate effort. Yet the critic's taste is good — indeed, it is precisely the kind of considered, cultivated taste that enables appreciation of works whose merits are not immediately apparent.
missing_condition: The definition requires immediacy, but good taste often involves the capacity for sustained reflection and the willingness to revise initial impressions. Many of the greatest works of art yield their beauty only to patient, reflective engagement, and a taste that responded only to what is immediately gratifying would be shallow rather than good.
passage: Suppose one defines good taste as follows: a person has good taste if and only if that person takes pleasure in objects that possess genuine aesthetic merit, is unmoved by objects that lack such merit, and responds to aesthetic objects immediately and unreflectively. The third condition demands that taste operate as a kind of spontaneous sensitivity — a faculty that perceives merit at once, without the mediation of thought or analysis. This has a certain appeal, for we sometimes speak of the person of good taste as one who simply sees what is beautiful, without needing to reason about it. Yet one must consider the case of a thoughtful critic who encounters a complex fugue by Bach for the first time. The work is austere, densely contrapuntal, and yields little immediate pleasure. The critic's first response is one of puzzlement rather than delight. But over repeated listenings, guided by careful analysis of the voice-leading and formal structure, the critic comes to perceive the work's extraordinary beauty and to take deep pleasure in it. The first two conditions are now satisfied: the critic takes pleasure in a work of genuine merit. But the response was not immediate and unreflective; it was the product of sustained, deliberate intellectual effort extended over weeks or months. The third condition fails. Yet the critic's taste is manifestly good — indeed, it is precisely the kind of cultivated, reflective taste that enables appreciation of works whose merits are not apparent upon a single hearing. The definition confuses one mode of aesthetic response — the spontaneous — with the only legitimate mode, when in truth the highest exercises of taste may require patience, effort, and reflection.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) W is the causal product of the artist's experience of E, (ii) the formal properties of W are causally shaped by E, and (iii) the artist created W with the conscious purpose of communicating E to an audience.
conditions: W is the causal product of the artist's experience of E
the formal properties of W are causally shaped by E
the artist created W with the conscious purpose of communicating E to an audience
counterexample: A poet, composing in tranquillity, recalls an experience of intense grief that occurred years earlier and crafts a poem that expresses that grief with great power. The poem's formal properties — its rhythm, its imagery, its tonal qualities — are shaped by the remembered emotion, and the poet consciously intends to communicate grief. Yet the poet does not feel grief during the act of composition; the poem is created in a state of calm reflective detachment. The first condition fails, since the work is not the causal product of a present experience of the emotion. Yet the poem expresses grief.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the artist feel the emotion during creation, but artists frequently express emotions in tranquillity, working from memory and craft rather than from present feeling. What matters is the capacity to give formal embodiment to an emotion, not the concurrent experience of it.
passage: One might propose that a work of art expresses an emotion if and only if the work is the causal product of the artist's experience of that emotion, the formal properties of the work are causally shaped by the emotion, and the artist created the work with the conscious purpose of communicating the emotion to an audience. The definition has considerable intuitive force. We naturally suppose that the artist who expresses grief must have felt grief, and that the grief must have entered into and shaped the work. Yet one must consider the poet who composes in tranquillity. Wordsworth himself described the poetic process as one in which emotion is recollected in tranquillity — the poet does not write in the grip of the emotion but at a temporal and psychological distance from it. The poet recalls an experience of intense grief that occurred months or years earlier and, in a state of calm reflective detachment, crafts a poem that gives formal expression to that grief with extraordinary power and precision. The formal properties of the poem — its cadences, its imagery, its tonal colourings — are shaped by the remembered emotion, and the poet consciously intends to communicate grief to the reader. The second and third conditions are satisfied. But the first is not: the poet does not feel grief during the act of composition. The work is not the causal product of a present experience of the emotion; it is the product of a recollected experience, mediated by craft, reflection, and deliberate artistic choice. Yet the poem expresses grief. The definition has mistaken the temporal relation between emotion and creation, assuming that expression requires simultaneity when in truth it may require distance.

definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E involves the perception of an object's formal properties, (ii) E is accompanied by a judgement that the object is good or bad of its kind, and (iii) E occurs in a setting designated for aesthetic contemplation, such as a gallery, concert hall, or theatre.
conditions: E involves the perception of an object's formal properties
E is accompanied by a judgement that the object is good or bad of its kind
E occurs in a setting designated for aesthetic contemplation
counterexample: A shepherd standing on a hillside at dawn perceives the formal properties of the landscape — the arrangement of light and shadow, the contours of the hills, the gradations of colour in the sky — and judges the scene to be supremely beautiful. The experience is aesthetic in every respect save one: it does not occur in a gallery, concert hall, or theatre. It occurs outdoors, in a setting designated for no purpose at all. The third condition fails, yet the experience is genuinely aesthetic.
missing_condition: The definition restricts aesthetic experience to institutional settings, but aesthetic experience may occur anywhere — in nature, in the street, in one's own home. What matters is the quality of perceptual attention, not the architectural context in which it takes place.
passage: Suppose one holds that an experience is aesthetic if and only if it involves the perception of an object's formal properties, it is accompanied by a judgement that the object is good or bad of its kind, and it occurs in a setting designated for aesthetic contemplation, such as a gallery, concert hall, or theatre. The third condition reflects a not uncommon view: that aesthetic experience is something that happens in special places set apart for the purpose, places where the conditions of lighting, silence, and social convention encourage and protect contemplative attention. And indeed, galleries and concert halls do facilitate aesthetic experience. But one must consider the case of a shepherd who stands upon a hillside at the break of day. The light is just beginning to touch the distant hills; the sky is a delicate gradation of rose, gold, and pale blue; the shadows in the valleys are deep and soft. The shepherd perceives these formal properties with keen attention and judges the scene to be of surpassing beauty. This is an aesthetic experience of the most vivid kind. It involves perception of formal properties; it is accompanied by a judgement of beauty. But it does not occur in a gallery or concert hall. It occurs on a hillside, in the open air, in a setting that has no institutional designation whatsoever. The third condition fails. The definition has confused a sufficient condition for facilitating aesthetic experience — the provision of appropriate settings — with a necessary condition of its occurrence. Aesthetic experience is not the creature of institutions; it may arise wherever there is an attentive perceiver and an object worthy of attention.

definition: An experience is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) the experience is occasioned by an object of great power or magnitude, (ii) the subject initially feels a check or inhibition of the vital powers, and (iii) the subject is a person of refined sensibility and philosophical education.
conditions: the experience is occasioned by an object of great power or magnitude
the subject initially feels a check or inhibition of the vital powers
the subject is a person of refined sensibility and philosophical education
counterexample: A young child standing at the edge of the Grand Canyon, possessing no philosophical education and only a rudimentary sensibility, may nevertheless experience the sublime. The child is confronted with an object of immense magnitude, feels a check or inhibition of its vital powers — a kind of stunned, breathless awe — and the experience is as genuine an instance of the sublime as any philosopher's. The third condition fails, yet the experience is sublime.
missing_condition: The definition restricts the sublime to persons of refined and educated sensibility, but the capacity for sublime experience appears to be a basic feature of human perception, available to the uneducated and the young no less than to the philosopher. What is required is the capacity for awe, not a particular kind of training.
passage: One might define the experience of the sublime as follows: an experience is sublime if and only if it is occasioned by an object of great power or magnitude, the subject initially feels a check or inhibition of the vital powers, and the subject is a person of refined sensibility and philosophical education. The third condition may seem defensible on the ground that the sublime involves a complex reflective response — not merely fear, but a kind of elevated awareness that transcends the initial feeling of being overwhelmed — and that such a response requires the cultivation that only education can provide. Yet one must consider the case of a child. A young child of five or six, standing for the first time at the rim of the Grand Canyon, may be struck dumb with awe. The child cannot articulate what it feels; it possesses no philosophical vocabulary and no training in aesthetic discrimination. Its sensibility, while genuine, is as yet unrefined. But the experience it undergoes — the sense of being confronted by something that vastly exceeds its capacity to comprehend, the momentary inhibition of its ordinary cheerful activity, the wide-eyed, breathless quality of its attention — bears all the marks of the sublime. Travellers and parents have described such reactions in children with great frequency, and there is no reason to deny that the child's experience is genuinely sublime. The third condition excludes it. The definition has restricted the sublime to the educated, when in truth the capacity for sublime experience appears to be among the most basic of human aesthetic responses, available to the unlettered and the young no less than to the philosopher.

definition: A work of art W represents a subject S if and only if (i) W contains elements that resemble S in appearance, (ii) W was created after direct observation of S, and (iii) the degree of resemblance is sufficient to permit identification of S.
conditions: W contains elements that resemble S in appearance
W was created after direct observation of S
the degree of resemblance is sufficient to permit identification of S
counterexample: Consider a painter who, working entirely from imagination, paints a scene of ancient Rome — the Forum as it might have appeared in the age of Augustus, with temples, columns, and togate figures. The painting represents ancient Rome; viewers identify its subject readily, and the elements of the painting resemble the reconstructed appearance of the ancient city. But the painter has never observed ancient Rome, for it no longer exists. The second condition fails, yet the painting represents its subject.
missing_condition: The definition requires direct observation, but artists routinely represent subjects they have never observed — historical scenes, mythological figures, imaginary landscapes. Representation may proceed from imagination, from textual description, or from second-hand knowledge, and no adequate account can restrict it to what has been directly perceived.
passage: Suppose one defines representation in art as follows: a work of art represents a subject if and only if it contains elements that resemble the subject in appearance, the work was created after direct observation of the subject, and the degree of resemblance is sufficient to permit identification. The definition captures many familiar cases. A portrait painter who works from a sitting model directly observes the sitter, and the resulting portrait resembles and can be identified as the sitter. A landscape painter who works en plein air directly observes the scene and produces a recognisable depiction of it. Yet one must consider the painter of historical or imaginary subjects. Consider an artist who paints a scene of ancient Rome — the Forum Romanum as it might have appeared in the time of Augustus, with its temples, its marble columns, its togate senators. The painting represents ancient Rome; viewers identify the subject with ease, guided by the architectural forms, the costumes, and the inscriptions. But the painter has never directly observed ancient Rome, for ancient Rome no longer exists. The painter has worked from archaeological reconstructions, textual descriptions, the study of surviving ruins, and the exercise of informed imagination. The second condition fails. Yet the painting represents its subject as surely as any portrait represents a living sitter. The same difficulty arises for paintings of mythological subjects, of imaginary landscapes, of future events. In each case, the artist represents something that has not been and perhaps cannot be directly observed. The definition has confined representation to the transcription of observed appearances, when in truth artistic representation draws upon imagination, memory, description, and inference no less than upon direct perception.

definition: An object x is a work of art if and only if (i) x is unique — no other object is qualitatively identical to x, (ii) x was produced with aesthetic intention, and (iii) x is the work of a single identifiable author.
conditions: x is unique — no other object is qualitatively identical to x
x was produced with aesthetic intention
x is the work of a single identifiable author
counterexample: A bronze sculpture cast in an edition of twelve — each cast produced from the same mould, each qualitatively identical to the others — is a work of art. Rodin's bronzes were produced in editions, and each cast is displayed in museums and treated as a work of art without reservation. The sculptures were produced with aesthetic intention and are the work of an identifiable author. Yet no individual cast is unique; there exist eleven other objects qualitatively identical to it. The first condition fails, yet each cast is a work of art.
missing_condition: The definition requires uniqueness, but many works of art — bronze editions, prints, photographs, literary works existing in multiple copies — are not unique objects. What matters is not material singularity but the artistic character of the work, which may be instantiated in multiple physical objects.
passage: One might propose that an object is a work of art if and only if it is unique — no other object being qualitatively identical to it — it was produced with aesthetic intention, and it is the work of a single identifiable author. The uniqueness condition has a certain appeal: we prize the original painting above the copy, and the aura of the singular, unrepeatable object seems bound up with our experience of art. Yet one must consider the bronze edition. Rodin produced many of his sculptures as bronze casts in editions of twelve or more. Each cast is produced from the same mould; each is qualitatively identical to the others in all material respects. The Thinker exists in numerous casts, distributed among museums and collections around the world. Each was produced with aesthetic intention, and each is the work of an identifiable author — Rodin himself, or his foundry working under his direction. The second and third conditions are satisfied. But the first is not: no individual cast is unique, for there exist multiple objects qualitatively identical to it. Yet each cast is unquestionably a work of art. The same difficulty arises for prints, etchings, photographs, and indeed for literary and musical works, which exist in indefinitely many copies without any loss of their status as art. The definition has mistaken a feature of certain works of art — the uniqueness of the individual painting or manuscript — for a necessary condition of art as such, when in truth many of the greatest works of art are by their very nature reproducible.

definition: An object x is beautiful if and only if (i) x conforms to the canons of classical proportion, (ii) x produces a calm and serene affective response, and (iii) x is apprehended through the sense of sight.
conditions: x conforms to the canons of classical proportion
x produces a calm and serene affective response
x is apprehended through the sense of sight
counterexample: A beautiful melody — the slow movement of a Mozart piano concerto, perhaps — produces a calm and serene response and possesses a kind of proportion in its phrasing and harmonic structure. It is beautiful by any reasonable standard. Yet it is not apprehended through the sense of sight; it is apprehended through the sense of hearing. The third condition fails, yet the melody is beautiful.
missing_condition: The definition restricts beauty to visual objects, thereby excluding musical beauty, the beauty of poetry as heard, and the beauty of natural sounds. Beauty is apprehended through multiple senses, and any adequate definition must accommodate auditory and perhaps even tactile beauty.
passage: Suppose one holds that an object is beautiful if and only if it conforms to the canons of classical proportion, it produces a calm and serene affective response, and it is apprehended through the sense of sight. The definition has deep historical roots: the identification of beauty with visual proportion is a venerable tradition, and the association of beauty with serenity is equally ancient. A Greek temple, a Renaissance painting, the proportions of the human body as codified by Vitruvius — all are visual, proportionate, and serene, and all are paradigmatically beautiful. Yet one must consider music. A beautiful melody — the Andante of Mozart's Piano Concerto No. 21, for instance — possesses a kind of proportion in its phrasing and harmonic structure that is analogous to visual proportion. It produces a calm and serene response in the listener. It is beautiful; no one who has heard it attentively would deny this. But it is not apprehended through the sense of sight. It is apprehended through the sense of hearing. The third condition excludes it. The same difficulty arises for the beauty of poetry as spoken, for the beauty of birdsong, and for the beauty of the human voice. If one insists that beauty must be visual, one excludes a vast and universally acknowledged domain of the beautiful — the domain of auditory beauty. The definition has mistaken the historical prominence of visual beauty in philosophical discussion for a genuine restriction upon the concept itself, when in truth beauty is apprehended through multiple senses and perhaps even, in certain cases, through the intellect alone.

definition: A painting P expresses emotion E if and only if (i) the subject matter of P depicts a person or persons experiencing E, (ii) the facial expressions and bodily postures of the depicted persons are legible as manifestations of E, and (iii) the viewer recognises E by attending to the depicted persons.
conditions: the subject matter of P depicts a person or persons experiencing E
the facial expressions and bodily postures of the depicted persons are legible as manifestations of E
the viewer recognises E by attending to the depicted persons
counterexample: A landscape painting by Caspar David Friedrich — a scene of mist-shrouded mountains and dark fir trees, with no human figure present — is widely described as expressing a profound melancholy and spiritual longing. The viewer apprehends this emotion through the colour, the composition, the quality of the light, and the atmospheric effects. Yet the painting depicts no persons, and there are no facial expressions or bodily postures to read. All three conditions fail, yet the painting expresses emotion.
missing_condition: The definition restricts emotional expression to the depiction of persons exhibiting emotion, but paintings may express emotion through purely formal means — colour, line, composition, light — without depicting any human figure at all. The expressive power of painting is not confined to the representation of emotional states in human subjects.
passage: One might propose that a painting expresses an emotion if and only if its subject matter depicts a person or persons experiencing that emotion, the facial expressions and bodily postures of the depicted persons are legible as manifestations of the emotion, and the viewer recognises the emotion by attending to the depicted persons. The definition works well for many paintings. Raphael's Madonnas express tenderness through the gentle inclination of the Virgin's head and the soft expression of her face. Caravaggio's Judith Beheading Holofernes expresses horror and determination through the visible facial expressions of the figures. The three conditions are satisfied, and the viewer reads the emotion from the depicted persons. Yet one must consider landscape painting. A painting by Caspar David Friedrich — a scene of mist-shrouded mountains rising above dark fir trees, suffused with a cold, grey light, and containing no human figure whatsoever — is widely and rightly described as expressing a profound melancholy, a sense of spiritual longing and solitude. The viewer apprehends this emotion not by reading facial expressions, for there are none, but through the purely formal means of the painting: the subdued palette, the receding planes of mist, the lonely verticals of the trees, the cold quality of the light. All three conditions of the definition fail: there are no depicted persons, no legible expressions, no bodily postures to attend to. Yet the painting is deeply expressive. The definition has assumed that pictorial expression is always mediated by the representation of human emotion, when in truth the formal properties of painting — colour, line, composition, and light — may express emotion directly and powerfully without any human figure.

definition: A judgement of taste is objective if and only if (i) the judgement concerns the formal properties of the object, (ii) the judgement can be verified by any competent observer under standard conditions of perception, and (iii) the judgement admits of no reasonable disagreement among competent observers.
conditions: the judgement concerns the formal properties of the object
the judgement can be verified by any competent observer under standard conditions of perception
the judgement admits of no reasonable disagreement among competent observers
counterexample: Consider the question whether Brahms's symphonies are more beautiful than those of Bruckner. Both composers' works possess complex formal properties, and competent listeners — trained musicians, experienced critics, conductors of long standing — have considered the question carefully under conditions of attentive listening. Yet they disagree, and their disagreement is reasonable: each side can cite formal properties that support its judgement. The third condition fails, since reasonable disagreement persists. Yet it seems wrong to conclude that no judgement of taste concerning these works is objective; the formal properties are real, and the judgements concern them.
missing_condition: The definition requires the absence of reasonable disagreement, but in matters of taste, reasonable disagreement among competent judges is pervasive and perhaps ineliminable. Objectivity in aesthetic judgement may consist not in the impossibility of disagreement but in the responsiveness of judgement to genuine features of the object, even where those features underdetermine a single verdict.
passage: Suppose one holds that a judgement of taste is objective if and only if it concerns the formal properties of the object, it can be verified by any competent observer under standard conditions of perception, and it admits of no reasonable disagreement among competent observers. The third condition is demanding: it requires that competent observers converge upon a single verdict, so that disagreement is a sign either of incompetence or of non-standard perceptual conditions. This may seem a reasonable requirement for objectivity. Yet one must consider cases in which competent observers disagree reasonably. Take the question whether the symphonies of Brahms are more beautiful than those of Bruckner. Both bodies of work possess rich and complex formal properties — intricate thematic development, sophisticated harmonic language, carefully wrought architecture. Competent listeners have considered the question with great seriousness: trained musicians, experienced critics, and conductors of long standing have weighed the formal merits of each composer's symphonies under conditions of attentive, repeated listening. And they disagree. Some find in Brahms a density and concision of formal argument that Bruckner lacks; others find in Bruckner a spaciousness and grandeur of formal conception that Brahms cannot match. The disagreement is reasonable — each side can point to genuine formal features that support its verdict — and it shows no sign of resolution. The third condition fails. Yet it seems wrong to conclude that no judgement of taste in this domain is objective, for the formal properties to which the judges respond are real, and the judgements are responsive to them. The definition has set too high a standard for objectivity, identifying it with consensus rather than with responsiveness to genuine features of the object.

definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E is an experience of a unified perceptual whole, (ii) E is detached from all emotion and affect, and (iii) E involves the apprehension of formal relations among the parts of the perceived object.
conditions: E is an experience of a unified perceptual whole
E is detached from all emotion and affect
E involves the apprehension of formal relations among the parts of the perceived object
counterexample: Listening to Schubert's Winterreise, a person apprehends the song cycle as a unified perceptual whole and attends closely to the formal relations among its parts — the harmonic progressions, the relation of text to melody, the structural arc of the cycle. Yet the experience is saturated with emotion: the listener is moved to something approaching despair by the desolation of the poems and the music's expressive power. The second condition fails, for the experience is anything but detached from emotion. Yet it is a paradigmatic aesthetic experience.
missing_condition: The definition requires detachment from emotion, but many of the deepest aesthetic experiences are pervaded by intense emotion. The apprehension of formal relations and the experience of emotion are not mutually exclusive but frequently inseparable aspects of aesthetic engagement. A definition that excludes emotion excludes much of what makes aesthetic experience valuable.
passage: One might propose that an experience is aesthetic if and only if it is an experience of a unified perceptual whole, it is detached from all emotion and affect, and it involves the apprehension of formal relations among the parts of the perceived object. The second condition reflects a certain formalist impulse — the view that aesthetic experience is essentially a matter of perceiving formal structure, and that emotion is an extraneous accompaniment that, if anything, distracts from genuine aesthetic apprehension. There is a tradition in aesthetics that defends something like this view, holding that the true aesthetic attitude is one of cool, dispassionate contemplation. Yet one must consider the experience of listening to Schubert's Winterreise. The listener apprehends the song cycle as a unified whole, attending to the harmonic progressions, the relation of the vocal line to the piano accompaniment, the way each song relates to the next, the formal architecture of the entire cycle. The third condition is satisfied, and the first condition is satisfied as well — the cycle is heard as a unified perceptual whole. But the experience is not detached from emotion. It is pervaded by emotion: by a deepening melancholy, by an apprehension of loneliness and loss, by moments of desolate beauty that move the listener to something approaching despair. The second condition fails utterly. Yet the experience is aesthetic in the fullest and most paradigmatic sense. The definition has driven a wedge between formal apprehension and emotional engagement, when in truth these are frequently inseparable. The perception of formal beauty in the Winterreise is not diminished by the emotion it occasions; the emotion is part of the aesthetic experience, not an impediment to it.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O exhibits a harmonious proportion among its parts, (ii) O produces in the normally constituted observer a feeling of disinterested pleasure, and (iii) O possesses a luminous or radiant surface quality that immediately attracts the eye.
conditions: O exhibits a harmonious proportion among its parts
O produces in the normally constituted observer a feeling of disinterested pleasure
O possesses a luminous or radiant surface quality that immediately attracts the eye
counterexample: Consider a weathered stone ruin standing in an open field. Its proportions are broken and irregular; columns have fallen, walls have crumbled, and nothing remains of the original harmony of parts. Its surface is rough, darkened by lichen and moss, and possesses no luminosity or radiance whatsoever. Yet the ruin produces in the observer a feeling of disinterested pleasure of the most intense kind, and is judged beautiful by nearly all who encounter it.
missing_condition: The definition requires harmonious proportion and luminous surface quality as necessary conditions, yet beauty may reside in objects whose proportions are shattered and whose surfaces are darkened and rough. What is wanted is a condition that accommodates the beauty of ruin, decay, and irregular form, which the present definition excludes by insisting upon proportion and radiance.
passage: One might propose that an object is beautiful if and only if it exhibits a harmonious proportion among its parts, produces in the normally constituted observer a feeling of disinterested pleasure, and possesses a luminous or radiant surface quality that immediately attracts the eye. The definition has considerable initial plausibility. One thinks of a Greek temple, whose columns stand in mathematically precise ratios, whose marble surfaces catch the Mediterranean light, and whose contemplation produces a pleasure wholly free of practical interest. One thinks, too, of a fine porcelain vase, whose curves are perfectly balanced, whose glaze gleams with a soft lustre, and which delights the eye without engaging any appetitive faculty. The three conditions appear to identify what is essential to beauty. Yet one must attend to the weathered ruin. A Roman aqueduct, let us suppose, stands in a field in the south of France. Its arches are partially collapsed; the stones have shifted and cracked over the centuries. The proportions that the architect intended are no longer discernible; what remains is a jagged, irregular silhouette against the sky. The surface of the stone is blackened by age, encrusted with lichen, and roughened by centuries of wind and rain; there is no luminosity, no radiance, nothing that immediately attracts the eye by its shining quality. Yet the ruin is beautiful. The traveller who comes upon it feels a pleasure that is entirely disinterested — he does not wish to own the ruin or to put it to any use — and the beauty he perceives is not diminished but enhanced by the irregularity of the broken arches and the darkness of the weathered stone. The definition, in requiring harmonious proportion and radiant surface quality, mistakes the conditions that characterise one familiar species of beauty — the beauty of the pristine, the symmetrical, the luminous — for conditions that are necessary to beauty as such.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O is the product of deliberate human craftsmanship, (ii) O exhibits a unity of design in which no element could be added or removed without diminishing the whole, and (iii) O is made of materials that are themselves prized for their intrinsic qualities, such as gold, marble, or fine wood.
conditions: O is the product of deliberate human craftsmanship
O exhibits a unity of design in which no element could be added or removed without diminishing the whole
O is made of materials that are themselves prized for their intrinsic qualities, such as gold, marble, or fine wood
counterexample: Consider a wildflower meadow in early summer. No human hand has cultivated it; it is the product of no craftsmanship whatever. Its elements are not unified by design; flowers appear in random profusion, and one could add or remove a dozen without altering the character of the whole. Its materials — common grasses, buttercups, clover — are not prized for any intrinsic quality. Yet the meadow is beautiful, and its beauty is of a kind that has moved poets and painters for centuries.
missing_condition: The definition restricts beauty to artefacts fashioned from prized materials, yet natural objects of no material value may be beautiful. What is needed is a conception of beauty that does not presuppose human craftsmanship or the intrinsic worth of the materials from which an object is composed.
passage: One might propose that an object is beautiful if and only if it is the product of deliberate human craftsmanship, exhibits a unity of design in which no element could be added or removed without diminishing the whole, and is made of materials that are themselves prized for their intrinsic qualities. The definition is not without force. A finely wrought silver chalice, fashioned by a master silversmith, satisfies all three conditions admirably: it is the product of deliberate craftsmanship, its form is such that the alteration of any element — the curve of the bowl, the proportion of the stem, the ornamentation of the base — would diminish the whole, and silver is a material prized for its lustre and malleability. One thinks, too, of a marble sculpture by a great Italian master, or a cabinet of fine walnut made by an English craftsman of the eighteenth century. Yet one must attend to the wildflower meadow. In early summer, the meadows of the English countryside are filled with buttercups, red clover, ox-eye daisies, and wild grasses, all growing in a profusion that owes nothing to human design. No craftsman has arranged these flowers; they have sprung up where the wind and the insects have carried their seeds. The meadow exhibits no unity of design; one could add a hundred more daisies or remove a swathe of clover without in any way diminishing or altering its essential character. And the materials of which it is composed — common grasses and wildflowers — are not prized for any intrinsic quality; they are the humblest of growing things. Yet the meadow is beautiful. Its beauty has been celebrated by English poets from Chaucer to the present day, and the pleasure it gives to the walker who comes upon it is as pure and disinterested as any pleasure that beauty affords. The definition, in requiring human craftsmanship, unity of design, and prized materials, identifies conditions that are characteristic of one important domain of beauty — the beauty of the decorative arts — but that are not necessary conditions of beauty as such.

definition: An object O is a work of art if and only if (i) O is produced by an individual possessing recognised technical skill in a specific artistic medium, (ii) O is intended by its maker to be exhibited for contemplation rather than put to practical use, and (iii) O is executed in one of the traditionally recognised fine arts: painting, sculpture, architecture, music, or poetry.
conditions: O is produced by an individual possessing recognised technical skill in a specific artistic medium
O is intended by its maker to be exhibited for contemplation rather than put to practical use
O is executed in one of the traditionally recognised fine arts: painting, sculpture, architecture, music, or poetry
counterexample: Consider a Persian carpet of the sixteenth century. It was woven not by a single individual possessing recognised artistic standing but by a workshop of anonymous craftswomen. It was intended not for exhibition and contemplation but for practical domestic use — to cover a floor and provide warmth. And carpet-weaving is not among the traditionally recognised fine arts. Yet such a carpet, with its intricate geometrical patterns and its subtle harmonies of colour, is a work of art by any reasonable standard, and is exhibited as such in the great museums of the world.
missing_condition: The definition restricts art to the products of recognised individual skill in the canonical fine arts, intended for contemplation rather than use. Yet art may be the product of anonymous collective labour, may be intended for practical purposes, and may be executed in media that fall outside the traditional canon. What is wanted is a broader conception that does not depend upon the social standing of the maker, the absence of practical function, or the traditional classification of the medium.
passage: One might propose that an object is a work of art if and only if it is produced by an individual possessing recognised technical skill in a specific artistic medium, is intended by its maker to be exhibited for contemplation rather than put to practical use, and is executed in one of the traditionally recognised fine arts. The definition appears to capture a wide range of paradigmatic cases. A painting by Titian satisfies all three conditions: Titian was a master of recognised skill, his paintings were intended for contemplation, and painting is among the canonical fine arts. A sonata by Mozart, a sonnet by Shakespeare, a marble figure by Bernini — each satisfies the conditions with equal ease. One might suppose that the definition identifies what is essential to art. Yet one must attend to the Persian carpet. In the sixteenth century, the workshops of Isfahan and Tabriz produced carpets of extraordinary beauty — carpets whose geometrical patterns display a mathematical sophistication that rivals any painting, whose harmonies of colour are as subtle and accomplished as those of any fresco. These carpets were not produced by a single individual of recognised artistic standing; they were woven by anonymous craftswomen working collectively in a workshop, following designs whose authorship is unknown.
