They were not intended for exhibition and contemplation; they were made to cover floors, to furnish rooms, to serve the practical needs of domestic life. And carpet-weaving is not among the traditionally recognised fine arts; it has been classified, when classified at all, among the decorative or applied arts, a category that the definition explicitly excludes. Yet these carpets are works of art. They are displayed in the Louvre, the Victoria and Albert Museum, and the Metropolitan Museum, and they are studied and admired by connoisseurs with the same seriousness that is accorded to painting and sculpture. The definition, in requiring recognised individual skill, contemplative intent, and execution in a canonical medium, mistakes the features of one historically contingent conception of art — the post-Renaissance European conception — for features that are necessary to art as such.

definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by an object of overwhelming physical magnitude, such as a mountain, an ocean, or a vast desert, (ii) E involves a feeling of terror or dread in the face of the object's power, and (iii) E is accompanied by a sense of one's own physical insignificance relative to the object.
conditions: E is occasioned by an object of overwhelming physical magnitude, such as a mountain, an ocean, or a vast desert
E involves a feeling of terror or dread in the face of the object's power
E is accompanied by a sense of one's own physical insignificance relative to the object
counterexample: Consider the experience of listening to the final movement of Beethoven's Ninth Symphony in a concert hall. The object is not one of overwhelming physical magnitude; it is merely sound produced by instruments and voices in an enclosed space. The listener does not feel terror or dread; what he feels is an exaltation, an elevation of spirit that is the very opposite of fear. And the experience is not accompanied by a sense of physical insignificance; rather, the listener feels enlarged, as though something in the music had expanded the boundaries of his own soul. Yet the experience is sublime.
missing_condition: The definition restricts the sublime to encounters with physically vast and terrifying natural objects, yet the sublime may be occasioned by works of art of no great physical magnitude, may involve exaltation rather than terror, and may enlarge rather than diminish the subject's sense of self. What is wanted is a condition that admits the sublime in art and in experiences of moral or spiritual elevation.
passage: One might propose that an experience is an experience of the sublime if and only if it is occasioned by an object of overwhelming physical magnitude, involves a feeling of terror or dread in the face of the object's power, and is accompanied by a sense of one's own physical insignificance relative to the object. The definition captures certain paradigmatic cases with considerable fidelity. The traveller who stands at the edge of the Grand Canyon and looks down into that immense abyss feels something that answers to all three conditions: the canyon is of overwhelming magnitude, the sheer depth of the chasm inspires a feeling of dread, and the traveller is acutely conscious of his own smallness before the vastness of the geological formation. Similarly, the mariner who encounters a storm at sea — the towering waves, the howling wind, the sense of nature's indifferent power — experiences something that the definition describes with tolerable accuracy. Yet one must attend to the experience of great music. A listener sits in a concert hall and hears the final movement of Beethoven's Ninth Symphony — the great choral setting of Schiller's Ode to Joy. The object that occasions his experience is not one of overwhelming physical magnitude; it is merely the sound produced by an orchestra and a chorus in a room of modest dimensions. The listener does not feel terror or dread; what he feels is an exaltation, a soaring elevation of spirit, a sense that the music has carried him beyond the limits of ordinary experience into a region where the boundaries between joy and solemnity dissolve. And the experience is not accompanied by a sense of physical insignificance; on the contrary, the listener feels enlarged, expanded, as though the music had revealed capacities of feeling that he had not known he possessed. Yet the experience is sublime — it is as sublime as any experience occasioned by a mountain or an ocean. The definition, in requiring overwhelming physical magnitude, terror, and a sense of insignificance, mistakes the features of the natural sublime for features that are necessary to sublimity as such.

definition: A person P possesses good taste if and only if (i) P has received systematic training in the appreciation of the fine arts, (ii) P's judgements of aesthetic merit are in accord with the prevailing consensus of educated opinion, and (iii) P is able to articulate the grounds of his judgements in the technical vocabulary of the relevant art form.
conditions: P has received systematic training in the appreciation of the fine arts
P's judgements of aesthetic merit are in accord with the prevailing consensus of educated opinion
P is able to articulate the grounds of his judgements in the technical vocabulary of the relevant art form
counterexample: Consider an unlettered farmer who has never received any training in the appreciation of the fine arts, who is wholly ignorant of the prevailing consensus of educated opinion, and who possesses no technical vocabulary whatever. He enters a cathedral for the first time and is struck by the beauty of a mediaeval stained-glass window. His response is immediate, sincere, and discriminating: he notices the subtlety of the colours, the expressiveness of the figures, the way the light transforms the glass into something more than mere decoration. His judgement is one of genuine taste, yet he satisfies none of the three conditions.
missing_condition: The definition requires formal training, agreement with educated consensus, and command of technical vocabulary, yet taste may be exercised by persons who lack all three. What is wanted is a condition pertaining to the sensitivity and discrimination of the person's perceptual and affective responses, independent of formal education or the capacity for articulate justification.
passage: One might propose that a person possesses good taste if and only if he has received systematic training in the appreciation of the fine arts, his judgements of aesthetic merit are in accord with the prevailing consensus of educated opinion, and he is able to articulate the grounds of his judgements in the technical vocabulary of the relevant art form. The definition has a certain social plausibility. The connoisseur who has studied painting at the Courtauld, whose opinions coincide with those expressed in the authoritative journals, and who can discourse fluently upon chiaroscuro, impasto, and pictorial composition is readily acknowledged as a man of taste. The definition captures, one might suppose, what distinguishes the man of taste from the untutored philistine. Yet one must attend to the unlettered farmer. A man of no formal education, who has spent his life tending sheep on the hills of the Lake District, enters Carlisle Cathedral for the first time. He stands before a mediaeval stained-glass window and is arrested by its beauty. He has received no training in the appreciation of the visual arts; he knows nothing of the prevailing consensus of educated opinion; and he possesses no technical vocabulary with which to articulate the grounds of his response. Yet his response is one of genuine discrimination. He notices, without being able to name, the subtlety with which the glass-maker has modulated his colours from deep blue to pale gold; he is struck by the expressiveness of the faces of the saints, which seem to him at once serene and sorrowful; and he perceives, without being able to articulate the perception, that the window is not mere decoration but something that transforms the light itself into an instrument of meaning. His judgement is a judgement of taste — a discriminating, sensitive response to aesthetic quality — yet it satisfies none of the three conditions. The definition, in requiring formal training, conformity with educated consensus, and technical articulacy, mistakes the social accoutrements of taste for the thing itself.

definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E is directed towards an object that has been deliberately set apart from ordinary life — placed in a gallery, performed upon a stage, or otherwise framed as an object of contemplation, (ii) E involves a suspension of all practical concerns and desires, and (iii) E is sustained over a continuous period of attentive regard.
conditions: E is directed towards an object that has been deliberately set apart from ordinary life — placed in a gallery, performed upon a stage, or otherwise framed as an object of contemplation
E involves a suspension of all practical concerns and desires
E is sustained over a continuous period of attentive regard
counterexample: Consider a man walking through a city street on his way to an appointment. He turns a corner and is suddenly struck by the pattern of light falling through the leaves of a plane tree onto the wet pavement. The experience is intense, vivid, and unmistakably aesthetic. Yet the object has not been set apart from ordinary life; it is an accidental configuration of light and shadow in a public thoroughfare. The man has not suspended all practical concerns; he is still conscious that he must reach his appointment. And the experience is not sustained over a continuous period; it lasts only a moment before he walks on.
missing_condition: The definition requires institutional framing, complete suspension of practical concern, and sustained attention, yet aesthetic experience may arise unbidden in the midst of ordinary life, may coexist with practical concerns, and may last only an instant. What is wanted is a condition that accommodates the fleeting, unsought aesthetic experience that overtakes us in the course of daily existence.
passage: One might propose that an experience is an aesthetic experience if and only if it is directed towards an object that has been deliberately set apart from ordinary life, involves a suspension of all practical concerns and desires, and is sustained over a continuous period of attentive regard. The definition captures a familiar and important class of cases. The visitor to the National Gallery who stands before a Vermeer, having put aside the cares of the day, and gives himself over to a prolonged contemplation of the painting's luminous interior — this visitor is having an aesthetic experience, and he satisfies all three conditions. The object has been set apart from ordinary life by being placed in a gallery; the visitor has suspended his practical concerns; and his attention is sustained over several minutes of quiet regard. Yet one must attend to a rather different case. A man is walking through a London street on a November afternoon, hurrying to keep an appointment. He turns a corner and is suddenly arrested by the pattern of late afternoon light falling through the leaves of a plane tree onto the wet pavement. The leaves cast intricate shadows, the light catches the water on the stones, and for a moment the ordinary street is transformed into something of extraordinary beauty. The experience is intense, vivid, and unmistakably aesthetic — it has the same quality of heightened perception, the same sense of significance, that the visitor to the gallery experiences before the Vermeer. Yet none of the three conditions is satisfied. The object has not been set apart from ordinary life; it is an accidental configuration of light and shadow in a public street. The man has not suspended all practical concerns; he is still conscious of his appointment and will resume his hurrying in a moment. And the experience is not sustained; it lasts perhaps five seconds before the man walks on. The definition, in requiring deliberate framing, complete suspension of practical concern, and sustained duration, mistakes the conditions of one paradigmatic form of aesthetic experience — the contemplation of art in an institutional setting — for conditions that are necessary to aesthetic experience as such.

definition: A work of art W represents a subject S if and only if (i) W bears a recognisable visual resemblance to S, (ii) the artist who produced W intended W to depict S, and (iii) a competent viewer, upon encountering W, would identify S as the subject of W without external guidance.
conditions: W bears a recognisable visual resemblance to S
the artist who produced W intended W to depict S
a competent viewer, upon encountering W, would identify S as the subject of W without external guidance
counterexample: Consider a piece of programme music — a symphonic poem, let us say, that represents a thunderstorm. The music bears no visual resemblance to a thunderstorm; sound and weather are objects of different senses entirely. The composer intended the work to represent a storm, and listeners who are told the title recognise the musical gestures — the rumbling of the timpani, the flash of the brass — as representing thunder and lightning. Yet a competent listener encountering the work without its title might not identify a thunderstorm as its subject at all. Two of the three conditions fail, yet the work represents a thunderstorm.
missing_condition: The definition restricts representation to cases of visual resemblance identifiable without external guidance, yet representation in art may operate through non-visual media and may depend upon conventions, titles, or contextual cues rather than upon unaided recognition. What is needed is a broader conception of representation that encompasses the symbolic and conventional means by which music, literature, and non-figurative art may represent their subjects.
passage: One might propose that a work of art represents a subject if and only if the work bears a recognisable visual resemblance to the subject, the artist intended the work to depict the subject, and a competent viewer would identify the subject without external guidance. The definition captures the central cases of pictorial representation with some fidelity. A portrait by Reynolds resembles the sitter; the painter intended to depict that particular person; and a competent viewer, upon seeing the portrait, would recognise it as a representation of a human face, and might, if acquainted with the sitter, identify the individual depicted. A landscape by Constable resembles the Stour Valley; the painter intended to depict that scene; and a viewer familiar with the English countryside would recognise the subject without difficulty. The three conditions appear to be jointly sufficient and severally necessary for representation. Yet one must attend to the case of programme music. Beethoven's Sixth Symphony, the Pastoral, represents scenes of country life — a brook, a gathering of peasants, a thunderstorm. The music does not bear a visual resemblance to any of these things; one cannot see a brook in a pattern of sound, however skilfully the strings imitate the murmur of flowing water. The relationship between the musical sounds and the scenes they represent is not one of visual resemblance but of suggestion, imitation, and convention. A competent listener encountering the symphony without its programmatic title might hear in it a mood of tranquillity succeeded by agitation and resolution, but he would not necessarily identify a thunderstorm or a gathering of peasants as the subject of the music. The representation depends upon the title, upon the conventions of programme music, and upon the listener's willingness to hear the musical gestures as standing for extra-musical events. The definition, in requiring visual resemblance and unaided identification, mistakes the features of pictorial representation for features that are necessary to representation as such, and thereby excludes the vast domain of musical, literary, and symbolic representation.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) the artist who created W was experiencing E at the time of creation, (ii) W exhibits formal properties — such as tempo, colour, or line — that are naturally associated with E, and (iii) a sensitive observer of W will be caused to feel E upon encountering the work.
conditions: the artist who created W was experiencing E at the time of creation
W exhibits formal properties — such as tempo, colour, or line — that are naturally associated with E
a sensitive observer of W will be caused to feel E upon encountering the work
counterexample: Consider a composer who writes a funeral march of the most profound and solemn grief whilst himself in a state of perfect cheerfulness and professional contentment. He is fulfilling a commission and brings to the task nothing but his technical skill and his understanding of musical convention. The march exhibits the formal properties associated with grief — slow tempo, minor key, heavy orchestration — and a sensitive listener will indeed feel something of the solemnity of grief upon hearing it. Yet the first condition fails entirely: the composer felt no grief whatever.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the artist experience the emotion expressed, yet works of art may express emotions that their creators did not feel. What is wanted is a condition that accommodates the craftsman's capacity to embody emotional significance in a work through skill and convention, independent of his own affective state at the time of creation.
passage: One might propose that a work of art expresses an emotion if and only if the artist was experiencing that emotion at the time of creation, the work exhibits formal properties naturally associated with the emotion, and a sensitive observer will be caused to feel the emotion upon encountering the work. The definition has a romantic appeal; it suggests an attractive picture of the artist pouring his feelings into his work, the work bearing the impress of those feelings, and the audience receiving them in turn. One thinks of the passionate canvases of van Gogh, in which the artist's inner turbulence seems to have passed directly into the swirling brushstrokes and violent colours. One thinks of the anguished letters of Beethoven and the stormy intensity of his late quartets, and one is tempted to see a direct causal link between the man's suffering and the music's expressive power. Yet one must attend to the case of the professional composer. A composer of established reputation receives a commission to write a funeral march for a state occasion. He sits down at his desk in good health and good spirits, with no particular occasion for grief, and writes a march of the most profound and solemn sorrow. He does so by deploying his technical understanding of the musical conventions of mourning: he chooses a slow tempo, a minor key, a heavy orchestration dominated by low brass and muffled drums. The march is deeply expressive of grief; a sensitive listener hearing it at the funeral will feel the solemnity and sorrow that the occasion demands. The formal properties associated with grief are present; the effect upon the listener is achieved. Yet the composer himself felt no grief. He was cheerful throughout the process of composition, and he regarded the commission as a welcome professional opportunity. The first condition — that the artist was experiencing the emotion at the time of creation — fails entirely. The definition, in requiring the artist's felt emotion as a necessary condition, mistakes one possible source of artistic expression for the only source, and overlooks the craftsman's capacity to embody emotional significance through skill, convention, and imaginative understanding.

definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by a natural phenomenon that is in principle beyond human control, (ii) E induces in the subject a momentary paralysis of the faculties of rational judgement, and (iii) E is followed by a conscious recognition of the superiority of human reason over mere natural force.
conditions: E is occasioned by a natural phenomenon that is in principle beyond human control
E induces in the subject a momentary paralysis of the faculties of rational judgement
E is followed by a conscious recognition of the superiority of human reason over mere natural force
counterexample: Consider the experience of standing beneath the dome of St Peter's Basilica in Rome. The dome is not a natural phenomenon; it is the product of human design and engineering. The experience does not paralyse the faculties of rational judgement; the visitor remains fully capable of thought and reflection throughout. Yet the experience is one of sublimity — the vastness of the interior, the soaring height of the dome, the grandeur of the conception overwhelm the visitor's ordinary sense of scale and produce an exaltation of spirit that is properly described as sublime.
missing_condition: The definition restricts the sublime to natural phenomena, requires paralysis of rational judgement, and demands a subsequent recognition of reason's superiority. Yet the sublime may be occasioned by works of human architecture, may leave the faculties of judgement intact, and may involve no dialectical movement from overwhelm to rational reassertion. What is needed is a broader account that admits the architectural and artistic sublime.
passage: One might propose that an experience is an experience of the sublime if and only if it is occasioned by a natural phenomenon beyond human control, induces a momentary paralysis of the faculties of rational judgement, and is followed by a conscious recognition of the superiority of human reason over mere natural force. The definition captures something important about certain celebrated instances of the sublime. The Alpine traveller who rounds a bend in the path and is confronted by the Matterhorn rising sheer above him may indeed feel a momentary suspension of his ordinary rational faculties — the mountain is so vast, so indifferent to human purposes, that for an instant his mind fails to comprehend what it sees. And this moment of overwhelm may be succeeded, upon reflection, by a recognition that the mind which can conceive of the mountain's magnitude, measure its height, and grasp its geological history possesses a dignity that surpasses any merely physical immensity. Yet one must attend to the dome of St Peter's. A visitor enters the basilica and stands beneath Michelangelo's dome. The experience is one of sublimity — the vast interior, the soaring curves of the dome, the play of light through the lantern produce an overwhelming sense of grandeur that defies ordinary description. But the dome is not a natural phenomenon; it is the supreme achievement of human architectural imagination. The visitor's rational faculties are not paralysed; he remains capable of thought and judgement throughout, and his awareness that the dome is the product of human genius is part of what makes the experience sublime. And the experience is not followed by a dialectical recognition of reason's superiority over natural force, for no natural force is in question; the sublimity lies precisely in the encounter with something that human reason and human hands have brought into being. The definition, in restricting the sublime to encounters with uncontrollable natural phenomena, mistakes one historically influential account of the sublime for a complete analysis, and thereby excludes the architectural sublime — one of the most powerful and widely acknowledged forms of sublime experience.

definition: A person P possesses good taste if and only if (i) P consistently prefers works of acknowledged excellence over works of inferior quality, (ii) P's preferences remain stable over time and are not subject to the fluctuations of fashion, and (iii) P derives his preferences from direct acquaintance with the works themselves rather than from the opinions of critics or the influence of social prestige.
conditions: P consistently prefers works of acknowledged excellence over works of inferior quality
P's preferences remain stable over time and are not subject to the fluctuations of fashion
P derives his preferences from direct acquaintance with the works themselves rather than from the opinions of critics or the influence of social prestige
counterexample: Consider a young man who, upon first acquaintance with serious music, genuinely prefers the lesser works of Salieri to the greater works of Mozart. Over the next decade, through sustained listening and the gradual refinement of his sensibility, he comes to prefer Mozart to Salieri. His preferences have shifted, and they have shifted in part because of what he has read and heard from more experienced listeners. Yet at the end of this process he possesses taste of a high order — his discrimination is finer, his perception more acute, his responses more deeply felt than they were at the outset.
missing_condition: The definition requires stable preferences derived solely from direct acquaintance, yet taste may develop through a process of education in which preferences change over time and are shaped by the testimony of others. What is wanted is a conception of taste that admits of cultivation and refinement rather than demanding an impossible self-sufficiency and fixity of judgement.
passage: One might propose that a person possesses good taste if and only if he consistently prefers works of acknowledged excellence over works of inferior quality, his preferences remain stable over time, and he derives his preferences from direct acquaintance with the works themselves rather than from the opinions of critics or the influence of social prestige. The definition captures something of the ideal of the independent-minded connoisseur. One thinks of the man who, having listened to a great deal of music, reliably gravitates towards the finest performances and the greatest compositions, whose judgement is not shaken by the enthusiasms of the moment, and who trusts his own ear rather than the pronouncements of the critical establishment. The three conditions appear to describe what distinguishes genuine taste from mere fashion-following or social conformity. Yet one must attend to the case of the developing listener. A young man of twenty, upon first encountering serious music, genuinely and sincerely prefers the pleasant but unremarkable works of Salieri to the profound and difficult works of Mozart. His preference is derived from direct acquaintance; he has listened to both composers and simply likes Salieri better. Over the next decade, through sustained and attentive listening, through reading, through conversation with more experienced listeners, and through the gradual refinement of his perceptual and affective capacities, he comes to prefer Mozart to Salieri. His preferences have changed. They have been influenced by the opinions of others. He has been educated. At the end of this process, he is a man of taste — his discrimination is finer, his perception more acute, his responses more nuanced and more deeply felt than they were at the outset. But his preferences were not stable; they shifted over the course of a decade. And they were not derived solely from direct acquaintance; they were shaped by the testimony and example of others. The definition, in requiring stability of preference and independence from the opinions of others, mistakes the endpoint of aesthetic education for a condition that must hold throughout, and thereby excludes the very process by which taste is formed.

definition: An object O is a work of art if and only if (i) O is unique — a singular, unrepeatable creation rather than one of many identical copies, (ii) O bears the visible mark of its maker's individual hand, and (iii) O is intended by its maker to convey a personal vision or interpretation of some aspect of human experience.
conditions: O is unique — a singular, unrepeatable creation rather than one of many identical copies
O bears the visible mark of its maker's individual hand
O is intended by its maker to convey a personal vision or interpretation of some aspect of human experience
counterexample: Consider a woodcut print by Dürer. The print exists not as a unique object but as one of many impressions pulled from the same block; each impression is, in the relevant sense, a copy. The visible mark of Dürer's hand is on the original woodblock, but the prints themselves bear the mark of the press rather than of the artist's direct touch. Yet each impression of a Dürer woodcut is a work of art of the highest order, and no serious critic would deny it that status.
missing_condition: The definition requires uniqueness and the visible mark of the maker's hand, yet works of art may exist in multiple copies and may be produced by mechanical processes that efface the direct trace of the artist's touch. What is wanted is a conception that admits printmaking, casting, and other reproductive techniques as legitimate means of artistic creation.
passage: One might propose that an object is a work of art if and only if it is unique — a singular, unrepeatable creation — bears the visible mark of its maker's individual hand, and is intended by its maker to convey a personal vision of some aspect of human experience. The definition captures something important about certain central cases of art. An oil painting by Rembrandt is unique; there is only one Night Watch, and no copy can replace it. The painting bears the visible mark of Rembrandt's hand in every brushstroke — the impasto, the glazes, the characteristic handling of light and shadow. And the painting conveys a personal vision of its subject. The three conditions appear to identify what is essential to art. Yet one must attend to the woodcut print. Dürer produced some of the greatest works of graphic art in the Western tradition: the Apocalypse series, the Knight, Death and the Devil, the Melancholia. Each of these works exists not as a unique object but as a series of impressions pulled from a single carved block. The impressions are, in the relevant sense, copies of one another; if one is destroyed, others survive, and no single impression can claim the status of the unique original in the way that the Night Watch can. The visible mark of Dürer's hand is on the woodblock itself, but the prints bear the mark of the press — the even pressure of the printing mechanism — rather than the direct trace of the artist's fingers. Yet each impression is a work of art. The definition, in requiring uniqueness and the visible mark of the maker's hand, mistakes the features of one medium — oil painting — for features that are essential to art as such, and thereby excludes printmaking, one of the oldest and most distinguished of artistic traditions.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) the formal properties of W — its colours, lines, rhythms, or tonal qualities — are isomorphic with the physiological and behavioural manifestations of E, (ii) this isomorphism is perceptible to any attentive observer regardless of cultural background, and (iii) W expresses only one emotion, which pervades the work as a single, unified affective quality.
conditions: the formal properties of W are isomorphic with the physiological and behavioural manifestations of E
this isomorphism is perceptible to any attentive observer regardless of cultural background
W expresses only one emotion, which pervades the work as a single, unified affective quality
counterexample: Consider a late quartet by Beethoven — the Opus 132, let us say. The work does not express a single emotion; it moves through a succession of emotional states — anguish, tenderness, gratitude, exuberance — that cannot be reduced to a single affective quality. The isomorphism between its musical properties and the physiological manifestations of any one emotion is at best partial and contested. And the perception of its emotional content is not independent of cultural background; a listener steeped in the traditions of Western classical music hears in the work emotional nuances that a listener from a quite different musical culture might not perceive. Yet the quartet is among the most expressively powerful works in the entire musical literature.
missing_condition: The definition requires a single emotion, universal perceptibility, and structural isomorphism with physiological expression, yet the most profoundly expressive works of art express complex, shifting, and culturally inflected emotional states. What is wanted is a conception of expression that admits of emotional complexity, ambiguity, and cultural mediation.
passage: One might propose that a work of art expresses an emotion if and only if its formal properties are isomorphic with the physiological and behavioural manifestations of that emotion, this isomorphism is perceptible to any attentive observer regardless of cultural background, and the work expresses only one emotion, which pervades it as a single unified affective quality. The definition has a certain theoretical elegance. A slow, descending melodic line in a minor key might be said to be isomorphic with the drooping posture and slowed movement of a person in the grip of sadness; the isomorphism is perceptible, one might suppose, to any listener; and if the entire work maintains this quality, it expresses sadness as a single, unified emotional state. Yet one must attend to the late quartets of Beethoven. The Quartet in A minor, Opus 132, is among the most expressively powerful works in the Western musical tradition. Its third movement, the Heiliger Dankgesang, moves between passages of ethereal, hymn-like gratitude and passages of vigorous, almost dance-like energy. The work as a whole traverses a vast emotional landscape — from the anguished chromaticism of the opening to the tender simplicity of the thanksgiving hymn to the exuberant vitality of the final movement. It does not express a single emotion; it expresses a succession of emotional states, and much of its expressive power lies precisely in the transitions between them, in the way that anguish yields to gratitude and gratitude to exuberance. The isomorphism between the musical properties and the physiological manifestations of any single emotion is at best partial; one cannot point to a single bodily state that corresponds to the complex emotional trajectory of the whole. And the perception of this emotional content is not independent of cultural background; a listener trained in the Western classical tradition hears nuances of expression — the significance of a particular harmonic progression, the emotional weight of a shift from minor to major — that a listener from a different musical culture might not perceive at all. The definition, in requiring a single emotion, universal perceptibility, and structural isomorphism, mistakes the features of simple, culturally transparent emotional expression for features that are necessary to artistic expression as such.

definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E produces in the subject a state of pleasurable absorption, (ii) the pleasure in E arises from the perception of formal properties such as symmetry, balance, and pattern, and (iii) E is wholly independent of the subject's moral judgements about the object experienced.
conditions: E produces in the subject a state of pleasurable absorption
the pleasure in E arises from the perception of formal properties such as symmetry, balance, and pattern
E is wholly independent of the subject's moral judgements about the object experienced
counterexample: Consider the experience of reading a tragic novel — Anna Karenina, let us suppose. The experience is aesthetic, yet it is not pleasurable in any straightforward sense; it involves pity, anguish, and a painful recognition of the waste of human possibility. The response does not arise from the perception of formal properties such as symmetry and balance; it arises from engagement with the moral situation of the characters, from the reader's sympathetic participation in their suffering. And the experience is not independent of moral judgement; the reader's aesthetic response to Anna's fate is inseparable from his moral assessment of the society that destroys her.
missing_condition: The definition restricts aesthetic experience to pleasurable, formally grounded, morally disengaged responses, yet aesthetic experience may involve painful emotions, may arise from engagement with content rather than form, and may be deeply entangled with moral perception. What is wanted is a broader conception that admits the aesthetic experience of tragedy, moral complexity, and painful beauty.
passage: One might propose that an experience is an aesthetic experience if and only if it produces a state of pleasurable absorption, the pleasure arises from the perception of formal properties such as symmetry, balance, and pattern, and the experience is wholly independent of the subject's moral judgements. The definition captures certain cases tolerably well. The pleasure one takes in a well-proportioned Georgian facade, with its symmetrical windows, its balanced pediment, and its harmonious proportions, is indeed a pleasure that arises from the perception of formal properties and that seems to have nothing to do with moral judgement. One is absorbed in the pattern; one's pleasure is disinterested and formally grounded. Yet one must attend to the experience of reading a tragic novel. A reader takes up Tolstoy's Anna Karenina and reads it through over the course of several days. The experience is aesthetic — it is an experience of art, and it engages the reader's perceptual and imaginative faculties at the highest level. But it is not pleasurable in any straightforward sense. The reader feels pity for Anna, anguish at the narrowness and cruelty of the society that constrains her, and a painful recognition of the waste of human possibility that her destruction represents. These are not pleasurable emotions; they are painful, and the reader may set the book aside more than once because the pain is too acute to bear. The aesthetic response does not arise from the perception of formal properties such as symmetry and balance; it arises from the reader's engagement with the moral situation of the characters, from his sympathetic participation in their suffering and his imaginative inhabitation of their world. And the experience is not independent of moral judgement; the reader's aesthetic response to Anna's fate is inseparable from his moral assessment of the social conventions that destroy her and the human weakness that fails to save her. The definition, in requiring pleasure, formal grounding, and moral independence, mistakes the features of one species of aesthetic experience — the contemplation of formal beauty — for the features of aesthetic experience as such.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O possesses a harmonious arrangement of its parts — that is, the parts stand in relations of proportion and balance, (ii) O produces in a normally constituted observer a feeling of disinterested pleasure, and (iii) O is the product of deliberate design or conscious artistic intention.
conditions: O possesses a harmonious arrangement of its parts — the parts stand in relations of proportion and balance
O produces in a normally constituted observer a feeling of disinterested pleasure
O is the product of deliberate design or conscious artistic intention
counterexample: Consider a sunset over the sea. The arrangement of colours — the reds, the golds, the deepening purples — and the gradual recession of light produce in the observer a feeling of disinterested pleasure that is, by common consent, an apprehension of beauty. The sunset possesses a kind of harmony, though it is the harmony of natural process rather than of deliberate composition. Yet the sunset is not the product of deliberate design or conscious artistic intention. No agent arranged the clouds or selected the palette. The third condition fails, yet the sunset is beautiful in the fullest sense of the term.
missing_condition: The definition requires that beauty be the product of deliberate design, but natural objects and scenes may be beautiful without any designing intelligence behind them. What is wanted is an account that allows beauty to arise from natural processes as well as from artistic intention.
passage: One might propose that an object is beautiful if and only if it possesses a harmonious arrangement of its parts, produces in a normally constituted observer a feeling of disinterested pleasure, and is the product of deliberate design or conscious artistic intention. The definition has considerable initial plausibility. A well-proportioned building, a finely composed painting, a carefully wrought sonata — in each case, the beauty of the object seems bound up with the skill and intention of its creator, who has arranged the parts in relations of proportion and balance so as to produce in the observer that peculiar pleasure which we call aesthetic. Yet one must consider a sunset over the sea. The sky is lit with bands of crimson and gold; the clouds are touched with violet; the light fades by imperceptible degrees into the deep blue of the coming night. The observer who stands upon the shore and watches this spectacle experiences a feeling of disinterested pleasure — a pleasure that is not bound up with any practical interest or any desire to possess the scene, but arises simply from the contemplation of its qualities. There is, moreover, a kind of harmony in the arrangement of the colours and the gradual transition of the light, a harmony that satisfies the eye as completely as any arrangement devised by a painter. But the sunset is not the product of deliberate design or conscious artistic intention. No agent selected the colours, no intelligence arranged the clouds, no will directed the recession of the light. The beauty of the sunset arises from the operation of natural processes — the refraction and scattering of sunlight by the atmosphere, the distribution of water vapour, the angle of the earth's rotation — none of which involves design or intention. The third condition of the definition fails. The analysis has taken a feature of artistic beauty — the presence of a designing intelligence — and elevated it to a necessary condition of beauty as such, when in truth some of the most striking instances of beauty in our experience are wholly natural, owing nothing to the deliberate arrangement of any conscious agent.

definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude — that is, an object that exceeds the ordinary scale of human perception, (ii) E involves a feeling of terror or awe that momentarily overwhelms the faculties, and (iii) E is followed by a feeling of elevation or exaltation as the mind recovers its composure.
conditions: E is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude
E involves a feeling of terror or awe that momentarily overwhelms the faculties
E is followed by a feeling of elevation or exaltation as the mind recovers its composure
counterexample: Consider the experience of listening to a passage of organ music in a cathedral — the final fugue of a great work, in which the voices pile upon one another in a structure of immense complexity. The listener may experience something aptly described as sublime: a feeling of awe at the inexhaustible intricacy of the musical architecture, a momentary sense that the understanding is overwhelmed, and an exaltation as the fugue resolves. Yet the organ music is not an object of overwhelming physical magnitude. It does not exceed the ordinary scale of human perception in the manner of a mountain range or a storm at sea; it is a sequence of sounds within a building. The first condition fails, yet the experience is genuinely sublime.
missing_condition: The definition restricts the sublime to objects of overwhelming physical magnitude, but the sublime may be occasioned by intellectual or artistic complexity that overwhelms the understanding without exceeding the scale of ordinary perception. What is wanted is a notion of the sublime that encompasses both the physically vast and the intellectually or artistically overwhelming.
passage: Suppose one holds that an experience is an experience of the sublime if and only if it is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude, it involves a feeling of terror or awe that momentarily overwhelms the faculties, and it is followed by a feeling of elevation or exaltation as the mind recovers its composure. The definition accords well with many familiar instances of the sublime. The traveller who stands at the edge of a great precipice and looks down into the abyss experiences terror at the vastness below, and then, as the initial shock subsides, a feeling of exaltation — as though the mind, having confronted something that exceeds its ordinary compass, has been enlarged by the encounter. The mariner who watches a storm at sea, the mountaineer who surveys an alpine panorama, the observer who contemplates the night sky — in each case, the overwhelming magnitude of the object occasions a complex emotional response that moves from awe through terror to elevation. Yet one must consider the experience of listening to a passage of organ music in a great cathedral. The fugue builds voice upon voice, theme upon counter-theme, in a structure of such contrapuntal complexity that the listener is momentarily overwhelmed. There is a feeling akin to awe — not the awe of physical vastness, but the awe of intellectual and artistic profundity, of a structure too rich and too intricate to be grasped at a single hearing. And as the fugue moves towards its resolution, there is a feeling of elevation, of exaltation, as the mind apprehends the coherence that underlies the complexity. This experience has every mark of the sublime. Yet the organ music is not an object of overwhelming physical magnitude. The sounds are produced within the walls of a building; they do not exceed the ordinary scale of human perception in the manner of an alpine prospect or an oceanic storm. The first condition fails. The definition has identified the sublime with the physically vast, when in truth the sublime may be occasioned by intellectual or artistic complexity that overwhelms the understanding without overwhelming the senses.

definition: A person P possesses good taste if and only if (i) P has been educated in the established canons of artistic excellence within a recognised tradition, (ii) P's aesthetic judgements agree with the judgements of acknowledged experts and critics, and (iii) P is able to articulate the reasons for preferring one work of art to another.
conditions: P has been educated in the established canons of artistic excellence within a recognised tradition
P's aesthetic judgements agree with the judgements of acknowledged experts and critics
P is able to articulate the reasons for preferring one work of art to another
counterexample: Consider an unlettered countryman who has received no formal education in the arts and knows nothing of canons or traditions, yet who, upon hearing a piece of music for the first time, responds with a depth of feeling and discrimination that astonishes even trained musicians. He cannot name the composer, cannot identify the form, cannot articulate in critical vocabulary why one passage moves him and another leaves him cold — yet his preferences, when tested, prove remarkably consonant with those of the most refined judges. The first and third conditions fail, yet his taste is, by any reasonable standard, good.
missing_condition: The definition requires formal education and the capacity for articulate critical reasoning, but good taste may be a natural endowment — a sensitivity of perception and feeling — that operates prior to and independently of education and critical discourse. What is wanted is an account that recognises the possibility of untutored taste.
passage: One might hold that a person possesses good taste if and only if the person has been educated in the established canons of artistic excellence, the person's aesthetic judgements agree with those of acknowledged experts and critics, and the person is able to articulate the reasons for preferring one work to another. The definition has considerable support in the practice of the arts. The connoisseur of painting has studied the masters; the critic of poetry has read widely and deeply in the tradition; the judge of music has been trained to distinguish the excellent from the merely competent. In each case, education has refined the faculty of judgement, agreement with expert opinion provides a check upon eccentricity, and the capacity for articulate reasoning distinguishes the true judge from the person who merely happens to like what is good. Yet one must consider the case of an unlettered countryman who has had no formal education in the arts whatever. He has not studied the canons, does not know the names of the masters, and has never read a word of criticism. He is brought, for the first time, to a concert of chamber music. He listens with close attention, and afterwards, when asked which of the pieces he preferred, he identifies without hesitation the work that the most discerning critics in the audience also judged finest. He cannot say why he prefers it; he lacks the vocabulary of musical analysis and the habits of critical articulation. He knows nothing of sonata form or of the development of thematic material. Yet his preference is unerring, and upon repeated trials his judgements continue to coincide with those of the most cultivated listeners. The first and third conditions of the definition fail outright: he has had no education in any canon, and he cannot articulate the grounds of his preferences. Yet his taste is, by any reasonable standard, good — perhaps better than that of some who have studied long and speak fluently of what they have studied. The definition has confused the instruments by which taste is commonly refined and expressed with the faculty of taste itself, which may be a natural endowment that operates prior to and independently of all education and all critical discourse.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) the artist who created W experienced E during the process of creation, (ii) the formal properties of W — its colours, lines, rhythms, or tones — are causally related to the artist's experience of E, and (iii) a competent audience, upon encountering W, experiences E or a close analogue of E.
conditions: the artist experienced E during the process of creation
the formal properties of W are causally related to the artist's experience of E
a competent audience experiences E or a close analogue of E upon encountering W
counterexample: Consider a composer who is commissioned to write a funeral march. He is in excellent spirits — contented, well-fed, looking forward to a holiday — and feels no grief whatever during the process of composition. Yet by skilful manipulation of tempo, harmony, and orchestration, he produces a march that is universally acknowledged to express profound sorrow. Every competent listener hears grief in the music. The first condition fails: the artist did not experience the emotion during creation. Yet the work expresses grief as surely as any work ever has.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the artist have experienced the expressed emotion during creation, but artists may express emotions they do not feel, through craft and the mastery of expressive conventions. What is wanted is an account that locates expression in the properties of the work rather than in the biography of the artist.
passage: One might propose that a work of art expresses an emotion if and only if the artist experienced that emotion during the process of creation, the formal properties of the work are causally related to the artist's experience of the emotion, and a competent audience, upon encountering the work, experiences the emotion or a close analogue of it. The definition has a natural appeal. When we say that a painting expresses anguish, we are inclined to suppose that the painter felt anguish as he worked, that the agitated brushstrokes and sombre colours are the traces of his inner turmoil, and that we, in viewing the painting, are brought to feel something of what he felt. The work is, on this account, a vehicle by which the artist's emotion is transmitted to the audience, and the formal properties of the work are the medium of that transmission. Yet one must consider the case of a composer who is commissioned to write a funeral march for a state occasion. The composer is, at the time of composition, in excellent spirits. He is contented with his domestic circumstances, satisfied with his professional standing, and looking forward to a holiday in the country. He feels no grief, no sorrow, no heaviness of heart. Yet he is a master of his craft. By the skilful manipulation of tempo — slow, measured, processional — of harmony — minor keys, dissonant suspensions resolving downward — and of orchestration — muted brass, the low register of the strings, the muffled beat of the drum — he produces a funeral march that is universally acknowledged to express the most profound sorrow. Every competent listener hears grief in the music; the march has been played at funerals for generations, and no one has doubted that it expresses what a funeral march ought to express. The first condition of the definition fails entirely: the composer did not experience grief during creation. The formal properties of the work are not causally related to any experience of sorrow on the part of the artist, but to his technical knowledge of the conventions by which sorrow is expressed in music. The definition has confused the causal history of the work with its expressive character, when in truth a work may express an emotion through the mastery of expressive conventions, quite independently of the artist's own feelings.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O exhibits a perceptible unity of form — that is, its parts are arranged so as to compose an integrated whole, (ii) O elicits in a suitably attentive observer a feeling of repose and contemplative satisfaction, and (iii) O admits of comparison with an ideal standard of its kind, such that it approximates that standard more closely than do ordinary specimens.
conditions: O exhibits a perceptible unity of form — its parts compose an integrated whole
O elicits in a suitably attentive observer a feeling of repose and contemplative satisfaction
O admits of comparison with an ideal standard of its kind and approximates that standard more closely than ordinary specimens
counterexample: Consider a piece of driftwood cast upon a beach by the tide. Its form is twisted and irregular; it was shaped by water, salt, and accident, and it approximates no ideal standard of any recognisable kind. Yet persons of cultivated taste frequently collect such pieces, display them in their homes, and describe them as beautiful. The driftwood elicits contemplative satisfaction and possesses a certain unity of form, but it fails the third condition entirely, since there is no ideal standard of driftwood against which it might be measured.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that beauty requires approximation to a type-ideal, but objects may be beautiful precisely in their singularity, where no standard of kind is applicable.
passage: One might propose that an object is beautiful if and only if it exhibits a perceptible unity of form, elicits in a suitably attentive observer a feeling of repose and contemplative satisfaction, and admits of comparison with an ideal standard of its kind, such that it approximates that standard more closely than do ordinary specimens. There is something to be said for this proposal. A fine Greek vase, a well-proportioned building, a handsome horse — in each case we seem to judge beauty by measuring the particular against the ideal of its class. The vase is beautiful because it is a supremely good vase; the building because it realises the form of a temple or a cathedral with unusual completeness. Yet one might observe that the proposal is too narrow. Consider a piece of driftwood cast upon a beach by the tide. Its form is twisted and irregular, shaped entirely by water, salt, and accident, and it approximates no ideal standard of any recognisable kind. There is no class of driftwood with a paradigmatic form. Yet persons of cultivated taste frequently collect such pieces, display them in their homes, and describe them without hesitation as beautiful. The driftwood elicits contemplative satisfaction and possesses a certain unity of form — the eye follows its curves with pleasure — but the third condition fails utterly. We should hesitate to say that a piece of driftwood is beautiful only to the extent that it resembles some Platonic driftwood. What the case reveals is that beauty may reside in singularity as much as in conformity to type, and that an adequate account must allow for objects whose beauty consists precisely in their departure from any recognisable standard.

definition: An artefact A is a work of art if and only if (i) A is the product of a human agent who intended to create it, (ii) A is fashioned with a degree of skill or craft that exceeds ordinary competence, and (iii) A is designed to be apprehended primarily through the senses — that is, it is addressed to perception rather than to practical use.
conditions: A is the product of a human agent who intended to create it
A is fashioned with a degree of skill or craft that exceeds ordinary competence
A is designed to be apprehended primarily through the senses rather than put to practical use
counterexample: Consider Duchamp's readymade — a manufactured bottle-rack, selected from a shop and placed upon a pedestal in a gallery. The bottle-rack is the product of a factory process, not of any skill that exceeds ordinary competence; indeed Duchamp himself performed no act of craft upon it. It was not designed to be apprehended through the senses in any special way, for it is an industrial object intended for drying bottles. Yet it is, by the consensus of the artworld, a work of art. The second and third conditions are not met, and yet the concept applies.
missing_condition: The definition fails to capture the role of institutional or contextual factors — the act of selection, presentation, and endorsement by the artworld — which may confer the status of art upon objects that lack craft or perceptual design.
passage: It appears initially plausible to define a work of art as an artefact that is the product of a human agent who intended to create it, that is fashioned with a degree of skill or craft exceeding ordinary competence, and that is designed to be apprehended primarily through the senses rather than put to practical use. A painting, a sculpture, a sonata — each satisfies these conditions. The painter exercises skill in the arrangement of pigments; the sculptor shapes marble with hard-won technique; the composer commands the resources of harmony and counterpoint. In each case the resulting object is addressed to perception — to the eye or the ear — rather than to any practical end. But one might observe that the definition encounters a difficulty when we turn to certain objects that have, since the early decades of this century, been accepted as works of art. Consider a manufactured bottle-rack, selected from a shop and placed upon a pedestal in a gallery. No act of craft was performed upon it; it was not fashioned with skill exceeding ordinary competence, for no one fashioned it at all in the relevant sense. It was designed for the practical purpose of drying bottles, not for perceptual apprehension. Yet it is, by the consensus of informed opinion, a work of art. The second and third conditions are plainly unsatisfied, and yet the concept of art applies. What the case reveals is that the definition has omitted a crucial factor: the role of context, selection, and institutional endorsement. An object may become art not through the exercise of craft but through an act of designation within a suitable framework, and no definition that ignores this possibility can be regarded as adequate.

definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude or power, (ii) E involves an initial moment of pain, fear, or awe at the inadequacy of one's faculties before the object, and (iii) E terminates in a feeling of elevation or exaltation as the mind recognises its own rational superiority over mere natural force.
conditions: E is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude or power
E involves an initial moment of pain, fear, or awe at the inadequacy of one's faculties
E terminates in a feeling of elevation or exaltation as the mind recognises its rational superiority over natural force
counterexample: Consider the experience of listening to the final movement of Beethoven's Ninth Symphony in a concert hall. Many listeners report an overwhelming feeling that answers in all respects to the sublime — a sense of being carried beyond oneself, a mingling of awe and exaltation. Yet the occasion is not an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude or power in any natural sense; it is a sequence of sounds produced by human musicians. The initial moment is not one of fear or pain at the inadequacy of one's faculties before brute force, but something closer to an astonishment at the expressive capacity of organised sound. The experience is sublime, yet conditions (i) and (ii) are not satisfied in the way the definition requires.
missing_condition: The definition restricts the sublime to responses to natural magnitude or power and neglects the possibility of an artistic or intellectual sublime, where the overwhelming quality resides in expressive or conceptual achievement rather than in brute physical force.
passage: One might propose that an experience is an experience of the sublime if and only if it is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude or power, it involves an initial moment of pain, fear, or awe at the inadequacy of one's faculties before the object, and it terminates in a feeling of elevation or exaltation as the mind recognises its own rational superiority over mere natural force. This is broadly the account one finds in the Kantian tradition, and it has considerable plausibility when applied to the paradigmatic cases — the vast mountain range, the raging storm, the limitless ocean viewed from a safe shore. In each instance the mind is first overwhelmed and then rebounds, as it were, into a consciousness of its own dignity. Yet it appears that the definition is too restrictive. Consider the experience of listening to the final movement of Beethoven's Ninth Symphony in a concert hall. Many listeners report a feeling that answers in all important respects to the sublime: a sense of being carried beyond the ordinary limits of experience, a mingling of awe and exaltation that is unlike any merely pleasant sensation. Yet the occasion is not an object of overwhelming natural magnitude or power. It is a sequence of sounds produced by human musicians in accordance with a score. The initial moment is not one of fear or pain at the inadequacy of one's faculties before brute force, but something closer to an astonishment at the expressive capacity of organised sound. The first two conditions are thus not satisfied in the manner the definition requires, and yet the experience is, one might insist, genuinely sublime. What is wanted is an account that allows the sublime to arise from artistic or intellectual sources as well as from natural ones, and that does not tie the concept exclusively to physical magnitude.

definition: A person P possesses good taste if and only if (i) P is able to discriminate fine differences in sensory and formal qualities of objects, (ii) P's judgements of aesthetic merit are consistent across relevantly similar cases, and (iii) P's judgements converge with those of other persons who satisfy conditions (i) and (ii).
conditions: P is able to discriminate fine differences in sensory and formal qualities of objects
P's judgements of aesthetic merit are consistent across relevantly similar cases
P's judgements converge with those of other persons who satisfy conditions (i) and (ii)
counterexample: Consider a critic of the visual arts who possesses exceptionally refined powers of discrimination — she notices subtleties of colour, line, and composition that escape less attentive observers — and whose judgements are entirely consistent. Yet she champions a style of painting that is regarded by the majority of equally discriminating critics as mediocre. Her taste is, let us suppose, genuinely independent: she perceives qualities in these works that others do not, and history later vindicates her judgement. At the time, however, condition (iii) fails, since her judgements do not converge with those of other discriminating observers. Yet we should be reluctant to deny that she possesses good taste.
missing_condition: The definition makes good taste depend upon convergence with a consensus of qualified judges, but a person of good taste may be ahead of prevailing opinion, and the ability to perceive value where others do not may itself be a mark of superior taste.
passage: It is tempting to define good taste as follows: a person possesses good taste if and only if she is able to discriminate fine differences in sensory and formal qualities, her judgements of aesthetic merit are consistent across relevantly similar cases, and her judgements converge with those of other persons who satisfy the first two conditions. The definition has a certain democratic appeal: taste is vindicated by agreement among the qualified. And in many ordinary cases it seems to work tolerably well. The person of good taste in wine, for instance, discriminates subtle flavours, judges consistently, and agrees in the main with other competent tasters. But one might observe that the definition encounters a serious difficulty. Consider a critic of the visual arts who possesses exceptionally refined powers of discrimination — she notices subtleties of colour, line, and composition that escape less attentive observers — and whose judgements are entirely consistent over time. Yet she champions a style of painting that the majority of equally discriminating critics regard as mediocre. Her taste is independent and, as it happens, history vindicates her: the works she championed come to be recognised as masterpieces. At the time of her advocacy, however, condition (iii) fails. Her judgements do not converge with those of other qualified observers. Must we say that she lacked good taste during the years when she stood alone? That conclusion seems absurd. What the case reveals is that the definition has confused good taste with orthodox taste, and that the capacity to perceive merit where others do not may itself be a distinguishing mark of the finest judgement.

definition: A mental state M is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) M is occasioned by attending to the formal or sensory properties of an object, (ii) M involves a pleasurable absorption in the object for its own sake, without regard to practical consequences, and (iii) M is free from conceptual or intellectual mediation — that is, it is a matter of direct sensory apprehension rather than of thought or inference.
conditions: M is occasioned by attending to the formal or sensory properties of an object
M involves a pleasurable absorption in the object for its own sake without regard to practical consequences
M is free from conceptual or intellectual mediation — it is a matter of direct sensory apprehension
counterexample: Consider the experience of reading a philosophical dialogue by Plato — the Symposium, let us say. The reader is absorbed in the text for its own sake, attending to the elegance of its structure, the beauty of its imagery, the rhythm of its arguments. The experience is pleasurable and disinterested in the relevant sense. Yet it is thoroughly conceptual and intellectual: one must understand the Greek, follow the reasoning, and grasp the dramatic situation. Condition (iii) is not satisfied, since the experience is pervasively mediated by thought and inference. Yet it is, one might insist, a paradigmatic aesthetic experience.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly excludes intellectual and conceptual engagement from aesthetic experience, but some of the richest aesthetic experiences — those afforded by literature, philosophy, and mathematics — are inseparable from acts of understanding.
passage: One might propose that a mental state is an aesthetic experience if and only if it is occasioned by attending to the formal or sensory properties of an object, it involves a pleasurable absorption in the object for its own sake without regard to practical consequences, and it is free from conceptual or intellectual mediation — that is, it is a matter of direct sensory apprehension rather than of thought or inference. There is a long tradition, stretching at least from certain passages in Kant to the formalist critics of the twentieth century, that favours something like this view. The aesthetic, on this account, is the realm of pure perception: we see the colours, hear the sounds, feel the textures, and our pleasure is immediate and unreflective. Yet it appears that this proposal is too restrictive. Consider the experience of reading a philosophical dialogue by Plato — the Symposium, let us say. The reader is absorbed in the text for its own sake, attending to the elegance of its structure, the beauty of its imagery, the rhythm of its arguments as they unfold. The experience is pleasurable and disinterested; the reader is not reading for any practical purpose. Yet it is thoroughly conceptual and intellectual. One must understand the language, follow the reasoning, and grasp the dramatic situation. Condition (iii) is plainly not satisfied. The experience is pervasively mediated by thought, inference, and interpretive activity. Yet it is, one might insist, a paradigmatic aesthetic experience — indeed, one of the finest that literature affords. What the case reveals is that the definition has drawn the boundary of the aesthetic too narrowly, excluding precisely those experiences in which intellectual engagement and sensory delight are most intimately fused.

definition: A picture P represents an object X if and only if (i) P bears a visual resemblance to X — that is, there is a discernible similarity in shape, colour, or spatial arrangement between P and X, (ii) the maker of P intended P to depict X, and (iii) a competent viewer, informed of the maker's intention, would recognise X in P.
conditions: P bears a visual resemblance to X — there is discernible similarity in shape, colour, or spatial arrangement
The maker of P intended P to depict X
A competent viewer informed of the maker's intention would recognise X in P
counterexample: Consider a highly abstract painting by Kandinsky, titled 'Composition VII.' The painter intended the work to represent the tumult and resolution of apocalyptic themes — destruction, rebirth, the flood. A viewer informed of this intention may indeed come to see these themes in the painting. Yet there is no visual resemblance between the arrangement of coloured forms on the canvas and any scene of destruction or rebirth. Condition (i) fails entirely: the shapes and colours do not look like the things they are said to represent. Yet the work does represent its subject, albeit in a non-mimetic fashion.
missing_condition: The definition ties representation to visual resemblance, but pictorial representation may operate through convention, association, or expressive analogy rather than through perceptible similarity of form.
passage: It appears natural to say that a picture represents an object if and only if it bears a visual resemblance to that object, the maker intended it to depict that object, and a competent viewer informed of the intention would recognise the object in the picture. This resemblance theory has an ancient pedigree and captures a good deal of ordinary pictorial practice. A portrait resembles its sitter; a landscape resembles the scene it depicts; a still life resembles the arrangement of fruit and crockery that the painter set before himself. In each case the viewer recognises the subject because the picture looks like the thing. But one might observe that the definition encounters a difficulty when applied to certain kinds of painting. Consider a highly abstract work by Kandinsky, titled 'Composition VII.' The painter intended the canvas to represent the tumult and resolution of apocalyptic themes — destruction, rebirth, the deluge. A viewer informed of this intention may come to apprehend these themes in the swirling forms and violent juxtapositions of colour. Yet there is no visual resemblance between what appears on the canvas and any scene of destruction or rebirth. The shapes do not look like floods; the colours do not look like flames. Condition (i) fails entirely, and yet the painting does represent its subject, albeit not through mimetic fidelity. What the case reveals is that pictorial representation is a broader phenomenon than resemblance, and that an adequate account must allow for modes of depiction — expressive, symbolic, conventional — that do not depend upon perceptible similarity between picture and object.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) the creator of W experienced E during the process of creation, (ii) W was produced as a direct consequence of the creator's undergoing E — that is, E causally contributed to the character of W, and (iii) a sensitive audience, upon apprehending W, is led to feel E or a close analogue of E.
conditions: The creator of W experienced E during the process of creation
W was produced as a direct consequence of the creator's undergoing E — E causally contributed to W's character
A sensitive audience upon apprehending W is led to feel E or a close analogue of E
counterexample: Consider a film composer who is commissioned to write music for a scene of intense grief. The composer is, at the time of writing, in perfectly good spirits — cheerful, well-rested, and occupied with domestic contentments. She draws upon her technical mastery of harmonic language, orchestration, and melodic contour to produce a passage that audiences universally describe as profoundly sorrowful. Condition (i) fails: the composer did not experience grief. Condition (ii) fails: the music was not causally produced by any episode of sorrow. Yet condition (iii) is satisfied, and the work does express grief. The concept of expression applies despite the absence of any felt emotion on the part of the creator.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the expressed emotion be experienced and causally operative in the creator, but a skilled artist may express emotions she does not feel, through mastery of the conventions and techniques by which emotions are characteristically rendered in the medium.
passage: One might propose that a work of art expresses an emotion if and only if the creator experienced that emotion during the process of creation, the work was produced as a direct consequence of the creator's undergoing that emotion, and a sensitive audience is led by the work to feel that emotion or a close analogue of it. This is, in broad outline, the expression theory in its classical form, and it has seemed to many philosophers and critics to capture something essential about art. When we say that a poem expresses sorrow, we seem to mean that the poet was sorrowful and that his sorrow flowed into the poem and through it reaches us. Yet it appears that the definition is insufficient. Consider a film composer who is commissioned to write music for a scene of intense grief. The composer is, at the time of writing, in perfectly good spirits — cheerful, well-rested, and preoccupied with domestic contentments. She is not in the least sorrowful. Drawing upon her technical mastery of harmonic language, orchestration, and melodic contour, she produces a passage that audiences universally describe as profoundly sorrowful. The music expresses grief; no one who hears it doubts this. Yet condition (i) fails, since the composer did not experience grief, and condition (ii) fails, since no episode of sorrow causally contributed to the character of the music. What produced the music was skill, not suffering. The case reveals that expression in art cannot be reduced to the causal transmission of felt emotion from creator to audience, and that an adequate account must allow for the possibility that an artist may express an emotion she does not feel, through mastery of the technical and conventional resources of her medium.

definition: A judgement J about an object O is a judgement of taste if and only if (i) J ascribes to O a property that is not reducible to any objective, measurable feature of O, (ii) J is made on the basis of the pleasure or displeasure that O occasions in the judge, and (iii) J claims universal validity — that is, the judge holds that all rational persons ought to agree with J.
conditions: J ascribes to O a property not reducible to any objective measurable feature of O
J is made on the basis of the pleasure or displeasure that O occasions in the judge
J claims universal validity — the judge holds that all rational persons ought to agree
counterexample: Consider a person who tastes a particular wine and says, 'This is a delightful wine.' She ascribes to the wine a property — delightfulness — that is not reducible to its measurable chemical composition. She makes the judgement on the basis of the pleasure the wine gives her. Yet she does not claim that all rational persons ought to agree. She says, quite explicitly, 'I find it delightful, though I daresay others may not.' The judgement satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) but fails condition (iii). It is, however, a perfectly good judgement of taste — indeed, it is just the sort of thing we ordinarily mean by a judgement of taste.
missing_condition: The definition requires that judgements of taste claim universal validity, but many sincere and characteristic judgements of taste are offered with an explicit or implicit concession that others may legitimately disagree, without ceasing to be judgements of taste.
passage: One might define a judgement of taste as a judgement that ascribes to an object a property not reducible to any objective, measurable feature, that is made on the basis of the pleasure or displeasure the object occasions in the judge, and that claims universal validity — the judge holding that all rational persons ought to agree.
