This third condition is, of course, characteristic of the Kantian analysis, and it is intended to distinguish genuine judgements of taste from mere reports of private preference. When I say that a painting is beautiful, I am not merely reporting that it pleases me; I am claiming that it ought to please anyone with the requisite sensibility. Yet one might observe that this requirement is too strong. Consider a person who tastes a particular wine and says, 'This is a delightful wine.' She ascribes to the wine a property — delightfulness — that is not reducible to its measurable chemical composition, and she does so on the basis of the pleasure it gives her. But she does not claim that all rational persons ought to agree. She says, quite explicitly, 'I find it delightful, though I daresay others may not; tastes in wine vary enormously.' She concedes the possibility of legitimate disagreement. The judgement satisfies the first two conditions but fails the third. Yet it is a perfectly good judgement of taste — indeed, it is precisely the sort of thing we ordinarily mean when we speak of a judgement of taste. What the case reveals is that the definition has confused one species of judgement of taste — the kind that aspires to universality — with the genus, and has thereby excluded a large and important class of aesthetic judgements that are characteristically tentative and personal.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O possesses a structure that rewards sustained perceptual attention — the more one looks or listens, the more one discovers, (ii) O produces in the observer a distinctive pleasure that is not merely sensory gratification but involves a satisfaction of the higher faculties, and (iii) O is such that the pleasure it produces is shared by all persons of normal sensibility who attend to it with care.
conditions: O possesses a structure that rewards sustained perceptual attention
O produces a distinctive pleasure involving satisfaction of the higher faculties, not merely sensory gratification
The pleasure O produces is shared by all persons of normal sensibility who attend to it with care
counterexample: Consider a wildflower — a common bluebell growing in a wood. It is beautiful; no one would deny it. Yet its structure does not reward sustained perceptual attention in the way the definition requires: one sees the bluebell, one apprehends its beauty, and further scrutiny reveals nothing new. The beauty is immediate and simple, not layered or inexhaustible. Moreover, the pleasure it produces is arguably a form of sensory gratification — a delight in the colour blue, in the delicacy of the stem — rather than a satisfaction of the higher faculties. Conditions (i) and (ii) fail, yet the bluebell is beautiful.
missing_condition: The definition is biased toward complex, richly structured objects and neglects the beauty of simple things, whose charm resides in an immediate sensory quality rather than in depth of structure or intellectual engagement.
passage: One might propose that an object is beautiful if and only if it possesses a structure that rewards sustained perceptual attention, produces in the observer a distinctive pleasure that is not merely sensory gratification but involves a satisfaction of the higher faculties, and is such that this pleasure is shared by all persons of normal sensibility who attend to the object with care. This definition has the merit of distinguishing beauty from mere agreeableness: a sweet taste is agreeable, but we do not call it beautiful, because it does not engage the higher faculties or reward prolonged attention. Yet it appears that the definition is too demanding. Consider a wildflower — a common bluebell growing in a wood. It is beautiful; no one would deny it. But its structure does not reward sustained perceptual attention in the sense the definition requires. One sees the bluebell, one apprehends its beauty, and further scrutiny reveals little that was not immediately apparent. The beauty is simple and direct, not layered or inexhaustible. Moreover, the pleasure it produces is arguably a form of sensory gratification — a delight in the particular shade of blue, in the delicacy of the drooping bell, in the slender curve of the stem. It is not obvious that the higher faculties are engaged in any significant way. One does not analyse the bluebell or discover hidden complexities in it. Conditions (i) and (ii) are thus unsatisfied, and yet the bluebell is beautiful. What the case reveals is that the definition privileges a certain kind of beauty — the beauty of complex, richly structured objects — and neglects the beauty of simple things, whose charm resides in an immediate sensory quality that defies further analysis.

definition: A mental state M is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) M is a response to the perceptual qualities of a particular object — its colours, shapes, sounds, or textures, (ii) M involves a detachment from practical concerns — the subject is not considering what use the object might serve, and (iii) M is intrinsically valuable to the subject — the subject would seek to prolong or repeat M for its own sake.
conditions: M is a response to the perceptual qualities of a particular object
M involves detachment from practical concerns
M is intrinsically valuable — the subject would seek to prolong or repeat M for its own sake
counterexample: Consider a mountaineer who, in the course of a difficult ascent, pauses on a narrow ledge and is struck by the extraordinary beauty of the view — the sweep of glaciers, the play of light on distant peaks. She is not detached from practical concerns; she is acutely aware that a misstep will cost her life, and she is planning her next move even as she gazes. Her experience is thoroughly pervaded by practical anxiety. Yet the experience is aesthetic: the beauty of the scene is apprehended, and apprehended as beautiful, even in the midst of danger. Condition (ii) fails, yet the aesthetic experience is genuine.
missing_condition: The definition requires detachment from practical concern as a condition of aesthetic experience, but aesthetic apprehension may occur in the midst of urgent practical engagement, and the interpenetration of practical and aesthetic awareness may even intensify the aesthetic response.
passage: It is sometimes proposed that a mental state is an aesthetic experience if and only if it is a response to the perceptual qualities of a particular object, it involves a detachment from practical concerns, and it is intrinsically valuable to the subject — the subject would seek to prolong or repeat it for its own sake. The requirement of practical detachment has a distinguished history: it echoes the Kantian notion of disinterestedness and the Schopenhauerian idea of will-less contemplation. When we stand before a painting in a gallery, we are not considering what use the canvas might serve; we attend to its colours and forms for their own sake. Yet one might observe that the requirement of detachment is too strong. Consider a mountaineer who, in the course of a difficult ascent, pauses on a narrow ledge and is struck by the extraordinary beauty of the view — the sweep of glaciers below, the play of light on distant peaks, the vast silence of the high altitude. She is not detached from practical concerns. She is acutely aware that a misstep will cost her life; she is planning her next move even as she gazes; her muscles are tense with the effort of maintaining her position. Her experience is thoroughly pervaded by practical anxiety. And yet the experience is aesthetic: the beauty of the scene is apprehended, and apprehended as beautiful, with a vividness that her perilous situation may actually intensify. Condition (ii) is plainly not satisfied. The subject is not detached from practical concerns; she is immersed in them. Yet no one who has had such an experience would deny its aesthetic character. What the case reveals is that aesthetic experience does not require the suspension of practical engagement, and that the interpenetration of the aesthetic and the practical may produce some of the most vivid and memorable encounters with beauty.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O possesses a harmony of parts — that is, the elements of O are so arranged as to produce a unified and proportionate whole, (ii) O occasions in a normally constituted observer a feeling of disinterested pleasure, and (iii) O is such that the pleasure it occasions is not dependent upon any desire to possess or make use of O.
conditions: O possesses a harmony of parts producing a unified and proportionate whole
O occasions in a normally constituted observer a feeling of disinterested pleasure
The pleasure occasioned is not dependent upon any desire to possess or make use of O
counterexample: Consider a perfectly symmetrical industrial gear, its teeth cut with mathematical precision, each part proportioned to every other with an exactness that satisfies the most exacting standard of harmony. A disinterested observer, unaware of its function, might well take pleasure in its form without any desire to possess it. All three conditions are met. Yet one would hesitate to call an industrial gear beautiful. Its harmony is the harmony of mechanical efficiency, and the pleasure it occasions, if any, is the pleasure of recognising functional fitness rather than the distinctive pleasure that attends the beautiful.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the harmony of parts be expressive or significant — that it convey or embody something beyond mere formal arrangement. A condition distinguishing expressive harmony from merely mechanical or functional regularity is needed.
passage: One might propose that an object is beautiful if and only if it possesses a harmony of parts, occasions in a normally constituted observer a feeling of disinterested pleasure, and is such that the pleasure it occasions is independent of any desire to possess or use the object. This definition draws upon a long tradition in aesthetic thought. The requirement of harmony captures the ancient conviction that beauty resides in proportion and order; the requirement of disinterested pleasure echoes the insight that aesthetic enjoyment differs from the gratifications of appetite or ambition; and the independence from possessive desire marks off the aesthetic from the acquisitive. Yet one might observe that these conditions, taken jointly, are satisfied by objects that we should hesitate to call beautiful. Consider a perfectly symmetrical industrial gear, its teeth machined with mathematical precision, each element proportioned to every other with an exactness that surpasses the proportions of many acknowledged works of art. A normally constituted observer, ignorant of its purpose, might well take pleasure in its form — a pleasure untainted by any desire to own or employ the gear. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet it would be strange to call the gear beautiful. Its harmony is the harmony of function, and the pleasure it gives, if it gives any, is the pleasure of recognising that form has been perfectly adapted to purpose. What is missing from the definition is a requirement that the harmony be expressive — that it convey or embody something beyond the merely formal, something that speaks to the sensibility and not merely to the eye for order.

definition: An artefact A is a work of art if and only if (i) A is produced with the intention of being contemplated for its own sake rather than for any practical utility, (ii) A exhibits a degree of skill or craftsmanship in its execution, and (iii) A belongs to a recognised medium — painting, sculpture, music, literature, or the like.
conditions: A is produced with the intention of being contemplated for its own sake
A exhibits a degree of skill or craftsmanship
A belongs to a recognised medium
counterexample: Consider a child's drawing, executed with no particular skill, depicting a house and a tree. The child may well have produced it with the intention of its being looked at and admired — contemplated, one might say, for its own sake. It belongs to a recognised medium, namely drawing. But the skill exhibited is negligible. Now contrast this with a piece of driftwood, selected and mounted by an artist in a gallery. The driftwood was not produced by anyone; it exhibits no craftsmanship; and its medium is uncertain. Yet the mounted driftwood may be regarded as a work of art, whilst the child's drawing, which satisfies conditions (i) and (iii), is not typically so regarded. The definition both includes what it should exclude and excludes what it might include.
missing_condition: The definition neglects the role of artistic context and institutional recognition — the fact that whether something counts as art depends in part upon its placement within a framework of galleries, critics, and artistic traditions, and not solely upon the intrinsic properties of the artefact.
passage: It is tempting to define a work of art as an artefact produced with the intention of being contemplated for its own sake, exhibiting a degree of skill or craftsmanship, and belonging to a recognised medium such as painting, sculpture, music, or literature. This definition has the merit of capturing what we ordinarily expect of works of art: that they are made rather than found, that they repay contemplation, that they display some competence in their making, and that they participate in an established tradition of artistic practice. The requirement of contemplative intention distinguishes art from mere craft or manufacture; the requirement of skill distinguishes it from accident; and the requirement of a recognised medium gives it a place within a tradition. Yet one might observe that these conditions, plausible as they seem, are neither jointly sufficient nor individually necessary. A child's drawing, produced with the evident intention of being admired, belongs to the medium of drawing, and yet the skill exhibited is negligible, and we should hesitate to call it a work of art. Conversely, a piece of driftwood, produced by no one and exhibiting no craftsmanship, may be selected, mounted, and displayed in a gallery, and in that context may function as a work of art. The definition fails to account for the role of context — the framework of institutions, traditions, and critical practices within which something comes to be recognised as art. What appears to be needed is a condition that acknowledges the institutional or contextual dimension of art, without which the definition remains blind to the manner in which objects cross the boundary between the artistic and the non-artistic.

definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude or power, (ii) E involves a feeling of awe that is mixed with an element of terror or dread, and (iii) E ultimately issues in a sense of elevation — a feeling that the mind has been enlarged or exalted by the encounter.
conditions: E is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude or power
E involves a feeling of awe mixed with terror or dread
E ultimately issues in a sense of elevation or exaltation
counterexample: Consider a mathematician who, in contemplating the proof of a theorem of extraordinary generality — say, a result that unifies vast and apparently disparate branches of mathematics — experiences a feeling of awe, a momentary vertigo at the scope of what has been achieved, and ultimately a sense of elevation, as though the mind has been stretched beyond its ordinary compass. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the mathematician is not confronted by an object of overwhelming physical magnitude or power; the proof is a chain of symbols on a page. If the definition is meant to capture the sublimity of mountain peaks and tempestuous seas, it has inadvertently captured the intellectual sublime as well, without any account of whether these are the same experience or merely analogous ones.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify whether the overwhelming magnitude or power must be sensory or perceptual, or whether intellectual or abstract objects may also occasion the sublime. A condition clarifying the nature of the object's magnitude would be required to delimit the concept.
passage: One might propose that an experience is an experience of the sublime if and only if it is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude or power, involves a feeling of awe mixed with an element of terror or dread, and ultimately issues in a sense of elevation or exaltation. This definition draws upon a tradition that stretches from Longinus through Burke to the Romantic poets: the sublime is the experience of being confronted by something that exceeds the ordinary compass of the mind, that threatens to overwhelm the perceiver, and that yet, in the very moment of threatening, elevates the spirit. The mountain, the storm, the limitless expanse of the sea — these are the paradigmatic occasions of the sublime. Yet one might observe that the definition, as stated, extends further than its authors may have intended. Consider a mathematician who contemplates a proof of extraordinary generality — a theorem that brings vast and apparently unrelated domains of mathematics under a single principle. The mathematician may experience genuine awe, a momentary vertigo at the immensity of what has been achieved, and a sense that the intellect has been stretched to its limits and enlarged in the stretching. All three conditions are satisfied. But the object is not a mountain or a storm; it is a chain of propositions on a page. The definition has captured what might be called the intellectual sublime alongside the natural sublime, without distinguishing between them or explaining whether they constitute a single experience or merely bear an analogical resemblance. What appears to be needed is a clarification of the kind of magnitude or power the object must possess — whether it must be sensory and perceptual, or whether abstract and intellectual grandeur may equally occasion the sublime.

definition: A person S has good taste if and only if (i) S is able to discriminate differences of quality among works within a given artistic medium, (ii) S's judgements of quality are consistent over time — that is, S does not reverse evaluations of the same work without reason, and (iii) S's judgements accord, in the main, with those of other persons acknowledged to possess good taste.
conditions: S is able to discriminate differences of quality among works
S's judgements of quality are consistent over time
S's judgements accord with those of other acknowledged persons of good taste
counterexample: Consider a person whose judgements of painting are perfectly consistent, who discriminates readily among works, and whose evaluations accord exactly with those of the prevailing circle of connoisseurs. But suppose that this person has never formed an independent judgement in his life — he simply defers to the opinions of the acknowledged experts, adopting their verdicts as his own and maintaining them with perfect consistency. All three conditions are met, yet one would hesitate to say that the person has good taste. He has good information about what other people think, but taste, it appears, requires something more than reliable deference.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the person's judgements be the product of genuine perception and independent response rather than mere conformity to the opinions of others. A condition requiring perceptual sensitivity or firsthand aesthetic engagement is needed.
passage: One might define good taste as the ability to discriminate differences of quality among works within a given medium, consistency of judgement over time, and accord with the judgements of other persons acknowledged to have good taste. This definition has the advantage of combining a perceptual element — the ability to discriminate — with a social element — agreement with acknowledged judges. The consistency requirement guards against caprice: a person whose evaluations shift with every change of mood or fashion can hardly be said to possess settled taste. And the agreement requirement provides a check against mere idiosyncrasy, anchoring individual judgement to a community of competent judges. Yet one might observe that the three conditions may be satisfied by a person who possesses no genuine taste at all. Suppose a man, wishing to be thought a connoisseur of painting, studies the published opinions of recognised critics and adopts their verdicts as his own. He discriminates among works — but only because he has been told which works are better. His judgements are consistent — because the critics to whom he defers are consistent. And his judgements accord with those of acknowledged persons of taste — because they are copies of those judgements. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the man has never formed an independent aesthetic response in his life. His consistency is the consistency of a mirror, not of a mind. What the definition appears to lack is a condition requiring that the person's judgements arise from genuine perceptual engagement with the works — that the discrimination be the product of the person's own sensibility rather than of secondhand report.

definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E is directed toward the formal or sensory properties of an object — its colour, shape, sound, rhythm, or the like, (ii) E is valued for its own sake and not as a means to any further end, and (iii) E involves a heightened attentiveness — the person is absorbed in the object in a manner that excludes practical concerns.
conditions: E is directed toward the formal or sensory properties of an object
E is valued for its own sake
E involves heightened attentiveness excluding practical concerns
counterexample: Consider a wine taster at a professional competition who attends with heightened concentration to the colour, aroma, and flavour of a wine, valued the experience of tasting for its own intrinsic interest, and is absorbed in the sensory properties of the wine to the exclusion of all other concerns. All three conditions are met. Yet the tasting, however refined, is ordinarily classified as a gustatory or sensory experience rather than an aesthetic one, and many philosophers would resist calling it aesthetic without further argument. The definition, as stated, draws no distinction between aesthetic experience and any experience of absorbed, disinterested attention to sensory qualities.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish aesthetic experience from other forms of absorbed sensory attention — such as wine tasting or perfume evaluation — by omitting any requirement that the experience involve an imaginative or contemplative dimension beyond mere sensory discrimination.
passage: It is natural to suppose that an aesthetic experience is one directed toward the formal or sensory properties of an object, valued for its own sake, and involving a heightened attentiveness that excludes practical concerns. This definition captures much of what we intuitively associate with the aesthetic: the attention to form and sensory quality, the sense that the experience is its own reward, and the absorption that lifts us out of the stream of practical life. When we stand before a painting and attend to the play of colour and line, or listen to a passage of music and follow its rhythm and harmonic movement, we exemplify the conditions the definition specifies. Yet one might observe that the definition extends to experiences that we should ordinarily hesitate to call aesthetic. Consider a professional wine taster at a competition. The taster attends with the most exquisite concentration to the colour, aroma, texture, and flavour of the wine — its formal and sensory properties, one might say. The taster values this attention for its own sake, finding intrinsic interest and even delight in the discriminations it affords. And the taster is absorbed in the activity to the exclusion of all practical concerns. All three conditions are satisfied. But it is far from obvious that professional wine tasting constitutes an aesthetic experience in the sense in which contemplating a symphony or a landscape does. What appears to be missing is a condition that distinguishes the aesthetic from the merely sensory — a requirement, perhaps, that the experience involve an imaginative or contemplative engagement with the object that goes beyond the discrimination of sensory qualities.

definition: A work of art W represents an object or state of affairs X if and only if (i) W bears a recognisable resemblance to X — that is, there is a perceptible similarity between the appearance of W and the appearance of X, (ii) W was produced with the intention of depicting X, and (iii) a competent viewer, acquainted with the relevant artistic conventions, would identify W as a depiction of X.
conditions: W bears a recognisable resemblance to X
W was produced with the intention of depicting X
A competent viewer would identify W as a depiction of X
counterexample: Consider a highly abstract painting — a canvas covered in jagged black lines and fields of deep red — which its artist intended as a representation of the bombing of a city. A competent viewer, informed of the title and the artist's stated intention, would identify the painting as a depiction of that event. But the painting bears no recognisable resemblance to a city, to an act of bombing, or to any visible feature of the event. The second and third conditions are met, but the first is not. Yet the painting may powerfully represent the horror and destruction of the bombing through its expressive qualities — the violence of the marks, the intensity of the colour — without resembling its subject in any perceptible way.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that representation requires perceptible resemblance, but artistic representation may proceed through expressive, symbolic, or conventional means that bear no visual similarity to the thing represented. A broader account of the relation between a work and its subject is needed.
passage: One might propose that a work of art represents an object or state of affairs if and only if the work bears a recognisable resemblance to that object, was produced with the intention of depicting it, and would be identified as a depiction by a competent viewer. This definition has an intuitive appeal: it grounds representation in the visible similarity between a painting and its subject, supplemented by the artist's intention and the viewer's competence. A portrait resembles its sitter; a landscape painting resembles the scene it depicts; a still life resembles the arrangement of objects it records. The definition seems to capture these cases admirably. Yet one might observe that it falters when confronted with the practices of modern art. Consider a large canvas covered in jagged black lines and fields of intense red, which its painter intended as a representation of the bombing of a city. The title confirms this intention, and a competent viewer, informed of the title and the historical context, would understand the painting as a depiction of that catastrophe. Yet the painting bears no recognisable resemblance to a city, to aircraft, to explosions, or to any visible feature of the event. It represents its subject not through resemblance but through expression — through the violence of its marks and the intensity of its colour. The first condition is not met, yet the painting represents its subject powerfully. What the definition has failed to accommodate is the possibility that representation may proceed by expressive, symbolic, or conventional means rather than by resemblance. A more adequate definition would need to specify the relation between a work and its subject in terms broad enough to include these non-mimetic forms of representation.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) the artist who created W experienced E during the process of creation, (ii) W possesses formal properties — rhythm, colour, line, harmonic structure, or the like — that are conventionally associated with E, and (iii) a sensitive perceiver of W would, upon attentive contemplation, come to feel E or something closely resembling it.
conditions: The artist experienced E during the creation of W
W possesses formal properties conventionally associated with E
A sensitive perceiver would come to feel E upon contemplation of W
counterexample: Consider a composer who, in a state of deep contentment and domestic happiness, composes a funeral march of the most harrowing solemnity. The music possesses the slow tempo, minor tonality, and heavy rhythmic tread conventionally associated with grief. A sensitive listener would, upon hearing it, feel a profound sorrow. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are met, but condition (i) is not: the composer experienced no grief whatever during the composition. Yet the work expresses grief as powerfully as any work composed in genuine sorrow. It appears that what the work expresses is independent of the psychological state of its creator.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires that the artist have experienced the expressed emotion during creation. Expression in art may be a matter of the work's formal and expressive properties rather than a record of the artist's psychological states. The biographical condition should be replaced by a condition concerning the work's capacity to embody or articulate the emotion through its own internal properties.
passage: One might suppose that a work of art expresses an emotion if and only if the artist experienced that emotion during the process of creation, the work possesses formal properties conventionally associated with the emotion, and a sensitive perceiver would come to feel the emotion upon attentive contemplation. This definition attempts to link the expression of emotion in art to three distinct elements: the psychological state of the creator, the conventional associations of formal properties, and the affective response of the audience. The biographical condition — that the artist actually felt the emotion — gives expression an air of authenticity; the formal condition ensures that the work bears the marks of the emotion in its structure; and the response condition guarantees that the emotion is communicated to the perceiver. Yet one might observe that the biographical condition is at once unnecessary and unverifiable. Consider a composer who, in a state of perfect contentment and domestic ease, sets about composing a funeral march. The result is a work of harrowing solemnity — slow in tempo, minor in tonality, heavy in its rhythmic tread. A sensitive listener, knowing nothing of the composer's circumstances, would feel a profound grief upon hearing it. The formal and response conditions are amply satisfied. But the biographical condition fails entirely: the composer felt no grief. Yet the work expresses grief as convincingly as any that was ever written in genuine sorrow. It appears, then, that what a work of art expresses need not mirror the psychological state of its creator. The definition has confused the expression of emotion in art with the expression of emotion in life, where a cry of pain does require that the person who cries be in pain. What is wanted is a condition that ties expression to the work's own properties rather than to the artist's biography.

definition: A judgement J about a work of art is a valid aesthetic judgement if and only if (i) J is based upon direct perceptual acquaintance with the work — that is, the person making J has seen, heard, or read the work, (ii) J concerns the formal or expressive properties of the work rather than its moral, political, or commercial value, and (iii) J is accompanied by reasons — that is, the person can cite features of the work that support the evaluation.
conditions: J is based upon direct perceptual acquaintance with the work
J concerns the formal or expressive properties of the work
J is accompanied by reasons citing features of the work
counterexample: Consider a person who has seen a painting, who judges it to be a fine work on account of its balanced composition, its subtle gradations of colour, and its expressive handling of light — all formal and expressive properties. The judgement is based on direct acquaintance, concerns the right kind of properties, and is accompanied by detailed reasons. Yet suppose the person is colour-blind and has mistaken the painting's blue tones for grey, its warm harmonies for cool ones. The reasons the person cites are based upon a perceptual error, and the features that actually support the judgement are not the features the painting possesses. All three conditions are met, yet the judgement rests upon a misperception of the work.
missing_condition: The definition requires direct acquaintance and the citation of reasons but does not require that the perception upon which the judgement is based be accurate or adequate. A condition ensuring the reliability or accuracy of the perceptual acquaintance is needed.
passage: It is natural to hold that a valid aesthetic judgement must be based upon direct perceptual acquaintance with the work, must concern the formal or expressive properties of the work rather than its extra-artistic value, and must be accompanied by reasons — specific features of the work that the person can point to in support of the evaluation. This definition reflects several plausible convictions: that one cannot judge what one has not experienced, that aesthetic judgement is concerned with the work's own properties rather than with its usefulness or its moral tendency, and that aesthetic evaluation, unlike mere preference, is a reasoned activity. The requirement of reasons is particularly important, for it distinguishes the aesthetic judgement from the brute expression of liking or disliking. Yet one might observe that the conditions, taken jointly, may be satisfied by judgements that are aesthetically defective. Consider a person who has stood before a painting and formed a judgement about its colour harmonies, its compositional balance, and its handling of light — judgements based on direct acquaintance, concerning formal properties, and accompanied by detailed reasons. But suppose the person is colour-blind. The blue tones of the painting appear grey; the warm harmonies are perceived as cool austerities; the subtle modulations of colour that constitute much of the painting's achievement are invisible. The judgement satisfies all three conditions — acquaintance, formal focus, and reasons — yet it rests upon a misperception of the very features it purports to evaluate. What the definition lacks is a condition ensuring that the perceptual acquaintance upon which the judgement is based be adequate to the properties of the work — that the perceiver actually apprehend the features that the reasons invoke.

definition: A natural scene S is beautiful if and only if (i) S presents to the observer a variety of forms, colours, and textures that are unified by an overarching pattern or order, (ii) S is free from any mark of human intervention — that is, it is entirely the product of natural processes, and (iii) S occasions in the observer a contemplative pleasure that is distinguishable from the pleasures of comfort, excitement, or novelty.
conditions: S presents a variety of forms unified by an overarching pattern or order
S is free from any mark of human intervention
S occasions a contemplative pleasure distinct from comfort, excitement, or novelty
counterexample: Consider a formal garden — say, the gardens at Versailles — in which hedges are trimmed into geometric shapes, flower beds are arranged in symmetrical patterns, and water features are disposed along carefully planned axes. The garden presents a variety of forms, colours, and textures unified by an overarching geometrical order. It occasions in the observer a contemplative pleasure quite distinct from the pleasures of comfort or novelty. But it is emphatically not free from human intervention; it is the product of deliberate design. The first and third conditions are met, but the second is not. Yet many would say that the garden is beautiful — indeed, that its beauty is enhanced rather than diminished by the intelligence of its design.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously restricts natural beauty to scenes untouched by human intervention, thereby excluding gardens, parks, and cultivated landscapes that are widely regarded as beautiful. The condition of freedom from human intervention should be dropped or replaced by a condition that allows for the beauty of nature shaped by human design.
passage: One might define the beauty of a natural scene as follows: the scene presents a variety of forms, colours, and textures unified by an overarching pattern or order; it is entirely the product of natural processes, free from any mark of human intervention; and it occasions in the observer a contemplative pleasure distinguishable from the pleasures of comfort, excitement, or novelty. This definition captures certain paradigmatic cases of natural beauty — an alpine meadow in flower, a sunset over an unbroken sea, a forest of ancient trees whose canopy forms a cathedral-like vault. In such cases, the variety of the scene is unified by natural patterns, no human hand has shaped the arrangement, and the pleasure one takes is quiet, contemplative, and quite unlike the excitement of sport or the comfort of a warm room. Yet one might observe that the second condition — the requirement that the scene be free from human intervention — excludes much that is ordinarily regarded as beautiful in nature. A formal garden, such as one might find at Versailles, presents a magnificent variety of forms unified by geometrical order, and occasions a contemplative pleasure of the purest kind. But it is entirely the product of human design. Its hedges are trimmed, its flower beds arranged, its waters channelled by artifice. The definition would exclude it from the class of beautiful natural scenes. Yet many would say that the garden is beautiful precisely because human intelligence has taken the materials of nature and brought them into an order that nature alone would not have achieved. The definition, it appears, has confused the accidental feature that many beautiful scenes happen to be untouched by human hands with the essential feature that makes them beautiful — their unified variety and the contemplative pleasure they afford.

definition: A piece of music M is expressive of sadness if and only if (i) M is composed in a minor key, (ii) M has a slow tempo — that is, it proceeds at a pace below allegro, and (iii) M employs descending melodic lines as a prominent feature of its thematic material.
conditions: M is composed in a minor key
M has a slow tempo below allegro
M employs descending melodic lines prominently
counterexample: Consider a lively Spanish dance in a minor key — a piece with a brisk tempo, vigorous rhythms, and melodic lines that descend only to leap upward again with renewed energy. The piece is in a minor key, and one might argue that some of its melodic figures are descending. But its tempo is rapid, and the overall effect is one of passionate energy rather than sadness. Condition (i) is met and condition (iii) is partially met, but condition (ii) is not, and the piece is not in the least sad. Conversely, consider a slow piece in a major key with predominantly descending melodic lines — such as certain passages of late Beethoven — that conveys a profound and unmistakable sadness despite its major tonality. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are met, but condition (i) is not, and yet the music is deeply sad.
missing_condition: The definition reduces musical expressiveness to a checklist of technical features — key, tempo, melodic contour — but the expressiveness of music depends upon the interaction of these features with rhythm, harmony, orchestration, phrasing, and context in ways that no simple conjunction of conditions can capture.
passage: One might attempt to specify the conditions under which a piece of music is expressive of sadness by stating that the piece must be composed in a minor key, must proceed at a slow tempo, and must employ descending melodic lines as a prominent feature. This definition reflects observations that are not without foundation: minor keys are indeed associated with sadness in the Western tonal tradition, slow tempi often convey gravity or melancholy, and descending melodic lines suggest falling, sighing, or subsiding. Many paradigmatically sad pieces of music satisfy all three conditions. Yet one might observe that the definition both includes what it should exclude and excludes what it should include. A lively Spanish dance in a minor key, with brisk rhythms and vigorous energy, may satisfy the first condition and partially satisfy the third, yet convey no sadness whatever — its minor tonality is the vehicle of passionate intensity, not of grief. Conversely, certain passages of late Beethoven in major keys, with slow tempi and descending melodic figures, convey a sadness so profound that it seems to transcend the resources of any single technical parameter. And there are pieces — one thinks of certain works of Schubert — in which the most heartbreaking passages occur precisely at the moment of a shift from minor to major, so that the major key becomes the very medium of sorrow. The difficulty is that musical expressiveness is not reducible to a conjunction of separable technical features. It arises from the interaction of key, tempo, rhythm, harmony, orchestration, phrasing, and the expectations established by the musical context. What the definition lacks is any recognition of this interactive complexity.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O possesses a perceptible formal harmony among its parts, (ii) the perception of O produces in a normally constituted observer a disinterested pleasure, and (iii) this pleasure is occasioned by O's sensory properties rather than by any extraneous association or practical interest.
conditions: O possesses a perceptible formal harmony among its parts
The perception of O produces in a normally constituted observer a disinterested pleasure
This pleasure is occasioned by O's sensory properties rather than by any extraneous association or practical interest
counterexample: Consider a perfectly symmetrical arrangement of coloured tiles upon a bathroom floor. The tiles exhibit a rigorous formal harmony among their parts; any normally constituted observer, attending to the arrangement disinterestedly, may well derive a certain pleasure from its regularity; and this pleasure arises from the sensory properties of the pattern rather than from any practical interest in the floor's function. Yet one would hesitate to call the tile arrangement beautiful. It satisfies a certain orderliness without rising to anything that could properly be termed beauty.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the formal harmony in question possess a degree of complexity or depth sufficient to engage the observer's faculties beyond mere recognition of regularity. A condition demanding that the harmony be non-trivial — that it reward sustained contemplation — appears to be needed.
passage: One might propose that an object is beautiful if and only if it possesses a perceptible formal harmony among its parts, the perception of it produces in a normally constituted observer a disinterested pleasure, and this pleasure is occasioned by the object's sensory properties rather than by any extraneous association or practical interest. Such a definition has the merit of excluding cases in which pleasure arises from mere appetite or utility: the hungry man's delight at the sight of a well-laden table would not count, since his pleasure is interested rather than disinterested. Nor would the collector's satisfaction in a rare stamp qualify, for that satisfaction is grounded in associations of rarity and monetary value rather than in sensory properties alone. Yet there exists a class of cases which reveals the insufficiency of this tripartite account. A perfectly symmetrical arrangement of coloured tiles upon a bathroom floor possesses formal harmony, occasions a disinterested pleasure that is traceable to sensory properties, and yet fails to strike us as beautiful. The pleasure it affords is too shallow, too quickly exhausted, to merit that designation. It appears that beauty demands not merely formal harmony but a harmony of sufficient complexity or depth to sustain and reward prolonged contemplation — a condition the present definition does not capture.

definition: An artefact A is a work of art if and only if (i) A is produced by a human agent with the intention of creating an object for aesthetic contemplation, (ii) A embodies some degree of skill or technique in its making, and (iii) A is presented to an audience or public as a candidate for appreciation.
conditions: A is produced by a human agent with the intention of creating an object for aesthetic contemplation
A embodies some degree of skill or technique in its making
A is presented to an audience or public as a candidate for appreciation
counterexample: Consider a child who, with earnest intention, painstakingly arranges pebbles on a beach into what she regards as a portrait of her mother. She possesses a clear intention to create an object for aesthetic contemplation; the arrangement embodies a degree of skill relative to her capacities; and she presents it proudly to her family as something to be admired. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might reasonably doubt whether the pebble arrangement constitutes a work of art, for it lacks any connection to a tradition or practice of art-making that would confer upon it the relevant standing.
missing_condition: The definition omits any requirement that the artefact stand in some recognisable relation to an existing tradition or institutional framework of art. Without such a condition, any deliberately crafted and publicly displayed object would qualify, however remote from the practices that constitute art as a going concern.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a work of art is simply an artefact produced with the intention of creating an object for aesthetic contemplation, embodying some degree of skill, and presented to an audience as a candidate for appreciation. This definition appears to exclude natural objects — a sunset cannot be a work of art, on this view, for it is not produced by a human agent — and it excludes mere accidents of manufacture, since the intention condition rules out cases in which an aesthetically pleasing form arises without design. Nevertheless, a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is not difficult to construct. A child arranging pebbles upon a beach into what she takes to be a portrait of her mother satisfies all three conditions: she intends the arrangement for aesthetic contemplation, she exercises such skill as she possesses, and she presents it to her family for appreciation. We should hesitate, however, to call this a work of art. What seems to be missing is any connection between the child's activity and the broader traditions, institutions, or practices that constitute art as a recognisable human endeavour. The definition, as it stands, is too permissive in its silence on this point.

definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude or power, (ii) E involves an initial feeling of being overpowered or diminished, and (iii) E gives rise to a subsequent feeling of exaltation or elevation in the subject.
conditions: E is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude or power
E involves an initial feeling of being overpowered or diminished
E gives rise to a subsequent feeling of exaltation or elevation in the subject
counterexample: Consider a man who stands before a great waterfall and is initially overpowered by its magnitude, feeling himself utterly diminished. He then experiences a rush of exaltation — but this exaltation arises not from any reflective recovery of his rational capacities, but from the sudden recollection that he owns the land on which the waterfall stands and that it will make him very wealthy through tourism. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the resulting state seems more properly described as acquisitive elation than as an experience of the sublime.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the subsequent feeling of exaltation arise from the subject's relation to the object itself — from a sense of the mind's capacity to comprehend or transcend what initially overwhelmed it — rather than from some extraneous cause such as personal advantage.
passage: One might analyse the experience of the sublime as follows: it is an experience occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude or power, involving an initial feeling of being overpowered or diminished, and giving rise to a subsequent feeling of exaltation or elevation. This tripartite analysis captures the characteristic arc of the sublime as described by writers from Burke onward — the moment of shock, the sense of one's own insignificance, and the curious recovery into a heightened state. Yet consider a man standing before a great waterfall. He is indeed overpowered by its magnitude and feels himself diminished. He then experiences a powerful exaltation — but this exaltation has its source in the sudden recollection that he owns the surrounding land and stands to profit handsomely from the spectacle. All three conditions of the definition are met, and yet one would not wish to call this an experience of the sublime. The exaltation must, it appears, bear the right sort of relation to the initial feeling of being overpowered; it must arise from the subject's reflective engagement with the object rather than from adventitious considerations of personal gain. The definition, as stated, leaves this requirement unspecified.

definition: A person P possesses good taste if and only if (i) P is able to discriminate among objects with respect to their aesthetic merits, (ii) P's discriminations agree with those of the majority of cultivated judges, and (iii) P's judgements are consistent over time.
conditions: P is able to discriminate among objects with respect to their aesthetic merits
P's discriminations agree with those of the majority of cultivated judges
P's judgements are consistent over time
counterexample: Consider a man who, having carefully studied the published opinions of the most respected critics, has trained himself to reproduce their verdicts with remarkable fidelity. He discriminates among objects precisely as the cultivated judges do, and his judgements are perfectly consistent over time, for he has committed the critics' rankings to memory. All three conditions are met. Yet one would hesitate to credit him with good taste, for his discriminations are not the product of genuine perceptual sensitivity but of rote conformity.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the person's discriminations arise from a genuine perceptual responsiveness to the aesthetic properties of the objects themselves, rather than from a derivative process of mimicking authoritative opinion.
passage: It is tempting to define good taste as the capacity to discriminate among objects with respect to their aesthetic merits, where one's discriminations agree with those of the majority of cultivated judges and are consistent over time. Such a definition has the advantage of anchoring taste in a public standard — one cannot claim good taste if one's judgements systematically diverge from those of competent critics — while also demanding a stability that rules out mere caprice. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account is easily devised. Imagine a man who, having made a careful study of the published opinions of the most respected critics of his day, has trained himself to reproduce their verdicts with perfect fidelity. He can rank the paintings in any exhibition precisely as the cultivated judges would rank them, and his judgements are entirely consistent, for he has memorised the relevant rankings. He satisfies all three conditions. But we should hesitate to say he possesses good taste. His discriminations, though correct by the proposed standard, do not arise from a genuine perceptual sensitivity to the objects before him. They are, as it were, borrowed rather than earned. It appears that the definition must be supplemented with a condition requiring that one's discriminations proceed from a direct responsiveness to the aesthetic properties of the objects in question.

definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E is directed at the sensory or formal properties of an object, (ii) E is valued for its own sake rather than as a means to some further end, and (iii) E involves a heightened attentiveness to the object's perceptible qualities.
conditions: E is directed at the sensory or formal properties of an object
E is valued for its own sake rather than as a means to some further end
E involves a heightened attentiveness to the object's perceptible qualities
counterexample: Consider a gemologist examining a diamond under a loupe. Her experience is directed entirely at the sensory and formal properties of the stone — its colour, clarity, symmetry, and the play of light within its facets. She values the act of examination for its own sake, finding it deeply absorbing quite apart from any commercial purpose. And she attends to the perceptible qualities of the diamond with an extraordinary degree of heightened attentiveness. Yet her experience seems more properly described as one of expert appraisal than as an aesthetic experience, for its governing concern is classificatory accuracy rather than appreciation.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the heightened attentiveness be governed by an appreciative rather than a classificatory or analytical orientation toward the object. A condition specifying that the subject's engagement be one of savouring or appreciating rather than merely scrutinising is needed.
passage: One might define an aesthetic experience as one directed at the sensory or formal properties of an object, valued for its own sake, and involving a heightened attentiveness to the object's perceptible qualities. This captures much of what we intend by the notion: an aesthetic experience is contrasted with a merely practical or instrumental engagement, and it is characterised by a focused awareness of how things look, sound, or otherwise present themselves to the senses. Yet consider the case of a gemologist who examines a diamond under a loupe with intense concentration. Her experience is directed at the sensory and formal properties of the stone; she finds the examination absorbing for its own sake; and her attentiveness to the diamond's perceptible qualities is extreme. All three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, one would be reluctant to describe her experience as aesthetic. Her attention, though heightened, is governed by an analytical interest in classification — in determining whether the diamond falls into this or that grade — rather than by an appreciative engagement with its beauty. It appears that the definition requires a further condition specifying the character of the attentiveness in question: it must be appreciative, not merely discriminative.

definition: A picture P represents an object O if and only if (i) P bears a visual resemblance to O, (ii) P was produced with the intention of depicting O, and (iii) a competent viewer can recognise O upon inspecting P.
conditions: P bears a visual resemblance to O
P was produced with the intention of depicting O
A competent viewer can recognise O upon inspecting P
counterexample: Consider a photograph of a man named Smith which, by an extraordinary coincidence of physiognomy, bears a striking visual resemblance to a quite different man named Jones. Suppose the photographer intended to depict Smith, not Jones, and that any competent viewer inspecting the photograph would immediately recognise the figure as Smith. The photograph satisfies all three conditions with respect to Smith. But now consider the photograph's relation to Jones: it bears a visual resemblance to Jones, yet it was not produced with the intention of depicting Jones, so it does not represent Jones on this account. The difficulty lies elsewhere — in certain abstract or non-figurative works that represent states of affairs without bearing any visual resemblance to them.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate cases in which representation proceeds without visual resemblance, as in abstract or diagrammatic depictions. A broader condition permitting representational relations that are conventional or symbolic rather than purely resemblance-based is needed.
passage: A natural first attempt at defining pictorial representation holds that a picture represents an object if and only if it bears a visual resemblance to the object, was produced with the intention of depicting it, and enables a competent viewer to recognise the object upon inspection. This definition handles straightforward cases well enough: a portrait represents its sitter because it looks like him, was painted with the aim of depicting him, and can be recognised by any viewer acquainted with his appearance. It also correctly excludes accidental resemblances in cases where no intention to depict is present. Yet the definition proves too narrow when we turn to certain familiar kinds of pictorial representation. A map of the London Underground represents the spatial relations among stations without bearing any visual resemblance to the actual tunnels and platforms. Diagrammatic and schematic pictures routinely represent their subjects by means of conventions rather than resemblance. All three conditions of the definition are unsatisfied in such cases, yet the pictures are representational. It appears that the definition must be broadened to admit representational relations that are conventional or symbolic, not merely imitative.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) the artist who created W experienced E during the process of creation, (ii) W possesses perceptible qualities that are standardly associated with E, and (iii) an attentive audience recognises E upon engaging with W.
conditions: The artist who created W experienced E during the process of creation
W possesses perceptible qualities that are standardly associated with E
An attentive audience recognises E upon engaging with W
counterexample: Consider a composer who, while in a state of buoyant cheerfulness, deliberately sets out to write a funeral march. Through consummate skill, she produces a piece whose sombre harmonies and heavy rhythms are standardly associated with grief. An attentive audience, hearing the piece, unhesitatingly recognises profound sorrow in it. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied, but condition (i) is not — the artist experienced cheerfulness, not grief. Yet the work plainly expresses grief. The definition wrongly denies this.
missing_condition: The definition errs in requiring that the artist have experienced the expressed emotion during creation. What seems to be needed instead is a condition requiring only that the work's perceptible qualities be such as to make the emotion available to a competent audience, regardless of the artist's actual psychological state.
passage: It has sometimes been supposed that a work of art expresses an emotion if and only if the artist experienced that emotion during the process of creation, the work possesses perceptible qualities standardly associated with the emotion, and an attentive audience recognises the emotion upon engaging with the work. The first condition is intended to ground expression in genuine feeling — to distinguish authentic expression from mere contrivance. The second ensures that the work itself bears the marks of the emotion in its sensory fabric. The third demands that the expression be publicly accessible rather than merely private. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account is readily available. A composer who is in a state of cheerful contentment may, through the exercise of her craft, produce a funeral march of the deepest solemnity. The piece possesses all the perceptible qualities associated with grief — its harmonies are dark, its rhythms ponderous — and any attentive listener will recognise sorrow in it. But the composer felt no sorrow in composing it. On the proposed definition, the work does not express grief, since condition (i) fails. This is an unacceptable result. It appears that expression is a property of the work's perceptible character rather than a biographical fact about the artist's psychology, and the definition must be revised accordingly.

definition: A judgement of taste J is objective if and only if (i) J attributes a property to an object rather than merely reporting the subject's response, (ii) J is supportable by reasons that appeal to features of the object, and (iii) J commands or is capable of commanding agreement among competent observers.
conditions: J attributes a property to an object rather than merely reporting the subject's response
J is supportable by reasons that appeal to features of the object
J commands or is capable of commanding agreement among competent observers
counterexample: Consider the judgement that a particular wine is dry. This judgement attributes a property — dryness — to the wine rather than merely reporting the speaker's subjective reaction. It is supportable by reasons appealing to features of the wine, such as its residual sugar content. And it commands agreement among competent tasters. All three conditions are met, yet the judgement that a wine is dry is not a judgement of taste in the aesthetic sense at all; it is a straightforward descriptive classification.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the property attributed be an aesthetic property — one whose ascription essentially involves the exercise of sensibility or appreciation rather than mere empirical classification. A condition restricting the relevant properties to those of an aesthetic character is needed.
passage: One might attempt to characterise the objectivity of judgements of taste by specifying that such a judgement is objective if and only if it attributes a property to an object rather than merely reporting the subject's response, is supportable by reasons appealing to features of the object, and commands or is capable of commanding agreement among competent observers. This tripartite account is attractive because it mirrors conditions we impose upon objectivity in other domains: an objective judgement is one that reaches beyond the subject, is rationally defensible, and converges across qualified judges. Yet the account proves too broad. Consider the judgement that a particular wine is dry. It attributes a property to the wine. It is supportable by reasons — one may cite the wine's residual sugar level, its astringent finish, and so forth. And it commands agreement among competent tasters. All three conditions are satisfied. But the judgement that a wine is dry is not an aesthetic judgement at all; it is a piece of straightforward oenological description. What the definition lacks is a condition specifying that the property attributed must be of an aesthetic character — must be the sort of property whose ascription requires the exercise of taste or sensibility rather than the application of a technical classification. Without this restriction, the definition captures objectivity in general rather than the objectivity proper to aesthetic judgement.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O produces in a qualified observer a feeling of pleasure upon contemplation, (ii) this pleasure is not contingent upon any desire that the observer has for O, and (iii) the observer judges that any other qualified observer would experience a similar pleasure.
conditions: O produces in a qualified observer a feeling of pleasure upon contemplation
This pleasure is not contingent upon any desire that the observer has for O
The observer judges that any other qualified observer would experience a similar pleasure
counterexample: Consider a perfectly executed mathematical proof displayed on a blackboard. A mathematician contemplating it feels a powerful pleasure that is entirely disinterested — she desires nothing from the proof — and she judges with confidence that any other qualified mathematician would feel the same pleasure. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might reasonably deny that the proof on the blackboard is beautiful in the aesthetic sense, for the pleasure it affords is intellectual rather than sensory or perceptual.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to specify that the pleasure in question must be of a characteristically aesthetic kind — one that arises from the perceptual or imaginative engagement with the object's sensory qualities, rather than from purely intellectual satisfaction.
passage: It has been proposed that an object is beautiful if and only if it produces in a qualified observer a feeling of pleasure upon contemplation, this pleasure is not contingent upon any desire the observer has for the object, and the observer judges that any other qualified observer would experience a similar pleasure. The definition is plainly indebted to a long tradition of thought about the disinterestedness of aesthetic response. By requiring that the pleasure be desire-independent, it excludes the hungry man's pleasure at the sight of food; by requiring universalisability, it distinguishes beauty from idiosyncratic preference. Yet there are cases which reveal the insufficiency of this analysis. A perfectly executed mathematical proof, displayed upon a blackboard, may produce in a qualified mathematician a pleasure that is entirely disinterested and that she confidently expects any other qualified mathematician to share. All three conditions are met. But it would be strange to call the proof beautiful in the same sense in which we call a landscape or a sonata beautiful. The pleasure afforded by the proof is intellectual through and through; it engages the understanding rather than the senses or the imagination. The definition, it appears, must be supplemented with a condition specifying that the relevant pleasure be perceptual or imaginative in character.

definition: A piece of music M expresses sadness if and only if (i) M is in a minor key, (ii) M has a slow tempo, and (iii) M employs descending melodic contours.
conditions: M is in a minor key
M has a slow tempo
M employs descending melodic contours
counterexample: Consider a lively tarantella in a minor key, performed at a brisk tempo with predominantly ascending melodic runs. Such a piece fails conditions (ii) and (iii), yet many competent listeners would describe it as expressing a dark or restless emotional quality closely allied to sadness. Conversely, consider a slow piece in a major key with descending melodic contours — a gentle lullaby, say — which satisfies conditions (ii) and (iii) but not (i), and which expresses tenderness rather than sadness.
missing_condition: The definition reduces the expression of sadness to a conjunction of technical features, failing to require that the overall character of the music be such as to dispose a competent listener to hear sadness in it. A condition grounded in the phenomenology of listening, rather than in isolable musical parameters, is needed.
passage: One might attempt to specify the conditions under which a piece of music expresses sadness by stipulating that it is in a minor key, has a slow tempo, and employs descending melodic contours. These three features are indeed frequently associated with musical sadness, and their conjunction captures a large class of paradigmatic cases — an adagio in C minor with falling phrases is, one might observe, a textbook instance of sad music. Yet the definition proves both too narrow and too broad. It is too narrow because a lively tarantella in a minor key, performed at a brisk tempo with predominantly ascending runs, may nonetheless strike a competent listener as expressing something very close to sadness — a dark restlessness or agitation that we should not wish to exclude from the domain of the sorrowful. It is too broad, or at least misdirected, because a slow lullaby in a major key with gently descending phrases may satisfy two of the three conditions while expressing nothing remotely resembling sadness. The difficulty is that the definition attempts to reduce an expressive quality to a checklist of technical parameters, when what is needed is a condition referring to the holistic impression the music makes upon a competent listener.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O possesses a harmony of parts such that no element could be altered without diminishing the whole, (ii) the perception of O produces a disinterested pleasure in a suitably attentive observer, and (iii) the pleasure so produced is of a contemplative rather than appetitive character.
conditions: O possesses a harmony of parts such that no element could be altered without diminishing the whole
The perception of O produces a disinterested pleasure in a suitably attentive observer
The pleasure produced is contemplative rather than appetitive
counterexample: Consider a vast alpine landscape viewed at dawn, where immense glaciers press against disordered scree fields and a violent cataract breaks the silence. No element stands in harmonious relation to any other; the scene is characterised by dissonance and overwhelming scale. Yet the attentive observer may experience a profound disinterested contemplative pleasure in beholding it. The first condition is plainly unmet, yet the observer would not hesitate to call the scene beautiful.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate cases where beauty arises from the sublime disruption of formal harmony rather than from the orderly arrangement of parts, and thus requires a disjunctive clause permitting beauty to arise from the powerful impression of magnitude or force.
passage: One might propose that an object is beautiful if and only if it possesses a harmony of parts, produces disinterested pleasure, and the pleasure is contemplative. This proposal captures something important about our response to a well-proportioned Greek vase or a balanced sonata. The requirement of harmony ensures that the object has formal merit; the requirement of disinterestedness excludes mere sensory gratification; and the requirement of a contemplative character distinguishes aesthetic pleasure from the satisfaction of appetite. Yet the proposal admits of a case which reveals its insufficiency. Consider a traveller who comes upon a great alpine landscape at dawn. Enormous glaciers press against fields of disordered rock. A violent cataract plunges from an overhang into a ravine below, shattering the stillness. There is no harmony here — the elements are vast, ungovernable, and chaotically arranged. No part stands in a proportioned relation to any other. Yet the traveller, if suitably attentive, may experience a disinterested and contemplative pleasure of the most intense kind. The pleasure is not appetitive; it is not interested; it is wholly contemplative. The second and third conditions are met, but the first is not. The scene is beautiful — perhaps supremely so — and yet it possesses no harmony of parts. What is missing is the recognition that beauty may arise not only from formal order but from the powerful impression of magnitude, force, or wildness that overwhelms the demand for harmony.

definition: An artefact A is a work of art if and only if (i) A is produced by a human agent with the intention of creating an object for aesthetic contemplation, (ii) A embodies a degree of skill or craft that distinguishes it from mere accident, and (iii) A is presented to an audience in a context that invites aesthetic appraisal.
conditions: A is produced by a human agent with the intention of creating an object for aesthetic contemplation
A embodies a degree of skill or craft
A is presented in a context inviting aesthetic appraisal
counterexample: Consider a piece of driftwood, shaped by the sea over many decades, which a collector places upon a pedestal in a gallery. The driftwood was not produced by a human agent, nor was it shaped with any intention whatsoever. Yet it is presented in a context that invites aesthetic appraisal, and visitors to the gallery may regard it as a work of art — indeed, as a more compelling one than many intentionally crafted objects nearby. The first and second conditions are entirely unsatisfied.
missing_condition: The definition neglects the possibility that the status of art may be conferred by contextual framing and institutional acceptance rather than by the intentions or skill of a human maker.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a work of art must be the product of human intention and skill, presented for aesthetic contemplation. One might therefore define a work of art as an artefact produced by a human agent with the intention of creating an object for aesthetic contemplation, embodying a degree of skill or craft, and presented in a context inviting aesthetic appraisal. This definition has the merit of excluding random natural formations, mere utilitarian objects, and private exercises that no one is invited to appraise. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is readily available. Suppose a collector discovers a piece of driftwood on a remote beach — a piece shaped by decades of tidal erosion into a form of striking grace. The collector places it upon a pedestal in a gallery, beside paintings and sculptures. No human agent produced the driftwood with any intention. No skill or craft was exercised in its formation. Yet the gallery context invites aesthetic appraisal, and visitors may find the driftwood more arresting than many intentionally crafted works on display. If pressed, many of them would call it a work of art. The definition cannot accommodate this verdict, for neither the first nor the second condition is met. What appears to be missing is the recognition that the status of art may be conferred by the act of contextual framing and by the acceptance of an audience or institution, independently of the maker's intentions or skill.

definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by an object or event that exceeds the observer's capacity for immediate comprehension, (ii) E involves a feeling of being overwhelmed or diminished, and (iii) E is accompanied by a subsequent elevation or exaltation of mind.
conditions: E is occasioned by an object exceeding the observer's capacity for immediate comprehension
E involves a feeling of being overwhelmed or diminished
E is accompanied by a subsequent elevation or exaltation of mind
counterexample: Consider a mathematician who, after years of labour, finally grasps the proof of a theorem of extraordinary generality. At no point does she feel overwhelmed or diminished; the experience is one of mounting clarity. Yet the proof exceeds ordinary comprehension, and upon grasping it she experiences a profound elevation of mind. She would describe the experience as sublime. The second condition — the feeling of being overwhelmed — is absent.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that the sublime must pass through a phase of felt inadequacy, but fails to account for cases in which the elevation arises from the triumphant exercise of intellectual comprehension rather than from any felt diminishment.
passage: One might analyse the sublime as an experience occasioned by that which exceeds immediate comprehension, involving a feeling of being overwhelmed, and accompanied by a subsequent elevation of mind. This analysis captures the classic instances admirably: the traveller before the boundless ocean, the observer of a violent storm, the contemplator of the starry heavens. In each case there is an initial sense of inadequacy before the vast or powerful, followed by an exaltation in which the mind discovers its own capacity to think beyond the limits of sense. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the intellectual sphere. Consider a mathematician who has struggled for years with a theorem of extraordinary generality and difficulty. When she finally grasps the proof, her experience is not one of being overwhelmed or diminished. She has not been defeated by the object; she has mastered it. Yet the proof exceeds ordinary comprehension, and the experience of grasping it is accompanied by a profound elevation of mind — a sense of having touched something that transcends the particular and the mundane. She would not hesitate to call the experience sublime. The definition requires that the sublime pass through a phase of felt inadequacy, but this case suggests that the elevation may arise directly from the triumphant exercise of comprehension, without any intervening sense of diminishment. A further condition, or a disjunctive reformulation, is needed.

definition: A person P possesses good taste if and only if (i) P's aesthetic judgements are in accord with the preponderance of cultivated opinion, (ii) P has had extensive experience of the relevant class of aesthetic objects, and (iii) P's judgements are not distorted by personal interest or extraneous association.
conditions: P's judgements accord with the preponderance of cultivated opinion
P has extensive experience of the relevant aesthetic objects
P's judgements are free from distortion by personal interest or extraneous association
counterexample: Consider a critic in the early nineteenth century who judges Turner's late paintings to be works of the highest merit at a time when the preponderance of cultivated opinion regards them as unintelligible daubs. The critic has extensive experience of painting, and her judgements are disinterested. Yet the first condition is not met: her judgements are at odds with cultivated opinion. Posterity, however, vindicates her assessment. She possessed better taste than her contemporaries, not worse.
missing_condition: The definition ties good taste to the agreement of cultivated contemporaries, but fails to accommodate the possibility that good taste may consist in the capacity to recognise merit that the prevailing consensus has not yet apprehended.
passage: A natural proposal holds that good taste consists in agreement with cultivated opinion, extensive experience, and freedom from distorting interests. One might observe that this proposal has considerable plausibility. Agreement with the cultivated ensures that taste is not merely idiosyncratic; experience ensures familiarity with the relevant domain; and the absence of distortion ensures that the judgement is genuinely aesthetic. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the history of art criticism.
