In the early decades of the nineteenth century, a handful of critics judged Turner's late canvases — those luminous, near-abstract studies of light and atmosphere — to be works of the highest order. The preponderance of cultivated opinion at the time regarded these paintings as unintelligible, formless, and perhaps evidence of declining powers. The dissenting critics had extensive experience of painting and their judgements were disinterested. Nevertheless, the first condition was not satisfied: their judgements were at odds with cultivated opinion. Posterity has decisively vindicated the dissenters. Turner's late works are now regarded as among the supreme achievements of European painting. The critics who recognised their merit possessed better taste, not worse, than their conforming contemporaries. The definition cannot accommodate this, for it equates good taste with conformity to the prevailing cultivated consensus, and thereby excludes the possibility that taste may sometimes run ahead of opinion.

definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E is directed toward the sensory or formal properties of an object, (ii) E is valued for its own sake rather than for any practical end, and (iii) E involves a heightened attentiveness to the qualities of the object as perceived.
conditions: E is directed toward sensory or formal properties of an object
E is valued for its own sake
E involves heightened attentiveness to perceived qualities
counterexample: Consider a wine merchant who tastes a Burgundy with the utmost attentiveness to its sensory qualities — its colour, bouquet, and finish. Her experience is directed toward sensory properties and involves heightened attentiveness. Yet she does not value the experience for its own sake; she tastes the wine in order to determine its market price. All conditions save the second appear to be met, and yet many philosophers would insist that her experience is genuinely aesthetic despite its practical motive, for the character of the attending is indistinguishable from that of a disinterested connoisseur.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the experience be valued for its own sake, but fails to recognise that the phenomenological character of an aesthetic experience may be present even when the experience also serves a practical purpose.
passage: One might define an aesthetic experience as one directed toward the sensory or formal properties of an object, valued for its own sake, and involving heightened attentiveness to perceived qualities. This definition excludes cursory glances and instrumental engagements: the commuter who barely registers a painting on the station wall, and the engineer who examines a bridge only for structural soundness, are both excluded. Yet the definition admits of a case which reveals its insufficiency. Consider a wine merchant who attends a tasting with the purpose of pricing a case of Burgundy. She holds the glass to the light, noting the depth of colour; she inhales the bouquet with exquisite care; she allows the wine to rest upon her palate, attending to every nuance of flavour and finish. Her experience is directed toward sensory properties and involves the most heightened attentiveness one could wish. Yet she does not value the experience for its own sake — her purpose is commercial. The second condition is unmet. Nevertheless, it appears that her experience, considered in its phenomenological character, is indistinguishable from that of a disinterested connoisseur who tastes the same wine purely for pleasure. If the character of the experience is the same, it seems arbitrary to deny it the title of aesthetic merely because an extrinsic motive is also present. The definition conflates the phenomenological character of the experience with the motives for undergoing it.

definition: A painting P represents a subject S if and only if (i) P resembles S in relevant visual respects, (ii) the artist intended P to depict S, and (iii) a competent viewer would recognise S upon inspection of P.
conditions: P resembles S in relevant visual respects
The artist intended P to depict S
A competent viewer would recognise S upon inspection of P
counterexample: Consider an extremely skilful trompe-l'oeil painting of a bowl of fruit. It resembles the fruit perfectly, the artist intended to depict fruit, and any viewer would recognise fruit upon inspection. Now consider a highly abstract painting by an artist who intends it to represent grief. The painting does not visually resemble grief in any respect, and no competent viewer, without a title or explanation, would recognise grief upon inspection. Yet the artist insists — and critics concur — that the painting represents grief. The first and third conditions are unmet, yet the painting represents grief nonetheless.
missing_condition: The definition restricts representation to cases of visual resemblance and viewer recognition, but fails to accommodate non-mimetic modes of representation in which the representational content is carried by expressive, symbolic, or conventional means rather than by likeness.
passage: A familiar account of pictorial representation holds that a painting represents its subject by resembling it, being intended to depict it, and being recognisable to competent viewers. This account works tolerably well for naturalistic painting: a portrait of a statesman resembles him, the painter intended to depict him, and viewers recognise his likeness. Yet the account admits of a case which reveals its insufficiency when we turn to non-figurative art. Consider a painter who produces a large canvas of dark, turbulent shapes — no recognisable figures, no identifiable objects. The painter titles the work and declares that it represents grief. Critics who study the painting concur: the brooding tones and violent gestures of paint convey grief with an intensity that no naturalistic depiction of a weeping figure could match. Yet the painting does not visually resemble grief in any ordinary sense. Grief has no visual appearance that could be copied. And no competent viewer, deprived of the title and the artist's statement, would recognise grief upon inspection. The first and third conditions are not met. Nevertheless, the painting represents grief — it does so through expressive means rather than through mimetic resemblance. The definition, in tying representation to visual likeness and recognisability, excludes the entire tradition of abstract and expressionist art.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) the creator of W experienced E during the process of creation, (ii) the formal properties of W are such as to evoke E in a suitably sensitive audience, and (iii) W was produced with the intention of communicating E.
conditions: The creator experienced E during the process of creation
The formal properties of W evoke E in a suitably sensitive audience
W was produced with the intention of communicating E
counterexample: Consider a composer who, in a state of serene contentment, writes a funeral march of overwhelming sorrow. She does not feel sorrow; she deploys her technical mastery to construct a piece whose formal properties — minor tonality, slow tempo, heavy orchestration — reliably evoke grief in audiences. She intends to communicate sorrow, and audiences do experience it. The second and third conditions are met, but the first is not. Yet the funeral march expresses sorrow as surely as any work composed in genuine anguish.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires that the artist have experienced the expressed emotion, failing to recognise that expression in art may be a matter of the skilful deployment of formal means rather than a direct transmission of felt states.
passage: It is tempting to suppose that artistic expression requires the artist to have felt what the work conveys. One might define the expression of emotion in art by requiring that the creator experienced the emotion during creation, that the formal properties of the work evoke it in audiences, and that the work was produced with the intention of communicating it. This definition captures a romantic picture of artistic creation — the anguished composer pouring his grief into a sonata, the joyful painter suffusing a canvas with delight. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by a composer who, in a state of perfect equanimity, writes a funeral march of extraordinary pathos. She does not feel sorrow; her mood is serene. Yet she possesses complete command of the formal means by which sorrow is conveyed in music: the minor key, the slow processional tempo, the heavy brass orchestration, the descending melodic lines. The result is a work that evokes the deepest grief in any suitably sensitive listener. She intended to communicate sorrow, and she succeeded. The second and third conditions are met. Yet the first condition is entirely absent — she felt no sorrow whatever. If expression required the artist's having felt the emotion, then this funeral march would express nothing, which is absurd. The definition confuses the biographical psychology of the artist with the expressive properties of the work.

definition: An aesthetic judgement J is objective if and only if (i) J is based upon features of the object that are accessible to any competent observer, (ii) J can be supported by reasons that refer to those features, and (iii) competent observers converge upon J under favourable conditions of appraisal.
conditions: J is based upon features accessible to any competent observer
J can be supported by reasons referring to those features
Competent observers converge upon J under favourable conditions
counterexample: Consider two highly cultivated music critics who, under ideal conditions of attentive listening, disagree profoundly about the merits of a late Beethoven quartet. Each bases her judgement upon features of the quartet accessible to any competent listener — its harmonic structure, motivic development, and formal proportions. Each supports her judgement with detailed reasons. Yet they do not converge. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not, and neither critic appears incompetent or inattentive.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that competent observers will converge, but fails to account for the possibility of irresolvable aesthetic disagreements among equally competent, attentive, and well-informed judges — a possibility that may be intrinsic to the aesthetic domain.
passage: One might propose that an aesthetic judgement is objective if it is based on publicly accessible features of the object, supported by reasons, and such that competent observers converge upon it under favourable conditions. This proposal is attractive because it distinguishes aesthetic judgement from mere personal preference: the judgement is anchored in the object's features, articulated in reasons, and ratified by consensus. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is not difficult to find. Consider two music critics of acknowledged cultivation and expertise. They listen to a late Beethoven quartet under ideal conditions — in a fine concert hall, in a state of alert attentiveness, with full familiarity with the musical tradition. Each bases her judgement upon features of the quartet accessible to any competent listener: the harmonic structure, the motivic development, the formal proportions. Each can articulate detailed reasons for her verdict. Yet one finds the quartet a supreme masterpiece, while the other finds it a noble failure. The first two conditions are satisfied: the judgements are grounded in accessible features and supported by reasons. But the third condition — convergence — is not met. Neither critic is incompetent, inattentive, or ignorant. The disagreement appears to be irresolvable. The definition equates objectivity with convergence, but the aesthetic domain may be one in which deep disagreement among competent judges is not a sign of error but a permanent feature of the subject-matter.

definition: A photograph P is a faithful representation of its subject S if and only if (i) P is produced by a causal process originating in S, (ii) the visual content of P corresponds to the visual appearance of S at the moment of capture, and (iii) no post-production alteration has been made to the content of P.
conditions: P is produced by a causal process originating in S
The visual content of P corresponds to the visual appearance of S
No post-production alteration has been made
counterexample: Consider a photograph of a building taken with an ultra-wide-angle lens, which causes straight lines to curve dramatically and makes the building appear to lean inward. The photograph is produced by a causal process originating in the building. No post-production alteration has been made. Yet the visual content does not correspond to the building's appearance as seen by the unaided eye; the distortion is an artefact of the lens. All three conditions may seem to be met — the building did look that way to the camera — yet the photograph is not a faithful representation of what a human observer would see.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to specify whose visual perspective determines the standard of correspondence, failing to recognise that the camera's optics may introduce systematic distortions that render the image unfaithful to ordinary human perception.
passage: A natural account of photographic fidelity holds that a photograph faithfully represents its subject when it is causally produced by the subject, its visual content corresponds to the subject's appearance, and no post-production alteration has been introduced. This account has obvious appeal: it grounds fidelity in causal connection, visual correspondence, and the absence of manipulation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises from the optical properties of lenses. Consider an architectural photographer who uses an ultra-wide-angle lens to capture a tall building. The resulting photograph shows the building's vertical lines curving dramatically inward; the structure appears to lean and swell in ways that bear little resemblance to the experience of a person standing before it. The photograph was produced by a causal process originating in the building; no post-production alteration has been made to the image. And in a sense the visual content corresponds to the subject — this is indeed what the building looked like to the camera at that focal length. Yet no human observer, standing where the photographer stood, would see anything of the kind. The photograph distorts the building's appearance in a manner determined by the optics of the lens, not by the properties of the subject. The definition fails to specify that visual correspondence must be judged relative to the visual experience of an ordinary human observer rather than the optical behaviour of an arbitrary imaging device.

definition: A passage of music M is expressive of sadness if and only if (i) M is composed in a minor key, (ii) M employs a slow tempo, and (iii) M features descending melodic contours.
conditions: M is composed in a minor key
M employs a slow tempo
M features descending melodic contours
counterexample: Consider the second movement of Barber's Adagio for Strings, which is widely regarded as one of the most profoundly sorrowful works in the orchestral repertoire. Portions of the movement employ ascending melodic lines that climb with an aching intensity, and the work's expressive power derives not from descending contours alone but from the tension between aspiration and resignation. The third condition is not uniformly met, yet the passage is unmistakably expressive of sadness.
missing_condition: The definition provides a rigid checklist of musical features but fails to recognise that the expressiveness of music arises from the complex interaction and contextual deployment of multiple parameters, such that any single parameter may be absent if the others compensate.
passage: One might attempt to specify the conditions under which music expresses sadness by pointing to three familiar musical features: a minor key, a slow tempo, and descending melodic contours. These features do frequently co-occur in music that is heard as sad, and a composer who wishes to convey sorrow will often employ all three. Yet it appears that the definition is too rigid to capture the full range of musically expressive sadness. Consider the slow movement of Barber's celebrated Adagio for Strings, a work universally heard as an expression of the most penetrating sorrow. While the movement does employ a slow tempo and passages in minor tonality, it also contains ascending melodic lines of extraordinary intensity — lines that climb upward with a yearning quality, only to fall back. The expressive effect of these ascending passages is not one of joy or resolution but of anguished aspiration, and the sorrow of the music is deepened, not diminished, by the upward motion. The third condition — descending melodic contours — is not uniformly satisfied. Yet the music is expressive of sadness beyond any reasonable doubt. The definition mistakes a common association for a necessary condition and fails to recognise that musical expressiveness arises from the interaction and contextual deployment of many parameters, such that the absence of one may be more than compensated by the presence of others.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O exhibits a harmonious arrangement of its parts, (ii) the perception of O produces a feeling of pleasure in a normally constituted observer, and (iii) the pleasure is disinterested — that is, it does not depend upon any desire to possess or make use of O.
conditions: O exhibits a harmonious arrangement of its parts
Perception of O produces pleasure in a normally constituted observer
The pleasure is disinterested
counterexample: Consider a rugged mountain landscape — vast, irregular, threatening in its scale. Its parts are not harmoniously arranged; the crags jut at violent angles, the precipices fall without symmetry, and the whole conveys an impression of disorder rather than proportion. Yet a normally constituted observer may experience an intense and disinterested pleasure in contemplating it. The landscape is widely judged beautiful despite violating condition (i), which reveals that harmonious arrangement of parts is not a necessary condition of beauty.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow that beauty may arise from grandeur, wildness, or expressive power in the absence of formal harmony, and thus confuses one species of beauty with the genus.
passage: One might propose that an object is beautiful if and only if it exhibits a harmonious arrangement of its parts, produces a disinterested pleasure in a normally constituted observer, and owes its pleasingness to that harmony rather than to any practical interest. This proposal has considerable appeal: it captures the classical intuition that beauty is a matter of proportion and order, and it excludes the merely agreeable — the pleasure of a warm bath, for instance — by insisting upon disinterestedness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is not difficult to find. Consider a mountain landscape of the sort that travellers in the Alps have admired since the eighteenth century. The scene is vast and irregular: precipices fall away without symmetry, ridges jut at violent angles, and the whole conveys an impression not of order but of sublime disorder. No normally constituted observer would describe the arrangement of its parts as harmonious. Yet the pleasure it produces is intense, immediate, and entirely disinterested — one does not wish to possess or use the mountain; one wishes only to contemplate it. If we insist that beauty requires harmonious arrangement, we must deny that such landscapes are beautiful, which is plainly at odds with the nearly universal judgement of those who have stood before them. One might observe that the definition has taken a feature characteristic of one species of beauty — the beauty of classical sculpture, of well-proportioned architecture — and elevated it to a necessary condition of the genus, thereby excluding the beauty of the wild, the irregular, and the grand.

definition: A person P possesses good taste if and only if (i) P responds with pleasure to objects that are widely acknowledged as excellent, (ii) P responds with displeasure to objects that are widely acknowledged as defective, and (iii) P's responses are consistent over time.
conditions: P responds with pleasure to widely acknowledged excellent objects
P responds with displeasure to widely acknowledged defective objects
P's responses are consistent over time
counterexample: Consider a critic who consistently takes pleasure in the acknowledged masterworks and displeasure in acknowledged failures, satisfying all three conditions. Yet this same critic is unable to form any independent judgement about new or controversial works — he merely waits for a consensus to form and then aligns his responses accordingly. His taste is perfectly calibrated to established opinion but contributes nothing to the recognition of new excellence. We should hesitate to say that such a person possesses genuinely good taste.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that good taste include the capacity for independent discrimination — the ability to recognise excellence or deficiency in works where no consensus yet exists.
passage: It appears natural to define good taste as a disposition to respond with pleasure to objects of acknowledged excellence, with displeasure to objects of acknowledged deficiency, and to do so consistently over time. This captures the ordinary expectation that a person of taste will admire what is admirable, deplore what is deplorable, and not vacillate capriciously between the two. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. Imagine a critic whose responses are perfectly aligned with received opinion: he admires every acknowledged masterpiece and deprecates every acknowledged failure, and he does so with unwavering consistency. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet this same critic is entirely unable to form a judgement about any new or controversial work. When confronted with a painting that has not yet been assessed by the critical establishment, he suspends judgement until a consensus has emerged, and then adjusts his response accordingly. His taste, so defined, is merely a species of conformity — a reliable echo of the judgements of others. One might observe that what we ordinarily value in good taste is precisely the capacity for independent discrimination, the ability to recognise merit where it has not yet been generally recognised or to detect deficiency where it has been overlooked. The definition, by tying good taste entirely to agreement with established verdicts, captures the derivative phenomenon of critical conformity but misses the generative capacity that distinguishes genuine taste from mere acquiescence.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) the artist who created W experienced E during or prior to the creation of W, (ii) the formal properties of W are such as to convey E to a suitably attentive audience, and (iii) the audience recognises E as the emotion expressed.
conditions: The artist experienced E during or prior to creation
The formal properties of W convey E to a suitably attentive audience
The audience recognises E as the emotion expressed
counterexample: Consider a film composer who is commissioned to write music for a scene depicting intense grief. The composer, by all accounts, is in a cheerful and contented state throughout the process of composition. Yet the resulting music — with its slow tempo, its descending chromatic lines, its sparse orchestration — conveys grief with such power that every attentive listener recognises the emotion at once. Condition (i) is not satisfied, since the composer did not experience grief, yet conditions (ii) and (iii) are fully met. We should not hesitate to say that the music expresses grief.
missing_condition: The definition mistakenly requires the artist's biographical experience of the emotion, when what matters is only that the formal properties of the work are sufficient to convey the emotion to a competent audience.
passage: One might propose that a work of art expresses an emotion if and only if the artist experienced that emotion during or prior to creation, the formal properties of the work convey it to a suitably attentive audience, and the audience recognises it as such. This proposal has a natural appeal, for it connects expression with sincerity — the artist, on this view, is not merely simulating but communicating a genuinely felt state. Yet the proposal encounters a serious difficulty when we consider the common practice of professional composition. A film composer commissioned to write music for a scene of intense grief need not be grieving; indeed, the evidence suggests that many such composers work in states of calm concentration or even good humour. The resulting music, however, may convey grief with extraordinary power: the slow tempo, the descending chromatic figures, the thinning of the orchestral texture all combine to produce in the listener an unmistakable impression of sorrow. Every attentive listener recognises the emotion at once. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are fully satisfied. But condition (i) is not, for the composer experienced no grief whatsoever. If we insist upon the biographical requirement, we must deny that such music expresses grief, which is plainly at odds with our critical practice. One might observe that the definition confuses the biographical genesis of a work with its expressive character, and that what determines whether a work expresses an emotion is not the artist's psychological state but the formal properties of the work itself and their capacity to convey the emotion to a competent audience.

definition: A work of art W is an imitation of a subject S if and only if (i) W reproduces the visible appearance of S, (ii) a competent observer can identify S by inspecting W alone, and (iii) fidelity to S's appearance is the governing aim of W.
conditions: W reproduces the visible appearance of S
A competent observer can identify S from W alone
Fidelity to appearance is the governing aim
counterexample: Consider a political caricature that depicts a well-known statesman. The drawing grossly exaggerates the subject's prominent nose, narrows his eyes to slits, and distorts his posture into a simian slouch. The visible appearance has been deliberately altered rather than faithfully reproduced, so condition (i) is violated. Yet a competent observer identifies the subject at once, and the caricature is universally understood to be an imitation — indeed, a peculiarly penetrating one — of the statesman. The work imitates its subject not by copying his appearance but by isolating and amplifying his characteristic features.
missing_condition: The definition requires faithful reproduction of visible appearance, but imitation may also proceed by selective exaggeration of characteristic features, which can yield a more recognisable likeness than faithful copying.
passage: It appears plausible to define imitation in art as the reproduction of the visible appearance of a subject, such that a competent observer can identify the subject from the work alone and fidelity to appearance is the governing aim. This definition captures straightforward cases — the portrait that allows us to recognise its sitter, the landscape that depicts a recognisable scene — with admirable directness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider caricature. A political caricaturist who depicts a well-known statesman does not reproduce the statesman's visible appearance: the prominent nose is grotesquely enlarged, the narrow eyes are reduced to slits, the posture is distorted into something barely human. Fidelity to appearance is plainly not the governing aim. Yet the result is immediately recognisable — indeed, more readily recognisable than many a faithful portrait. The caricature isolates and exaggerates precisely those features that are most characteristic of the subject, producing a likeness that is distorted in its particulars but uncanny in its overall effect. If we restrict imitation to faithful reproduction, we must exclude caricature from the category of imitative art, which seems perverse, given that caricature depends for its very intelligibility upon its imitative relation to a known subject. One might observe that the definition has confused one technique of imitation — faithful copying — with imitation as such, and that selective exaggeration of characteristic features is an alternative and sometimes superior method of achieving the imitative aim.

definition: A work of art W is original if and only if (i) W does not reproduce or closely resemble any prior work, (ii) the techniques employed in W have not been previously used, and (iii) the conception of W arose independently in the mind of the artist without borrowing from others.
conditions: W does not reproduce or closely resemble any prior work
The techniques employed have not been previously used
The conception arose independently without borrowing
counterexample: Consider Bach's The Art of Fugue, which employs the technique of fugal writing that had been practised for centuries and closely resembles prior fugal compositions in its basic procedures. The conception of writing a cycle of fugues on a single subject was itself not without precedent. Yet The Art of Fugue is universally regarded as one of the most original compositions in the history of Western music, because Bach brought the inherited techniques to an unprecedented level of complexity and rigour. Conditions (i), (ii), and (iii) are all at least partially violated, yet the work's originality is beyond dispute.
missing_condition: The definition treats originality as novelty of technique, resemblance, and conception, but originality may equally consist in the unprecedented mastery, depth, or transformation of inherited materials and methods.
passage: One might define originality in art as the property of a work that does not reproduce any prior work, employs techniques not previously used, and proceeds from a conception that arose independently in the artist's mind. This definition captures the romantic ideal of the artist as solitary inventor, bringing forth something entirely new. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by Bach's The Art of Fugue. The technique of fugal composition was centuries old by the time Bach took it up; the basic procedures — the statement of a subject, the entry of voices at the fifth, the use of inversion and augmentation — were standard pedagogical fare. The conception of writing a systematic cycle of fugues on a single subject was not unprecedented. The work closely resembles prior fugal compositions in its surface procedures. By the lights of the definition, The Art of Fugue should be judged unoriginal. Yet no competent critic has ever denied its originality. What makes it original is not the novelty of its techniques but the depth, rigour, and inventiveness with which inherited techniques are employed. Bach transforms the fugue from within, discovering possibilities that no prior composer had imagined. One might observe that the definition confuses originality with novelty, and that the most profound originality in art often consists not in the invention of new techniques but in the unprecedented exploitation of familiar ones — in showing that inherited forms contain resources that had gone unsuspected.

definition: An experience is sublime if and only if (i) the object of the experience is of overwhelming magnitude or power, (ii) the subject initially feels a sense of being diminished or threatened by the object, and (iii) the subject subsequently experiences an elevation or exaltation arising from the awareness of his own rational or moral capacities.
conditions: The object is of overwhelming magnitude or power
The subject initially feels diminished or threatened
The subject subsequently experiences elevation or exaltation
counterexample: Consider the experience of witnessing a perfectly clear night sky in a remote location, where the stars are visible in extraordinary profusion. The sky is vast, but it does not threaten or diminish the observer; it produces instead a profound stillness and a sense of serene wonder from the very first moment. There is no initial phase of terror or diminishment followed by exaltation — the experience is one of quiet absorption from beginning to end. Yet many observers would describe this experience as sublime. The definition's insistence upon a two-phase structure of initial threat followed by recovery does not accommodate this quieter variety of sublimity.
missing_condition: The definition assumes a two-phase structure — diminishment followed by exaltation — but fails to accommodate experiences of serene or contemplative sublimity in which vastness produces wonder without any intervening sense of threat.
passage: One might propose that an experience is sublime if and only if its object is of overwhelming magnitude or power, the subject initially feels diminished or threatened, and there subsequently arises a sense of elevation or exaltation connected to the subject's awareness of his own rational capacities. This is a distinguished proposal, and it captures with some precision the experience of standing before a violent storm or at the edge of a great cataract — one feels small, then recovers a sense of one's own dignity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider the experience of the night sky under ideal conditions. In a remote place, far from artificial light, the sky reveals itself in a manner that many observers describe as sublime. The stars are visible in extraordinary profusion, and the depths of the sky seem to extend without limit. Yet the experience is not one of initial threat followed by recovery. The sky does not threaten; it does not diminish. The experience from its very onset is one of quiet, absorbed wonder — a stillness rather than a turbulence, a sense of being drawn outward rather than of being first repelled and then reassured. There is no two-phase dialectic of terror and exaltation; there is a single, sustained state of serene contemplation. If the definition requires this dialectical structure, it must exclude the sublimity of the night sky, which seems an unhappy result. One might observe that the definition has been constructed from the model of the terrifying sublime — the storm, the precipice — and has mistaken this dramatic variety for the whole of the category, neglecting the contemplative sublime in which vastness produces wonder without any admixture of fear.

definition: A dramatic work W is a tragedy if and only if (i) the protagonist of W suffers a reversal from good fortune to bad, (ii) the reversal is brought about by a flaw in the protagonist's character, and (iii) the audience experiences pity and fear in response to the protagonist's fate.
conditions: The protagonist suffers a reversal from good fortune to bad
The reversal is caused by a character flaw
The audience experiences pity and fear
counterexample: Consider Sophocles' Oedipus at Colonus, in which the aged Oedipus, already in bad fortune, achieves a mysterious apotheosis at the end — a movement from suffering to a kind of transcendence. The protagonist does not undergo a reversal from good fortune to bad; if anything, the movement is the reverse. Yet the play is universally classified as a tragedy, and the audience experiences pity and awe throughout. Condition (i) is not satisfied, yet the work is unquestionably tragic.
missing_condition: The definition requires a reversal from good fortune to bad, but tragedy may also consist in the portrayal of suffering that is irreversible from the outset, or in the depiction of a fate that is terrible in its very culmination, without any prior state of good fortune.
passage: One might define tragedy as a dramatic work in which the protagonist suffers a reversal from good fortune to bad, brought about by a flaw in the protagonist's character, and the audience experiences pity and fear in response. This definition has the authority of a long tradition behind it, and it accommodates many of the central cases — Macbeth, King Lear, Phèdre — with considerable adequacy. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be found in the Attic tradition itself. In Sophocles' Oedipus at Colonus, the aged Oedipus arrives as a blind and impoverished exile — his reversal from good fortune to bad lies in the distant past. The action of the play does not trace a fall but rather a mysterious elevation: Oedipus, after years of suffering, is received at Colonus and undergoes a transfiguration at the moment of his death. The movement is from destitution towards something resembling grace. Condition (i) is plainly not satisfied, for there is no reversal from good fortune to bad within the play. Yet no scholar has denied that Oedipus at Colonus is a tragedy, and the pity and awe it produces are, if anything, more intense than those produced by its predecessor. One might observe that the definition has been too closely modelled on a single pattern of tragic action — the downfall of the great — and has neglected the possibility that tragedy may reside in the depiction of suffering itself, in the contemplation of a fate that is terrible regardless of the direction of the protagonist's fortunes.

definition: A composition C is harmonious if and only if (i) the parts of C stand in relations of proportion and balance to one another, (ii) no part of C could be removed or altered without diminishing the effect of the whole, and (iii) the relations among the parts conform to established rules or conventions of the relevant art form.
conditions: Parts stand in relations of proportion and balance
No part can be removed or altered without diminishing the whole
Relations conform to established rules or conventions
counterexample: Consider the late string quartets of Beethoven, which were widely regarded upon their first appearance as violating every established convention of harmonic and formal propriety. The relations among their parts do not conform to the received rules of the period; the proportions are unconventional; passages of jarring dissonance interrupt moments of lyrical beauty. Yet the quartets are now recognised as possessing a profound inner harmony — a sense that every note, however unexpected, is exactly where it must be. Condition (iii) is violated, yet the works are regarded as paradigms of compositional harmony.
missing_condition: The definition requires conformity to established conventions, but genuine harmony may arise from the discovery of new relations among parts that violate established rules while achieving an internal coherence that is recognised only in retrospect.
passage: It appears reasonable to propose that a composition is harmonious when its parts stand in relations of proportion and balance, when no part could be removed without diminishing the whole, and when the relations among the parts conform to established rules. This proposal captures the harmony of a well-proportioned Palladian facade or a fugue by Handel with considerable precision. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the late string quartets of Beethoven. When these works were first performed, they were widely condemned as incoherent, dissonant, and structurally perverse. The relations among their parts violated every established convention of Viennese quartet writing. Passages of extreme dissonance appeared where convention demanded resolution; movements were of unprecedented length and complexity; the proportions were unlike anything previously encountered. Condition (iii) was flagrantly violated. Yet with the passage of time these quartets have come to be recognised as possessing a harmony more profound than that of any conventionally correct composition. Every note, however strange it may have seemed to the first audiences, is felt to be in its rightful place. The quartets possess the second property — that no part could be removed without diminishing the whole — in the highest degree. One might observe that the definition confuses harmony with conformity to convention, and that the most profound harmony in art may consist in the discovery of new relations among parts that established rules had not anticipated.

definition: An artist A has a distinctive style if and only if (i) A's works exhibit recurring formal features that are recognisable across different works, (ii) these features are deliberately chosen by A as a means of artistic expression, and (iii) the features distinguish A's works from those of other practitioners in the same art form.
conditions: A's works exhibit recurring recognisable formal features
The features are deliberately chosen
The features distinguish A from other practitioners
counterexample: Consider the case of a potter working in a traditional village workshop who produces vessels that are immediately recognisable as her own — the particular curve of the lip, the characteristic thickness of the wall, the distinctive irregularity of the glaze. These features recur across all her works and distinguish them from those of other potters in the village. Yet the potter has not deliberately chosen these features as a means of expression; they arise from the habitual movements of her hands, her particular way of working the clay, and the idiosyncrasies of her kiln. Condition (ii) is not met, yet we should not hesitate to attribute a distinctive style to her work.
missing_condition: The definition requires deliberate choice, but a distinctive style may emerge from habitual practice, bodily disposition, and the accumulated effects of working within a tradition, without any conscious decision to adopt characteristic features.
passage: One might propose that an artist possesses a distinctive style when her works exhibit recurring formal features that are recognisable across different works, when these features are deliberately chosen, and when they distinguish her works from those of other practitioners. This is a plausible account: it captures the sense in which a Rembrandt is recognisably a Rembrandt and a Vermeer a Vermeer, and it insists that style is a matter of artistic choice rather than accident. Yet the proposal encounters difficulty when we consider artists working within traditional craft practices. A potter in a traditional workshop may produce vessels that are immediately identifiable as her own — the particular curve of the lip, the weight of the wall, the character of the glaze all bear her distinctive mark. These features recur across all her works and set them apart from those of her neighbours. But the potter has not deliberately chosen these features; they have emerged from the habitual movements of her hands, from her particular manner of centering the clay, from the idiosyncrasies of her kiln's temperature distribution. If asked why her pots look as they do, she might say only that this is how she has always worked. One might observe that the definition confuses style with stylistic policy — the deliberate adoption of formal features — and that a great deal of what we recognise as style arises not from choice but from the accumulated effects of habitual practice, bodily disposition, and the particular circumstances of making.

definition: An audience undergoes catharsis through a work of art W if and only if (i) W arouses strong emotions — such as pity, fear, or sorrow — in the audience, (ii) the emotions are discharged or purged through the experience of W, leaving the audience in a calmer state, and (iii) the purgation is experienced as pleasurable.
conditions: W arouses strong emotions in the audience
The emotions are discharged or purged through the experience
The purgation is experienced as pleasurable
counterexample: Consider the experience of reading a novel that depicts, with great art, the slow disintegration of a family through misunderstanding and pride. The reader's emotions are powerfully aroused — pity for the characters, indignation at their stubbornness, sorrow at the waste of human possibility. But upon finishing the novel, the reader does not feel calmer or purged. She feels, rather, a heightened and clarifying awareness of the complexities of human relationship — an intensification of feeling rather than a discharge. The experience is deeply valuable, but condition (ii) is not satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition requires that emotions be discharged, but catharsis may also consist in the clarification or deepening of emotional understanding, without any purgation or reduction in the intensity of feeling.
passage: One might define catharsis as a process in which a work of art arouses strong emotions in the audience, these emotions are discharged or purged through the experience, and the purgation is felt as pleasurable. This is a venerable proposal, and it captures with some accuracy the experience of weeping at a tragic performance and feeling afterwards a species of relief. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider the effect of certain novels upon attentive readers. A novel that depicts the slow disintegration of a family through accumulated misunderstandings may arouse the most intense emotions — pity, indignation, sorrow — and sustain them over many hours of reading. Upon finishing the novel, however, the reader does not feel purged or calmer. The emotions have not been discharged; if anything, they have been intensified and given a new clarity. The reader sees the complexities of human relationship with a heightened awareness that did not exist before the reading. The experience is valuable — profoundly so — but it is not a purgation. One might observe that the definition has been built upon the model of emotional relief, in which art functions as a kind of safety valve, but that art may equally function as a lens that sharpens and deepens our emotional perception without in any way diminishing its intensity. The definition captures one mode of aesthetic response and mistakes it for the whole.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O possesses a harmonious arrangement of its parts, (ii) O elicits a disinterested pleasure in a normally sensitive observer, and (iii) O admits of no practical or utilitarian purpose that governs the observer's response.
conditions: O possesses a harmonious arrangement of its parts
O elicits a disinterested pleasure in a normally sensitive observer
O admits of no practical or utilitarian purpose that governs the observer's response
counterexample: Consider a mathematical proof of extraordinary elegance — say, Euler's identity relating the fundamental constants. Mathematicians report a disinterested pleasure upon contemplating it, and the proof possesses a harmonious internal structure. Yet the proof is not an object with spatial parts arranged harmoniously in the ordinary sense, and one might well regard it as beautiful despite the fact that its primary purpose is demonstrative and thus, in a broad sense, utilitarian. The definition cannot accommodate this case without considerable strain.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to allow that beauty may attach to formal or intellectual structures whose harmony is abstract rather than sensory, and whose purpose, though in some sense practical, does not diminish the aesthetic response.
passage: One might propose that an object is beautiful if and only if it possesses a harmonious arrangement of its parts, elicits a disinterested pleasure in a normally sensitive observer, and admits of no practical or utilitarian purpose that governs the observer's response. This proposal has deep roots in the tradition and captures something important about the contemplation of natural scenery, of sculpture, and of music. Yet one should hesitate to accept it as fully adequate. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when we consider the beauty attributed to mathematical proofs. Euler's identity, which expresses a relation among five fundamental constants in a single equation of astonishing simplicity, is frequently described as beautiful by those competent to appreciate it. The proof possesses a kind of harmony — its elements are arranged with an economy and elegance that strikes the mathematical sensibility as deeply satisfying. The pleasure it elicits appears to be disinterested in the relevant sense: one does not enjoy the proof because it serves one's personal advantage. Yet the proof is demonstrative in purpose — it establishes a truth — and this purpose is, broadly speaking, utilitarian. Moreover, the harmony in question is not an arrangement of sensory parts but an abstract relation among concepts. It appears that the definition, by tying beauty to sensory arrangement and the absence of practical purpose, fails to accommodate the beauty of formal structures whose harmony is intellectual rather than perceptual.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) the artist who produced W experienced E during the process of creation, (ii) the formal properties of W are such as to communicate E to a competent audience, and (iii) E is the dominant emotional quality that a competent audience attributes to W upon careful attention.
conditions: The artist who produced W experienced E during the process of creation
The formal properties of W are such as to communicate E to a competent audience
E is the dominant emotional quality that a competent audience attributes to W upon careful attention
counterexample: Consider a composer who, whilst in a state of perfect contentment and good humour, writes a funeral march of devastating grief. She is a consummate craftsman who understands precisely which harmonic progressions and rhythmic patterns will convey sorrow. A competent audience unhesitatingly attributes profound grief to the work. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied, yet condition (i) fails: the artist experienced no grief whatever during the process of creation. It would be perverse to deny that the funeral march expresses grief.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly requires that the artist have experienced the emotion expressed, when in fact a skilled artist may express emotions through mastery of formal conventions without having undergone those emotions during the creative process.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a work of art expresses an emotion when the artist experienced that emotion during creation, when the formal properties of the work communicate the emotion to a competent audience, and when the audience identifies that emotion as the dominant quality of the work. This account has the virtue of connecting artistic expression to genuine feeling and of requiring that the feeling be successfully communicated. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal arises in the domain of musical composition. One might consider a composer who, whilst in a state of perfect contentment, writes a funeral march of devastating grief. She possesses a thorough command of musical convention and understands precisely which harmonic progressions, rhythmic patterns, and orchestral textures will convey sorrow to an audience. The audience, upon hearing the work, unhesitatingly attributes profound grief to it. The formal properties of the composition communicate grief with exceptional clarity, and grief is the dominant emotional quality attributed to the work upon careful attention. Yet the composer experienced no grief whatever during the process of creation. It appears that one should say the work expresses grief despite the absence of the corresponding experience in the artist. The definition, by requiring that the artist have undergone the expressed emotion, fails to acknowledge the role of craft, convention, and technical mastery in the production of expressive art.

definition: A person P has good taste if and only if (i) P's aesthetic judgements agree with those of the majority of cultivated persons, (ii) P's judgements are consistent across relevantly similar cases, and (iii) P is able to articulate reasons in support of each judgement.
conditions: P's aesthetic judgements agree with those of the majority of cultivated persons
P's judgements are consistent across relevantly similar cases
P is able to articulate reasons in support of each judgement
counterexample: Consider a critic in the early nineteenth century who insists, contrary to the prevailing opinion of cultivated persons, that the late quartets of Beethoven are works of the highest genius. The majority of cultivated persons at the time regard these works as confused and intractable. The critic's judgements are consistent and she articulates penetrating reasons. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are met, but condition (i) is not. Yet posterity has vindicated the critic, and we should now say that she, rather than the majority, possessed good taste.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that good taste may on occasion outrun the prevailing consensus of cultivated persons, and that the mark of fine discrimination is sometimes the capacity to discern merit where the majority cannot yet perceive it.
passage: One might define good taste as the condition in which a person's aesthetic judgements agree with those of the majority of cultivated persons, are consistent across relevantly similar cases, and are supported by articulate reasons. This definition has the merit of anchoring taste in a community of informed opinion and of excluding the merely capricious. Yet one should hesitate to regard it as entirely satisfactory. A case which reveals the insufficiency of the proposal arises when we consider the reception of Beethoven's late string quartets. In the years following their composition, the majority of cultivated musical opinion regarded these works as baffling, incoherent, and perhaps the products of a mind disordered by deafness. A critic who insisted, against this consensus, that the quartets were works of supreme genius would have failed condition (i) of the definition. Yet her judgements might well have been consistent across the quartets and she might have articulated compelling reasons — attending to the harmonic daring, the structural innovation, and the depth of feeling in these works. Posterity has decisively vindicated such a critic. One might observe that the definition, by tying good taste to majority agreement, cannot accommodate the possibility that a person of fine discrimination may perceive merit where the cultivated majority, bound by the conventions of their period, cannot yet discern it.

definition: A work of art W is an imitation of a subject S if and only if (i) the artist intended W to represent S, (ii) a competent observer can recognise S in W, and (iii) the resemblance between W and S is achieved through the artist's skill in reproducing the sensory appearance of S.
conditions: The artist intended W to represent S
A competent observer can recognise S in W
The resemblance between W and S is achieved through the artist's skill in reproducing the sensory appearance of S
counterexample: Consider a caricature of a well-known statesman. The artist intends to represent the statesman, and a competent observer immediately recognises him in the drawing. Yet the resemblance is achieved not by reproducing the statesman's sensory appearance faithfully but by deliberately exaggerating certain features — the prominent nose, the heavy brow — whilst suppressing others. The caricature imitates the statesman by distortion rather than by faithful reproduction, and it is often a more telling likeness than a photographic reproduction would be.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow that imitation may be achieved through selective emphasis and deliberate distortion rather than through faithful reproduction of sensory appearance, and that such distortion may in fact produce a more penetrating representation than literal copying.
passage: It is tempting to characterise artistic imitation as the condition in which a work of art represents a subject through the artist's skill in reproducing its sensory appearance, such that a competent observer can recognise the subject. This account captures the central tradition of representational painting and sculpture, in which the artist's mastery is displayed in the fidelity of the likeness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when we consider the art of caricature. A gifted caricaturist who draws a well-known statesman intends to represent that individual, and a competent observer recognises the statesman at once — indeed, the recognition may be more immediate and more vivid than that produced by a faithful portrait. Yet the caricature achieves its effect not by reproducing the statesman's sensory appearance but by deliberately exaggerating certain features and suppressing others. The prominent nose is made enormous, the heavy brow is darkened and thickened, whilst the unremarkable chin is reduced to a mere suggestion. One might observe that the resulting image, though it departs conspicuously from the actual appearance of the subject, captures something essential about him that a photographic reproduction might miss. The definition, by requiring that resemblance be achieved through faithful reproduction of sensory appearance, cannot accommodate the possibility that distortion and selective emphasis may constitute a more penetrating form of imitation.

definition: An experience X is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) X is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude or power, (ii) X involves an initial feeling of being diminished or threatened, and (iii) X culminates in a feeling of exaltation or elevation.
conditions: X is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude or power
X involves an initial feeling of being diminished or threatened
X culminates in a feeling of exaltation or elevation
counterexample: Consider a listener who, upon hearing a passage of extraordinary stillness in a late Schubert sonata, experiences something she is strongly inclined to call sublime. The passage is not overwhelming in magnitude or power — it is, on the contrary, quiet, sparse, and intimate. She feels no threat or diminishment, but rather a profound and serene expansion of feeling that she describes as elevation. Conditions (ii) and, arguably, (i) are unsatisfied, yet the experience has the unmistakable character of the sublime.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the sublime may arise from experiences of profound stillness, intimacy, or austere simplicity, and not only from encounters with overwhelming magnitude or power.
passage: One might define an experience of the sublime as one that is occasioned by an object of overwhelming magnitude or power, involves an initial feeling of diminishment or threat, and culminates in a feeling of exaltation. This definition captures a familiar pattern — the traveller who stands before a vast mountain range, feels herself insignificant and imperilled, and then experiences an uplifting sense of the grandeur of nature. Yet one should hesitate to regard this as the only form the sublime may take. A case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition arises in the experience of certain musical passages of extraordinary stillness. Consider a listener who encounters a passage in a late Schubert sonata — one of those moments in which the music seems to withdraw into a silence more eloquent than sound. The passage is quiet, sparse, and intimate; it possesses no overwhelming magnitude or power. The listener feels no threat or diminishment. Yet she experiences something she is strongly inclined to call sublime: a profound, serene expansion of feeling, an elevation that is not preceded by any sense of danger or insignificance. It appears that the sublime may arise from encounters with the profoundly still and austere, and not only from confrontations with the vast and terrible. The definition, by insisting upon overwhelming magnitude and initial threat, excludes this gentler but no less genuine form of sublimity.

definition: A work of art W is original if and only if (i) W introduces formal or thematic elements not present in any prior work, (ii) these novel elements are the product of the artist's own creative activity rather than of accident or mechanical process, and (iii) the novel elements contribute significantly to the aesthetic value of W.
conditions: W introduces formal or thematic elements not present in any prior work
These novel elements are the product of the artist's own creative activity rather than accident or mechanical process
The novel elements contribute significantly to the aesthetic value of W
counterexample: Consider Bach's Art of the Fugue. Nearly every formal element it employs — fugal structure, inversion, augmentation, stretto — was well established in the contrapuntal tradition before Bach composed the work. One would be hard pressed to identify a single formal or thematic element in it that is not present in some prior composition. Yet the work is universally regarded as one of the most original achievements in the history of music, for it combines familiar elements with a depth, rigour, and expressive power that transforms them entirely.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that originality may consist not only in the introduction of wholly new elements but in the unprecedented combination, development, or transformation of existing elements, such that familiar materials are made to yield effects no prior work had achieved.
passage: One might propose that a work of art is original when it introduces formal or thematic elements not found in any prior work, when these elements are the product of the artist's creative activity rather than accident, and when they contribute significantly to the work's aesthetic value. This proposal captures something important about the originality of radical innovators — one thinks of the earliest cubist paintings, which introduced genuinely unprecedented formal elements. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account arises when one considers Bach's Art of the Fugue. Every formal element the work employs — fugal structure, inversion, augmentation, diminution, stretto, mirror counterpoint — was well established in the contrapuntal tradition long before Bach composed the work. One would be hard pressed to identify a single formal device in the Art of the Fugue that cannot be found in the works of earlier composers. Yet the work is universally regarded as one of the most original achievements in the history of Western music. Its originality lies not in the introduction of new elements but in the unprecedented depth, rigour, and expressive power with which familiar elements are combined and developed. One might observe that the definition, by requiring the introduction of elements not present in prior works, fails to accommodate the possibility that originality may consist in the transformative recombination of existing materials.

definition: A dramatic work D is a tragedy if and only if (i) D depicts the downfall of a protagonist of elevated social standing, (ii) the downfall is brought about by a flaw or error in the protagonist's character or judgement, and (iii) D arouses pity and fear in the audience.
conditions: D depicts the downfall of a protagonist of elevated social standing
The downfall is brought about by a flaw or error in the protagonist's character or judgement
D arouses pity and fear in the audience
counterexample: Consider Georg Buechner's Woyzeck. The protagonist is a common soldier of the lowest social rank — a barber who serves as a medical subject for a few coins. His downfall is brought about not by any flaw in his own character but by the cruelty and exploitation of those above him. The work arouses pity and fear with devastating power. It is universally regarded as a tragedy, yet conditions (i) and (ii) are both unsatisfied.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring elevated social standing and a personal flaw, thereby excluding tragedies whose protagonists are ordinary persons destroyed by social forces beyond their control.
passage: The traditional account holds that a dramatic work is a tragedy when it depicts the downfall of a protagonist of elevated standing, when the downfall results from a flaw or error in the protagonist's character, and when the work arouses pity and fear in the audience. This account captures the structure of the great Athenian tragedies admirably — Oedipus is a king, his downfall flows from his relentless pursuit of truth conjoined with the terrible facts of his origin, and the audience is moved to the deepest pity and fear. Yet one should hesitate to accept this account as universally adequate. A case which reveals its insufficiency is Georg Buechner's Woyzeck, a work of the early nineteenth century. Woyzeck is a common soldier of the very lowest social standing — a barber who submits to humiliating medical experiments for a pittance. His downfall is brought about not by any flaw in his own character or judgement but by the systematic cruelty and exploitation of those placed above him: the Doctor who uses him as an experimental subject, the Captain who mocks him, the Drum-Major who seduces his companion. The work arouses pity and fear with a power that few dramas of any period surpass. Yet neither condition (i) nor condition (ii) is satisfied. It appears that the definition, shaped by the conventions of Athenian tragedy, cannot accommodate the modern tragedy of the common man destroyed by social forces.

definition: A composition C exhibits harmony if and only if (i) the elements of C stand in relations of mutual proportion and balance, (ii) no element of C appears superfluous or deficient in relation to the whole, and (iii) the arrangement of elements in C produces a sense of repose or completeness in the observer.
conditions: The elements of C stand in relations of mutual proportion and balance
No element of C appears superfluous or deficient in relation to the whole
The arrangement of elements produces a sense of repose or completeness in the observer
counterexample: Consider a late painting by Cezanne in which the forms of the landscape are rendered in patches of colour that do not quite resolve into a stable spatial arrangement. The viewer's eye moves restlessly among the patches, finding no point of repose. Yet the painting is experienced as profoundly harmonious — each patch of colour is precisely the right hue and the right size, and nothing could be added or removed without disturbing the whole. Conditions (i) and (ii) are arguably met, yet condition (iii) — repose or completeness — is conspicuously absent, replaced by an energetic tension that is itself felt as a form of harmony.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that harmony may manifest as a dynamic tension or equilibrium among elements rather than as repose, and that the sense of rightness in a composition need not take the form of settled completeness.
passage: One might define harmony in a composition as the condition in which the elements stand in relations of mutual proportion and balance, no element appears superfluous or deficient, and the arrangement produces a sense of repose or completeness in the observer. This definition captures the harmony of a Greek temple or a Mozart symphony, in which every part seems to occupy its proper place and the whole rests in serene equilibrium. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the late landscapes of Cezanne. In these paintings, patches of colour are set beside one another in relations that do not quite resolve into a stable spatial arrangement. The viewer's eye moves restlessly among the colour patches, finding no fixed point of repose. The painting does not produce a sense of settled completeness. Yet one might observe that each patch is precisely the right hue, the right size, and in precisely the right position — nothing could be added or subtracted without disturbing the whole. The painting is experienced as deeply harmonious, but its harmony takes the form not of repose but of a dynamic tension, an equilibrium of forces that are held in balance without being resolved. It appears that the definition, by requiring repose or completeness, fails to accommodate the possibility that harmony may consist in an energetic and unresolved tension among perfectly calibrated elements.

definition: An artist A has a distinctive style if and only if (i) there exists a set of formal features that recur consistently across A's works, (ii) these recurring features are sufficiently unusual to distinguish A's works from those of other artists, and (iii) the recurring features are the result of deliberate artistic choices.
conditions: There exists a set of formal features that recur consistently across A's works
These recurring features are sufficiently unusual to distinguish A's works from those of other artists
The recurring features are the result of deliberate artistic choices
counterexample: Consider an ageing painter whose hand trembles involuntarily, producing a characteristic wavering line that pervades all her late work. This wavering line recurs consistently, and it distinguishes her late paintings from those of every other artist. Critics recognise her late works instantly by this feature. Yet the trembling line is not the result of a deliberate artistic choice but of a physical infirmity. Condition (iii) is unsatisfied, yet one might well say that the wavering line has become an integral part of her style.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that style may emerge from involuntary habits, physical conditions, or unconscious tendencies as well as from deliberate choice, provided the recurring features become aesthetically integrated into the body of work.
passage: It is natural to suppose that an artist has a distinctive style when a set of formal features recurs consistently across her works, when these features are unusual enough to distinguish her works from those of others, and when the features result from deliberate artistic choices. This account captures the style of a painter who consciously adopts a limited palette or an idiosyncratic brushwork, and it has the merit of tying style to artistic agency. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the phenomenon of involuntary stylistic features. An ageing painter whose hand trembles with increasing severity produces, in her late work, a characteristic wavering line that pervades every canvas. This trembling line recurs consistently across her late paintings and is sufficiently distinctive that critics and connoisseurs recognise her work at a glance. Yet the wavering quality is not the result of deliberate artistic choice — it is the consequence of a physical infirmity that the painter cannot control and might well prefer to be without. One might observe that, despite its involuntary origin, the trembling line has become aesthetically integrated into her work: it imparts a quality of fragility and urgency that deepens the expressive power of the late paintings. It appears that the definition, by requiring deliberate choice, fails to accommodate those cases in which style emerges from involuntary conditions that become, through their aesthetic consequences, genuinely stylistic.

definition: The form of a work of art W consists in the arrangement of its sensory elements if and only if (i) the sensory elements of W can be identified independently of the work's representational content, (ii) the arrangement is governed by principles internal to the medium, and (iii) the aesthetic value of W is determined primarily by this arrangement rather than by any external associations.
conditions: The sensory elements can be identified independently of the work's representational content
The arrangement is governed by principles internal to the medium
The aesthetic value is determined primarily by this arrangement rather than by external associations
counterexample: Consider a Renaissance altarpiece in which the formal arrangement of figures — the triangular composition, the balance of masses, the rhythm of gesture — is inseparable from the representational content. The triangular composition is the triangular grouping of the Madonna and saints; the balance of masses is the balance of their bodies. One cannot identify the sensory elements independently of the representational content, for the two are fused. Yet the formal properties of the altarpiece contribute decisively to its aesthetic value, and no competent critic would deny that the work possesses form.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that in many works of art, particularly representational ones, formal and representational elements are not separable, and that form may be constituted through, rather than independently of, the work's representational content.
passage: One might define the form of a work of art as the arrangement of its sensory elements, where these elements can be identified independently of representational content, the arrangement is governed by principles internal to the medium, and aesthetic value is determined primarily by this arrangement. This definition reflects the formalist tradition and captures something important about the appreciation of abstract painting and absolute music, where representational content is absent or negligible. Yet one should hesitate to accept it as a general account. A case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider a Renaissance altarpiece of the highest quality. The formal arrangement of the altarpiece — the triangular composition, the balance of masses, the rhythm of gesture and drapery — is inseparable from its representational content. The triangular composition is the triangular grouping of the Madonna and Child flanked by saints. The balance of masses is the balance of their bodies. The rhythm of gesture is the rhythm of their arms and hands. One cannot identify these sensory elements independently of what they represent, for the formal and the representational are thoroughly fused. Yet the formal properties of the altarpiece contribute decisively to its aesthetic value, and no competent critic would deny that the work possesses form of the highest order. It appears that the definition, by requiring the separability of sensory elements from representational content, fails to accommodate the pervasive fusion of form and content in representational art.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O produces in any normal observer a feeling of disinterested pleasure, (ii) the pleasure produced by O is occasioned by O's formal properties — its proportion, symmetry, or unity — rather than by its material content, and (iii) O is such that the pleasure it produces is accompanied by a sense of necessity, so that the observer judges that all others ought to share in the pleasure.
conditions: O produces in any normal observer a feeling of disinterested pleasure
The pleasure produced by O is occasioned by O's formal properties — its proportion, symmetry, or unity — rather than by its material content
O is such that the pleasure it produces is accompanied by a sense of necessity, so that the observer judges that all others ought to share in the pleasure
counterexample: Consider a sunset over the sea in which the colours are distributed in no discernible pattern and the forms possess neither symmetry nor unity. A normal observer may experience disinterested pleasure upon beholding it, and may feel that all others ought to share in that pleasure. Yet the pleasure is not occasioned by any formal property — it arises from the sheer intensity and variety of colour, which is material content rather than formal arrangement. All three conditions appear to be met only if we strain the notion of formal property beyond recognition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow that beauty may arise from sensory qualities — vivid colour, rich tone, or fragrance — independently of formal arrangement, and thus excludes cases in which material content itself is the source of aesthetic pleasure.
passage: One might attempt to analyse beauty as the capacity of an object to produce disinterested pleasure through its formal properties, accompanied by a sense of universal necessity. This proposal has deep roots in the tradition stemming from Kant and finds echoes in the writings of many subsequent philosophers. The requirement of disinterestedness excludes mere appetite; the requirement of formal occasion excludes mere sensory titillation; and the requirement of necessity elevates the judgement above private preference. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be found in the contemplation of certain natural phenomena. Consider a sunset in which the colours are distributed without discernible pattern — no symmetry, no proportion, no unity of design. The sky is a chaos of crimson, gold, and violet. A normal observer, setting aside all practical interest, experiences profound pleasure and feels that any person of sensibility ought to share it. The pleasure is genuine, disinterested, and felt as necessary. But it appears to be occasioned not by any formal property but by the sheer material intensity of the colours themselves. One might observe that the definition, by insisting upon formal properties as the sole legitimate occasion of aesthetic pleasure, cannot accommodate the beauty of phenomena whose power lies entirely in their sensory richness.

definition: An artefact A is a work of art if and only if (i) A is produced by a human agent with the intention of creating an object for aesthetic contemplation, (ii) A possesses at least one aesthetically relevant property — such as expressive quality, formal complexity, or representational content, and (iii) A is presented to a public as a candidate for appreciation.
conditions: A is produced by a human agent with the intention of creating an object for aesthetic contemplation
A possesses at least one aesthetically relevant property — such as expressive quality, formal complexity, or representational content
A is presented to a public as a candidate for appreciation
counterexample: Consider a piece of driftwood, shaped by the sea into a form of striking elegance, which is placed in a gallery by a curator who recognises its aesthetic merit. It possesses expressive quality and formal complexity. It is presented to a public as a candidate for appreciation. Yet it was not produced by a human agent with any intention whatever; it is a product of natural forces. All conditions save the first are satisfied, and yet many would wish to say that the driftwood, so displayed, functions as a work of art.
