missing_condition: The definition fails to account for cases in which an object not produced with artistic intention is nevertheless adopted into the category of art by an appropriate institutional or curatorial act, so that the origin of the object is less important than its mode of reception.
passage: A natural proposal for defining the work of art holds that it must be a human artefact produced with the intention of creating an object for aesthetic contemplation, that it must possess at least one aesthetically relevant property, and that it must be presented to a public. This characterisation excludes mere natural objects, mere tools, and private exercises, and it captures much of what we ordinarily mean by a work of art. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be constructed with little difficulty. Suppose that a piece of driftwood, sculpted by decades of tidal action into a form of remarkable elegance, is discovered on a beach and placed by a curator in a gallery of modern sculpture. The driftwood possesses expressive quality and formal complexity in abundance. It is presented to a public as a candidate for appreciation, and the public responds with genuine aesthetic engagement. Nevertheless, it was not produced by any human agent, let alone with the intention of creating an object for contemplation. It is a product of impersonal natural forces. One might observe that the definition, by requiring human intentional production, cannot accommodate the widespread practice of exhibiting natural objects as art — a practice that suggests the boundary between art and non-art is drawn not solely by the origin of the object but in part by its institutional reception and the manner in which it is offered for contemplation.

definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by an object or scene that overwhelms the senses by its magnitude or power, (ii) E involves a moment of displeasure or terror arising from the subject's sense of inadequacy before the object, and (iii) E is followed by a feeling of elevation in which the subject recognises the superiority of reason over sensory limitation.
conditions: E is occasioned by an object or scene that overwhelms the senses by its magnitude or power
E involves a moment of displeasure or terror arising from the subject's sense of inadequacy before the object
E is followed by a feeling of elevation in which the subject recognises the superiority of reason over sensory limitation
counterexample: Consider a mathematician who, upon grasping for the first time the proof that the real numbers are uncountable, experiences a profound sense of overwhelm — the concept exceeds anything the imagination can picture. There follows a moment of intellectual vertigo, a feeling of inadequacy before the infinite. And yet the experience culminates in exhilaration and a sense of rational triumph. All three conditions appear satisfied. But the experience is not occasioned by any sensory object or scene; it is entirely intellectual, arising from the contemplation of an abstract proof.
missing_condition: The definition restricts the sublime to experiences occasioned by sensory overwhelm and thereby excludes cases of intellectual sublimity in which the overwhelm arises from the contemplation of abstract concepts that exceed the imagination without engaging the senses at all.
passage: The received analysis of the sublime holds that it is an experience occasioned by objects of overwhelming magnitude or power, passing through a moment of displeasure or terror, and culminating in a feeling of elevation through the recognition of reason's superiority. This analysis, broadly Kantian in inspiration, has the merit of distinguishing the sublime from the merely beautiful and from the merely terrifying. Yet one might observe that it is too narrowly tied to sensory experience. Consider the case of a mathematician who encounters Cantor's diagonal argument for the first time. The concept of an infinity that exceeds all countable infinities overwhelms the imagination — no sensory image can represent it. The mathematician experiences a moment of intellectual vertigo, a sense of inadequacy before an idea that defies intuitive comprehension. And yet this moment gives way to exhilaration and a conviction that reason has penetrated beyond the limits of the imagination. All three conditions of the definition appear to be satisfied. Yet the experience is not occasioned by any sensory object or scene whatever; it arises entirely from the contemplation of an abstract mathematical truth. It appears that the definition, by requiring that the sublime be occasioned by objects that overwhelm the senses, cannot accommodate the intellectual sublime — those experiences in which it is the mind, rather than the eye or ear, that is overwhelmed.

definition: A person P possesses good taste if and only if (i) P's aesthetic judgements consistently accord with the judgements of the majority of cultivated observers, (ii) P is capable of articulating reasons for those judgements by citing specific features of the objects judged, and (iii) P's judgements remain stable over time and are not swayed by fashion or social pressure.
conditions: P's aesthetic judgements consistently accord with the judgements of the majority of cultivated observers
P is capable of articulating reasons for those judgements by citing specific features of the objects judged
P's judgements remain stable over time and are not swayed by fashion or social pressure
counterexample: Consider a critic in the early nineteenth century who, contrary to the prevailing opinion of cultivated observers, championed the poetry of John Donne — then widely dismissed as obscure and unmusical. The critic articulated detailed reasons, citing Donne's metaphysical conceits and dramatic immediacy. The critic's judgement remained stable across decades. Posterity vindicated the critic's assessment. Yet at the time, the critic's judgements did not accord with the majority of cultivated observers, and the definition would have denied that the critic possessed good taste.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for the possibility that good taste may be exercised in advance of communal recognition, so that a perceptive critic may discern merit where the majority of cultivated observers, influenced by the conventions of their age, do not.
passage: One might propose that good taste is constituted by consistent agreement with cultivated opinion, the capacity to articulate reasons by citing specific features, and stability of judgement over time. This proposal has intuitive appeal, for it distinguishes the person of taste from the philistine (who lacks agreement with cultivated opinion), the inarticulate enthusiast (who cannot give reasons), and the slave of fashion (whose judgements shift with the times). Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when we consider the early champions of artists later recognised as great. In the early nineteenth century, the metaphysical poetry of John Donne was widely dismissed by cultivated opinion as wilfully obscure and metrically perverse. A critic who championed Donne at that time — citing the originality of his conceits, the dramatic force of his openings, and the intellectual energy of his verse — would have failed the first condition, for cultivated opinion ran against Donne. Yet posterity has vindicated exactly such a critic. One might observe that the definition, by tying good taste to contemporary cultivated consensus, cannot accommodate the case of the prescient critic whose perceptions outstrip the conventions of the age. It appears that good taste may sometimes consist precisely in the capacity to discern merit where the majority of cultivated observers, constrained by period assumptions, do not.

definition: An experience E is a genuinely aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E is directed at an object attended to for its own sake rather than for any practical purpose, (ii) E involves heightened perceptual attention to the sensory and formal qualities of the object, and (iii) E is affectively charged — that is, it is accompanied by feelings of pleasure, displeasure, or emotional engagement.
conditions: E is directed at an object attended to for its own sake rather than for any practical purpose
E involves heightened perceptual attention to the sensory and formal qualities of the object
E is affectively charged — that is, it is accompanied by feelings of pleasure, displeasure, or emotional engagement
counterexample: Consider a wine-taster at a competition who attends with exquisite care to the colour, aroma, body, and finish of each wine, experiencing intense pleasure or displeasure, and directing attention to the wine for its own sake during the tasting. All three conditions are met. Yet the taster's purpose is to rank the wines for a commercial competition, and many philosophers would hesitate to classify the experience as aesthetic, regarding it instead as a refined exercise of practical discrimination.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish aesthetic experience from highly attentive sensory discrimination exercised within a practical or commercial framework, for one may attend to an object for its own sake during the moment of perception whilst being embedded in a fundamentally non-aesthetic context.
passage: It is natural to characterise aesthetic experience as attention directed at an object for its own sake, involving heightened perception of its sensory and formal qualities, and accompanied by affective engagement. This account distinguishes the aesthetic attitude from the practical, the scientific, and the merely casual. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the domain of professional sensory evaluation. Consider a wine-taster at a competitive tasting who attends with minute care to the colour, bouquet, and structure of each wine. The attention is exquisitely heightened. The experience is richly affective — certain wines occasion intense pleasure, others mild displeasure. And during the moment of tasting, the wine-taster is plausibly attending to the wine for its own sake, absorbed in its qualities rather than in any extrinsic end. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet one might observe that the context is fundamentally commercial: the taster's ultimate purpose is to rank wines for a competition, and the refined perceptual skills are exercised as instruments of professional judgement. It appears that the definition, by specifying only the phenomenological features of the experience — disinterested attention, heightened perception, affective charge — cannot distinguish genuinely aesthetic experience from the refined practical discrimination of the connoisseur whose perceptual engagement, however intense, is embedded in a non-aesthetic institutional framework.

definition: A painting P represents a subject S if and only if (i) P resembles S in respect of shape, colour, or spatial arrangement, (ii) the artist who produced P intended that P depict S, and (iii) a competent viewer, acquainted with the relevant pictorial conventions, would recognise S in P.
conditions: P resembles S in respect of shape, colour, or spatial arrangement
The artist who produced P intended that P depict S
A competent viewer, acquainted with the relevant pictorial conventions, would recognise S in P
counterexample: Consider a highly abstract painting by an artist who intends to depict the emotion of grief. The painting employs slashing black forms and muted grey tones. A competent viewer, acquainted with the artist's oeuvre and the conventions of abstract expressionism, recognises the painting as a depiction of grief. Yet the painting does not resemble grief in shape, colour, or spatial arrangement — grief has no shape or colour. The first condition is not satisfied, yet we might wish to say that the painting genuinely represents grief.
missing_condition: The definition requires resemblance in perceptible properties and thereby cannot accommodate the representation of abstract states, emotions, or concepts that possess no perceptible shape, colour, or spatial arrangement to be resembled.
passage: A natural analysis of pictorial representation holds that a painting represents its subject by resembling it in shape, colour, or spatial arrangement, being intended by the artist to depict it, and being recognisable to a competent viewer. This analysis accords well with representational painting in the Western tradition: a portrait resembles its sitter, the artist intended to depict the sitter, and any competent viewer recognises the likeness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises in the domain of abstract painting. Suppose an artist produces a canvas of slashing black forms against a ground of muted grey, intending thereby to depict the emotion of grief. A viewer conversant with the conventions of abstract expressionism, and familiar with the artist's work, may readily recognise the painting as a depiction of grief. The intention is present. The recognition is present. But the resemblance is not, for grief is not the sort of thing that possesses shape, colour, or spatial arrangement. One might observe that the definition, by requiring resemblance in perceptible properties, restricts representation to objects that themselves have perceptible properties. It thereby excludes the representation of abstract states, emotions, and concepts — precisely the domain in which much of the most ambitious painting of the twentieth century has operated.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) the artist who created W experienced E during the process of creation, (ii) W is so fashioned as to communicate E to an appropriately sensitive audience, and (iii) the audience's experience of E upon engaging with W is caused by the properties of W rather than by extraneous associations.
conditions: The artist who created W experienced E during the process of creation
W is so fashioned as to communicate E to an appropriately sensitive audience
The audience's experience of E upon engaging with W is caused by the properties of W rather than by extraneous associations
counterexample: Consider a film composer who, working under a tight deadline and feeling only professional detachment, composes a score of profound melancholy for a scene of bereavement. The score communicates grief to every sensitive listener, and the grief experienced by the audience is caused by the harmonic and melodic properties of the music rather than by any extraneous association. Yet the composer experienced no melancholy whatever during the composition; the emotion was crafted by technique, not felt. The second and third conditions are met, but the first is not.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the artist have experienced the expressed emotion during creation, and thereby cannot account for cases in which consummate technique enables an artist to express an emotion without having felt it, as when a skilled composer crafts music of profound sadness whilst in a state of emotional neutrality.
passage: One might analyse artistic expression as follows: a work expresses an emotion if the artist experienced that emotion during creation, the work communicates the emotion to a sensitive audience, and the audience's response is caused by the work's own properties. This analysis captures an intuitive connection between the artist's inner life and the expressive character of the work. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the practice of professional composition. Consider a film composer who, working under deadline pressure and feeling nothing but professional concentration, produces a score of deep melancholy to accompany a scene of bereavement. The music employs minor harmonies, descending melodic lines, and a slow tempo — properties that communicate grief to any sensitive listener. The audience's experience of grief is manifestly caused by these musical properties, not by extraneous association. Yet the composer felt no melancholy during the composition. The emotion was conjured by technique, by a mastery of the conventions that link musical structures to affective responses. One might observe that the definition, by requiring the artist to have experienced the emotion, conflates expression with self-expression. It cannot accommodate the common and artistically legitimate case in which a craftsman, through skill and convention, fashions a work that expresses an emotion the craftsman does not feel.

definition: An aesthetic judgement J is objective if and only if (i) J ascribes a property to an object that the object possesses independently of any observer's response, (ii) J can in principle be confirmed or disconfirmed by appeal to the object's observable features, and (iii) competent observers who attend carefully to the object will converge upon J.
conditions: J ascribes a property to an object that the object possesses independently of any observer's response
J can in principle be confirmed or disconfirmed by appeal to the object's observable features
Competent observers who attend carefully to the object will converge upon J
counterexample: Consider the judgement that a particular passage of atonal music is graceful. Competent listeners — trained musicians with extensive experience of twentieth-century music — may attend carefully to the passage and converge upon this judgement. The judgement cites an observable feature, namely the fluency and economy of the voice-leading. Yet it appears doubtful that gracefulness is a property the passage possesses independently of any observer's response; it seems rather to be constituted in part by the listener's trained perceptual habits and cultural expectations. All three conditions are formally met, but the objectivity secured is of a different kind from that enjoyed by judgements about the passage's pitch-class content.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between properties that are genuinely observer-independent and those whose apparent objectivity is an artefact of convergence among observers who share a common training and cultural background, so that socially constituted agreement is mistaken for mind-independent fact.
passage: One might attempt to secure the objectivity of aesthetic judgements by requiring that such a judgement ascribe an observer-independent property to the object, that it be confirmable by appeal to observable features, and that competent observers converge upon it. This analysis promises to put aesthetic judgements on a footing comparable to perceptual judgements about shape or colour. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider judgements about the grace or elegance of musical passages. Suppose that a group of trained musicians, all with extensive experience of twentieth-century music, attend carefully to a passage of atonal composition and converge upon the judgement that it is graceful, citing the fluency of its voice-leading and the economy of its motivic development. All three conditions are satisfied: the judgement cites observable features, the observers converge, and gracefulness is ascribed as a property of the passage. Yet one might observe that gracefulness, unlike pitch-class content, does not seem to be a property that the passage possesses wholly independently of any observer's trained perceptual habits. Listeners from a tradition unfamiliar with atonal music might find no grace whatever in the same passage. It appears that the definition, by treating convergence among competent observers as sufficient for objectivity, cannot distinguish genuine observer-independence from the socially constituted agreement of observers who share a training and a set of cultural expectations.

definition: A natural scene N is beautiful if and only if (i) N presents an appearance of purposive design — an arrangement that looks as though it were fashioned for aesthetic effect, (ii) N is apprehended without the mediation of concepts drawn from natural science, and (iii) the pleasure occasioned by N is free from any admixture of moral or practical interest.
conditions: N presents an appearance of purposive design — an arrangement that looks as though it were fashioned for aesthetic effect
N is apprehended without the mediation of concepts drawn from natural science
The pleasure occasioned by N is free from any admixture of moral or practical interest
counterexample: Consider a naturalist who, understanding the physics of light refraction, observes a rainbow and experiences pure aesthetic pleasure. The naturalist's apprehension of the rainbow is mediated by scientific concepts — the naturalist sees the rainbow as a refraction phenomenon — yet this scientific understanding arguably deepens rather than diminishes the aesthetic pleasure. The scene presents an appearance of purposive design, and the pleasure is free from practical interest. Conditions (i) and (iii) are met, but condition (ii) is violated, even though the experience seems unambiguously one of beauty.
missing_condition: The definition excludes cases in which scientific understanding enhances rather than impedes aesthetic appreciation, thereby assuming a rigid separation between scientific cognition and aesthetic perception that does not hold in practice.
passage: A proposal with Kantian ancestry holds that natural beauty requires the appearance of purposive design, apprehension free from scientific concepts, and pleasure unmixed with practical interest. This proposal aims to locate natural beauty in a distinctive mode of perception — one that is simultaneously purposive and concept-free. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when we consider the experience of informed naturalists. Suppose that a naturalist who understands the physics of light refraction observes a rainbow spanning a valley after a storm. The naturalist sees the rainbow not merely as a band of colours but as the product of refraction and internal reflection in suspended water droplets. The apprehension is thoroughly mediated by scientific concepts. Yet the naturalist experiences pure aesthetic pleasure — pleasure free from any practical or moral interest — and the rainbow presents an appearance of exquisite purposive design. Indeed, the naturalist may report that understanding the physics deepens the beauty, for it reveals the elegant simplicity of the mechanism that produces so striking an appearance. It appears that the definition, by requiring that natural beauty be apprehended without the mediation of scientific concepts, assumes a rigid partition between scientific and aesthetic cognition. One might observe that this partition does not hold in practice, and that some of the most intense experiences of natural beauty are enjoyed by those whose scientific understanding informs and enriches their perception.

definition: An interpretation I of a work of art W is correct if and only if (i) I is consistent with all the observable features of W, (ii) I accords with the intentions of the artist as recoverable from documentary evidence, and (iii) I renders W a more coherent and aesthetically rewarding whole than any rival interpretation.
conditions: I is consistent with all the observable features of W
I accords with the intentions of the artist as recoverable from documentary evidence
I renders W a more coherent and aesthetically rewarding whole than any rival interpretation
counterexample: Consider a novel whose author, in private correspondence, stated that the central character is meant to embody cowardice. A critic offers an interpretation on which the character embodies moral caution — an interpretation consistent with every observable feature of the text and one that renders the novel a richer and more coherent work than the author's own reading. All three conditions cannot be simultaneously satisfied: the interpretation that maximises coherence and aesthetic reward conflicts with the author's documented intention. The definition provides no principled way of resolving the conflict.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify what is to be done when the demands of authorial intention and those of aesthetic coherence pull in opposite directions, and thereby presupposes a harmony between intentional and aesthetic criteria that may not obtain.
passage: One might propose that a correct interpretation of a work of art must be consistent with its observable features, accord with the artist's recoverable intentions, and render the work maximally coherent and rewarding. This proposal has the merit of combining textual fidelity, historical responsibility, and aesthetic sensitivity. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when these criteria conflict. Suppose that a novelist, in private correspondence preserved in an archive, states unambiguously that the central character of a certain novel is intended to embody cowardice. A critic, attending to every detail of the text, offers an interpretation on which the character embodies not cowardice but moral caution — a reading that accounts for every observable feature of the novel and that renders it a richer, more nuanced, and more coherent work than the author's own reading permits. The critic's interpretation satisfies conditions (i) and (iii) but violates condition (ii). The author's reading satisfies (i) and (ii) but is inferior on (iii). The definition requires all three conditions to be met simultaneously, yet in this case no interpretation can satisfy all three. One might observe that the definition presupposes a harmony between the demands of authorial intention and those of aesthetic coherence — a harmony that, as literary practice abundantly demonstrates, may not obtain.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O possesses a sensible form that produces pleasure in a suitably attentive observer, (ii) the pleasure arises from the perception of the form itself rather than from any desire the observer has for the object, and (iii) the observer's pleasure is such that it can be communicated to and shared by other suitably attentive observers.
conditions: O possesses a sensible form that produces pleasure in a suitably attentive observer
The pleasure arises from the perception of the form itself rather than from any desire the observer has for the object
The observer's pleasure is such that it can be communicated to and shared by other suitably attentive observers
counterexample: Consider an observer who contemplates a mathematical proof of exquisite concision. The proof possesses a form that produces pleasure. The pleasure is disinterested, arising from the perception of the proof's structure rather than from any desire to use it. And the pleasure can be communicated to other mathematically trained observers, who will share it. Yet we should hesitate to call a mathematical proof beautiful in the same sense in which we call a landscape or a sonata beautiful, for the proof lacks any sensible form whatever — its apprehension is purely intellectual.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the form apprehended be given through sensory perception rather than through purely intellectual apprehension, thereby admitting objects whose 'beauty' is merely metaphorical.
passage: One might propose that an object is beautiful if and only if it possesses a sensible form producing disinterested pleasure that can be shared among suitably attentive observers. This proposal captures three features long thought essential to beauty: formal organisation, disinterestedness, and communicability. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from the practice of mathematics. Suppose a mathematician contemplates a proof of singular elegance — say, Euclid's demonstration that the prime numbers are without limit. The proof possesses a definite form: the reductio structure, the spare deployment of premises, the inevitability of the conclusion. The mathematician's pleasure is disinterested, arising not from any practical advantage the proof confers but from the apprehension of its internal organisation. And the pleasure is communicable: any competent mathematician, apprised of the proof, will share the delight. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet it appears strained to classify the proof as beautiful in the sense in which one calls a sunset or a fugue beautiful. The proof is apprehended by the intellect alone; it makes no appeal to the senses. What the definition omits is the requirement that beauty be given through sensory experience. One might observe that the word 'sensible' in condition (i) was intended to secure this, but it fails to do so, for one can speak of the 'form' of a proof without absurdity, and the pleasure the proof produces is genuine. The definition, by attending to form, disinterestedness, and communicability whilst neglecting the sensory character of aesthetic apprehension, admits cases that strain our ordinary use of the term.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) W possesses qualities that are experienced by a competent observer as corresponding to E, (ii) the artist who created W intended to convey E through those qualities, and (iii) a competent observer, attending to W, actually feels E or a recognisable analogue of E.
conditions: W possesses qualities that are experienced by a competent observer as corresponding to E
The artist who created W intended to convey E through those qualities
A competent observer, attending to W, actually feels E or a recognisable analogue of E
counterexample: Suppose a composer, intending to write a joyful scherzo, produces a piece that every competent listener experiences as profoundly melancholic. The listeners feel genuine sadness; the qualities of the music — its minor tonality, its slow harmonic rhythm — correspond to melancholy. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied with respect to melancholy. Yet the artist intended joy, so condition (ii) fails for melancholy and is satisfied only for joy. The definition thus yields the result that the work expresses joy — an emotion no one hears in it — rather than the melancholy everyone perceives.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow that a work may express an emotion the artist did not intend, provided the work's qualities are such that competent observers consistently perceive and feel that emotion; it gives the artist's intention an authority that the work's actual character may override.
passage: It is natural to suppose that artistic expression involves a convergence of the work's qualities, the artist's intention, and the observer's response. One might accordingly propose that a work expresses an emotion if and only if it possesses qualities corresponding to that emotion, the artist intended those qualities to convey it, and a competent observer actually feels it or something closely analogous. This tripartite definition has a satisfying symmetry: it anchors expression in the objective features of the work, the subjective purpose of the creator, and the affective experience of the audience. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises whenever the artist's intention and the work's character diverge. Suppose a composer sets out to write a joyful scherzo but, through some failure of craft or some unconscious influence, produces a piece in a minor key with a slow harmonic rhythm that every competent listener experiences as deeply melancholic. The listeners feel genuine sadness; the qualities of the music correspond unmistakably to melancholy. One might observe that conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied with respect to melancholy, but the artist intended joy, not sadness, so condition (ii) holds only for joy. The definition therefore declares that the work expresses joy — an emotion that no one who hears the music would attribute to it. It appears that the requirement of artistic intention, though plausible in the abstract, can produce absurd results when the work's qualities speak more plainly than the artist's purposes.

definition: A person P has good taste if and only if (i) P is disposed to take pleasure in objects that possess genuine aesthetic merit, (ii) P is disposed to withhold pleasure from objects that lack such merit, and (iii) P's dispositions are stable across variations in mood, fashion, and social pressure.
conditions: P is disposed to take pleasure in objects that possess genuine aesthetic merit
P is disposed to withhold pleasure from objects that lack such merit
P's dispositions are stable across variations in mood, fashion, and social pressure
counterexample: Consider a person whose dispositions perfectly track genuine aesthetic merit and who is entirely unmoved by fashion or social pressure. He takes pleasure in the finest paintings and withholds it from the mediocre. Yet his responses are purely mechanical: he has memorised a catalogue of approved works and simply consults it, feeling a conditioned satisfaction upon encountering a listed item and indifference upon encountering an unlisted one. He has never attended to the internal qualities of any work. All three conditions are satisfied, yet we should hesitate to attribute good taste to a person whose responses bear no relation to the perceptual or emotional engagement that taste seems to require.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P's pleasure arise from direct perceptual or emotional engagement with the aesthetic qualities of the object, rather than from some extrinsic mechanism such as rote memorisation of approved judgements.
passage: One might characterise good taste as a stable disposition to take pleasure in objects of genuine aesthetic merit and to withhold pleasure from those that lack it. This characterisation captures two features that have seemed essential to the concept: sensitivity to merit and resistance to the distortions of fashion and social influence. A person of good taste, on this account, is reliably pleased by the excellent and reliably unmoved by the mediocre, and this reliability persists across shifting moods and passing enthusiasms. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be imagined without great difficulty. Suppose a man has committed to memory a comprehensive catalogue of works acknowledged by competent critics to possess genuine merit. Upon encountering any work on the list, he feels a conditioned satisfaction; upon encountering any work not on the list, he feels indifference. His dispositions track merit perfectly. They are stable across variations in mood and fashion, for the catalogue does not change with the season. Every condition is met. Yet one might observe that this man has never truly attended to the colour of a painting, the phrasing of a melody, or the rhythm of a verse. His responses are, as it were, mediated entirely by the catalogue rather than by any engagement with the works themselves. It appears that the definition, by specifying only the extensional correctness and stability of the person's dispositions, cannot distinguish genuine taste from a merely mechanical surrogate.

definition: A work of art W is an imitation of a subject S if and only if (i) W reproduces the perceptible appearance of S with sufficient fidelity that a competent observer can recognise S in W, (ii) the artist intended W to represent S, and (iii) the medium of W is distinct from the medium in which S naturally exists.
conditions: W reproduces the perceptible appearance of S with sufficient fidelity that a competent observer can recognise S in W
The artist intended W to represent S
The medium of W is distinct from the medium in which S naturally exists
counterexample: Suppose a sculptor creates a trompe-l'oeil sculpture of a pile of bricks, using actual bricks arranged in exactly the configuration one might find on a building site. The work reproduces the appearance of a pile of bricks with perfect fidelity. The sculptor intended to represent a pile of bricks. Yet the medium of the work — bricks — is identical to the medium in which the subject naturally exists. Condition (iii) fails. The definition thus denies that this is an imitation, even though the work clearly represents its subject and was intended to do so.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate cases in which artistic imitation may employ the same medium as the subject, provided the work is presented within a context of artistic intention and framing that distinguishes it from a mere instance of the subject.
passage: One might propose that artistic imitation consists in reproducing the perceptible appearance of a subject with sufficient fidelity for recognition, where the artist intends the representation and the medium differs from that of the subject itself. The requirement of a distinct medium seems initially well-motivated: a painting imitates a landscape by rendering it in pigment rather than in earth and foliage, and a marble sculpture imitates the human form by rendering it in stone rather than in flesh. The distinctness of medium appears to be what separates the imitation from the thing imitated. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this condition arises in the practice of modern sculpture. Suppose an artist constructs a work consisting of actual bricks arranged precisely as they might be found on a building site. The appearance of a pile of bricks is reproduced with perfect fidelity — indeed, with a fidelity no painting could match. The artist intended the arrangement to represent a pile of bricks, to invite reflection upon the aesthetic character of ordinary objects. A competent observer recognises the subject immediately. Yet the medium is bricks, and the subject naturally exists in the medium of bricks. Condition (iii) therefore fails. The definition is compelled to deny that this is an imitation, even though the work functions representationally in every respect that matters. It appears that what distinguishes artistic imitation from mere instantiation is not a difference of medium but a difference of context and intention — the work is framed as art, presented for contemplation, and thereby separated from the ordinary objects it imitates.

definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by an object or phenomenon that exceeds the observer's capacity for immediate comprehension, (ii) E involves a felt tension between attraction and awe or dread, and (iii) E culminates in an elevating sense of the observer's own rational or moral superiority over the overwhelming object.
conditions: E is occasioned by an object or phenomenon that exceeds the observer's capacity for immediate comprehension
E involves a felt tension between attraction and awe or dread
E culminates in an elevating sense of the observer's own rational or moral superiority over the overwhelming object
counterexample: Suppose a person stands at the edge of an active volcano. The eruption exceeds her capacity for immediate comprehension. She feels a profound tension between attraction and dread. Yet the experience does not culminate in any sense of her own superiority: she feels only diminishment, a complete overwhelm from which no elevating resolution emerges. All she retains is the memory of terrified fascination. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met, but condition (iii) fails, even though most observers would recognise her experience as paradigmatically sublime.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that an experience of the sublime may consist in the overwhelm itself, without any elevating resolution, and that the felt insignificance of the observer before an incomprehensible phenomenon can itself constitute sublimity.
passage: It has been a common view that the experience of the sublime involves three moments: an initial overwhelm occasioned by something that exceeds our comprehension, a felt tension between attraction and dread, and a culminating elevation in which we recognise our own rational or moral superiority over the object that overwhelms us. This three-stage account captures a great deal of what the tradition has associated with sublimity. The vast mountain range, the stormy sea, the starry sky — all occasion an initial failure of comprehension, a mingling of fascination and fear, and, on this account, a concluding sense that we are greater than the spectacle because we can think it, even if we cannot contain it in perception. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers experiences that satisfy the first two conditions without producing any elevation whatever. Suppose a person stands at the rim of an active volcano and watches the eruption at close quarters. The phenomenon exceeds her capacity for immediate comprehension. She feels a powerful tension between attraction and dread. Yet no elevating resolution arrives. She does not feel her rational nature assert itself over the spectacle; she feels only smallness, a thoroughgoing diminishment from which she does not recover even upon reflection. One might observe that her experience is nonetheless paradigmatically sublime — more so, perhaps, than the safely distanced contemplation of a mountain vista. It appears that the definition, by requiring an elevating conclusion, excludes the most overwhelming instances of the very phenomenon it seeks to characterise.

definition: A work of art W is original if and only if (i) W is not a copy or close imitation of any prior work, (ii) W introduces some element — of form, technique, or subject — that has no precedent in the tradition to which W belongs, and (iii) the novel element in W is the product of the artist's own inventive activity rather than of accident.
conditions: W is not a copy or close imitation of any prior work
W introduces some element of form, technique, or subject that has no precedent in the tradition to which W belongs
The novel element in W is the product of the artist's own inventive activity rather than of accident
counterexample: Suppose a painter, working in complete ignorance of the Impressionist movement, independently develops a technique of applying pigment in small, distinct strokes to capture the effect of light. Her work is not a copy of any Impressionist painting. Her technique has no precedent in the tradition as she understands it. And the technique is the product of her own inventive activity. Yet the technique does have a precedent in the broader tradition, one she simply failed to encounter. Condition (ii) requires that the element have 'no precedent in the tradition to which W belongs,' and it appears arbitrary whether her ignorance of Impressionism suffices to place her work outside that tradition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify whether precedent is to be assessed relative to the tradition as the artist understands it or as it objectively exists, leaving the concept of originality hostage to the accidents of the artist's acquaintance with prior work.
passage: One might define originality as the introduction, through the artist's own inventive activity, of an unprecedented element of form, technique, or subject, where the work is not a copy of any prior work. This definition aims to distinguish the genuinely original from the merely novel and from the accidentally new. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be imagined as follows. Suppose a painter, working in a provincial town in the late nineteenth century, develops entirely on her own initiative a technique of applying pigment in small, separate strokes to capture the transient effects of natural light. She has never seen an Impressionist painting, never read an account of the movement, never heard the name Monet. Her work is not a copy of any prior work. The technique, as far as she is concerned, has no precedent whatever. And it is plainly the product of her own inventive activity. Every condition of the definition is satisfied. Yet the technique does have a precedent in the broader artistic tradition — it was developed independently by a group of Parisian painters some decades earlier. Whether we call her work original thus depends upon how we delimit the 'tradition to which W belongs.' If we define the tradition broadly to include all of Western painting, her work is not original in the relevant sense. If we define it narrowly to include only the artistic practice she has encountered, it is original. The definition, by leaving this question open, renders the attribution of originality indeterminate in precisely the cases where it most needs to be settled.

definition: A dramatic work W is a tragedy if and only if (i) W depicts the downfall of a protagonist of elevated station, (ii) the downfall arises from a flaw or error in the protagonist's own character or judgement, and (iii) the audience's response to the downfall involves pity for the protagonist and fear that a similar fate could befall anyone.
conditions: W depicts the downfall of a protagonist of elevated station
The downfall arises from a flaw or error in the protagonist's own character or judgement
The audience's response to the downfall involves pity for the protagonist and fear that a similar fate could befall anyone
counterexample: Consider Arthur Miller's Death of a Salesman. The protagonist, Willy Loman, is not a person of elevated station: he is an ordinary travelling salesman. His downfall involves pity and fear, and it arises from flaws in his character. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are met, but condition (i) fails. Yet the play is widely and reasonably regarded as a tragedy. It appears that elevated station is not essential to tragedy, even though many paradigmatic tragedies happen to feature such protagonists.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow that a protagonist of ordinary station may be the subject of genuine tragedy, provided the magnitude of the downfall and the quality of the audience's response are sufficient to sustain the tragic effect.
passage: One might propose that tragedy is a dramatic form defined by three conditions: the protagonist must be of elevated station, the downfall must arise from the protagonist's own flaw or error, and the audience must respond with pity and fear. This proposal codifies a venerable tradition stretching back to Aristotle's remarks in the Poetics and has the merit of accounting for a large range of paradigmatic cases — Oedipus, Macbeth, Lear. The requirement of elevated station ensures that the fall is from a great height; the requirement of a flaw or error ensures that the fall is intelligible rather than merely accidental; and the requirement of pity and fear ensures that the audience is engaged in the morally and emotionally distinctive way that tragedy demands. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is provided by modern dramatic practice. Consider a play depicting the decline of an ordinary travelling salesman who, possessing no distinction of rank whatever, is destroyed by his own delusions and the hollowness of the commercial values he has absorbed. The audience feels pity for him and fears that his fate is not remote from anyone's possible experience. The flaw condition is satisfied. The affective condition is satisfied. But the protagonist is not of elevated station, and the definition therefore denies the work the title of tragedy. One might observe that this denial conflicts with the settled judgement of both critics and audiences, who recognise in the salesman's story the essential character of the tragic.

definition: A composition C exhibits harmony if and only if (i) the parts of C stand in relations of mutual proportion and balance, (ii) no part of C appears superfluous or deficient in relation to the whole, and (iii) the relations among the parts produce in a competent observer a sense of completeness and repose.
conditions: The parts of C stand in relations of mutual proportion and balance
No part of C appears superfluous or deficient in relation to the whole
The relations among the parts produce in a competent observer a sense of completeness and repose
counterexample: Consider a Bach fugue in which a dissonant passage — a stretto with clashing entries — is introduced precisely to create tension before its resolution. During the stretto, the parts do not stand in relations of mutual proportion and balance; the clashing entries produce not repose but agitation. Yet the fugue as a whole is paradigmatically harmonious. If we assess harmony at the level of the passage, the definition excludes it; if we assess it at the level of the whole, the dissonant passage is not superfluous but essential to the harmony it ultimately produces.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the role of localised dissonance and tension in producing large-scale harmony, and thereby cannot account for the fact that a harmonious whole may require parts that are, considered in isolation, inharmonious.
passage: One might characterise harmony as the mutual proportion and balance of parts, where no part is superfluous or deficient and the whole produces in a competent observer a sense of completeness and repose. This characterisation has an ancient pedigree and captures an important feature of many works that we call harmonious. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the analysis of musical composition. Consider a fugue by Bach in which the composer introduces a stretto — a passage in which the subject entries overlap and clash, producing momentary dissonance. During this passage, the parts do not stand in relations of mutual proportion and balance; the entries are deliberately misaligned. Nor does the passage produce repose; it produces agitation and tension. Condition (i) fails for the passage, and condition (iii) fails as well. Yet the fugue as a whole is among the most harmonious compositions in the Western repertoire. One might observe that the stretto is essential to the harmony of the whole: without the tension it creates, the subsequent resolution would lack its power, and the fugue would be merely pleasant rather than profoundly harmonious. It appears that the definition, by requiring that all parts stand in relations of proportion and balance, cannot account for the paradox that harmony sometimes requires, and is even constituted by, the incorporation and resolution of local dissonance.

definition: An artist A has a distinctive style if and only if (i) there exists a set of recurring formal features — of line, colour, rhythm, or structure — that are present across A's body of work, (ii) these features are sufficiently consistent that a competent observer can identify A's work by them alone, and (iii) the features are the result of A's deliberate artistic choices rather than of external constraint or convention.
conditions: There exists a set of recurring formal features present across A's body of work
These features are sufficiently consistent that a competent observer can identify A's work by them alone
The features are the result of A's deliberate artistic choices rather than of external constraint or convention
counterexample: Suppose a medieval icon painter works under strict ecclesiastical rules prescribing the size of figures, the arrangement of faces, the palette of colours, and the use of gold leaf. His works display a set of recurring formal features — the elongated faces, the rigid frontality, the prescribed colour scheme — that are sufficiently consistent for a competent observer to identify them as belonging to the Byzantine tradition. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. But the features are the result of external ecclesiastical constraint, not of the painter's deliberate artistic choices, so condition (iii) fails. Yet the painter may still have a recognisable personal style within those constraints — a particular treatment of drapery folds, a subtlety of expression — that the definition cannot capture because it treats constraint and choice as mutually exclusive.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that style may emerge from the manner in which an artist works within, and subtly transforms, externally imposed constraints, rather than requiring that the defining features be entirely free of such constraints.
passage: One might propose that a distinctive artistic style consists in a set of recurring formal features that permit identification and that arise from deliberate artistic choice rather than from external constraint. This proposal separates genuine personal style from the mere adherence to convention or the obedience to rule. An artist has a style, on this view, only when the identifying features of her work are freely chosen. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this requirement may be found in the history of Byzantine icon painting. The icon painters of the medieval period worked under exacting ecclesiastical prescriptions: the proportions of figures, the arrangement of faces, the permissible colours, and the use of gold were all determined by rule. A competent observer can readily identify a body of work as Byzantine by these recurring features. Conditions (i) and (ii) are plainly satisfied. But condition (iii) fails, since the features are imposed from without. The definition is therefore compelled to deny that these painters had a distinctive style. Yet one might observe that within the constraints of the ecclesiastical programme, individual painters developed recognisable manners — a characteristic treatment of drapery, a particular quality of facial expression, a distinctive approach to the modulation of colour within the prescribed palette. It appears that style is not opposed to constraint but may emerge precisely from the way an artist inhabits and subtly transforms the constraints under which she works. The definition, by requiring that the relevant features be entirely the product of free choice, excludes a large and important class of cases.

definition: An artefact A is kitsch if and only if (i) A is designed to produce an immediate and effortless emotional response in the observer, (ii) A achieves this effect by employing conventional symbols and familiar sentimental associations rather than by presenting the observer with a genuine aesthetic challenge, and (iii) the emotional response A produces is shallow in the sense that it does not survive reflective scrutiny.
conditions: A is designed to produce an immediate and effortless emotional response in the observer
A achieves this effect by employing conventional symbols and familiar sentimental associations
The emotional response A produces is shallow in the sense that it does not survive reflective scrutiny
counterexample: Consider a greeting card bearing an image of a sunset over a calm sea, accompanied by a reassuring platitude. It is designed for immediate emotional effect. It employs conventional symbols — the sunset, the tranquil water — and familiar sentimental associations. The response it produces does not survive reflective scrutiny. All three conditions are met, and we rightly call it kitsch. But now consider a work by a sophisticated artist who deliberately employs the imagery of kitsch — the same sunset, the same calm sea — in order to produce a complex critical reflection upon sentimentality itself. The work satisfies conditions (i) and (ii), but the emotional response it produces does survive scrutiny, because it is ironic and self-aware. Condition (iii) fails. Yet many critics would still classify the work as kitsch, albeit of a self-conscious variety.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the possibility that an artefact may be kitsch in virtue of the materials and strategies it employs, independently of whether the emotional response it produces survives reflective scrutiny, so that even ironic or self-conscious deployments of kitsch conventions remain, in a relevant sense, kitsch.
passage: One might define kitsch as an artefact designed to produce an immediate and effortless emotional response through the use of conventional symbols and familiar sentimental associations, where the response is shallow in the sense that it does not survive reflective scrutiny. This definition has the merit of capturing the central cases: the mass-produced figurine, the sentimental postcard, the formulaic popular song. These are designed for easy emotion, they achieve it through convention rather than challenge, and the emotion they produce evaporates upon reflection. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises in the practice of certain modern artists who deliberately appropriate the materials and strategies of kitsch for critical or ironic purposes. Suppose an artist creates a large-scale painting that employs, with perfect fidelity, the imagery of a greeting card — a sunset over a tranquil sea, rendered in the vivid, uncomplicated colours of commercial illustration. The work is designed to produce an immediate emotional response. It employs conventional symbols and familiar associations. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. Yet the response the work produces in a competent observer is not shallow: it is layered, ironic, and critically engaged with the very sentimentality it appears to endorse. The response survives, and is even deepened by, reflective scrutiny. Condition (iii) therefore fails. The definition is compelled to deny that the work is kitsch. Yet one might observe that there is a clear sense in which it remains kitsch — it is made of the stuff of kitsch, and its critical power depends upon its remaining recognisably so.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O exhibits a unity of diverse elements arranged in a harmonious whole, (ii) the perception of this unity produces in a disinterested observer a feeling of pleasure, and (iii) the pleasure so produced is immediate, arising upon perception rather than upon reflection or inference.
conditions: O exhibits a unity of diverse elements arranged in a harmonious whole
The perception of this unity produces in a disinterested observer a feeling of pleasure
The pleasure so produced is immediate, arising upon perception rather than upon reflection or inference
counterexample: Consider a Gothic cathedral whose facade exhibits a profusion of flying buttresses, pointed arches, gargoyles, and ornamental tracery. On first encounter the observer is bewildered rather than pleased; the diversity of elements overwhelms the eye. Only after extended study does the observer come to perceive the underlying structural logic that unifies these elements. The pleasure, when it arrives, is genuine and disinterested, and the unity is real. Yet the pleasure is not immediate but arises upon reflection, violating condition (iii), even though most would regard the cathedral as a paradigm of architectural beauty.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that some forms of beauty reveal themselves only upon sustained contemplation, requiring the observer to discern unity through reflection rather than grasping it immediately upon perception.
passage: One might propose that beauty consists in the harmonious unity of diverse elements, perceived with immediate disinterested pleasure. This characterisation captures a long-standing intuition: that beautiful objects integrate variety into coherence, and that the recognition of this coherence is spontaneous rather than laboured. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from architecture. Consider a visitor who enters Chartres Cathedral for the first time. The facade presents a vast array of sculptural programmes, asymmetrical towers, and layered ornamental schemes spanning several centuries of construction. Far from producing immediate pleasure, the initial encounter may occasion confusion or even a species of visual fatigue. The diversity is real, and the unity is real, but the perception of how the diversity is unified requires sustained attention. The visitor must trace the proportional relationships among the nave, the transept, and the choir; must observe how the flying buttresses distribute weight so as to permit the enormous windows; must attend to the way the sculptural programme on the west portal anticipates themes developed in the interior. Only then does the pleasure arrive — genuine, disinterested, and deep. All three conditions of the definition are eventually satisfied, save that the pleasure is not immediate. It appears, then, that the definition excludes from the class of beautiful objects precisely those whose beauty demands patient contemplation. One might observe that the most profound experiences of beauty are often of just this character: the fugues of Bach, the late quartets of Beethoven, the prose of Henry James. If beauty can be the reward of effort rather than the gift of immediacy, the definition requires amendment.

definition: A person P possesses good taste if and only if (i) P reliably takes pleasure in objects that are genuinely excellent in their kind, (ii) P's judgements of excellence agree with those of other persons of recognised discernment, and (iii) P's pleasure and judgements are not influenced by fashion, social pressure, or personal interest.
conditions: P reliably takes pleasure in objects that are genuinely excellent in their kind
P's judgements of excellence agree with those of other persons of recognised discernment
P's pleasure and judgements are not influenced by fashion, social pressure, or personal interest
counterexample: Consider a critic in 1850 who genuinely delights in the paintings of Vermeer, at a time when Vermeer is almost entirely forgotten and no persons of recognised discernment express any admiration for his work. The critic's pleasure is not influenced by fashion — indeed, it runs contrary to fashion. The critic's judgements later prove correct when Vermeer is rediscovered and universally admired. Yet at the time of the judgement, condition (ii) fails: no recognised authorities concur. The definition thus denies good taste to the very critic whose independent discernment most exemplifies it.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for the possibility that a person of genuinely good taste may be in advance of the consensus of recognised judges, recognising excellence before others do.
passage: One might characterise good taste as the reliable capacity to take pleasure in genuinely excellent objects, a capacity confirmed by agreement with other discerning persons and uncorrupted by fashion or interest. This definition has the merit of combining a subjective element — the pleasure of the individual — with an objective check — the consensus of the discerning. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from art history. Suppose a critic working in the mid-nineteenth century develops a deep admiration for the paintings of Johannes Vermeer. At that date Vermeer is all but unknown; his works have been misattributed to other artists, and no living authority regards him as a painter of the first rank. The critic's admiration is not a product of social pressure, for there is none; nor of fashion, for Vermeer is wholly unfashionable. It arises from a direct and sustained engagement with the paintings themselves — the luminosity, the compositional precision, the extraordinary treatment of light upon surfaces. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied: the critic takes genuine pleasure in objects that are, as posterity will confirm, excellent in their kind, and this pleasure is entirely independent of external influence. But condition (ii) fails, for no recognised authorities share the critic's view. The definition therefore denies good taste to a person who possesses it in an especially striking degree. It appears that the definition, by requiring agreement with existing authorities, cannot accommodate the phenomenon of taste that leads rather than follows the consensus of the discerning.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) W was produced by an artist who experienced E during the creation of W, (ii) the formal properties of W are such as to make E recognisable to a suitably sensitive audience, and (iii) W communicates E to that audience in virtue of those formal properties.
conditions: W was produced by an artist who experienced E during the creation of W
The formal properties of W are such as to make E recognisable to a suitably sensitive audience
W communicates E to that audience in virtue of those formal properties
counterexample: Consider a film composer who is commissioned to write music for a scene of profound grief. The composer is in excellent spirits throughout the composition — cheerful, well-rested, and thoroughly enjoying the technical challenge. The resulting score employs dark orchestral textures, descending chromatic lines, and sustained dissonances that communicate grief to every listener. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied, yet condition (i) fails entirely, for the composer experienced no grief whatever. Nevertheless, the music expresses grief as surely as any requiem composed in sorrow.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly requires that the artist experience the expressed emotion during composition, when in fact an artist may express an emotion through craft and technique alone, without undergoing the emotion at all.
passage: One might suppose that artistic expression requires a transfer of emotion from artist to audience: the artist feels, the work embodies, and the audience receives. This view has a long pedigree and considerable intuitive appeal. It explains why we speak of a painting as expressing anguish or a sonata as expressing joy — we imagine these emotions in the artist's breast, flowing through the medium into our own experience. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the practice of musical composition for dramatic purposes. Suppose a composer of film scores is asked to provide music for a scene depicting a mother's grief at the death of her child. The composer approaches the task with professional detachment and even a degree of relish at the technical difficulties involved. She selects a particular orchestral palette — low strings, solo oboe, muted brass — and constructs a harmonic progression that moves through a sequence of unresolved dissonances before settling, at last, on a bare open fifth. The result is music of devastating emotional power. Every listener recognises the grief it communicates; many are moved to tears. Yet the composer felt no grief during the act of creation. She felt the satisfaction of a craftsman solving a formal problem. If expression required the artist to undergo the expressed emotion, this music would fail to express grief. But it plainly does express grief. One might observe that what matters for expression is not the psychological state of the artist but the capacity of the formal properties to communicate emotion to a receptive audience. The definition, by insisting upon the artist's actual emotional experience, confuses the causal history of the work with its expressive character.

definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude or power, (ii) E involves a moment of displeasure or pain arising from the inadequacy of the subject's faculties to comprehend the object, and (iii) E resolves into a pleasure derived from the subject's awareness of possessing a rational nature that transcends mere sensory limitation.
conditions: E is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude or power
E involves a moment of displeasure or pain arising from the inadequacy of the subject's faculties to comprehend the object
E resolves into a pleasure derived from the subject's awareness of possessing a rational nature that transcends mere sensory limitation
counterexample: Consider a mountaineer standing at the summit of a Himalayan peak. The panorama is of overwhelming magnitude: ranges upon ranges stretching to the horizon, chasms of immense depth, clouds far below. The mountaineer experiences awe and exhilaration. Yet her response involves no moment of displeasure or felt inadequacy whatever; she does not feel her faculties overwhelmed but rather expanded and liberated. The pleasure is direct and unmediated by any prior pain. The experience is paradigmatically sublime, yet condition (ii) fails entirely.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate experiences of the sublime in which the subject is elevated and expanded without any intermediate moment of displeasure or felt inadequacy, thereby excluding the most direct and untroubled forms of sublime experience.
passage: One might characterise the sublime as a dialectical experience: first pain, occasioned by the overwhelming magnitude of the object, then pleasure, arising from the recognition that one's rational nature transcends what the senses cannot compass. This characterisation captures a genuine and important phenomenon — the experience of standing before a violent storm or contemplating the vastness of the starry heavens, in which one feels at once insignificant and exalted. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from the experience of the natural world. Suppose a mountaineer who has spent weeks ascending a Himalayan peak emerges at last upon the summit ridge. The view is of incomprehensible vastness — a sea of peaks extending in every direction, glaciers catching the light of the setting sun, the curvature of the earth faintly visible at the horizon. The mountaineer is seized by an emotion of extraordinary intensity. But this emotion contains no element of displeasure, no sense of the inadequacy of her faculties. She does not feel overwhelmed; she feels, on the contrary, that her perceptual and emotional capacities are operating at their fullest. The pleasure is immediate and entire, unmediated by any preceding pain. It appears that the definition, by requiring a dialectical movement from displeasure to pleasure, legislates a particular psychological structure onto all sublime experience. Yet one might observe that many of our most vivid encounters with sublime objects bypass this dialectic entirely. The mountaineer's experience is not less sublime for being untroubled; if anything, its directness and purity mark it as a limiting case of the very phenomenon the definition aims to capture.

definition: A work of art W is an imitation of a subject S if and only if (i) the artist who produced W intended to produce a likeness of S, (ii) W resembles S in respect of those features that are perceptually salient, and (iii) a competent observer, upon perceiving W, would recognise S as the subject represented.
conditions: The artist who produced W intended to produce a likeness of S
W resembles S in respect of those features that are perceptually salient
A competent observer, upon perceiving W, would recognise S as the subject represented
counterexample: Consider a caricaturist who draws a political figure with an enormously exaggerated nose, a tiny body, and wildly distorted proportions. Every competent observer immediately recognises the subject — condition (iii) is met — and the artist intended to depict that person — condition (i) is met. Yet the drawing does not resemble the subject in respect of perceptually salient features; on the contrary, it deliberately distorts those very features. Condition (ii) fails, and yet the caricature is plainly an imitation — indeed, a peculiarly effective one.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that imitation can proceed by selective exaggeration and distortion of features rather than by faithful resemblance, and that such distortion may actually enhance rather than diminish the recognisability of the subject.
passage: One might propose that artistic imitation consists in the intentional production of a likeness that resembles its subject in perceptually salient respects and is recognisable as such by a competent observer. This proposal unites three conditions that appear individually necessary: the artist's intention to depict, the objective resemblance between work and subject, and the observer's capacity to recognise what is depicted. Together they seem to distinguish genuine imitation from accidental resemblance, from abstract pattern-making, and from failed attempts at representation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the art of caricature. Suppose a skilled caricaturist undertakes to depict a well-known public figure. The resulting drawing presents the figure with a nose three times its actual size, a chin reduced to a sliver, and a posture of exaggerated rigidity. No feature of the drawing faithfully resembles the corresponding feature of the subject. The proportions are systematically violated, the textures ignored, the colours absent. Condition (ii) is not satisfied: the work does not resemble the subject in respect of perceptually salient features; it grotesquely departs from them. And yet every observer who knows the public figure recognises the subject instantly — perhaps more readily than they would recognise a carefully executed photographic portrait. The caricature captures something about its subject that mere resemblance does not. One might observe that what makes the caricature an imitation is not that it duplicates the appearance of its subject but that it isolates and amplifies those features by which the subject is most characteristically identified. The definition, by requiring perceptual resemblance, cannot accommodate this mode of depiction and must either exclude caricature from the class of imitations or strain the concept of resemblance beyond its ordinary meaning.

definition: A dramatic work W is a tragedy if and only if (i) W depicts the downfall of a protagonist of elevated stature, (ii) the protagonist's downfall is brought about by a flaw in his character or by an error of judgement, and (iii) the audience's response to the depicted downfall includes both pity and fear.
conditions: W depicts the downfall of a protagonist of elevated stature
The protagonist's downfall is brought about by a flaw in his character or by an error of judgement
The audience's response to the depicted downfall includes both pity and fear
counterexample: Consider a dramatic work depicting a common labourer who, through no flaw or error of his own, is destroyed by an indifferent economic system. The audience experiences intense pity and a species of fear — the recognition that they, too, might be similarly crushed. The emotional response is of precisely the character associated with tragedy. Yet condition (i) fails, for the protagonist is not of elevated stature, and condition (ii) fails, for the destruction is not brought about by any deficiency in the protagonist. The work is, by any reasonable standard, a tragedy, though the definition excludes it.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for tragedies in which the protagonist is of ordinary station and the downfall arises from external forces wholly beyond the protagonist's control, thereby excluding the modern social tragedy from the class of tragic works.
passage: One might define tragedy as a dramatic form in which a protagonist of elevated stature is brought to ruin through a flaw of character or an error of judgement, producing pity and fear in the audience. This definition has ancient credentials and captures much of what we find in the Greek tragedians: Oedipus falls through his insistence on pursuing the truth; Creon through his inflexibility. The requirement of elevated stature explains why the fall is terrible; the requirement of a flaw or error explains why it is intelligible; and the requirement of pity and fear identifies the characteristic emotional response. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from modern dramatic literature. Suppose a playwright depicts the life of an ordinary factory worker in an industrial city. The worker is honest, diligent, and kind to his family. He commits no error and possesses no tragic flaw. But the factory closes, the neighbourhood declines, his savings are consumed, and he is at last destroyed — not by any deficiency of his own but by the vast and impersonal movements of capital. The audience watches his destruction with pity that is, if anything, more acute than what Oedipus occasions, for the worker's innocence removes the consolation that he brought his fate upon himself. And the audience experiences fear: the fear that such destruction could befall anyone. The definition excludes this work from the class of tragedies, for the protagonist is not of elevated stature and the downfall is not attributable to a personal flaw. One might observe that this exclusion impoverishes the concept of tragedy precisely where it is most needed — in the analysis of dramatic works that derive their power from the vulnerability of ordinary persons to forces they can neither understand nor resist.

definition: An artist A possesses a distinctive style if and only if (i) there is a set of formal features that recurs consistently across A's works, (ii) these recurring features are the product of A's deliberate artistic choices rather than of accident or convention, and (iii) the recurring features are sufficiently distinctive to permit an informed observer to attribute a previously unseen work to A on the basis of formal inspection alone.
conditions: There is a set of formal features that recurs consistently across A's works
These recurring features are the product of A's deliberate artistic choices rather than of accident or convention
The recurring features are sufficiently distinctive to permit an informed observer to attribute a previously unseen work to A on the basis of formal inspection alone
counterexample: Consider a painter who suffers from a tremor in his right hand. The tremor produces a characteristic wavering quality in every brushstroke. Over the course of his career this wavering becomes so recognisable that any informed observer can identify his paintings at a glance. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied: the formal features recur consistently and are highly distinctive. But condition (ii) fails, for the wavering is not a product of deliberate artistic choice but of a physiological involuntary condition. Yet the painter's works possess a style as unmistakable as any in the history of art.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that style may arise from involuntary or accidental features of the artist's practice that, through consistent recurrence, become as artistically significant and identifying as any deliberate formal choice.
passage: One might characterise artistic style as the consistent recurrence, across a body of work, of formal features that are both deliberately chosen and sufficiently distinctive to enable attribution. This characterisation combines three plausible requirements: consistency, intentionality, and distinctiveness. It explains why we speak of Rembrandt's style, or Cézanne's, while hesitating to attribute style to a factory that mass-produces identical objects. The requirement of deliberate choice distinguishes genuine style from mere mannerism or mechanical repetition.
