Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the visual arts. Suppose a painter of considerable talent develops, in middle age, a progressive tremor in his painting hand. The tremor introduces into every canvas a distinctive wavering quality — a slight irregularity in contour, a softness at the edges of forms, a vibrato-like oscillation in long brushstrokes. The painter does not choose this quality; he cannot suppress it. Yet over the decades it becomes the most recognisable feature of his work. Connoisseurs learn to identify his paintings at once by the characteristic tremor-mark. They speak of his style in the same terms they use of any other artist's deliberately cultivated manner. Conditions (i) and (iii) are amply satisfied: the features recur consistently and are unmistakably distinctive. But condition (ii) fails, for the features arise from a physiological condition entirely beyond the artist's control. One might observe that the definition, by insisting on deliberate choice, draws an artificial line through the phenomenon of style. It cannot explain why we regard the tremor-marked paintings as possessing a genuine style, nor why we value them precisely for the quality that the tremor introduces. The involuntary has become, through consistent presence, an integral part of the artistic identity.

definition: A work of art W is original if and only if (i) W is not a copy or close imitation of any prior work, (ii) W introduces formal or thematic elements that have no precedent in the tradition to which it belongs, and (iii) the novel elements in W are the product of the artist's creative imagination rather than of mere chance.
conditions: W is not a copy or close imitation of any prior work
W introduces formal or thematic elements that have no precedent in the tradition to which it belongs
The novel elements in W are the product of the artist's creative imagination rather than of mere chance
counterexample: Consider a composer who, working within the sonata form in the late eighteenth century, produces a string quartet that introduces no formal or thematic element without precedent. The harmonic language, the instrumental textures, the structural proportions are all firmly within the tradition established by Haydn. Yet within these inherited forms the composer achieves a result of extraordinary individuality — a particular quality of phrasing, a distinctive approach to development, a characteristic way of distributing melodic interest among the four instruments. Critics and musicians recognise the work as unmistakably original. Condition (ii) fails, for nothing in the work is strictly unprecedented, yet the work is manifestly original.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that originality may consist not in the introduction of unprecedented elements but in the distinctive manner in which familiar elements are combined, inflected, and deployed.
passage: One might define originality as the introduction, through creative imagination, of formal or thematic elements without precedent in the relevant tradition. This definition appeals to our sense that the original work breaks new ground — that it offers something not previously available. It also excludes mere novelty produced by chance, insisting that the new elements arise from the artist's creative activity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from musical history. Suppose a composer working in Vienna in the 1780s produces a string quartet that is, in every identifiable formal respect, thoroughly conventional. The sonata-allegro first movement, the slow movement in the subdominant, the minuet and trio, the rondo finale — all follow established patterns. The harmonic vocabulary does not extend beyond what Haydn had already employed. No thematic device, no textural innovation, no structural experiment can be found that lacks precedent. Condition (ii) is not satisfied, for nothing in the work is without antecedent in the tradition. And yet the quartet is unmistakably original. It possesses a quality that no other composer's work possesses — a particular way of shaping a phrase, of distributing melodic interest among the instruments, of managing the tension between regularity and surprise within inherited forms. One might observe that this species of originality — the originality of manner rather than of material — is in fact the more common and perhaps the more important kind. Most works we call original do not introduce unprecedented elements; they employ familiar elements with a distinctive voice. The definition, by requiring the presence of strictly novel formal or thematic content, cannot accommodate this pervasive and valuable form of artistic originality.

definition: A mental act M is an exercise of imagination if and only if (i) M involves the formation of a mental image or representation that is not directly prompted by present sensory experience, (ii) the content of M is under the voluntary control of the subject, and (iii) the subject is aware that the content of M does not correspond to present reality.
conditions: M involves the formation of a mental image or representation that is not directly prompted by present sensory experience
The content of M is under the voluntary control of the subject
The subject is aware that the content of M does not correspond to present reality
counterexample: Consider a novelist who reports that her characters, once created, seem to acquire a life of their own — that they 'refuse' to perform certain actions and 'insist' upon others. The novelist is aware that the characters are fictional and that the mental representations do not correspond to present reality. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. But condition (ii) fails, for the novelist experiences the imaginative content as beyond her voluntary control. The characters develop in ways she did not plan and cannot redirect. Yet this involuntary imaginative activity is among the most creatively productive forms of imagination.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that some of the most significant exercises of imagination are characterised by a partial or complete loss of voluntary control over imaginative content, as when fictional characters or musical ideas develop according to their own internal logic.
passage: One might propose that imagination consists in the voluntary formation of mental representations that are not prompted by present sensation and are recognised as non-actual. This proposal captures the ordinary contrast between imagining and perceiving: when I imagine a golden mountain, I form a representation not given in sensation, I do so voluntarily, and I am aware that no golden mountain stands before me. The three conditions together distinguish imagination from perception, from involuntary hallucination, and from delusion. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the testimony of creative artists. Novelists frequently report a phenomenon in which characters, once established in the writer's mind, seem to acquire an autonomous existence. The characters resist certain plot developments and demand others. The novelist may set out to write a scene in which a particular character performs a particular action, only to find that the character will not cooperate — that the action is, as the novelist puts it, not in character. The mental representations involved are not prompted by present sensation, and the novelist is fully aware that the characters are fictional. But the content of the imaginative activity is not under voluntary control. The novelist cannot simply will the character to behave differently; the character's internal logic constrains the imaginative development. Condition (ii) is therefore unsatisfied. Yet it would be absurd to deny that the novelist is exercising imagination in such moments. One might observe that this involuntary dimension of imagination is not a deficiency but a mark of its most productive operation. The definition, by requiring voluntary control, excludes precisely those exercises of imagination that are most valued in creative practice.

definition: A composition C is harmonious if and only if (i) the elements of C stand in relations of mutual support, such that each element contributes to the effect of the whole, (ii) no element of C could be removed or altered without diminishing the effect of the whole, and (iii) the relations among the elements are such as to produce a sense of completeness and repose in the observer.
conditions: The elements of C stand in relations of mutual support, such that each element contributes to the effect of the whole
No element of C could be removed or altered without diminishing the effect of the whole
The relations among the elements are such as to produce a sense of completeness and repose in the observer
counterexample: Consider a Japanese ink painting in the sumi-e tradition, in which a single brushstroke depicts a bamboo stalk against a vast expanse of empty paper. The painting strikes every informed observer as profoundly harmonious. Yet condition (ii) is problematic: one could remove the empty space — or rather, one could fill it with additional elements — and it is the absence of additional elements, not the impossibility of their removal, that constitutes the harmony. More pointedly, the empty space is not an 'element' in any ordinary sense; it is the absence of elements. The definition, framed in terms of mutual support among elements, cannot accommodate harmony achieved through emptiness and restraint.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that harmony may be constituted not merely by the mutual support of present elements but by the strategic absence of elements — by what the artist has chosen to omit as much as by what she has included.
passage: One might characterise harmony as the mutual support of elements within a composition, where each element contributes to the whole, none could be removed without loss, and the result is a sense of completeness and repose. This characterisation has considerable plausibility. It explains why a well-proportioned Greek temple strikes us as harmonious: every column, every moulding, every interval between elements contributes to a unified effect, and the alteration of any one would disturb the whole. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from East Asian painting. Consider a sumi-e ink painting of a single bamboo stalk executed in three or four brushstrokes against a field of blank paper that constitutes the greater part of the composition. The painting is, by the universal testimony of those versed in the tradition, profoundly harmonious. But the definition struggles to accommodate it. The elements that are present — a few strokes of ink — do indeed contribute to the whole. But the harmony resides at least as much in the vast empty space as in the strokes themselves. The emptiness is not an element in the sense intended by the definition; it is the deliberate absence of elements. One cannot say that removing the empty space would diminish the effect, for the empty space is precisely the non-presence of additional marks. What the artist has chosen to leave out is as important to the harmony as what she has put in. One might observe that this principle — that harmony is as much a matter of omission as of inclusion — is not confined to East Asian art. The spare prose of a Hemingway story, the silences in a Webern piece, the unadorned surfaces of a Bauhaus building all achieve harmony through restraint. The definition, by attending exclusively to the mutual support of present elements, overlooks the contribution of strategic absence to the harmony of the whole.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O exhibits a harmonious arrangement of its parts, (ii) O produces a feeling of disinterested pleasure in a suitably attentive observer, and (iii) O possesses sensory qualities that are immediately agreeable.
conditions: O exhibits a harmonious arrangement of its parts
O produces a feeling of disinterested pleasure in a suitably attentive observer
O possesses sensory qualities that are immediately agreeable
counterexample: Consider a landscape painting depicting a storm at sea — dark, turbulent waves crashing against jagged rocks beneath a sky of brooding grey. The painting is widely regarded as beautiful, and the attentive viewer experiences a disinterested pleasure in contemplating it. Yet the sensory qualities are not immediately agreeable; they are harsh, violent, and unsettling. The harmony lies not in pleasantness of colour or form but in the expressive unity of the composition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for beauty that arises from expressive or dramatic unity rather than from immediately agreeable sensory qualities — it conflates beauty with prettiness and thereby excludes works whose beauty derives from the powerful coherence of their less pleasant elements.
passage: One might propose that beauty consists in the harmonious arrangement of parts, the production of disinterested pleasure, and the possession of immediately agreeable sensory qualities. This analysis captures a familiar class of beautiful objects — the well-proportioned Greek temple, the serene pastoral landscape, the melodious passage of Mozart. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the visual arts. Consider a painting of a storm at sea: dark, turbulent waves crash against jagged rocks beneath a sky of unrelenting grey. The colours are sombre, the forms violent, and the overall impression far from what one would ordinarily call agreeable. Nevertheless, the painting may be — and frequently is — regarded as beautiful. The attentive observer experiences a genuine disinterested pleasure in contemplating it, and the arrangement of its parts displays a powerful coherence. All the conditions save the third appear satisfied, yet the third is plainly violated; the sensory qualities are harsh rather than agreeable. It appears that the proposed definition, by requiring immediate agreeableness, cannot accommodate the beauty of works whose power derives from the expressive unity of elements that are, taken individually, far from pleasant. One might observe that beauty admits of a wider range than mere prettiness, and that the definition confuses one species of beauty with the genus.

definition: An artefact A is a work of art if and only if (i) A is produced by a human being with the intention of creating an aesthetic object, (ii) A requires skill or craft in its production, and (iii) A is presented to an audience for contemplation.
conditions: A is produced by a human being with the intention of creating an aesthetic object
A requires skill or craft in its production
A is presented to an audience for contemplation
counterexample: Suppose that an artist, wishing to challenge prevailing conventions, selects an ordinary manufactured bottle-rack from a department store and places it upon a pedestal in a gallery. The artist has produced nothing in the sense of fabrication; no skill or craft has been exercised in the making of the object. Yet the art world — critics, curators, fellow artists — regards the result as a work of art, and it is contemplated, discussed, and interpreted as such.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for works of art whose status derives not from the exercise of craft in production but from the act of selection, recontextualisation, or conceptual framing — it assumes that art must be made rather than designated.
passage: One might define a work of art as a human-made artefact produced with aesthetic intention, requiring skill in its fabrication, and presented to an audience for contemplation. This characterisation has the merit of capturing the paradigmatic cases — the carefully wrought painting, the sculpted marble figure, the composed sonata. Yet a case which tells against the proposed definition may be readily constructed. Suppose an artist, seeking to challenge prevailing conventions regarding the nature of art, selects an ordinary manufactured bottle-rack from a department store and places it upon a pedestal in a gallery. No skill or craft has been exercised in the making of the object; the artist has fabricated nothing. The bottle-rack was mass-produced by industrial machinery. Nevertheless, the art world — critics, curators, and fellow artists — treats the result as a work of art. It is discussed, interpreted, and contemplated with the same seriousness accorded to any sculpture. The first and second conditions are violated: the artefact was not produced by the artist, and its production required no artistic skill. Yet it functions as art. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon skilled production, cannot accommodate works whose artistic status derives from the act of selection, recontextualisation, or conceptual framing rather than from fabrication.

definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by an object or phenomenon of overwhelming magnitude or power, (ii) E involves a feeling of the inadequacy of the imagination to comprehend the object, and (iii) E ultimately resolves into a pleasurable awareness of the superiority of reason over sensory limitation.
conditions: E is occasioned by an object or phenomenon of overwhelming magnitude or power
E involves a feeling of the inadequacy of the imagination to comprehend the object
E ultimately resolves into a pleasurable awareness of the superiority of reason over sensory limitation
counterexample: Consider a mountaineer standing upon a narrow ridge in a violent thunderstorm, with lightning striking the peaks around her and the wind threatening to hurl her into the abyss. The phenomenon is of overwhelming power, and her imagination is wholly inadequate to comprehend the danger. Yet there is no pleasurable resolution into rational superiority — there is only terror. The experience is sublime by any ordinary reckoning, yet it fails to satisfy the third condition.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate cases of the terrible sublime in which the experience does not resolve into rational pleasure but remains arrested at the stage of overwhelming awe or fear — it presupposes a cognitive resolution that is not always present.
passage: One might characterise the sublime as an experience occasioned by overwhelming magnitude or power, involving a felt inadequacy of the imagination, and resolving into a pleasurable awareness of rational superiority. This analysis, drawing upon a venerable tradition, captures a recognisable pattern: the contemplation of vast mountain ranges or infinite starry skies that first overwhelms and then elevates. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from direct experience of natural danger. Consider a mountaineer standing upon a narrow ridge during a violent thunderstorm. Lightning strikes the surrounding peaks; the wind threatens at every moment to hurl her into the abyss below. The phenomenon is of overwhelming power, and her imagination is wholly inadequate to comprehend the scale of the danger confronting her. By any ordinary reckoning, the experience is sublime. Yet there is no pleasurable resolution into an awareness of rational superiority. There is only terror — a terror that does not abate so long as the danger persists. The third condition is violated, yet the experience retains its sublimity. It appears that the definition, by requiring a pleasurable cognitive resolution, cannot accommodate cases of what one might call the terrible sublime, in which awe and fear remain unresolved and the experience is valued precisely for its capacity to overwhelm rather than to elevate.

definition: A person P possesses good taste if and only if (i) P's aesthetic preferences are consistent over time, (ii) P's preferences align with those of recognised experts in the relevant art form, and (iii) P is able to articulate reasons for preferring one work over another.
conditions: P's aesthetic preferences are consistent over time
P's preferences align with those of recognised experts in the relevant art form
P is able to articulate reasons for preferring one work over another
counterexample: Suppose a wealthy collector whose preferences have been entirely consistent for decades, who always purchases what the most eminent critics recommend, and who can eloquently articulate reasons for each preference — but whose reasons are entirely borrowed from critical reviews she has memorised, and whose selections are made not by genuine discrimination but by a policy of deferring to expert opinion. She has never formed an independent aesthetic judgement in her life. We should hesitate to say that she possesses good taste, for her consistency and articulacy are parasitic upon the judgements of others.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the person's preferences arise from genuine independent discrimination — from a capacity to perceive and respond to aesthetic qualities on one's own — rather than from mere deference to the judgements of others.
passage: One might propose that good taste consists in consistency of preference over time, alignment with expert opinion, and the ability to articulate reasons for one's aesthetic choices. This characterisation has a certain plausibility, for it captures features we frequently associate with the tasteful person: steadiness of judgement, agreement with the discerning, and the capacity for reasoned appraisal. Yet a case which tells against this definition may be constructed without great difficulty. Suppose a wealthy collector of paintings whose preferences have remained entirely consistent over several decades. She invariably purchases what the most eminent critics recommend, and she can eloquently articulate reasons for preferring one work to another. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose further that her reasons are, in every case, borrowed from critical reviews she has committed to memory, and that her selections are governed not by genuine discrimination but by a standing policy of deferring to expert opinion. She has never formed an independent aesthetic judgement in her life. One might observe that we should hesitate to ascribe good taste to such a person. Her consistency and articulacy, whilst genuine, are parasitic upon the judgements of others. The definition, by attending to the outward marks of taste — consistency, expert agreement, articulacy — without requiring that these arise from independent perception and response to aesthetic qualities, admits a species of aesthetic deference that falls short of genuine taste.

definition: An experience E is aesthetic if and only if (i) E is directed towards the sensory properties of an object, (ii) E is valued for its own sake rather than for any practical end, and (iii) E involves heightened attention to form, colour, sound, or texture.
conditions: E is directed towards the sensory properties of an object
E is valued for its own sake rather than for any practical end
E involves heightened attention to form, colour, sound, or texture
counterexample: Consider the experience of reading a philosophical dialogue by Plato — an experience widely regarded as aesthetic. The reader attends not primarily to sensory properties such as the visual appearance of the printed page but to the logical structure of the arguments, the elegance of the dialectical progression, and the depth of the ideas expressed. The experience is valued for its own sake, yet it is directed towards intellectual rather than sensory properties. The third condition is at best marginally satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate aesthetic experiences directed towards intellectual, structural, or conceptual properties of an object — it restricts the aesthetic to the sensory and thereby excludes the aesthetic appreciation of mathematical proofs, philosophical arguments, and literary ideas.
passage: One might define aesthetic experience as experience directed towards the sensory properties of an object, valued for its own sake, and involving heightened attention to form, colour, sound, or texture. This characterisation appears well suited to paradigmatic cases: the contemplation of a sunset, the absorbed listening to a string quartet, the tactile pleasure of fine silk. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the literary arts. Consider the experience of reading one of the philosophical dialogues of Plato. It is widely agreed that such reading may constitute an aesthetic experience of the highest order. The reader is absorbed, the experience is valued for its own sake, and there is a felt quality of intellectual delight. Yet the experience is directed not primarily towards the sensory properties of the printed page — the typeface, the quality of the paper — but towards the logical structure of the arguments, the elegance of the dialectical progression, and the profundity of the ideas expressed. The heightened attention is to intellectual rather than sensory features. It appears that the definition, by restricting the aesthetic to the sensory, cannot accommodate the aesthetic appreciation of mathematical proofs, philosophical arguments, or the structural qualities of literary works. One might observe that the aesthetic is a broader category than the sensuous, and that the proposed analysis mistakes one of its species for the whole.

definition: A painting P represents an object O if and only if (i) P visually resembles O, (ii) the artist intended P to depict O, and (iii) a competent viewer would recognise O upon inspecting P.
conditions: P visually resembles O
The artist intended P to depict O
A competent viewer would recognise O upon inspecting P
counterexample: Consider a highly abstract painting by a cubist artist who intends to depict a violin. The painting bears little visual resemblance to a violin as ordinarily seen; the instrument is decomposed into geometrical fragments and reassembled from multiple perspectives. A viewer unfamiliar with the conventions of cubism would not recognise a violin upon inspecting the canvas. Yet within the relevant tradition, the painting is understood to represent a violin, and this representation is one of its central artistic functions.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for representational conventions and traditions that enable depiction without visual resemblance — it assumes that representation must proceed through likeness rather than through established pictorial codes.
passage: One might propose that a painting represents an object when it visually resembles that object, the artist intended the depiction, and a competent viewer would recognise the object. This account captures the representational success of naturalistic painting with considerable accuracy. Yet a case which tells against it may be constructed from the practice of cubist art. Consider a cubist painting in which the artist intends to depict a violin. The instrument is decomposed into geometrical fragments and reassembled upon the canvas from multiple simultaneous perspectives. The resulting image bears little visual resemblance to a violin as it would appear to an observer positioned at a single point in space. A viewer unfamiliar with the conventions of cubism — and thus arguably the paradigm of the competent viewer in ordinary circumstances — would not recognise a violin upon inspecting the canvas. The first and third conditions are violated. Yet within the tradition of cubist painting, the work is understood to represent a violin, and this representation is central to its artistic meaning. It appears that the definition, by grounding representation in visual resemblance and ordinary recognition, cannot accommodate forms of depiction that operate through established pictorial conventions rather than through likeness. Representation, one might observe, is not merely a matter of looking like but of functioning within a system of pictorial reference.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) the artist experienced E during the creation of W, (ii) W possesses formal properties that are conventionally associated with E, and (iii) W tends to arouse E in a qualified audience.
conditions: The artist experienced E during the creation of W
W possesses formal properties that are conventionally associated with E
W tends to arouse E in a qualified audience
counterexample: Consider a composer who writes a funeral march of the most profound solemnity whilst in a state of cheerful good humour, motivated purely by a commission. The march possesses formal properties — slow tempo, minor key, measured cadences — conventionally associated with grief, and it reliably arouses feelings of sorrow in qualified listeners. Two of the three conditions are met, but the first is violated. Yet the work expresses grief as powerfully as any composition written in genuine sorrow.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that artistic expression is a property of the work rather than a biographical fact about the artist — the emotion expressed is determined by the formal and structural features of the work itself, not by the psychological state of its creator.
passage: One might characterise artistic expression as a relation between a work, an emotion, and an artist, requiring that the artist experienced the emotion during creation, that the work possesses conventionally associated formal properties, and that the work arouses the emotion in a qualified audience. This analysis has a natural appeal, for it connects expression to both the inner life of the creator and the responsive experience of the audience. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from musical composition. Consider a composer who writes a funeral march of the most profound solemnity — slow tempo, minor key, measured cadences, a melody of grave dignity — whilst in a state of cheerful good humour, motivated purely by a generous commission. The march possesses all the formal properties conventionally associated with grief and reliably arouses feelings of sorrow and solemn reflection in qualified listeners. The second and third conditions are satisfied. The first is plainly violated: the composer felt no grief whatsoever. Yet the work expresses grief as powerfully as any composition written in the depths of genuine bereavement. It appears that the definition, by making the artist's psychological state a necessary condition, confuses the cause of artistic creation with the expressive character of the created work. One might observe that expression is a property of the work itself — of its formal structure and its capacity to embody an emotional quality — rather than a biographical fact about its creator.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O exhibits symmetry or regularity of form, (ii) O is perceived as unified rather than chaotic, and (iii) contemplation of O produces serenity in the observer.
conditions: O exhibits symmetry or regularity of form
O is perceived as unified rather than chaotic
O contemplation produces serenity in the observer
counterexample: Consider a Japanese garden designed according to the principles of wabi-sabi, in which asymmetry, incompleteness, and the marks of natural decay are deliberately cultivated. A weathered stone is placed off-centre beside a mossy path; a cracked earthenware bowl sits upon an uneven wooden board. The garden is widely regarded as beautiful, and it is perceived as unified, but it exhibits neither symmetry nor regularity, and the feeling it produces is closer to melancholy than to serenity.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate traditions of beauty that prize asymmetry, imperfection, and the poignancy of transience — it universalises one culturally specific ideal of beauty and thereby excludes others of equal legitimacy.
passage: One might propose that beauty consists in symmetry or regularity of form, perceived unity, and the production of serenity. This account captures a familiar classical ideal: the balanced facade of the Parthenon, the regular proportions of the Vitruvian figure, the calm induced by a still lake at dawn. Yet a case which tells against this definition may be drawn from the aesthetic traditions of Japan. Consider a garden designed according to the principles of wabi-sabi, in which asymmetry, incompleteness, and the marks of natural decay are deliberately cultivated as aesthetic virtues. A weathered stone is placed off-centre beside a mossy path; a cracked earthenware bowl sits upon an uneven wooden board; a single branch of plum blossom, slightly withered, is arranged in a rough ceramic vase. The garden is widely regarded as beautiful — indeed, as embodying one of the most refined aesthetic sensibilities known to any culture. It is perceived as unified. Yet it exhibits neither symmetry nor regularity, and the feeling it produces is closer to a gentle melancholy than to serenity. The first and third conditions are violated. It appears that the definition, by universalising one culturally specific ideal, cannot accommodate aesthetic traditions in which beauty is found precisely in imperfection, asymmetry, and the bittersweet awareness of transience.

definition: A judgement of taste J is objective if and only if (i) J is based upon properties that inhere in the object rather than in the observer, (ii) J is in principle verifiable by any competent observer, and (iii) disagreement about J can be resolved by closer attention to the object.
conditions: J is based upon properties that inhere in the object rather than in the observer
J is in principle verifiable by any competent observer
Disagreement about J can be resolved by closer attention to the object
counterexample: Consider two highly competent music critics who disagree about whether a particular performance of a Beethoven sonata is expressive or merely mannered. Both attend closely to the same performance; both are sensitive to nuances of tempo, dynamics, and phrasing. Their disagreement persists after repeated hearings and careful discussion. The properties upon which each bases her judgement inhere in the performance, and each regards her judgement as verifiable. Yet the disagreement appears irresolvable by closer attention alone.
missing_condition: The definition fails to acknowledge that certain aesthetic properties — expressiveness, grace, garishness — are response-dependent in a way that makes them irreducible to observer-independent features, so that competent observers may permissibly disagree even under ideal conditions.
passage: One might define an objective judgement of taste as one based upon properties inhering in the object, verifiable by any competent observer, and resolvable by closer attention to the object in cases of disagreement. This characterisation captures a widespread aspiration: the hope that aesthetic disputes are, in the final analysis, disputes about facts that careful observation can settle. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from music criticism. Suppose two highly competent critics disagree about whether a particular performance of a Beethoven sonata is genuinely expressive or merely mannered. Both attend closely to the very same performance on multiple occasions. Both are exquisitely sensitive to nuances of tempo, dynamics, articulation, and phrasing. Their disagreement persists after repeated hearings and extended discussion. Each bases her judgement upon properties that inhere in the performance — the rubato at bar forty-seven, the dynamic shading in the development section — and each regards her judgement as in principle verifiable. The first two conditions appear satisfied, yet the third is violated: closer attention does not resolve the dispute. It appears that certain aesthetic properties — expressiveness, grace, garishness — are response-dependent in a manner that makes them irreducible to observer-independent features. The definition, by assuming that all aesthetic disagreement can be resolved by more careful observation, overlooks the possibility that competent observers may permissibly differ even under ideal conditions.

definition: A piece of music M expresses sadness if and only if (i) M is in a minor key, (ii) M has a slow tempo, and (iii) M employs descending melodic lines.
conditions: M is in a minor key
M has a slow tempo
M employs descending melodic lines
counterexample: Consider the scherzo movement of a Chopin sonata in B-flat minor, which is in a minor key but is played at a rapid tempo with vigorous, leaping melodic figures. The movement is widely regarded as expressing not sadness but defiance, fury, or desperate energy. Conversely, certain passages of Barber's Adagio for Strings employ ascending lines and yet are regarded as among the most sorrowful passages in the orchestral repertoire.
missing_condition: The definition reduces the expression of sadness to a conjunction of local musical features and thereby fails to capture the holistic quality of musical expressiveness, which depends upon harmonic context, orchestration, rhythmic character, and the cumulative trajectory of the composition as a whole.
passage: One might propose that a piece of music expresses sadness if and only if it is in a minor key, has a slow tempo, and employs descending melodic lines. This analysis codifies a familiar association: the mournful quality of slow, minor-key music with falling melodic contours. Yet a case which tells against this definition may be readily produced. Consider the scherzo movement of a Chopin sonata in B-flat minor. It is in a minor key, satisfying the first condition. But it is played at a rapid tempo, with vigorous, leaping melodic figures — violating the second and third conditions. Yet the movement is not without expressive power; it is widely regarded as expressing defiance, fury, or a species of desperate energy rather than sadness. The minor key alone does not suffice for sadness. Conversely, certain passages of Barber's Adagio for Strings employ ascending melodic lines — violating the third condition — yet are regarded as among the most sorrowful passages in the orchestral repertoire. It appears that the definition, by reducing expressiveness to a conjunction of isolable musical features, fails to capture the holistic character of musical expression, which depends upon harmonic context, orchestration, rhythmic character, and the cumulative trajectory of the work as a whole. One might observe that sadness in music is not a mechanical product of key, tempo, and contour but an emergent quality of the total musical structure.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O possesses a harmonious arrangement of its parts, (ii) the perception of O produces pleasure in the observer, and (iii) the pleasure produced is disinterested, in the sense that it does not depend upon any desire to possess or make use of O.
conditions: O possesses a harmonious arrangement of its parts
The perception of O produces pleasure in the observer
The pleasure produced is disinterested, not depending upon any desire to possess or make use of O
counterexample: Consider a perfectly symmetrical arrangement of surgical instruments laid out upon a tray before an operation. The instruments are harmoniously arranged, each positioned in relation to the others with meticulous spatial balance. A visiting aesthete, knowing nothing of surgery, perceives the arrangement and experiences genuine pleasure, a pleasure entirely disinterested since the aesthete has no desire to possess or use the instruments. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call the arrangement beautiful, for it possesses a sterile, clinical regularity that strikes most observers as merely orderly rather than genuinely beautiful.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the harmonious arrangement exhibit a kind of expressive or qualitative richness — that the relations among the parts display variety, tension, or organic unity sufficient to engage the imagination, rather than a merely mechanical or schematic regularity.
passage: One might propose that beauty consists in the harmonious arrangement of an object's parts, the production of disinterested pleasure in the observer, and the absence of any possessive or utilitarian motivation in the enjoyment. This formulation captures something central to our notion of the beautiful: it excludes mere sensory gratification, and it requires a structural basis in the object for the pleasure experienced. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the domain of practical life. Suppose a visiting aesthete enters a surgical theatre and observes a tray of instruments arranged with perfect symmetry — each scalpel, forceps, and clamp positioned in precise spatial relation to the others. The aesthete, knowing nothing of surgery, experiences genuine pleasure in the arrangement, a pleasure entirely disinterested, for the aesthete has no wish to possess or employ any of the instruments. The arrangement is harmonious in a clear and uncontroversial sense. All three conditions of the proposed definition are thus fulfilled. Nevertheless, one might observe that the arrangement, for all its regularity, is not beautiful. It possesses the sterile, mechanical quality of mere order — each item placed according to a simple rule of equal spacing — without the qualitative richness or imaginative engagement that we associate with genuine beauty. It appears that the definition, by requiring only harmony, pleasure, and disinterest, fails to demand that the arrangement exhibit the kind of expressive variety or organic unity that distinguishes beauty from mere tidiness.

definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by an object or phenomenon that exceeds the observer's capacity to comprehend it fully, (ii) E involves a feeling of being overwhelmed or diminished, and (iii) E ultimately resolves into an elevation of the observer's sense of rational or moral power.
conditions: E is occasioned by an object or phenomenon that exceeds the observer's capacity to comprehend it fully
E involves a feeling of being overwhelmed or diminished
E ultimately resolves into an elevation of the observer's sense of rational or moral power
counterexample: Suppose a student sits before an examination paper in advanced mathematics and encounters a problem whose complexity entirely exceeds the student's capacity to comprehend it. The student feels overwhelmed and diminished by the sheer unintelligibility of the material. After some reflection, the student resolves to dedicate more effort to the study of mathematics, and this resolution produces a genuine elevation of the student's sense of rational power and self-mastery. All three conditions are met. Yet one would not ordinarily describe this as an experience of the sublime; it is merely the frustration of encountering a difficult examination, followed by a motivational resolution.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the object or phenomenon possess a sensory or imaginative magnitude — a vastness, power, or boundlessness perceptible in experience — rather than a merely intellectual or cognitive difficulty that happens to overwhelm the observer's understanding.
passage: It has often been supposed that the sublime is characterised by three features: the presence of something that exceeds our capacity to comprehend it, a feeling of being overwhelmed or diminished, and a subsequent elevation of our sense of rational or moral power. This tripartite analysis captures something important about the experience of standing before a vast mountain range or contemplating the starry heavens. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from ordinary academic life. Suppose a student of modest mathematical ability sits before an examination paper in advanced number theory and encounters a problem of such complexity that it entirely exceeds the student's power to grasp it. The student feels genuinely overwhelmed and diminished — the sheer unintelligibility of the symbols produces a sense of intellectual helplessness. After a period of reflection, however, the student resolves to devote greater effort to the discipline, and this resolution produces a genuine elevation of the student's sense of rational power and self-mastery. Each of the three conditions is satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to describe this as an experience of the sublime. The student's experience is one of mundane frustration followed by motivational recovery, not the exalted response that we associate with the contemplation of natural grandeur or limitless power. It appears that the definition, by requiring only incomprehensibility, a feeling of being overwhelmed, and subsequent elevation, fails to demand that the occasioning object possess a perceptible sensory or imaginative magnitude — a vastness or power apprehended in experience — rather than a merely intellectual opacity.

definition: A judgement J is a judgement of taste if and only if (i) J attributes a positive or negative aesthetic quality to an object, (ii) J is grounded in the subject's own perceptual experience of the object, and (iii) J claims universal assent — that is, the subject expects all competent observers to agree.
conditions: J attributes a positive or negative aesthetic quality to an object
J is grounded in the subject's own perceptual experience of the object
J claims universal assent, expecting all competent observers to agree
counterexample: Consider a perfumer who, upon smelling a newly formulated fragrance, judges that it is exquisite. The judgement attributes a positive aesthetic quality. It is grounded in the perfumer's own perceptual experience. And the perfumer, confident in professional expertise, fully expects that all competent noses in the industry will concur. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the perfumer's judgement is motivated entirely by the commercial calculation that the fragrance will sell well, and the attribution of aesthetic quality is merely a professional reflex prompted by market considerations rather than by genuine aesthetic response. We should hesitate to regard this as a genuine judgement of taste.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the judgement be motivated by a genuine aesthetic response to the perceptual qualities of the object — that it arise from attentive, contemplative engagement with the object's sensory character, rather than from instrumental, commercial, or habitual considerations that merely happen to accompany the perceptual experience.
passage: One might define a judgement of taste as one that attributes an aesthetic quality to an object, is grounded in the subject's perceptual experience, and claims the universal agreement of competent observers. This formulation captures the subjective yet universalising character of aesthetic judgement — its rootedness in individual perception together with its aspiration to intersubjective validity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed from the world of commercial perfumery. Suppose a perfumer of considerable standing smells a newly formulated fragrance and judges it to be exquisite. The judgement attributes a positive aesthetic quality to the fragrance. It is grounded in the perfumer's own olfactory experience; the perfumer has actually smelled the product. And the perfumer, confident in a well-trained professional palate, fully expects that every competent nose in the industry will arrive at the same verdict. Each condition of the definition is thus fulfilled. Yet suppose the perfumer's judgement is in truth motivated not by any genuine aesthetic response to the fragrance's qualities but by a commercial calculation: the scent resembles a competitor's best-selling product, and the perfumer's attribution of aesthetic excellence is a professional reflex prompted by market considerations. The perceptual experience is present, but it does not generate the judgement; the judgement is generated by extraneous commercial reasoning. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only the attribution of aesthetic quality, perceptual grounding, and a claim to universal assent, fails to demand that the judgement arise from a genuine and attentive aesthetic engagement with the object's sensory character.

definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E is directed towards the sensory or formal properties of an object, (ii) E involves absorbed attention, such that the subject is wholly engaged with the object, and (iii) E is valued for its own sake, not as a means to any further end.
conditions: E is directed towards the sensory or formal properties of an object
E involves absorbed attention, wholly engaging the subject with the object
E is valued for its own sake, not as a means to any further end
counterexample: Suppose a safe-cracker, in the course of a burglary, presses an ear against a vault door and attends with complete absorption to the formal properties of the sounds produced by the turning mechanism — the precise intervals between clicks, the tonal quality of each tumbler falling into place. The safe-cracker's attention is directed entirely at these sensory and formal properties. The absorption is genuine and total. Moreover, the safe-cracker, being also a lover of mechanical sounds, values this listening experience for its own sake, quite apart from the practical objective of opening the vault. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to describe this as an aesthetic experience, for it occurs within a context of criminal activity that seems to preclude the requisite contemplative stance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the absorbed attention occur within a contemplative or receptive orientation — one in which the subject has set aside practical exigencies and adopted a stance of open receptivity towards the object's qualities, rather than attending to sensory properties within a context dominated by ulterior practical purposes.
passage: One might propose that aesthetic experience consists in absorbed attention directed towards the sensory or formal properties of an object, valued for its own sake. This characterisation aims to isolate what is distinctive about the aesthetic mode of engagement: its attentiveness, its focus on perceivable qualities, and its intrinsic rather than instrumental value. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from an unexpected quarter. Suppose a safe-cracker, in the course of a burglary, presses an ear against the door of a vault and listens with complete absorption to the sounds produced by the turning mechanism. The safe-cracker attends to the precise intervals between clicks, the tonal quality of each tumbler falling into place, the rhythmic pattern of the sequence. This attention is directed wholly at sensory and formal properties. The absorption is total — the safe-cracker is as wholly engaged with the sounds as any concert-goer with a string quartet. Furthermore, the safe-cracker, who happens to be a connoisseur of mechanical sounds, values this listening experience for its own sake, quite independently of its usefulness in opening the vault. Each of the three conditions is thus satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should hesitate to call this an aesthetic experience. It occurs in the midst of a criminal enterprise, and the safe-cracker's orientation towards the object is not one of contemplative openness but one embedded in practical exigency. The definition, by requiring only sensory attention, absorption, and intrinsic valuation, fails to demand a contemplative or receptive stance — a setting-aside of practical concerns that allows the subject to encounter the object's qualities in the mode appropriate to genuine aesthetic experience.

definition: A work W represents a subject S if and only if (i) W bears a recognisable resemblance to S, (ii) the maker of W intended W to stand for S, and (iii) a competent audience can identify S upon perceiving W.
conditions: W bears a recognisable resemblance to S
The maker of W intended W to stand for S
A competent audience can identify S upon perceiving W
counterexample: Consider a portrait painter who paints a likeness of her friend, intending the painting to represent the friend. The painting bears a strong resemblance to the friend, and any competent observer who knows the friend can identify the subject immediately. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the friend has an identical twin, and the painting, owing to the perfect resemblance between the twins, equally resembles and is equally identifiable as the twin. The painting represents the friend and not the twin, but the definition cannot distinguish between the two, since all three conditions are equally satisfied with respect to both individuals.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require a suitable causal or historical connection between the work and the particular subject it represents — that W stand in an appropriate generative relation to S, such as having been produced in the presence of S or on the basis of information specifically about S, so that the representation is anchored to one subject rather than any equally resembling individual.
passage: One might define representation as the conjunction of resemblance, intention, and audience recognisability: a work represents a subject when it resembles that subject, the maker intended it to stand for that subject, and competent observers can identify the subject upon perceiving the work. This analysis captures the intuition that representation involves a kind of visual correspondence, directed by the maker's purposes and accessible to an audience. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from portraiture. Suppose a portrait painter paints a likeness of her friend, with the clear intention that the painting represent that friend. The resemblance is striking, and any competent observer who knows the friend can identify the subject at once. Each condition is satisfied. Now suppose the friend has an identical twin. Because the twins are visually indistinguishable, the painting bears an equally recognisable resemblance to the twin. A competent observer who knows both individuals cannot, on the basis of the painting's visible properties alone, determine which twin is depicted. The three conditions of the definition are fulfilled with respect to the friend and equally fulfilled with respect to the twin. Yet the painting represents the friend and not the twin. It appears that the definition, by relying on resemblance, intention, and recognisability, fails to require the kind of causal or historical connection — having been painted in the friend's presence, or from information specifically about the friend — that anchors the representation to one particular subject rather than any equally resembling individual.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) W possesses perceptible properties that are conventionally associated with E, (ii) a sensitive and informed audience perceives E in W, and (iii) W was created during a period in which its maker experienced E.
conditions: W possesses perceptible properties conventionally associated with E
A sensitive and informed audience perceives E in W
W was created during a period in which its maker experienced E
counterexample: Suppose a composer, during a period of profound grief following a bereavement, writes a brisk and exuberant march. The march possesses perceptible properties — a bright major key, lively tempo, and vigorous rhythms — conventionally associated with joy. A sensitive and informed audience perceives joy in the march. And the composer was indeed experiencing grief when the work was created. But suppose the composer also experienced intermittent moments of relief and even joy during the same period, so that the condition of experiencing the emotion is technically met with respect to joy. All three conditions are then satisfied with respect to joy. Yet the march does not express the composer's grief, which was the dominant emotion of the period, and one might question whether it genuinely expresses joy in any artistically significant sense, given that the work was produced as a deliberate distraction from overwhelming sorrow.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the maker's experience of the emotion play a motivating or generative role in the creation of the work — that the emotion be a cause of, or substantive contributor to, the artistic choices embodied in W, rather than a temporally coincident mental state that bears no productive relation to the work's character.
passage: One might propose that a work of art expresses an emotion when it possesses perceptible properties conventionally associated with that emotion, when a qualified audience perceives the emotion in the work, and when the maker experienced the emotion during the period of creation. This analysis seems to unite the objective, the intersubjective, and the biographical dimensions of artistic expression. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the practice of musical composition. Suppose a composer, stricken by profound grief after a bereavement, writes a brisk and exuberant march. The march is in a bright major key with a lively tempo and vigorous rhythms — properties conventionally associated with joy. A sensitive and informed audience, hearing the march, perceives joy in its character. The composer, during the same period, experienced moments of relief and even fleeting joy alongside the dominant grief, so that the biographical condition regarding the experience of joy is technically met. All three conditions of the definition are thus satisfied with respect to joy. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to say that the march expresses joy in any artistically significant sense. The work was produced as a deliberate distraction from overwhelming sorrow; the momentary flickers of joy played no role in shaping the artistic choices that produced the march. Meanwhile, the composer's grief — the dominant emotion — is entirely absent from the work's perceptible character. It appears that the definition, by requiring only conventional association, audience perception, and the contemporaneous experience of the emotion, fails to demand that the maker's experience of the emotion be causally or generatively connected to the work's artistic properties.

definition: An artefact A is a work of art if and only if (i) A is produced by a human agent with the intention that it be regarded aesthetically, (ii) A possesses some degree of formal complexity, and (iii) A is presented within a context that invites aesthetic contemplation.
conditions: A is produced by a human agent with the intention that it be regarded aesthetically
A possesses some degree of formal complexity
A is presented within a context that invites aesthetic contemplation
counterexample: Consider a fraudster who, intending to deceive a gallery into exhibiting the piece and thereby to collect insurance money, produces an elaborately patterned forgery of a nonexistent painting, full of formal complexity, and presents it within a reputable gallery context that invites aesthetic contemplation. The artefact is produced by a human agent who intends it to be regarded aesthetically (for the deception to succeed, it must be so regarded). It possesses formal complexity. It is presented in a context that invites aesthetic contemplation. All three conditions are met. Yet one might doubt whether a fraudulent artefact, produced solely as an instrument of financial deception and lacking any genuine artistic purpose, ought to count as a work of art.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the intention behind the artefact's production include a genuine artistic purpose — an aim to communicate, explore, or embody aesthetic values — rather than merely the instrumental aim that the artefact be perceived as art for reasons entirely extraneous to artistic practice.
passage: One might define a work of art as a human-made artefact intended for aesthetic regard, possessing formal complexity, and presented within a context that invites aesthetic contemplation. This characterisation captures much of what we intuitively expect: intentional making, structural interest, and an appropriate setting for appreciation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the world of art fraud. Suppose a fraudster, motivated solely by the desire to collect insurance money, produces an elaborate forgery of a nonexistent painting. The forgery is intricate and formally complex, featuring layered brushwork, subtle colour gradations, and a carefully composed arrangement of figures. The fraudster intends the work to be regarded aesthetically, for the deception depends upon the gallery treating it as a genuine object of aesthetic contemplation. The work is then exhibited in a reputable gallery, a context that paradigmatically invites such contemplation. Each of the three conditions is satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the fraudster's artefact, produced exclusively as a vehicle for financial deception and devoid of any genuine artistic ambition, sits uncomfortably within the category of art. The fraudster does not aim to explore, communicate, or embody aesthetic values; the intention that the object be regarded aesthetically is merely instrumental, a means to the ulterior end of extracting money from the insurance company. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the intention to be regarded aesthetically, formal complexity, and an appropriate context, fails to require a genuine artistic purpose — an aim directed at aesthetic values for their own sake, rather than the mere strategic desire that an audience treat the artefact as art.

definition: A person P possesses good taste if and only if (i) P reliably prefers objects that are widely regarded as aesthetically excellent, (ii) P can articulate reasons for these preferences that refer to the objects' perceptible properties, and (iii) P's preferences are consistent across relevantly similar objects.
conditions: P reliably prefers objects that are widely regarded as aesthetically excellent
P can articulate reasons for these preferences referring to perceptible properties
P's preferences are consistent across relevantly similar objects
counterexample: Consider a social climber who, having studied the pronouncements of respected critics, reliably prefers exactly those paintings, musical compositions, and literary works that are widely acknowledged as excellent. The social climber can articulate reasons for these preferences by citing the perceptible properties that critics have identified — the balance of a composition, the purity of a melodic line, the elegance of a prose style. These preferences are consistent across similar objects. All three conditions are fulfilled. Yet the social climber's preferences are driven entirely by a desire for social prestige rather than by genuine sensitivity to aesthetic qualities. One should hesitate to say that this person possesses good taste.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P's preferences arise from a genuine perceptual sensitivity to the aesthetic qualities of the objects — that P's discriminations be grounded in first-hand experience of the objects' sensory and formal character, rather than in the imitation of received critical opinion for social advantage.
passage: One might propose that a person possesses good taste when that person reliably prefers objects widely regarded as aesthetically excellent, can offer reasons for those preferences that refer to perceptible properties, and maintains consistency across similar objects. This account unites correct preference, articulacy, and stability — three features that seem central to the exercise of refined aesthetic judgement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from social life. Suppose a person of considerable social ambition, having undertaken a careful study of the pronouncements of the most esteemed critics, reliably prefers precisely those paintings, symphonies, and novels that are widely acknowledged as masterpieces. This person can cite reasons for each preference in terms of perceptible properties: the balance of a certain composition, the luminosity of a certain canvas, the rhythmic assurance of a certain prose style. These reasons are drawn faithfully from critical literature. The preferences are consistent — the person favours the same kinds of qualities wherever they appear. All three conditions of the proposed definition are thus met. Yet the person's preferences are motivated entirely by the desire for social prestige. The engagement with art is not guided by any genuine perceptual sensitivity but by the strategic imitation of authoritative critical opinion. One might observe that such a person does not possess good taste, however closely the outward behaviour mimics it. The definition, by requiring only reliable preference, articulacy, and consistency, fails to demand that the preferences arise from a first-hand perceptual sensitivity to the aesthetic properties of the objects in question.

definition: A passage of music M is expressive of sadness if and only if (i) M is in a minor key, (ii) M has a slow tempo, and (iii) M employs descending melodic contours.
conditions: M is in a minor key
M has a slow tempo
M employs descending melodic contours
counterexample: Consider a lullaby composed in a minor key, at a slow tempo, with gently descending melodic lines. The piece employs all three specified features. Yet the lullaby does not express sadness; it expresses tenderness and calm. Its minor tonality imparts a quality of gentle pensiveness, its slow tempo creates a soothing rhythm, and its descending contours suggest the settling of a child into sleep. Listeners consistently describe the piece as serene rather than sad. All three conditions are met, but sadness is not what the passage expresses.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the musical features produce, in combination, a quality of lamentation, loss, or emotional pain — that the overall character of the passage convey an affective content recognisable as sadness, rather than other emotional qualities such as tenderness or tranquillity that may equally be carried by minor keys, slow tempi, and descending contours.
passage: It is tempting to identify the expression of sadness in music with a conjunction of readily specifiable features: a minor key, a slow tempo, and descending melodic contours. These properties are indeed frequently associated with musical sadness, and their co-occurrence might seem sufficient for a serviceable definition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the repertoire of lullabies. Suppose a composer writes a lullaby in a minor key, at a slow and rocking tempo, with gently descending melodic lines that trace the arc of a child settling into sleep. Each of the three conditions is satisfied: the key is minor, the tempo is slow, and the melodic contours descend. Yet competent listeners consistently describe the piece not as sad but as tender and serene. The minor tonality imparts a gentle pensiveness rather than grief; the slow tempo creates a soothing pulse rather than a mournful drag; and the descending lines suggest peaceful settling rather than dejection. One might observe that sadness, tenderness, and calm are distinct affective qualities, even though they may share certain surface-level musical features. It appears that the definition, by identifying sadness with the presence of a minor key, slow tempo, and descending contours, fails to require that the combination of these features produce a specific affective character — a quality of lamentation, loss, or emotional pain — that is recognisable as sadness rather than as any of the other emotional states that may be carried by the very same musical means.

definition: A natural landscape L is beautiful if and only if (i) L exhibits variety among its elements, (ii) L exhibits unity among its elements, and (iii) the perception of L produces a feeling of serenity in the observer.
conditions: L exhibits variety among its elements
L exhibits unity among its elements
The perception of L produces a feeling of serenity in the observer
counterexample: Suppose a traveller gazes upon a landscape consisting of a stagnant marsh bordered by a few listless willows, all reflected in the flat, still water. The landscape exhibits variety — marsh, trees, water, reflections — and unity, for the elements are bound together by the pervading stillness and the continuity of the reflections. The traveller, fatigued from a long journey, experiences a genuine feeling of serenity upon contemplating the scene. All three conditions are met. Yet the landscape is not beautiful; it is dreary. Its variety is of a monotonous kind, and its unity derives from stagnation rather than from the living interconnection of flourishing forms.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the variety and unity be of a qualitatively vital kind — that the elements display liveliness, contrast, or dynamic interrelation, so that the landscape's diversity and coherence reflect a richness of character rather than a mere absence of disruption or a uniformity born of lifelessness.
passage: One might propose that a natural landscape is beautiful when it exhibits variety and unity among its elements and produces a feeling of serenity in the observer. This characterisation captures the thought that beauty in nature requires both diversity and coherence, and that its apprehension is accompanied by a distinctive tranquillity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from an unpromising corner of the natural world. Suppose a weary traveller pauses beside a stagnant marsh. The landscape comprises the marsh itself, a few listless willows drooping at its edge, and a broad expanse of flat, still water in which these elements are reflected. There is variety: marsh, trees, water, and reflections constitute distinct elements. There is unity: a pervading stillness binds everything together, and the continuity of the reflections mirrors the scene above into the scene below, creating a coherent whole. The traveller, exhausted from a long journey, experiences a genuine feeling of serenity upon contemplating this scene — the stillness answers to the traveller's desire for rest. Each of the three conditions of the proposed definition is satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to describe this landscape as beautiful. It is dreary rather than beautiful — its variety is of a monotonous kind, and its unity derives not from the living interconnection of flourishing forms but from stagnation, from the absence of movement and vitality. It appears that the definition, by requiring only variety, unity, and the production of serenity, fails to demand that the variety and unity be of a qualitatively vital character — that the landscape's elements display liveliness, contrast, or dynamic interrelation rather than the dull coherence of lifelessness.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O possesses formal harmony among its parts, (ii) O elicits a disinterested pleasure in a competent observer, and (iii) O is capable of sustaining prolonged contemplation without diminishment of the pleasure it occasions.
conditions: O possesses formal harmony among its parts
O elicits a disinterested pleasure in a competent observer
O is capable of sustaining prolonged contemplation without diminishment of the pleasure it occasions
counterexample: Consider a case in which a certain arrangement of coloured glass fragments, produced entirely by accident when a stained-glass window shattered during a storm, is reassembled at random into a new configuration. The resulting mosaic possesses a striking formal harmony: its proportions satisfy classical ratios, its colours complement one another, and the arrangement of shapes displays a pleasing symmetry. Competent observers report a disinterested pleasure upon viewing it, and prolonged contemplation does not diminish their enjoyment. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that an identical arrangement, if discovered to have been produced by a machine calibrated to maximise irritation, would elicit the same formal properties and the same pleasure. The definition captures nothing about the relation between the object and its expressive or communicative dimension, and it appears that beauty in the fullest sense requires some capacity of the object to convey or intimate significance beyond mere formal agreeableness.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that O possess an expressive or significative dimension — that its formal harmony bear some intelligible relation to a content, mood, or meaning that the object communicates or intimates to the observer.
passage: One might propose that an object is beautiful when it possesses formal harmony among its parts, elicits a disinterested pleasure in a competent observer, and sustains prolonged contemplation without diminishment of that pleasure. This characterisation captures the structural, affective, and durational features that we commonly associate with the beautiful. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the decorative arts. Suppose that a stained-glass window in a parish church is shattered during a violent storm, and the fragments are reassembled at random by an untrained labourer who has no conception of design. By sheer accident, the resulting mosaic displays a formal harmony that satisfies classical proportions: the colours complement one another, the shapes interlock with pleasing regularity, and the whole composition exhibits a balanced symmetry. Competent observers, upon viewing the mosaic, report a disinterested pleasure of considerable intensity, and prolonged contemplation does not diminish their satisfaction. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to affirm that the mosaic is beautiful in the fullest sense of the term. It appears that something is wanting: the formal harmony of the object bears no intelligible relation to any content, mood, or significance that the mosaic might communicate. A genuinely beautiful object, one might observe, does not merely please by its structure but intimates a meaning or expressive quality through that very structure. Without this significative dimension, formal harmony reduces to a mere pattern of agreeable stimulation, and the distinction between beauty and pleasant decoration is lost.

definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by an object or phenomenon that overwhelms the ordinary capacities of perception, (ii) E involves an initial moment of displeasure or awe arising from the perceived inadequacy of the subject's faculties, and (iii) E culminates in an elevated feeling of the subject's rational superiority over the overwhelming object.
conditions: E is occasioned by an object or phenomenon that overwhelms the ordinary capacities of perception
E involves an initial moment of displeasure or awe arising from the perceived inadequacy of the subject's faculties
E culminates in an elevated feeling of the subject's rational superiority over the overwhelming object
counterexample: Suppose that a mountaineer, standing at the edge of an immense glacial crevasse, experiences a perceptual overwhelm at the vast depth and scale before him. He feels a sharp initial displeasure and awe at the inadequacy of his senses to take in the spectacle. He then experiences an elevated feeling — but this feeling is not one of rational superiority. Rather, it is a feeling of dissolution, of being absorbed into the immensity of nature, accompanied by a profound humility and a sense that his rational faculties are themselves dwarfed. All three conditions appear satisfied, yet the experience lacks the characteristic note of rational elevation and instead involves a surrender of the self to the overwhelming object.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the elevated feeling in stage (iii) be specifically one of rational self-assertion rather than a feeling of self-dissolution or absorbed humility, thereby failing to distinguish the sublime from experiences of mystical or pantheistic absorption in nature.
passage: One might analyse the experience of the sublime as comprising three stages: the perceptual overwhelm occasioned by a vast or powerful object, the initial displeasure arising from the subject's sense of his own inadequacy before it, and the culminating elevation in which the subject recognises his rational superiority over the merely sensuous immensity. This account captures the dynamic, self-reflexive character of the sublime and its distinctive movement from discomfort to exaltation. Yet a case which tells against the proposed definition may be drawn from the experience of mountaineers. Suppose a climber stands at the rim of an immense glacial crevasse whose depth and breadth exceed what his perceptual faculties can encompass. He feels a sharp moment of awe and displeasure at his inability to take in the spectacle. There follows an elevated feeling of great intensity. But the character of this elevation is not what the definition requires. The climber does not feel his rational powers rising above the sensuous immensity; rather, he feels himself dissolved into the landscape, absorbed by the very vastness that overwhelms him. His experience is one of profound humility and self-surrender, not of rational self-assertion. One might observe that all three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied — there is overwhelm, initial displeasure, and a culminating elevation — and yet the experience differs in kind from the sublime as traditionally conceived. The definition, by specifying only that the culmination involve an elevated feeling of rational superiority, fails to distinguish genuine rational self-assertion from the quite different phenomenon of self-dissolution or pantheistic absorption. It appears that the analysis must require not merely elevation but a specific mode of elevation in which the subject's rational autonomy is affirmed rather than surrendered.

definition: A person P possesses good taste if and only if (i) P reliably discriminates between objects of higher and lower aesthetic merit, (ii) P's aesthetic judgements are formed independently of social pressure or fashion, and (iii) P's judgements are consistent over time.
conditions: P reliably discriminates between objects of higher and lower aesthetic merit
P's aesthetic judgements are formed independently of social pressure or fashion
P's judgements are consistent over time
