counterexample: Suppose that a retired art dealer, having spent decades immersed in a single narrow tradition of Dutch still-life painting, reliably discriminates between better and worse examples within that tradition, forms his judgements with complete independence from social fashion, and maintains perfect consistency over time. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet when confronted with Japanese ink wash paintings, Indian miniatures, or modernist abstraction, he dismisses them all as devoid of merit, applying the standards of Dutch still life to every domain. We should hesitate to attribute good taste to someone whose reliable discrimination is confined to a single tradition and who lacks the breadth of sensibility required to appreciate merit in diverse forms.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P's discriminative capacity extend across a sufficiently broad range of aesthetic traditions and forms, rather than being confined to a single narrow domain in which reliable judgement has been cultivated at the expense of wider receptivity.
passage: One might propose that a person possesses good taste if he reliably discriminates between objects of higher and lower aesthetic merit, forms his judgements independently of social pressure, and maintains consistency over time. This characterisation captures the accuracy, autonomy, and stability that we associate with refined aesthetic sensibility. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the world of art connoisseurship. Suppose a retired dealer in Dutch still-life paintings has spent forty years immersed in that tradition. He can reliably distinguish a superior Heda from an inferior imitation, and his judgements are formed with complete independence from the fashions of the contemporary art world. Moreover, his assessments have remained perfectly consistent over the decades. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, when this dealer is presented with a masterwork of Japanese ink wash painting, he dismisses it as formless. Confronted with an Indian miniature of the Mughal period, he pronounces it garish. Shown a canvas by a leading abstract expressionist, he declares it devoid of merit. In each case, he applies the standards appropriate to Dutch still life — tonal subtlety, descriptive precision, compositional balance of a particular kind — to traditions that operate by entirely different principles. One might observe that we should not attribute good taste to a person whose reliable discrimination is confined to a single narrow tradition and who lacks the breadth of sensibility required to recognise merit in diverse aesthetic forms. The definition, by requiring only reliability, independence, and consistency, fails to demand that the person's discriminative capacity extend across a sufficiently broad range of traditions and modes of artistic expression.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) the creator of W experienced E during the process of creating W, (ii) W possesses formal properties that are conventionally associated with E, and (iii) competent observers of W reliably identify E as the emotion expressed.
conditions: The creator of W experienced E during the process of creating W
W possesses formal properties that are conventionally associated with E
Competent observers of W reliably identify E as the emotion expressed
counterexample: Suppose a composer, while suffering from acute melancholy, composes a funeral march. The march is written in a minor key at a slow tempo with descending melodic lines — formal properties conventionally associated with grief. Competent listeners reliably identify grief as the emotion expressed. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the composer's melancholy was caused not by bereavement or any occasion related to the work's subject, but by a severe toothache, and the funeral march was composed as a commission for a civic ceremony to which the composer was entirely indifferent. The emotion experienced during composition and the emotion the work expresses are, in this case, only accidentally connected: the toothache-induced melancholy happens to coincide with the conventional emotional character of a funeral march.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the creator's experience of E stand in a non-accidental relation to the content or expressive character of W — that the emotion experienced be directed towards or arise from the subject matter or artistic intentions embodied in the work, rather than from extraneous causes that merely coincide with the work's conventional emotional associations.
passage: One might analyse artistic expression by proposing that a work of art expresses an emotion when the creator experienced that emotion during the creative process, the work possesses formal properties conventionally associated with that emotion, and competent observers reliably identify the emotion in question. This account captures the subjective, structural, and intersubjective dimensions of expression. Yet a case which tells against this analysis may be drawn from musical composition. Suppose a composer is commissioned to write a funeral march for a civic ceremony. During the period of composition, the composer suffers from a severe toothache that plunges him into acute melancholy. The resulting march is composed in a minor key, at a slow tempo, with descending melodic lines and sombre orchestration — all formal properties conventionally associated with grief. Competent listeners, upon hearing the march, reliably identify grief as the emotion expressed. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied: the composer experienced grief, the work possesses the conventional formal markers of grief, and observers correctly identify grief as the expressed emotion. Nevertheless, one might observe that the connection between the composer's emotional state and the work's expressive character is entirely accidental. The melancholy arose from a toothache, not from any engagement with the subject of death or mourning. Had the composer been in excellent spirits, he might well have composed an identical funeral march, drawing upon his technical mastery of the relevant conventions. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the creator experience the emotion during the act of creation, fails to demand that the experience stand in a non-accidental relation to the expressive content of the work. Without this requirement, the coincidence of a toothache and a funeral commission suffices for expression, which is surely too permissive an account.

definition: A painting P represents a subject S if and only if (i) P bears a visual resemblance to S, (ii) the artist who produced P intended P to depict S, and (iii) a suitably informed viewer can identify S upon inspecting P.
conditions: P bears a visual resemblance to S
The artist who produced P intended P to depict S
A suitably informed viewer can identify S upon inspecting P
counterexample: Consider a case in which a painter sets out to paint a portrait of his friend John, who happens to be the identical twin of a man named James. The finished portrait bears a perfect visual resemblance to John, the artist intended to depict John, and any suitably informed viewer — knowing that the portrait is meant to be of John — can identify John upon inspecting it. All three conditions are satisfied with respect to John. Yet the portrait also bears a perfect visual resemblance to James, and a viewer informed that it is meant to be of James could equally well identify James in the painting. The definition cannot distinguish the portrait as a representation of John rather than of James, despite the clear intuition that it represents John and not his twin.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that there be an appropriate causal or historical connection between the subject S and the production of P — that S sat for the portrait, or that the artist worked from observations of S specifically — rather than relying solely on resemblance, intention, and recognisability.
passage: One might propose that a painting represents a subject when it bears a visual resemblance to the subject, the artist intended to depict the subject, and a suitably informed viewer can identify the subject upon inspection. This characterisation unites the three elements most commonly invoked in discussions of pictorial representation: resemblance, intention, and recognisability. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from portraiture. Suppose a painter undertakes to paint a portrait of his friend John. John happens to have an identical twin brother named James, whom the painter has never met. The painter works from sittings with John and produces a finished portrait that bears a perfect visual resemblance to John. The painter intended throughout to depict John, and any viewer who is told that the portrait is of John can readily identify him. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied with respect to John as the subject. Yet the portrait also bears a perfect visual resemblance to James. If a viewer were told instead that the portrait is of James, he could identify James in the painting with equal ease. The definition, as it stands, provides no means of distinguishing the portrait as a representation of John rather than of James. The resemblance is identical, and if the stated intention were altered, the recognisability condition would be satisfied equally for either twin. One might observe that what is wanting is a requirement that the subject stand in an appropriate causal or historical relation to the production of the painting — that John, rather than James, sat for the portrait, or that the artist worked from direct observations of John specifically. Without such a requirement, the definition cannot ground the intuitive distinction between depicting John and merely producing an image that happens to look like him.

definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E is directed towards the sensory properties of an object, (ii) E is valued for its own sake rather than for any practical consequence, and (iii) E involves a heightened state of attention.
conditions: E is directed towards the sensory properties of an object
E is valued for its own sake rather than for any practical consequence
E involves a heightened state of attention
counterexample: Suppose a wine taster at a competition focuses her heightened attention entirely upon the sensory properties of a wine — its colour, aroma, texture, and flavour. She values this experience for its own sake, taking genuine intrinsic pleasure in the sensory engagement. Her state of attention is exceptionally heightened, as the competition demands. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the experience is not an aesthetic experience in the relevant sense; it is an exercise in sensory discrimination aimed at grading and classification. The taster attends to the wine's properties not in order to apprehend any aesthetic quality — no unity of form and content, no expressive character, no imaginative engagement — but solely to identify varietal markers and detect faults.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the heightened sensory attention in E be accompanied by an imaginative or contemplative engagement with the object's form, expression, or meaning, rather than being merely an exercise in analytical sensory discrimination.
passage: One might characterise aesthetic experience as an experience directed towards the sensory properties of an object, valued for its own sake rather than for any practical consequence, and involving a heightened state of attention. This formulation captures the sensory orientation, intrinsic valuation, and attentive quality that we associate with the aesthetic. Yet a case which tells against this analysis may be drawn from the practice of competitive wine tasting. Suppose a highly trained taster at a prestigious competition directs her attention entirely to the sensory properties of a wine: its colour in the glass, the complexity of its aroma, the texture and weight upon the palate. She values this sensory engagement for its own sake, finding genuine intrinsic pleasure in the exercise of her refined discriminative faculties. Her state of attention is exceptionally heightened, as the demands of the competition require her to notice the subtlest variations in bouquet and flavour. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to classify this experience as aesthetic in the philosophically relevant sense. The taster attends to the wine's sensory properties not in order to apprehend some aesthetic quality — a unity of form and content, an expressive character, or an imaginative resonance — but solely in order to identify varietal markers, assess ripeness, and detect faults. Her heightened attention is analytical and classificatory rather than contemplative or imaginative. It appears that the definition, by requiring only sensory orientation, intrinsic valuation, and heightened attention, fails to demand that the attention be of the right kind — that it involve an imaginative or contemplative engagement with the object's form, expression, or meaning, rather than a mere exercise in trained sensory discrimination.

definition: An artefact A is a work of art if and only if (i) A is produced by a human agent with the intention of creating an object for aesthetic contemplation, (ii) A belongs to a recognised artistic medium such as painting, sculpture, music, or literature, and (iii) A is presented to an audience as a candidate for aesthetic appreciation.
conditions: A is produced by a human agent with the intention of creating an object for aesthetic contemplation
A belongs to a recognised artistic medium such as painting, sculpture, music, or literature
A is presented to an audience as a candidate for aesthetic appreciation
counterexample: Suppose a commercial sign-painter is commissioned to paint a billboard advertisement for a brand of soap. He has no intention of creating an object for aesthetic contemplation; his sole aim is to produce an effective advertisement. The billboard belongs to the recognised medium of painting. And it is presented to the public, who may certainly contemplate it. Yet no one would regard the billboard as a work of art. Now reverse the case: suppose an artist working in a wholly novel medium — arranging industrial machinery in a warehouse — intends to create an object for aesthetic contemplation and presents it to an audience. All conditions except (ii) are met, since the arrangement does not belong to any recognised artistic medium. Yet it appears plainly to be a work of art.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the possibility that works of art may be created in media that are not yet recognised as artistic, and by requiring membership in a recognised medium it excludes genuinely innovative works that extend the boundaries of art into new forms.
passage: One might propose that an artefact is a work of art if it is produced by a human agent intending to create an object for aesthetic contemplation, belongs to a recognised artistic medium, and is presented to an audience as a candidate for aesthetic appreciation. This characterisation captures the intentional, institutional, and communicative aspects commonly associated with art. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by considering artistic innovation. Suppose an artist, working in the mid-twentieth century, decides to arrange industrial machinery — conveyor belts, presses, and welding apparatus — within a disused warehouse, intending the arrangement to be contemplated aesthetically. He invites critics and the public to view the installation. His intention is clearly to create an object for aesthetic contemplation, and he presents it to an audience as a candidate for appreciation. Yet the arrangement does not belong to any recognised artistic medium: it is not painting, nor sculpture in the traditional sense, nor music, nor literature. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. Nevertheless, it appears that the arrangement is a work of art. The definition, by requiring membership in a recognised medium, cannot accommodate the very innovations by which the category of art is historically enlarged. One might observe further that a commercial sign-painter who produces a billboard advertisement satisfies condition (ii), since the billboard belongs to the medium of painting, yet we should not regard the billboard as a work of art if the painter has no intention beyond commercial efficacy. The definition thus proves both too narrow — excluding genuine innovations — and insufficiently attentive to the manner in which new media come to be recognised as artistic through the very acts of creation that the definition would exclude.

definition: A judgement of taste J is objective if and only if (i) J can be supported by reasons that any competent judge would accept, (ii) J is not determined by the personal preferences or idiosyncratic associations of the judge, and (iii) J concerns a feature of the object rather than a state of the judging subject.
conditions: J can be supported by reasons that any competent judge would accept
J is not determined by the personal preferences or idiosyncratic associations of the judge
J concerns a feature of the object rather than a state of the judging subject
counterexample: Suppose two equally competent art critics examine the same painting and arrive at conflicting judgements: one pronounces the colour scheme bold and harmonious, the other pronounces it garish and discordant. Each supports his judgement with reasons — formal analysis, historical comparison, structural observations — that the other acknowledges as legitimate. Neither judgement is determined by personal preference; both critics have demonstrated objectivity across decades of practice. And both judgements concern features of the painting itself. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied for each judgement. Yet the judgements contradict one another, and if both are objective, the definition permits objective judgements to be mutually inconsistent about the same feature of the same object.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that objective judgements of taste be convergent — that competent judges, reasoning correctly from the same evidence, tend towards agreement rather than reaching incompatible conclusions about the same object.
passage: One might propose that a judgement of taste is objective when it can be supported by reasons any competent judge would accept, is not determined by personal preference, and concerns a feature of the object. This characterisation captures the rational, impersonal, and object-directed aspects we associate with objective aesthetic assessment. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the practice of art criticism. Suppose two critics of established competence and long experience examine the same painting. The first pronounces the colour scheme bold and harmonious, citing the controlled use of complementary hues and the balance between warm and cool tones. The second pronounces the same colour scheme garish and discordant, citing the excessive saturation and the absence of tonal modulation. Each critic supports his judgement with formal reasons that the other acknowledges as legitimate modes of analysis. Neither critic's judgement is determined by personal preference or idiosyncratic association; both have demonstrated remarkable consistency and impersonality across decades of critical practice. And both judgements concern features of the painting itself — its colour scheme, tonal relations, and chromatic intensity — rather than states of the judging subject. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied for each judgement. Yet the judgements are mutually contradictory: the same colour scheme cannot be both harmonious and discordant in the same respect. If both judgements are objective, the definition permits two incompatible objective judgements about the same feature of the same object. It appears that the definition, by requiring only supportability by reasons, impersonality, and object-directedness, fails to demand that objective aesthetic judgements exhibit a tendency towards convergence among competent judges. Without such a requirement, the concept of objectivity loses its capacity to adjudicate between conflicting assessments.

definition: A natural landscape L is beautiful if and only if (i) L exhibits variety within unity — a multiplicity of elements organised into a coherent whole, (ii) L occasions a feeling of restfulness in a contemplative observer, and (iii) L is free from any features that produce disgust or revulsion.
conditions: L exhibits variety within unity — a multiplicity of elements organised into a coherent whole
L occasions a feeling of restfulness in a contemplative observer
L is free from any features that produce disgust or revulsion
counterexample: Suppose a contemplative observer gazes upon a frozen tundra stretching to the horizon: a vast expanse of white snow under a grey sky, with no trees, no rivers, no variation in terrain. The landscape is unified but possesses no variety whatsoever. It occasions a profound restfulness. It contains nothing that produces disgust or revulsion. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are met, but condition (i) is not, since there is no variety. Yet the observer, and many others, would pronounce the landscape beautiful — its beauty arising precisely from its austere, unvaried immensity.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the beauty of austerity and simplicity — landscapes whose aesthetic power derives from the absence of variety rather than from variety within unity, and whose beauty lies in the contemplative effect of undifferentiated vastness.
passage: One might propose that a natural landscape is beautiful when it exhibits variety within unity, occasions a feeling of restfulness in a contemplative observer, and is free from features that produce disgust. This characterisation captures the structural, affective, and negative conditions that many theorists have associated with natural beauty. Yet a case which tells against this analysis may be drawn from the contemplation of extreme landscapes. Suppose an observer stands upon a frozen tundra that stretches without interruption to the horizon. The landscape consists of an unbroken expanse of white snow beneath a uniformly grey sky. There are no trees, no rivers, no hills, no variation in colour or texture — nothing to introduce variety into the scene. The landscape is unified in the highest degree, but it possesses no multiplicity of elements whatsoever. Condition (i) of the proposed definition is not satisfied. Yet the observer experiences a profound restfulness in contemplating the scene, and the landscape contains nothing that produces disgust or revulsion. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are met. And the observer, together with many others who have contemplated such landscapes, would without hesitation pronounce the tundra beautiful. Its beauty arises not from variety within unity but from the austere immensity of its undifferentiated expanse — from the very absence of variety. One might observe that the definition, by requiring variety within unity as a necessary condition, excludes an entire category of natural beauty: the beauty of the stark, the austere, and the simple. It appears that a satisfactory account of natural beauty must accommodate landscapes whose aesthetic power derives from the contemplative effect of unvaried vastness, not merely from the pleasing arrangement of diverse elements.

definition: A musical passage M is expressive of sadness if and only if (i) M is composed in a minor key, (ii) M employs a slow tempo, and (iii) M uses descending melodic contours.
conditions: M is composed in a minor key
M employs a slow tempo
M uses descending melodic contours
counterexample: Suppose a composer writes a passage in a minor key at a slow tempo with consistently descending melodic lines, yet the passage is scored for bright, piercing instruments playing staccato with wide dynamic contrasts. The overall effect is not one of sadness but of eerie menace or suspense. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied, yet the passage is expressive of menace rather than sadness. Conversely, consider a passage in a major key at a moderate tempo with ascending melodic lines — none of the conditions is met — yet it is performed with a particular rubato, timbral warmth, and harmonic colouring that render it unmistakably expressive of a gentle, wistful sadness.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the overall effect of M — as shaped by orchestration, timbre, dynamics, articulation, and harmonic context — be one of sadness, rather than relying on a fixed set of individual parameters any one of which may be overridden by other musical features.
passage: One might propose that a musical passage is expressive of sadness when it is composed in a minor key, employs a slow tempo, and uses descending melodic contours. This characterisation identifies three features that are commonly associated with musical sadness and which, in many cases, do contribute to a sorrowful effect. Yet a case which tells against the proposed definition may be drawn from the practice of orchestration and performance. Suppose a composer writes a passage in a minor key at a slow tempo with descending melodic lines throughout. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet the passage is scored for high, bright instruments — piccolos and trumpets — playing staccato, with sharp dynamic contrasts and an insistent rhythmic pattern. The overall effect upon the listener is not one of sadness but of eerie menace or taut suspense. The minor key, slow tempo, and descending contours are overridden by the orchestration, articulation, and dynamic profile, which transform the character of the passage entirely. Conversely, one might consider a passage in a major key at a moderate tempo with gently ascending melodic lines — a passage that satisfies none of the three stated conditions — yet which, through a particular rubato, a warm timbral palette, and subtle harmonic inflections, is unmistakably expressive of a gentle, wistful sadness. It appears that the definition, by identifying sadness with a fixed combination of key, tempo, and melodic contour, fails to acknowledge that the expressive character of music is determined by the interaction of many parameters, and that any individual parameter may be overridden by the combined effect of the others. A satisfactory account must attend to the overall expressive gestalt of the passage rather than to any particular set of isolable features.

definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O possesses a harmonious arrangement of its parts, (ii) O elicits a feeling of disinterested pleasure in a competent observer, and (iii) the pleasure elicited by O is directed at O's perceptible form rather than at any practical use to which O might be put.
conditions: O possesses a harmonious arrangement of its parts
O elicits a feeling of disinterested pleasure in a competent observer
The pleasure elicited by O is directed at O's perceptible form rather than at any practical use
counterexample: Consider a case in which a traveller comes upon a vast and desolate mountain landscape, scarred by ice and shadow, presenting no harmonious arrangement of parts but rather a formless immensity that overwhelms the senses. The traveller experiences a disinterested pleasure that is directed entirely at the perceptible form of the scene rather than at any practical use. Yet the pleasure is not occasioned by harmony but by the very absence of it, by the landscape's capacity to exceed the boundaries of ordinary perception. The second and third conditions are satisfied, but the first is not, and yet most competent observers would unhesitatingly call the scene beautiful.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the beauty of the formless, the vast, and the overwhelming, where pleasure arises not from harmonious arrangement but from the object's capacity to exceed the limits of perceptual comprehension, suggesting that harmony is not a necessary condition of beauty.
passage: One might propose that an object is beautiful when it possesses a harmonious arrangement of its parts, elicits disinterested pleasure in a competent observer, and when that pleasure is directed at the object's perceptible form rather than at any practical use. This characterisation captures a good deal of what we ordinarily intend by the term, and it has the merit of distinguishing aesthetic pleasure from the satisfactions of appetite or utility. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the experience of natural scenery. Suppose a traveller comes upon a vast and desolate mountain landscape, scarred by ancient glaciers, presenting no harmonious arrangement of parts but rather a formless immensity that overwhelms the senses. The traveller experiences a disinterested pleasure directed entirely at the perceptible form of the scene. The second and third conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet the pleasure arises not from any harmony among the landscape's elements but from the very absence of it, from the scene's capacity to exceed the boundaries of ordinary perception. We should hesitate to deny that such a landscape is beautiful merely because its parts are not harmoniously arranged. It appears that the proposed definition, by insisting upon harmony as a necessary condition, excludes a significant class of objects whose beauty consists precisely in their formlessness, their vastness, and their power to overwhelm the faculties of perception. The definition, in short, captures only one species of beauty and mistakes it for the genus.

definition: An artefact A is a work of art if and only if (i) A is produced by a human agent with the intention of creating an aesthetic object, (ii) A employs a recognisable medium such as paint, stone, sound, or language, and (iii) A is presented to an audience for contemplation rather than for practical use.
conditions: A is produced by a human agent with the intention of creating an aesthetic object
A employs a recognisable medium such as paint, stone, sound, or language
A is presented to an audience for contemplation rather than for practical use
counterexample: Suppose that a piece of driftwood, shaped by the action of wind and sea into a form of extraordinary grace, is collected by a gallery curator and placed upon a pedestal for contemplation. The driftwood employs a recognisable material medium and is presented to an audience for contemplation rather than practical use. Yet it was not produced by a human agent with the intention of creating an aesthetic object; it was produced by natural forces alone. The second and third conditions are satisfied, but the first is not. Nevertheless, visitors to the gallery regard it as a work of art and discuss its formal properties with the same seriousness they accord to carved sculpture.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the status of an artefact as a work of art may depend upon the act of selection and presentation by an institutional authority, rather than upon the original intentions of a human maker, so that objects not produced with aesthetic intention may acquire the status of art through curatorial or institutional endorsement.
passage: One might define a work of art as an artefact produced by a human agent with the intention of creating an aesthetic object, employing a recognisable medium, and presented to an audience for contemplation. This definition has considerable intuitive appeal, for it distinguishes art from natural objects on the one hand and from practical implements on the other. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the practice of modern galleries. Suppose that a piece of driftwood, shaped by the action of wind and sea into a form of extraordinary grace, is collected by a gallery curator and placed upon a pedestal for contemplation by the public. The driftwood employs a recognisable material medium and is presented to an audience for contemplation rather than for any practical purpose. The second and third conditions are satisfied. Yet it was not produced by a human agent with any aesthetic intention whatsoever; it was produced entirely by natural forces. One might object that this is merely a natural curiosity and not a work of art. But we should observe that visitors to the gallery discuss its formal properties with the same seriousness they accord to carved sculpture, and critics write about it as they would about any exhibited piece. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon human productive intention as a necessary condition, fails to account for those cases in which the status of art is conferred not by the maker's intention but by the institutional act of selection and presentation. The curator's decision to exhibit the driftwood transforms it, in the eyes of the art world, into a work of art, and the definition has no resources with which to explain this transformation.

definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude or power, (ii) E involves an initial feeling of displeasure or inadequacy in the face of the object, and (iii) E culminates in a feeling of elevation or exaltation as the subject recognises the superiority of reason over sensible nature.
conditions: E is occasioned by an object or scene of overwhelming magnitude or power
E involves an initial feeling of displeasure or inadequacy in the face of the object
E culminates in a feeling of elevation or exaltation as the subject recognises the superiority of reason over sensible nature
counterexample: Suppose that a listener, attending a performance of a quiet and intimate string quartet, experiences an overwhelming sense of the infinite suggested by a passage of sustained harmonic tension that seems to open onto a depth beyond the notes themselves. The listener feels no displeasure or inadequacy but rather an immediate and serene exaltation, a sense of being lifted beyond the ordinary boundaries of experience. The experience culminates in a feeling of elevation, and the listener recognises something that exceeds the capacity of the senses. Yet the object is neither vast nor powerful in any obvious sense; it is a soft, contained, and intimate musical passage. The first and second conditions are not satisfied, yet the experience is recognisably one of the sublime.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the sublime as it arises from intimacy, stillness, or suggestion rather than from overwhelming magnitude or power, and it wrongly requires an initial moment of displeasure, thereby excluding experiences of immediate serene exaltation that are nonetheless genuinely sublime.
passage: One might propose that the sublime is an experience occasioned by an object of overwhelming magnitude or power, involving an initial displeasure or sense of inadequacy, and culminating in exaltation as reason asserts its superiority over sensible nature. This characterisation captures a familiar pattern in the literature on the sublime, and it has the virtue of distinguishing the sublime from the merely beautiful. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the experience of music. Suppose that a listener, attending a performance of a quiet and intimate string quartet, experiences an overwhelming sense of the infinite suggested by a passage of sustained harmonic tension. The passage is soft, contained, and intimate; it does not assault the senses with magnitude or power. The listener feels no displeasure or inadequacy but rather an immediate and serene exaltation, a sense of being lifted beyond the ordinary boundaries of experience into contact with something that exceeds the capacity of the senses to contain. The first condition is not satisfied, for the object is neither vast nor powerful in the relevant sense. The second condition is not satisfied, for there is no initial displeasure. Yet the experience is, by the testimony of the listener and the consensus of cultivated taste, recognisably an experience of the sublime. It appears that the proposed definition, by tying the sublime to overwhelming magnitude and to an initial moment of displeasure, captures only one variety of the sublime and fails to accommodate the sublime of intimacy, of stillness, and of suggestion, in which exaltation arises without any prior sense of inadequacy.

definition: A judgement of taste J is valid if and only if (i) J is made by a subject who has extensive experience with the relevant class of aesthetic objects, (ii) J is formed in a state of disinterested contemplation free from personal bias, and (iii) J agrees with the consensus of other experienced and disinterested judges.
conditions: J is made by a subject with extensive experience with the relevant class of aesthetic objects
J is formed in a state of disinterested contemplation free from personal bias
J agrees with the consensus of other experienced and disinterested judges
counterexample: Suppose that a critic of painting, possessed of vast experience, contemplates a newly exhibited canvas in a state of perfect disinterestedness. She judges it to be a work of the highest order. Every other experienced and disinterested critic concurs. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose that the painting is in fact a clever forgery, and that the consensus of experienced judges rests upon a shared misidentification of its provenance. When the forgery is revealed, the same critics revise their judgements drastically, though the perceptible properties of the canvas have not changed in the slightest. The validity of the original judgement is called into question not by any deficiency of experience, disinterestedness, or consensus, but by the discovery that the judges were responding in part to the believed identity of the painter rather than to the aesthetic properties of the work itself.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the judgement be responsive solely to the perceptible aesthetic properties of the object rather than to extrinsic facts such as provenance, authorship, or reputation, so that a consensus formed under the influence of such extrinsic considerations does not guarantee validity.
passage: One might define a valid judgement of taste as one made by an experienced subject in a state of disinterested contemplation, where the judgement agrees with the consensus of similarly qualified judges. This characterisation captures the twin requirements of competence and impartiality that we naturally associate with aesthetic authority. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of art criticism. Suppose that a critic of painting, possessed of vast experience, contemplates a newly exhibited canvas in a state of perfect disinterestedness and judges it to be a work of the highest order. Every other experienced and disinterested critic concurs without reservation. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet suppose that the painting is in fact a clever forgery, and that the consensus of experienced judges rests upon a shared misidentification of its provenance. When the forgery is revealed, the same critics revise their judgements drastically, though the perceptible properties of the canvas have not changed in the slightest. One might observe that the critics were responding not solely to the aesthetic properties of the work but in part to the believed identity of the painter, a consideration that is extrinsic to the aesthetic character of the object. The definition, by requiring only experience, disinterestedness, and consensus, fails to demand that the judgement be responsive solely to the perceptible aesthetic properties of the work. A consensus formed under the influence of extrinsic considerations such as provenance and reputation does not guarantee validity, however experienced and disinterested the judges may be.

definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E is directed at the sensory properties of an object, (ii) E is attended by a heightened awareness of the formal relations among the object's parts, and (iii) E is valued for its own sake rather than as a means to some further end.
conditions: E is directed at the sensory properties of an object
E is attended by a heightened awareness of the formal relations among the object's parts
E is valued for its own sake rather than as a means to some further end
counterexample: Suppose that a reader, engrossed in a novel, experiences a profound imaginative identification with a fictional character whose moral dilemma mirrors her own. The experience is valued for its own sake and is directed at the sensory properties of the language on the page. Yet the heightened awareness that attends the experience is not an awareness of formal relations among parts but rather an awareness of the emotional and moral significance of the narrative content. The reader is absorbed not in the arrangement of words and sentences but in the imagined world they conjure. The first and third conditions are satisfied, but the second is not, and yet the experience is paradigmatically aesthetic.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for aesthetic experiences in which the salient feature is imaginative or emotional engagement with content rather than formal awareness of structural relations, thereby excluding literary and narrative experiences that are valued for their own sake but whose aesthetic character resides in imaginative absorption rather than formal contemplation.
passage: One might propose that an aesthetic experience is one directed at the sensory properties of an object, attended by a heightened awareness of the formal relations among the object's parts, and valued for its own sake. This characterisation captures a significant range of aesthetic encounters, particularly those involving the visual arts and music. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the experience of literary fiction. Suppose a reader, engrossed in a novel, experiences a profound imaginative identification with a fictional character whose moral dilemma mirrors her own. The experience is valued entirely for its own sake; the reader has no further end in view. The experience is directed, in some sense, at the sensory properties of the language on the page. Yet the heightened awareness that attends the experience is not an awareness of the formal relations among the novel's structural parts but rather an awareness of the emotional and moral significance of the narrative content. The reader is absorbed not in the arrangement of sentences but in the imagined world those sentences conjure. The first and third conditions are satisfied, but the second is not. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to deny that this is an aesthetic experience, for it is paradigmatically so. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon formal awareness as a necessary condition, is too narrow: it captures the aesthetic experience of pattern, symmetry, and structure but fails to accommodate the equally genuine aesthetic experience of imaginative and emotional absorption in narrative content.

definition: A work W represents a subject S if and only if (i) W bears a perceptible resemblance to S, (ii) the artist who created W intended W to depict S, and (iii) a competent viewer, informed of the conventions of the relevant medium, would recognise W as a depiction of S.
conditions: W bears a perceptible resemblance to S
The artist who created W intended W to depict S
A competent viewer informed of the relevant conventions would recognise W as a depiction of S
counterexample: Suppose an abstract painter intends to depict the emotional turmoil of grief. She produces a canvas of violent red and black brushstrokes that bear no perceptible resemblance to any object, scene, or figure associated with grief. Yet when competent viewers, informed of the conventions of abstract expressionism, are told the title of the work, they recognise it as a representation of grief and report that the painting captures something essential about the experience. The second and third conditions are satisfied, but the first is not: the work does not perceptibly resemble grief in any straightforward sense. Nevertheless, it represents grief.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that representation may operate through expressive or symbolic means rather than through perceptible resemblance, so that a work may represent a subject by embodying its emotional character or by invoking conventional associations, even when no visual or auditory likeness obtains.
passage: One might define artistic representation as obtaining when a work bears a perceptible resemblance to its subject, the artist intended to depict that subject, and a competent viewer would recognise the depiction. This analysis has the merit of capturing the intuitive connexion between representation and likeness that underlies our everyday understanding of pictorial art. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from abstract painting. Suppose an abstract painter intends to represent the emotional turmoil of grief. She produces a canvas of violent red and black brushstrokes that bear no perceptible resemblance to any object, scene, or figure associated with grief in any straightforward visual sense. No face contorted in anguish appears; no funeral scene is depicted; no tears are visible. Yet when competent viewers, informed of the conventions of abstract expressionism, are told the title of the work, they recognise it as a representation of grief and report that the painting captures something essential about the experience. The second and third conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied, but the first is not: the work does not perceptibly resemble grief. Nevertheless, it represents grief, and it does so not through resemblance but through expressive means, through the emotional character embodied in the colour, gesture, and rhythm of the brushstrokes. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon perceptible resemblance as a necessary condition, excludes a significant class of representational works in which the representational relation operates through expression, embodiment, or symbolic association rather than through visual likeness.

definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) the artist who created W experienced E during the process of creation, (ii) W possesses perceptible properties that are conventionally associated with E, and (iii) a sensitive audience member, upon encountering W, comes to feel E.
conditions: The artist who created W experienced E during the process of creation
W possesses perceptible properties conventionally associated with E
A sensitive audience member upon encountering W comes to feel E
counterexample: Suppose a composer, feeling no sorrow whatsoever but rather a calm professional detachment, writes a piece of music in a minor key with slow tempo and descending melodic lines, all of which are conventionally associated with sorrow. A sensitive listener, upon hearing the piece, is moved to tears and reports feeling a profound sadness. The second and third conditions are satisfied, but the first is not: the composer felt no sorrow during creation. Yet the music undeniably expresses sorrow; to deny this would be to deny what every competent listener recognises.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly requires that the artist experience the expressed emotion during creation, failing to recognise that artistic expression may be a matter of craft and convention rather than autobiographical sincerity, so that a work may express an emotion its creator did not feel.
passage: One might propose that a work of art expresses an emotion when the artist experienced that emotion during creation, the work possesses perceptible properties conventionally associated with the emotion, and a sensitive audience member comes to feel the emotion upon encountering the work. This characterisation captures the romantic intuition that art is the overflow of genuine feeling, transmitted through the medium to the audience. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the practice of musical composition. Suppose a composer, feeling no sorrow whatsoever but rather a calm professional detachment, writes a piece of music in a minor key with slow tempo, sustained dissonances, and descending melodic lines. All of these perceptible properties are conventionally associated with sorrow. A sensitive listener, upon hearing the piece, is moved to tears and reports feeling a profound sadness. The second and third conditions are satisfied in full. Yet the first condition is not met: the composer experienced no sorrow during the process of creation. One might insist that the music therefore does not truly express sorrow, but this conclusion is difficult to sustain, for every competent listener recognises the piece as expressive of sorrow, and the composer's craft has succeeded in producing exactly the expressive effect she intended. It appears that the definition, by requiring the artist's own emotional experience as a necessary condition, confuses the cause of expression with its nature. Artistic expression may be a matter of craft, skill, and command of convention rather than of autobiographical sincerity, and a work may express an emotion its creator did not feel.

definition: A judgement of taste J is objective if and only if (i) J attributes an aesthetic property to an object on the basis of the object's perceivable features, (ii) J is such that any competent and attentive observer would arrive at the same judgement under favourable conditions, and (iii) J can be supported by reasons that appeal to the formal properties of the object.
conditions: J attributes an aesthetic property on the basis of perceivable features
Any competent and attentive observer would arrive at the same judgement under favourable conditions
J can be supported by reasons appealing to the formal properties of the object
counterexample: Suppose a competent and attentive observer judges a particular piece of Persian calligraphy to be graceful. The judgement attributes an aesthetic property on the basis of perceivable features, and it can be supported by reasons that appeal to the formal properties of the strokes, their balance, rhythm, and proportion. The first and third conditions are satisfied. Yet an equally competent and attentive observer from a culture in which a different calligraphic tradition is valued arrives at the opposite judgement, finding the same work stiff and ungainly. Both observers are competent, both are attentive, and both appeal to formal properties, yet they disagree. The second condition is not satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to conclude that neither judgement is objective; both appear to capture genuine aesthetic features of the work as perceived through different but legitimate frameworks of aesthetic sensibility.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the role of culturally shaped frameworks of aesthetic sensibility in determining what counts as a competent observer and what formal properties are salient, so that the requirement of universal convergence among competent observers is unrealisable when competence itself is partly constituted by cultural training.
passage: One might propose that a judgement of taste is objective when it attributes an aesthetic property on the basis of perceivable features, when any competent and attentive observer would arrive at the same judgement, and when it can be supported by reasons appealing to formal properties. This characterisation aims to secure objectivity by anchoring aesthetic judgement in perceivable form and requiring convergence among qualified judges. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the appreciation of calligraphy across cultures. Suppose a competent and attentive observer judges a particular piece of Persian calligraphy to be graceful, supporting this judgement by appeal to the balance, rhythm, and proportion of the strokes. The first and third conditions are satisfied. Yet an equally competent and attentive observer, trained in a different calligraphic tradition, finds the same work stiff and ungainly, supporting this contrary judgement by appeal to different formal properties that her tradition regards as salient. Both observers are competent, both are attentive, and both appeal to formal features, yet they reach opposite conclusions. The second condition is not met. One might object that one of the observers must lack genuine competence, but this response begs the question, for competence in aesthetic judgement is partly constituted by cultural training, and neither tradition can claim exclusive authority. It appears that the definition, by requiring universal convergence among competent observers, presupposes a culturally invariant standard of competence that may not exist.

definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E involves sustained perceptual attention to an object, (ii) E is accompanied by a felt sense of unity among the object's diverse elements, and (iii) E is distinguished from ordinary experience by its intensity and by the subject's absorption in the object.
conditions: E involves sustained perceptual attention to an object
E is accompanied by a felt sense of unity among the object's diverse elements
E is distinguished from ordinary experience by its intensity and absorption
counterexample: Suppose a viewer contemplates a cubist painting in which the elements are deliberately fragmented and refuse to cohere into any unified whole. The viewer sustains her perceptual attention and is intensely absorbed in the painting, attending to each fractured plane and displaced perspective with rapt concentration. The first and third conditions are satisfied. Yet the viewer does not experience a felt sense of unity among the painting's elements; on the contrary, the painting's deliberate disunity is precisely what holds her attention. The aesthetic experience consists in the apprehension of fragmentation, tension, and irresolution rather than of unity.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for aesthetic experiences whose character consists in the apprehension of deliberate disunity, fragmentation, or irresolution, wrongly assuming that all aesthetic experience involves a felt sense of unity and thereby excluding a significant class of encounters with works that exploit disjunction and incoherence as aesthetic strategies.
passage: One might define aesthetic experience as involving sustained perceptual attention, a felt sense of unity among the object's diverse elements, and an intensity that distinguishes it from ordinary experience. This characterisation captures a recognisable pattern in our encounters with harmonious and well-integrated works of art. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the experience of cubist painting. Suppose a viewer contemplates a cubist canvas in which the elements are deliberately fragmented: perspectives are displaced, forms are shattered, and the composition refuses to cohere into any unified whole. The viewer sustains her attention with rapt concentration and is intensely absorbed in the painting. The first and third conditions are satisfied. Yet she does not experience a felt sense of unity among the painting's elements. On the contrary, the painting's deliberate disunity is precisely what engages her: she apprehends the fragmentation, the tension among incompatible perspectives, the irresolution that the work cultivates as an end in itself. It appears that the definition, by requiring a felt sense of unity, is too narrow. There exists a significant class of aesthetic experiences whose character consists not in the apprehension of unity but in the apprehension of deliberate disunity, and a definition that excludes these experiences fails to capture the full range of what we recognise as aesthetically valuable.

definition: A piece of music M is expressive of an emotion E if and only if (i) M contains structural features, such as tempo, mode, and melodic contour, that are conventionally associated with E, (ii) a competent listener hearing M under normal conditions would identify E as the emotion expressed, and (iii) the expressiveness of M can be explained entirely by reference to these structural features.
conditions: M contains structural features conventionally associated with E
A competent listener under normal conditions would identify E as the emotion expressed
The expressiveness of M can be explained entirely by reference to structural features
counterexample: Suppose a piece of music in a major key with a brisk tempo and ascending melodic lines, all structural features conventionally associated with joy, is performed at a memorial service for a beloved figure. A competent listener, hearing the piece in this context, identifies the emotion expressed not as joy but as bittersweet nostalgia or dignified remembrance. The first condition is satisfied, for the structural features are conventionally associated with joy. Yet the second condition is not satisfied in the way the definition requires, for the competent listener identifies a different emotion than the one the structural features conventionally encode. The expressiveness of the music in this context cannot be explained entirely by its structural features; it depends crucially upon the circumstances of performance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the role of performance context, occasion, and extra-musical associations in shaping the expressiveness of music, so that a piece whose structural features encode one emotion may express a quite different emotion when performed in a context that transforms its affective significance.
passage: One might define musical expressiveness as consisting in the possession of structural features conventionally associated with an emotion, the identification of that emotion by competent listeners, and the complete explicability of the expressiveness by reference to those features. This characterisation has the virtue of grounding expressiveness in objectively specifiable properties of the musical surface. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the influence of performance context. Suppose a piece of music in a major key with a brisk tempo and ascending melodic lines, all features conventionally associated with joy, is performed at a memorial service for a beloved public figure. A competent listener, hearing the piece in this solemn context, does not identify joy as the emotion expressed. Instead, she hears the music as expressive of bittersweet nostalgia or dignified remembrance. The structural features remain unchanged; every note, tempo marking, and dynamic indication is exactly as the composer wrote it. Yet the expressiveness of the music is transformed by the occasion. The first condition is satisfied, for the structural features are present and conventionally associated with joy. But the competent listener identifies a different emotion, and the expressiveness cannot be explained by structural features alone. It appears that the definition, by insisting that expressiveness is entirely explicable by structural features, neglects the role of context, occasion, and extra-musical association in shaping what a piece of music expresses.

definition: An object is beautiful if and only if it possesses formal harmony, elicits a disinterested pleasure in the observer, and exhibits sensory vividness.
conditions: The object possesses formal harmony
The object elicits a disinterested pleasure in the observer
The object exhibits sensory vividness
counterexample: Consider a case in which an arrangement of grey stones along a shoreline possesses a striking formal harmony in its repetition and curvature, and elicits in the beholder a pleasure wholly free from personal interest or desire for possession. The stones further exhibit a sensory vividness owing to the play of late-afternoon light upon their wet surfaces. Yet one may hesitate to call this arrangement beautiful, for its effect upon the observer is chiefly one of melancholy and solemnity rather than any positive aesthetic commendation. The formal harmony and vivid sensation conspire to produce an experience closer to the austere or the grave than to the beautiful, suggesting that the analysis neglects the qualitative character of the pleasure involved.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the disinterested pleasure be of a specifically affirmative or approving character, rather than merely any disinterested affective response such as solemnity or melancholy.
passage: It has long been supposed that beauty may be captured by an enumeration of its necessary marks: formal harmony, disinterested pleasure, and a certain vividness of sensory presentation. One might observe that a great many objects we are disposed to call beautiful do indeed satisfy these conditions jointly. A well-proportioned facade, a melody whose intervals resolve with apparent inevitability, a landscape suffused with colour — each exhibits harmony, occasions pleasure untainted by appetite, and strikes the senses with force. Yet we should hesitate to regard this enumeration as sufficient. Suppose that one encounters, along the margin of an estuary, a procession of smooth grey stones, arranged by the tides into a pattern of gentle curvature. The arrangement is formally harmonious; the eye traces its arc with satisfaction. The pleasure one derives is disinterested, for one does not wish to own or consume the stones. And the surfaces, dampened by the retreating water, possess a vivid gleam. Nevertheless, the experience is not aptly described as an apprehension of beauty. It is, rather, an apprehension of something solemn, austere, even mournful. The conditions are met, yet the concept of beauty appears to outrun them. We are led to conclude that the analysis omits a crucial requirement concerning the positive, approving quality of the pleasure elicited.

definition: Something is a work of art if and only if it is an artefact, it is created with the intention of being presented to an audience, and it possesses an internal structure that invites interpretation.
conditions: It is an artefact
It is created with the intention of being presented to an audience
It possesses an internal structure that invites interpretation
counterexample: Suppose that a civil engineer designs an elaborate motorway interchange whose sweeping curves and layered ramps are created solely to manage traffic flow. The interchange is undeniably an artefact. It is intended to be encountered by a public — the motorists who traverse it — and thus may be said to be presented to an audience. Its complex internal structure of interlocking gradients and sight-lines invites interpretation in the sense that one may discern an organising logic and a formal coherence within it. Yet it appears forced to classify this interchange as a work of art, for its maker had no artistic purpose and its audience engages with it purely as a functional conveyance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the intention behind creation includes an artistic or aesthetic purpose, as opposed to a merely functional or practical one.
passage: The attempt to define the concept of a work of art by means of necessary and sufficient conditions has exercised philosophers for some considerable time. One familiar proposal holds that an object qualifies as art provided it is an artefact, is intended for presentation to an audience, and possesses an internal structure that invites interpretation. These conditions appear, at first inspection, to capture a wide range of paradigmatic cases: paintings hung in galleries, poems published for readers, and symphonies performed before listeners all satisfy them without difficulty. Yet one might construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Let us imagine a civil engineer who designs a large motorway interchange. The interchange is an artefact of considerable complexity. Its sweeping ramps and layered carriageways are intended to be encountered by a public, namely the motorists who daily traverse them. Moreover, its internal structure — the interplay of gradients, sight-lines, and curvatures — possesses a coherence that invites a kind of interpretation, for one may discern in it an organising logic. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet it appears most natural to deny that this interchange is a work of art. The engineer harboured no artistic intention; the motorists engage with the structure purely as a functional conveyance. The case suggests that the definition must incorporate some reference to artistic or aesthetic purpose on the part of the maker.

definition: An experience is sublime if and only if it involves an apprehension of overwhelming magnitude, produces a feeling of the inadequacy of one's faculties, and ultimately issues in a pleasurable elevation of spirit.
conditions: The experience involves an apprehension of overwhelming magnitude
It produces a feeling of the inadequacy of one's faculties
It ultimately issues in a pleasurable elevation of spirit
counterexample: Consider a case in which a traveller stands before an immense glacier whose scale dwarfs all human measure. The traveller apprehends its overwhelming magnitude and feels acutely the inadequacy of her perceptual and imaginative faculties to encompass the scene. Yet the experience terminates not in a pleasurable elevation of spirit but in a persistent, unresolved terror — a dread that does not give way to exaltation. The first two conditions are met, yet the experience seems to many observers to remain sublime despite the absence of any final pleasure, suggesting that the third condition is not in fact necessary.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires that the experience conclude in pleasurable elevation; some experiences are recognised as sublime precisely because they remain irreducibly terrifying without any compensating pleasure.
passage: Accounts of the sublime have generally converged upon three marks: an apprehension of overwhelming magnitude, a consequent feeling that one's faculties are inadequate to the object, and a final resolution in which the mind, recovering from its initial shock, experiences a pleasurable elevation. One might observe that this three-part structure captures many familiar instances. The traveller who gazes upon a vast mountain range may feel first the immensity of the prospect, then the poverty of imagination before it, and at last a peculiar exaltation. Yet we should hesitate to regard the third condition as truly necessary. Suppose that a traveller stands before an immense glacier, whose extent defeats every effort of the eye to compass it. She apprehends its overwhelming magnitude with an almost physical force. Her faculties — perceptual, imaginative, conceptual — are plainly inadequate to the object. Yet the experience does not resolve into pleasurable elevation. Instead, there persists a dread, a terror that refuses to yield to any compensating exaltation. The traveller departs shaken, not uplifted. It appears, however, that we should not on that account deny the experience the title of sublimity. Indeed, one may argue that the case is paradigmatically sublime, and that the analysis has imposed a condition — the pleasurable resolution — which belongs not to the sublime as such but only to one species of it. The insufficiency of the definition is thereby made apparent.

definition: A person has good taste if and only if she reliably identifies objects of genuine aesthetic merit, she takes pleasure in those objects, and her judgements are formed through extensive acquaintance with works in the relevant tradition.
conditions: She reliably identifies objects of genuine aesthetic merit
She takes pleasure in those objects
Her judgements are formed through extensive acquaintance with works in the relevant tradition
counterexample: Suppose that a scholar of Renaissance painting has spent decades studying the tradition and reliably identifies works of genuine aesthetic merit with remarkable accuracy. She takes pleasure in those works, savouring the mastery of technique and compositional balance. Yet her taste is widely regarded as narrow and unadventurous — she is unable to recognise merit in unfamiliar forms, dismissing modernist and non-Western art without serious consideration. Her judgements, though reliable within one tradition, reflect a rigidity that one would hesitate to call good taste in the fuller sense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require a capacity for aesthetic receptivity beyond any single tradition — that is, an openness or sensitivity that enables the recognition of merit in novel or unfamiliar forms.
passage: The notion of good taste has long been analysed in terms of reliability, pleasure, and acquaintance. One holds that a person possesses good taste when she reliably discerns objects of genuine aesthetic merit, derives pleasure from them, and has formed her judgements through extensive familiarity with works in the relevant tradition. It appears at first that these conditions capture the concept adequately. The seasoned critic who has studied a tradition closely and who takes delight in its finest achievements seems a paradigm of good taste. Yet one might construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Let us imagine a scholar of Renaissance painting who has devoted several decades to the study of that tradition. She reliably identifies the masterworks and distinguishes them from lesser productions with considerable accuracy. She takes genuine pleasure in them — the balance of composition, the mastery of chiaroscuro, the subtlety of colour. Her judgements are formed through the most extensive acquaintance one could desire. Nevertheless, her taste is widely and, one may think, justly regarded as narrow. She is unable to recognise merit in forms that depart from the tradition she has mastered. Modernist painting she dismisses without examination; the arts of other civilisations leave her unmoved. We are led to conclude that the definition omits a condition of aesthetic receptivity or openness — a willingness and capacity to apprehend merit in forms beyond the boundaries of any single tradition.

definition: An experience is aesthetic if and only if it involves sustained attention to the sensory and formal properties of an object, it is valued for its own sake rather than for any instrumental end, and it is accompanied by an affective response.
conditions: It involves sustained attention to the sensory and formal properties of an object
It is valued for its own sake rather than for any instrumental end
It is accompanied by an affective response
counterexample: Consider a case in which a gemologist examines a sapphire, attending with great care to its colour, clarity, and the geometry of its facets. She values this examination for its own sake, finding it intrinsically absorbing quite apart from its professional utility. She experiences a strong affective response — a feeling of wonder at the stone's optical properties. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the experience seems better described as one of scientific fascination or intellectual curiosity than as an aesthetic experience proper, for her attention is directed toward classifying the stone's properties rather than apprehending them as elements of a unified perceptual whole.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the attention to sensory and formal properties be directed toward apprehending them as elements of a unified perceptual or expressive whole, rather than toward analysis, classification, or enumeration.
passage: Philosophers have frequently proposed that aesthetic experience may be distinguished by three jointly sufficient conditions: sustained attention to sensory and formal properties, intrinsic valuation of the experience, and an accompanying affective response. One might observe that these conditions appear to capture a wide range of cases we should ordinarily classify as aesthetic. The gallery-goer absorbed in the play of light across a canvas, the listener rapt before a fugue, the reader who savours the cadence of a sentence — each attends to sensory and formal qualities, values the experience for its own sake, and is moved by it. Yet we should hesitate to conclude that these conditions are truly sufficient. Suppose that a gemologist examines a sapphire with sustained attention to its colour, the geometry of its facets, and the patterns of light refracted within it. She finds this examination intrinsically absorbing and values it quite apart from any professional purpose it may serve. She is, moreover, accompanied throughout by a strong affective response — a feeling of wonder at the stone's remarkable optical properties. All three conditions are met. Yet it appears that the experience is more naturally described as one of scientific fascination or intellectual curiosity than as aesthetic experience in the proper sense. The gemologist's attention, though directed at sensory properties, is oriented toward classification and analysis rather than toward the apprehension of those properties as elements of a unified perceptual whole. The case reveals that the definition requires a further condition concerning the manner in which attention is directed.

definition: A painting represents an object if and only if the painting visually resembles that object, the painter intended to depict that object, and a competent viewer would recognise the object in the painting.
conditions: The painting visually resembles the object
The painter intended to depict that object
A competent viewer would recognise the object in the painting
counterexample: Suppose that a painter intends to depict a cathedral and produces a canvas that visually resembles a cathedral in its proportions, verticality, and arrangement of forms. Competent viewers readily recognise a cathedral in the image. Yet the painting is executed in a style so heavily stylised that the forms on the canvas equally resemble a cluster of tall cypress trees — and indeed many viewers, upon being told the title, express surprise, for they had taken the painting to represent a grove. The visual resemblance is genuine but radically ambiguous, and one may question whether the painting truly represents a cathedral in any determinate sense despite all three conditions being satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the visual resemblance be sufficiently determinate to distinguish the intended object from other objects the painting equally resembles.
passage: It is natural to suppose that pictorial representation may be analysed in terms of resemblance, intention, and recognition. A painting represents an object, one might say, when it visually resembles that object, when the painter intended to depict it, and when a competent viewer would recognise it. These conditions appear to account for the standard cases without difficulty. A portrait that looks like its sitter, that was painted with the aim of depicting him, and that is recognised by viewers as a likeness of him satisfies all three requirements. Yet one might construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Let us imagine a painter who intends to depict a cathedral and produces a canvas in a heavily stylised manner. The painting's proportions, verticality, and arrangement of forms do visually resemble a cathedral, and competent viewers can indeed recognise a cathedral in the image. However, the same forms equally resemble a cluster of tall cypress trees, and a substantial number of viewers, upon being informed of the painter's intention, express genuine surprise, for they had taken the painting to represent a grove rather than a building. The visual resemblance, though real, is radically ambiguous. All three conditions are satisfied with respect to the cathedral, yet one may question whether the painting determinately represents a cathedral. The case suggests that the definition must require the resemblance to be sufficiently determinate to distinguish the intended object from other objects the painting equally resembles.

definition: A musical composition expresses an emotion if and only if the composer experienced that emotion during composition, the music possesses structural features conventionally associated with that emotion, and listeners typically attribute that emotion to the music.
conditions: The composer experienced that emotion during composition
The music possesses structural features conventionally associated with that emotion
Listeners typically attribute that emotion to the music
counterexample: Suppose that a composer, while writing a funeral march, is in a state of considerable personal contentment, having recently received joyful news. She does not experience grief or solemnity during the act of composition. Nevertheless, the march she produces possesses all the structural features conventionally associated with mourning — slow tempo, minor tonality, heavy orchestration — and listeners overwhelmingly attribute grief and solemnity to it. The second and third conditions are satisfied, yet the first is not. It appears, however, that the music genuinely expresses grief despite the composer's cheerful state, suggesting that the first condition is not necessary.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires that the composer have experienced the expressed emotion; a composition may express an emotion through its structural features and their reception regardless of the composer's actual affective state.
passage: A familiar analysis of musical expression holds that a composition expresses an emotion when three conditions are jointly satisfied: the composer experienced that emotion during the act of writing, the music possesses structural features conventionally associated with the emotion, and listeners typically attribute the emotion to the music. One might observe that this analysis accords well with many paradigmatic instances. When a composer writes out of genuine sorrow, employing the conventional markers of lament, and when audiences hear sorrow in the result, it seems natural to say the music expresses sorrow. Yet we should hesitate to accept all three conditions as necessary. Let us imagine a composer who, while engaged in writing a funeral march, is privately in a state of considerable contentment. She has received joyful news and composes in high spirits.
